Motivation: Token-wise streaming compression lacks explicit optimization for representativeness of accumulated history; need memory-efficient representations that preserve geometry and retrieval structure for long-context reasoning.

Method: Formulates KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem with joint KV representation; proposes a bicriteria objective balancing coverage in key and value spaces; adds an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion connected to log-determinant subset selection; evaluates on four open-source VLMs and five long/streaming video benchmarks.

Result: Achieves improvements over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget across diverse models and datasets; demonstrates that representative coreset selection better preserves history for streaming video understanding.

Conclusion: Representative coreset-based memory compression outperforms token-wise pruning for memory-constrained streaming VLMs, suggesting a principled direction for scalable long-context video understanding.

Abstract: Streaming video understanding with large vision-language models (VLMs) requires a compact memory that can support future reasoning over an ever-growing visual history. A common solution is to compress the key-value (KV) cache, but existing streaming methods typically rely on local token-wise heuristics, such as recency, temporal redundancy, or saliency, which do not explicitly optimize whether the retained cache is representative of the accumulated history. We propose to view KV-cache compression as a coreset selection problem: rather than scoring tokens independently for retention, we select a small subset that covers the geometry of the accumulated visual cache. Our method operates in a joint KV representation and introduces a bicriteria objective that balances coverage in key and value spaces, preserving both retrieval structure and output-relevant information. To encourage a more diverse retained subset, we further introduce an orthogonality-driven diversity criterion that favors candidates contributing new directions beyond the current selection, and connect this criterion to log-determinant subset selection. Across four open-source VLMs and five long-video and streaming-video benchmarks, our method improves over heuristic streaming compression baselines under a fixed cache budget. These results highlight that representative coreset selection offers a more effective principle, than token-wise pruning, for memory-constrained streaming video understanding.

</details>


### [28] [TurboVGGT: Fast Visual Geometry Reconstruction with Adaptive Alternating Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14315)
*David Huang,Guile Wu,Chengjie Huang,Bingbing Liu,Dongfeng Bai*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TurboVGGT introduces adaptive sparse global attention and frame-level attention in a visual geometry transformer to enable fast, scalable multi-view 3D reconstruction with competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods trade reconstruction quality for efficiency; current efficient VGTs use fixed sparsity and lack adaptive token selection, hindering performance across frames and layers.

Method: End-to-end trainable TurboVGGT with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection to capture global relationships across frames, plus frame attention to aggregate local details within each frame. Adaptive learning of representative tokens with varying sparsity levels, considering frame variability, token abstraction levels, and reliance on structurally informative regions.

Result: Experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks show fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality relative to state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Adaptive alternating attention enables efficient, scalable multi-view 3D reconstruction with maintained quality; project page provided.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods, such as visual geometry transformers, have substantially advanced the traditional per-scene optimization paradigm by enabling effective multi-view reconstruction in a single forward pass. However, most existing methods struggle to achieve a balance between reconstruction quality and computational efficiency, which limits their scalability and efficiency. Although some efficient visual geometry transformers have recently emerged, they typically use the same sparsity ratio across layers and frames and lack mechanisms to adaptively learn representative tokens to capture global relationships, leading to suboptimal performance. In this work, we propose TurboVGGT, a novel approach that employs an efficient visual geometry transformer with adaptive alternating attention for fast multi-view 3D reconstruction. Specifically, TurboVGGT employs an end-to-end trainable framework with adaptive sparse global attention guided by adaptive sparsity selection to capture global relationships across frames and frame attention to aggregate local details within each frame. In the adaptive sparse global attention, TurboVGGT adaptively learns representative tokens with varying sparsity levels for global geometry modeling, considering that token importance varies across frames, attention layers operate tokens at different levels of abstraction, and global dependencies rely on structurally informative regions. Extensive experiments on multiple 3D reconstruction benchmarks demonstrate that TurboVGGT achieves fast multi-view reconstruction while maintaining competitive reconstruction quality compared with state-of-the-art methods. Project page: https://turbovggt.github.io/.

</details>


### [29] [D2-CDIG: Controlled Diffusion Remote Sensing Image Generation with Dual Priors of DEM and Cloud-Fog](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14326)
*Zuopeng Zhao,Ying Liu,Kanyaphakphachsorn Pharksuwan,Su Luo,Xiaoyu Li,Maocai Ning*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diffusion-based remote sensing image generation with dual priors (DEM and cloud-fog) for joint terrain and atmosphere control, improving realism and utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current controllable RS image generation relies on segmentation/edge techniques that fail to capture complex terrains and atmospheric phenomena, limiting realism and applicability for large RS models.

Method: Integrates diffusion models with a dual-prior control mechanism (DEM ground prior and cloud-fog atmospheric prior). Decouples terrain and atmospheric generation via separate ground and atmospheric branches, includes a refined cloud-fog slider, and injects control signals at multiple layers during training to ensure seamless transitions.

Result: Qualitative improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism over traditional segmentation/edge-based methods; demonstrates flexible, precise generation of ground features and atmospheric effects for RS model training and downstream tasks.

Conclusion: D2-CDIG offers a flexible, precise remote sensing image generation framework leveraging dual priors to separately control terrain and atmosphere, yielding higher-quality data for training large RS models and downstream tasks.

Abstract: Remote sensing image generation provides a reliable data foundation for remote sensing large models and downstream tasks. However, existing controllable remote sensing image generation methods typically rely on traditional techniques such as segmentation and edge detection, which do not fully leverage terrain or atmospheric conditions. As a result, the generated images often lack accuracy and naturalness when dealing with complex terrains and atmospheric phenomena. In this paper, we propose a novel remote sensing image generation framework, D2-CDIG, which integrates diffusion models with a dual-prior control mechanism. By incorporating both Digital Elevation Model (DEM) and cloud-fog information as dual prior knowledge, D2-CDIG precisely controls ground features and atmospheric phenomena within the generated images. Specifically, D2-CDIG decouples the terrain and atmospheric generation processes through independent control of ground and atmospheric branches. Additionally, a refined cloud-fog slider is introduced to flexibly adjust cloud thickness and distribution. During training, ground and atmospheric control signals are injected in layers to ensure a seamless transition within the images. Compared to traditional methods based on segmentation or edge detection, D2-CDIG shows significant improvements in image quality, detail richness, and realism. D2-CDIG offers a flexible and precise solution for remote sensing image generation, providing high-quality data for training large remote sensing models and downstream tasks.

</details>


### [30] [InsightTok: Improving Text and Face Fidelity in Discrete Tokenization for Autoregressive Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14333)
*Yang Yue,Fangyun Wei,Tianyu He,Jinjing Zhao,Zanlin Ni,Zeyu Liu,Jiayi Guo,Lei Shi,Yue Dong,Li Chen,Ji Li,Gao Huang,Dong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: InsightTok presents a content-aware discrete visual tokenizer (16k codebook, 16x downsampling) that improves text readability and facial fidelity, boosting reconstruction and autoregressive generation (InsightAR).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Discrete tokenizers in autoregressive image generation are bottlenecked by aggressive downsampling/quantization and weak alignment with legibility and facial fidelity; current objectives optimize generic reconstruction, harming perceptual-critical content.

Method: Introduce InsightTok, a discrete visual tokenizer trained with localized, content-aware perceptual losses. Uses a compact 16k-codebook and 16× downsampling. Demonstrates improved text/face reconstruction and enhanced downstream generation in InsightAR.

Result: Outperforms existing tokenizers on text and face reconstruction without sacrificing general reconstruction quality. Gains transfer to autoregressive image generation, yielding clearer text and more faithful facial details in generated images.

Conclusion: Tailored, content-aware supervision during tokenizer training can substantially advance discrete image generation, especially for text and facial content, by aligning tokenization objectives with perceptual fidelity.

Abstract: Text and faces are among the most perceptually salient and practically important patterns in visual generation, yet they remain challenging for autoregressive generators built on discrete tokenization. A central bottleneck is the tokenizer: aggressive downsampling and quantization often discard the fine-grained structures needed to preserve readable glyphs and distinctive facial features. We attribute this gap to standard discrete-tokenizer objectives being weakly aligned with text legibility and facial fidelity, as these objectives typically optimize generic reconstruction while compressing diverse content uniformly. To address this, we propose InsightTok, a simple yet effective discrete visual tokenization framework that enhances text and face fidelity through localized, content-aware perceptual losses. With a compact 16k codebook and a 16x downsampling rate, InsightTok significantly outperforms prior tokenizers in text and face reconstruction without compromising general reconstruction quality. These gains consistently transfer to autoregressive image generation in InsightAR, producing images with clearer text and more faithful facial details. Overall, our results highlight the potential of specialized supervision in tokenizer training for advancing discrete image generation.

</details>


### [31] [Systematic Discovery of Semantic Attacks in Online Map Construction through Conditional Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14396)
*Chenyi Wang,Ruoyu Song,Raymond Muller,Jean-Philippe Monteuuis,Jonathan Petit,Z. Berkay Celik,Ryan Gerdes,Ming F. Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MIRAGE shows semantic environmental perturbations—not pixel glitches—can subvert HD-map based perception and planning, outperforming pixel-level attacks and bypassing defenses by using diffusion-model–driven scene variants that preserve road topology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety-critical HD maps for autonomous driving must be robust to not only pixel perturbations but also realistic semantic changes in the environment (e.g., shadows, wet roads) that can mislead mappings and planning.

Method: MIRAGE exploits the latent manifold learned by diffusion models to generate semantically mutated scenes adjacent to the ground truth with the same road topology. It targets two attacks on mapping: boundary removal and boundary injection, and evaluates robustness using nuScenes. The realism of perturbations is assessed with two independent vision-language models.

Result: Boundary removal suppresses 57.7% of detections and corrupts 96% of planned trajectories. Boundary injection uniquely succeeds in injecting fictitious boundaries; Pixel PGD and AdvPatch fail. Attacks stay potent under various defenses. MIRAGE realism: 80–84% pass as realistic vs 97–99% for clean data; AdvPatch 0–9%.

Conclusion: Semantic-level perturbations that reflect legitimate environmental variation pose a categorical gap in defenses, being harder to mitigate than pixel-level perturbations. The work advocates defenses that address semantically plausible changes to HD-map-based perception and planning.

Abstract: Autonomous vehicles depend on online HD map construction to perceive lane boundaries, dividers, and pedestrian crossings -- safety-critical road elements that directly govern motion planning. While existing pixel perturbation attacks can disrupt the mapping, they can be neutralized by standard adversarial defenses. We present MIRAGE, a framework for systematic discovery of semantic attacks that bypass adversarial defenses and degrade mapping predictions by finding plausible environmental variation (e.g. shadows, wet roads). MIRAGE exploits the latent manifold of real-world data learned by diffusion models, and searches for semantically mutated scenes neighboring the ground truth with the same road topology yet mislead the mapping predictions. We evaluate MIRAGE on nuScenes and demonstrate two attacks: (1) boundary removal, suppressing 57.7% of detections and corrupting 96% of planned trajectories; and (2) boundary injection, the only method that successfully injects fictitious boundaries, while pixel PGD and AdvPatch fail entirely. Both attacks remain potent under various adversarial defenses. We use two independent VLM judges to quantify realism, where MIRAGE passes as realistic 80--84% of the time (vs. 97--99% for clean nuScenes), while AdvPatch only 0--9%. Our findings expose a categorical gap in current adversarial defenses: semantic-level perturbations that manifest as legitimate environmental variation are substantially harder to mitigate than pixel-level perturbations.

</details>


### [32] [IG-Diff: Complex Night Scene Restoration with Illumination-Guided Diffusion Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14337)
*Yifan Chen,Fei Yin,Chunle Guo,Chongyi Li,Yujiu Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes complex nighttime datasets with simulated simultaneous illumination and other degradations, and an illumination-guided diffusion module to restore lighting while preserving texture.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of restoring night scenes affected by multiple degradations when paired data are scarce, enabling end-to-end restoration.

Method: Create complex nighttime scene datasets that simulate illumination degradation and other deterioration; integrate an illumination-guided module into a diffusion model to guide illumination restoration and preserve texture under multiple degradations.

Result: Model preserves texture fidelity while handling various degradations in low-light scenarios, demonstrating robustness on the proposed datasets.

Conclusion: Dataset and illumination-guided diffusion approach provide a viable path toward comprehensive, end-to-end restoration of complex nighttime scenes with multiple degradations.

Abstract: In nighttime circumstances, it is challenging for individuals and machines to perceive their surroundings. While prevailing image restoration methods adeptly handle singular forms of degradation, they falter when confronted with intricate nocturnal scenes, such as the concurrent presence of weather and low-light conditions. Compounding this challenge, the lack of paired data that encapsulates the coexistence of low-light situations and other forms of degradation hinders the development of a comprehensive end-to-end solution. In this work, we contribute complex nighttime scene datasets that simulate both illumination degradation and other forms of deterioration. To address the complexity of night degradation, we propose an integration of an illumination-guided module embedded in the diffusion model to guide the illumination restoration process. Our model can preserve texture fidelity while contending with the adversities posed by various degradation in low-light scenarios.

</details>


### [33] [SceneFunRI: Reasoning the Invisible for Task-Driven Functional Object Localization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14704)
*Posheng Chen,Powen Cheng,Gueter Josmy Faure,Hung-Ting Su,Winston H. Hsu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A benchmark called SceneFunRI evaluates reasoning about invisible objects in 2D, built from SceneFun3D; shows current vision-language models struggle with occluded/invisible regions; three prompting strategies analyzed; results indicate instability and need for better integration of task intent, priors, and spatial grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans infer occluded object locations using context and commonsense; current VLMs lack robust invisible-region reasoning, hindering real-world applicability.

Method: Create SceneFunRI from SceneFun3D via a semi-automatic pipeline to form a 2D spatial reasoning dataset with 855 instances. Task: infer locations of invisible functional objects from task instructions and commonsense reasoning. Evaluate with prompts: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE). Baseline: Gemini 3 Flash. Metrics: CAcc@75, mIoU, Dist.

Result: Best baseline (Gemini 3 Flash) achieves CAcc@75 = 15.20, mIoU = 0.74, Dist = 28.65; results show invisible-region reasoning is unstable in current VLMs; analysis suggests the need for integrated task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

Conclusion: Invisible-region reasoning remains challenging; future work should focus on integrating task semantics, priors, and robust search strategies to improve occluded-object localization in VLMs.

Abstract: In real-world scenes, target objects may reside in regions that are not visible. While humans can often infer the locations of occluded objects from context and commonsense knowledge, this capability remains a major challenge for vision-language models (VLMs). To address this gap, we introduce SceneFunRI, a benchmark for Reasoning the Invisible. Based on the SceneFun3D dataset, SceneFunRI formulates the task as a 2D spatial reasoning problem via a semi-automatic pipeline and comprises 855 instances. It requires models to infer the locations of invisible functional objects from task instructions and commonsense reasoning. The strongest baseline model (Gemini 3 Flash) only achieves an CAcc@75 of 15.20, an mIoU of 0.74, and a Dist of 28.65. We group our prompting analysis into three categories: Strong Instruction Prompting, Reasoning-based Prompting, and Spatial Process of Elimination (SPoE). These findings indicate that invisible-region reasoning remains an unstable capability in current VLMs, motivating future work on models that more tightly integrate task intent, commonsense priors, spatial grounding, and uncertainty-aware search.

</details>


### [34] [AnyBand-Diff: A Unified Remote Sensing Image Generation and Band Repair Framework with Spectral Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14341)
*Zuopeng Zhao,Ying Liu,Xiaoyu Li,Su Luo,Lu Li,Wenwen Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AnyBand-Diff is a physics-aware diffusion framework for spectral reconstruction in remote sensing, combining a masked conditional diffusion backbone, dual stochastic masking, physics-guided sampling, and multi-scale physical loss to ensure radiometric fidelity and spectral integrity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diffusion models optimized for natural images often violate physical laws when applied to remote sensing, causing spectral distortion and radiometric inconsistency. There is a need for physics-aware generative models that preserve spectral and radiometric properties for scientifically useful EO data.

Method: 1) Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone with dual stochastic masking to recover full spectral information from arbitrary band subsets. 2) Physics-Guided Sampling that uses gradients from a differentiable physical model to steer the denoising trajectory toward physically plausible solutions. 3) Multi-Scale Physical Loss enforcing constraints across pixel, region, and global levels in a joint objective.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that AnyBand-Diff produces reliable imagery with accurate spectral reconstruction, outperforming baselines in spectral fidelity and radiometric consistency and showcasing the practicality of physics-aware diffusion in Earth observation.

Conclusion: The work advances physics-aware generative methods for Earth observation by integrating physics-guided denoising with spectral priors, enabling robust spectral reconstruction and more trustworthy synthetic EO data.

Abstract: Existing diffusion models have made significant progress in generating realistic images. However, their direct adaptation to remote sensing imagery often disregards intrinsic physical laws. This oversight frequently leads to spectral distortion and radiometric inconsistency, severely limiting the scientific utility of generated data. To address this issue, this paper introduces AnyBand-Diff, a novel spectral-prior-guided diffusion framework tailored for robust spectral reconstruction. Specifically, we design a Masked Conditional Diffusion backbone integrated with a dual stochastic masking strategy, empowering the model to recover complete spectral information from arbitrary band subsets. Subsequently, to ensure radiometric fidelity, a Physics-Guided Sampling mechanism is proposed, leveraging gradients from a differentiable physical model to explicitly steer the denoising trajectory toward the manifold of physically plausible solutions. Furthermore, a Multi-Scale Physical Loss is formulated to enforce rigorous constraints across pixel, region, and global levels in a joint manner. Extensive experiments confirm the effectiveness of AnyBand-Diff in generating reliable imagery and achieving accurate spectral reconstruction, contributing to the advancement of physics-aware generative methods for Earth observation.

</details>


### [35] [EARL: Towards a Unified Analysis-Guided Reinforcement Learning Framework for Egocentric Interaction Reasoning and Pixel Grounding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14742)
*Yuejiao Su,Xinshen Zhang,Zhen Ye,Lei Yao,Lap-Pui Chau,Yi Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EARL is a two-stage egocentric analysis-guided RL framework that converts coarse interaction semantics into query-driven textual answers and pixel masks, achieving strong pixel grounding and grounding transfer on egocentric benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the gap in multimodal LLMs for egocentric vision where accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding are challenging, enabling better assistive robotics and embodied agents.

Method: A two-stage parsing framework: (1) coarse-grained interpretation that yields a structured textual description of egocentric interactions; (2) a response stage that generates textual answers and pixel-level grounding masks. A global interaction descriptor serves as semantic prior and is integrated via an Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) to support query-oriented reasoning. Training uses a multi-faceted reward function and GRPO to optimize heterogeneous outputs (text, boxes, masks). Evaluation on Ego-IRGBench (65.48% cIoU for grounding, +8.37% over prior RL methods) and EgoHOS (OOD grounding) demonstrates transferability.

Result: EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding on Ego-IRGBench, surpassing previous RL-based approaches by 8.37 percentage points; shows effective generalization to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios (OOD) on EgoHOS.

Conclusion: The framework successfully bridges coarse interaction semantics and fine-grained grounding/QA in egocentric vision, with strong RL-based optimization and demonstrated transferability to unseen tasks.

Abstract: Understanding human--environment interactions from egocentric vision is essential for assistive robotics and embodied intelligent agents, yet existing multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still struggle with accurate interaction reasoning and fine-grained pixel grounding. To this end, this paper introduces EARL, an Egocentric Analysis-guided Reinforcement Learning framework that explicitly transfers coarse interaction semantics to query-oriented answering and grounding. Specifically, EARL adopts a two-stage parsing framework including coarse-grained interpretation and fine-grained response. The first stage holistically interprets egocentric interactions and generates a structured textual description. The second stage produces the textual answer and pixel-level mask in response to the user query. To bridge the two stages, we extract a global interaction descriptor as a semantic prior, which is integrated via a novel Analysis-guided Feature Synthesizer (AFS) for query-oriented reasoning. To optimize heterogeneous outputs, including textual answers, bounding boxes, and grounding masks, we design a multi-faceted reward function and train the response stage with GRPO. Experiments on Ego-IRGBench show that EARL achieves 65.48% cIoU for pixel grounding, outperforming previous RL-based methods by 8.37%, while OOD grounding results on EgoHOS indicate strong transferability to unseen egocentric grounding scenarios.

</details>


### [36] [Learning with Semantic Priors: Stabilizing Point-Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection via Hierarchical Knowledge Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14346)
*Yuanhang Yao,Ping Qian,Zhu Liu,Long Ma,Weimin Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a bilevel, VFM-guided knowledge distillation framework with SCAM and dynamic reweighting to enhance point-supervised infrared small target detection (ISTD), achieving robust training and improved accuracy across backbones; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address high annotation cost and semantic gap in lightweight CNNs for ISTD under point supervision, which leads to noisy pseudo-masks and unstable optimization.

Method: A hierarchical, bilevel optimization where an inner loop adapts a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training data, and an outer loop transfers validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student. Introduces Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) to inject VFM semantics into CNN features at multiple layers, and a dynamic collaborative learning strategy with cluster-level sample reweighting to mitigate pseudo-label noise.

Result: Empirical results show consistent gains in detection accuracy and training stability across diverse ISTD backbones on challenging cases.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that freezing a Vision Foundation Model during training, combined with SCAM and dynamic reweighting, yields robust point-supervised ISTD with improved performance; code is available.

Abstract: Single-frame Infrared Small Target Detection (ISTD) aims to localize weak targets under heavy background clutter, yet dense pixel-wise annotations are expensive. Point supervision with online label evolution reduces annotation cost; however, lightweight CNN detectors often lack sufficient semantics, leading to noisy pseudo-masks and unstable optimization. To address this, we propose a hierarchical VFM-driven knowledge distillation framework that uses a frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM) during training. We formulate point-supervised learning as a bilevel optimization process: the inner loop adapts a VFM-embedded teacher on reweighted training samples, while the outer loop transfers validation-guided knowledge to a lightweight student to mitigate pseudo-label noise and training-set bias. We further introduce Semantic-Conditioned Affine Modulation (SCAM) to inject VFM semantics into CNN features at multiple layers. In addition, a dynamic collaborative learning strategy with cluster-level sample reweighting enhances robustness to imperfect pseudo-masks. Experiments on diverse challenging cases across multiple ISTD backbones demonstrate consistent improvements in detection accuracy and training stability. Our code is available at https://github.com/yuanhang-yao/semantic-prior.

</details>


### [37] [Delta Forcing: Trust Region Steering for Interactive Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14382)
*Yuheng Wu,Xiangbo Gao,Tianhao Chen,Xinghao Chen,Qing Yin,Zhengzhong Tu,Dongman Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Delta Forcing constrains teacher supervision within an adaptive trust region to mitigate conditional bias in autoregressive video generation, improving long-horizon consistency without sacrificing reactivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reactivity-stability trade-off in interactive real-time video generation; conditional bias from teacher guidance causes drift during condition changes; need mechanism to preserve global coherence while responding to new events.

Method: Delta Forcing uses a Trust-Region-inspired framework. It quantifies transition consistency via the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories, and uses this to regulate supervision strength and enforce a monotonic continuity objective, effectively constraining unreliable teacher-induced shifts.

Result: Empirical results show notable gains in temporal consistency over long horizons while maintaining responsiveness to events; robust to condition changes.

Conclusion: Delta Forcing offers a simple, effective approach to reduce conditional bias in distillation-based autoregressive video generation, enabling better long-term coherence without sacrificing reactivity.

Abstract: Interactive real-time autoregressive video generation is essential for applications such as content creation and world modeling, where visual content must adapt to dynamically evolving event conditions. A fundamental challenge lies in balancing reactivity and stability: models must respond promptly to new events while maintaining temporal coherence over long horizons. Existing approaches distill bidirectional models into autoregressive generators and further adapt them via streaming long tuning, yet often exhibit persistent drift after condition changes. We identify the cause as conditional bias, where the teacher may provide condition-aligned but trajectory-agnostic guidance, biasing generation toward locally valid yet globally inconsistent modes. Inspired by Trust Region Policy Optimization, we propose Delta Forcing, a simple yet effective framework that constrains unreliable teacher supervision within an adaptive trust region. Specifically, Delta Forcing estimates transition consistency from the latent delta between teacher and generator trajectories, and uses it to balance teacher supervision with a monotonic continuity objective. This suppress unreliable teacher-induced shifts while preserving responsiveness to new events. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Delta Forcing significantly improves consistency while maintaining event reactivity.

</details>


### [38] [Evo-Depth: A Lightweight Depth-Enhanced Vision-Language-Action Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14950)
*Tao Lin,Yuxin Du,Jiting Liu,Nuobei Zhu,Yunhe Li,Yuqian Fu,Yinxinyu Chen,Hongyi Cai,Zewei Ye,Bing Cheng,Kai Ye,Yiran Mao,Yilei Zhong,MingKang Dong,Junchi Yan,Gen Li,Bo Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight depth-enhanced Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework, Evo-Depth, augments spatial understanding for robotic manipulation without extra sensors or heavy geometry models by encoding depth from multi-view RGB and fusing it via depth-aware modulation; trained with Progressive Alignment to align depth-enhanced representations with action learning, achieving strong performance with 0.9B parameters and high efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA systems struggle with precise 3D spatial reasoning when relying on 2D visuals; explicit 3D inputs add hardware and complexity and implicit 3D models increase training cost. A lightweight, sensor-free depth representation can improve spatial grounding without sacrificing deployment efficiency.

Method: Introduce an Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. Integrate these features into vision-language representations through a Spatial Enhancement Module using depth-aware modulation. Train with a Progressive Alignment strategy to align depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning.

Result: Achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks with only 0.9B parameters. Real-world tests show the highest average success rate, and the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

Conclusion: Evo-Depth provides an efficient, depth-aware augmentation for VLA, enabling improved spatial manipulation without additional sensors or heavy models, suitable for real-time robotic tasks.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models have emerged as a promising paradigm for robotic manipulation by unifying perception, language grounding, and action generation. However, they often struggle in scenarios requiring precise spatial understanding, as current VLA models primarily rely on 2D visual representations that lack depth information and detailed spatial relationships. While recent approaches incorporate explicit 3D inputs such as depth maps or point clouds to address this issue, they often increase system complexity, require additional sensors, and remain vulnerable to sensing noise and reconstruction errors. Another line of work explores implicit 3D-aware spatial modeling directly from RGB observations without extra sensors, but it often relies on large geometry foundation models, resulting in higher training and deployment costs. To address these challenges, we propose Evo-Depth, a lightweight depth-enhanced VLA framework that enhances spatially grounded manipulation without relying on additional sensing hardware or compromising deployment efficiency. Evo-Depth employs a lightweight Implicit Depth Encoding Module to extract compact depth features from multi-view RGB images. These features are incorporated into vision-language representations through a Spatial Enhancement Module via depth-aware modulation, enabling efficient spatial-semantic enhancement. A Progressive Alignment Training strategy is further introduced to align the resulting depth-enhanced representations with downstream action learning. With only 0.9B parameters, Evo-Depth achieves superior performance across four simulation benchmarks. In real-world experiments, Evo-Depth attains the highest average success rate while also exhibiting the smallest model size, lowest GPU memory usage, and highest inference frequency among compared methods.

</details>


### [39] [Dual-Latent Collaborative Decoding for Fidelity-Perception Balanced Image Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14391)
*Qi Mao,Zijian Wang,Zhengxue Cheng,Lingyu Zhu,Siwei Ma*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MoDE introduces a dual-latent framework for learned image compression that uses a scalar-quantized fidelity-focused branch and a vector-quantized perception-focused branch, coupled by Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE) and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM) to achieve better fidelity-perception balance across bitrates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single latent representations struggle to satisfy both fidelity and perceptual realism across diverse bitrates. SQ latents excel at rate-scalable fidelity but lose perceptual details at low rates, while VQ latents preserve semantic cues but constrain structural fidelity and bitrate scalability.

Method: A dual-latent architecture with SQ and VQ branches; decoder-side modules ESE to preserve branch-specific references and CEM to enable selective transfer between experts, enabling cross-latent collaboration within a shared dual-stream bitstream.

Result: Empirical evaluations show MoDE achieves a more favorable trade-off between fidelity and perceptual realism across a wide bitrate range than distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent baselines.

Conclusion: Decoder-side collaboration between latent experts is effective for achieving wide-range fidelity-perception balanced learned image compression.

Abstract: Learned image compression (LIC) increasingly requires reconstructions that balance distortion fidelity and perceptual realism across a wide range of bitrates. However, most existing methods still rely on a single compressed latent representation to simultaneously carry structural details, semantic cues, and perceptual priors, requiring the same latent representation to serve multiple, potentially conflicting roles. This tension becomes evident across different latent paradigms: scalar-quantized (SQ) continuous latents provide rate-scalable fidelity but tend to lose perceptual details at low rates, while vector-quantized (VQ) discrete tokens preserve compact semantic cues but suffer from limited structural fidelity and bitrate scalability. To address this issue, we propose Mixture of Decoder Experts (MoDE), a dual-latent collaborative decoding framework that decomposes reconstruction responsibilities across complementary latent paradigms. Specifically, MoDE treats the SQ branch as a fidelity-oriented expert and the VQ branch as a perception-oriented expert, and coordinates them through two decoder-side modules: Expert-Specific Enhancement (ESE), which preserves branch-specific expert references, and Cross-Expert Modulation (CEM), which enables selective complementary transfer during reconstruction. The resulting framework supports selective cross-latent collaboration under a shared dual-stream bitstream and enables both fidelity-anchored and perception-anchored decoding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MoDE achieves a more favorable fidelity-perception balance than representative distortion-oriented, perception-oriented, generative, and dual-latent baselines across a wide bitrate range, highlighting decoder-side expert collaboration as an effective design for wide-range fidelity-perception balanced LIC.

</details>


### [40] [Articraft: An Agentic System for Scalable Articulated 3D Asset Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15187)
*Matt Zhou,Ruining Li,Xiaoyang Lyu,Zhaomou Song,Zhening Huang,Chuanxia Zheng,Christian Rupprecht,Andrea Vedaldi,Shangzhe Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A scalable LLM-driven pipeline generates articulated 3D assets by program synthesis against a domain SDK, enabling Articraft-10K and improving asset quality for robotics/VR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity and limited diversity of articulated 3D datasets by leveraging LLMs to autonomously generate assets at scale.

Method: An LLM writes code against a domain-specific SDK to define parts, assemble geometry, specify joints, and write tests. A restricted harness validates assets, provides structured feedback, and abstracts away URDF/complex environments. This enables automatic generation of articulated assets without manual asset authoring.

Result: The approach yields higher-quality articulated assets than state-of-the-art generators and general-purpose coding agents. Articraft-10K (>10k assets across 245 categories) demonstrates utility for training models and downstream tasks like robotics simulation and VR.

Conclusion: Programmatic LLM-assisted asset generation with a constrained harness effectively scales articulated asset creation, producing a large, diverse dataset and improving asset quality for practical applications.

Abstract: A bottleneck in learning to understand articulated 3D objects is the lack of large and diverse datasets. In this paper, we propose to leverage large language models (LLMs) to close this gap and generate articulated assets at scale. We reduce the problem of generating an articulated 3D asset to that of writing a program that builds it. We then introduce a new agentic system, Articraft, that writes such programs automatically. We design a programmatic interface and harness to help the LLM do so effectively. The LLM writes code against a domain-specific SDK for defining parts, composing geometry, specifying joints, and writing tests to validate the resulting assets. The harness exposes a restricted workspace and interface to the LLM, validates the resulting assets, and returns structured feedback. In this way, the LLM is not distracted by details such as authoring a URDF file or managing a complex software environment. We show that this produces higher-quality assets than both state-of-the-art articulated-asset generators and general-purpose coding agents. Using Articraft, we build Articraft-10K, a curated dataset of over 10K articulated assets spanning 245 categories, and show its utility both for training models of articulated assets and in downstream applications such as robotics simulation and virtual reality.

</details>


### [41] [Analogical Trajectory Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14393)
*Junho Kim,Eun Sun Lee,Gwangtak Bae,Seunggu Kang,Young Min Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A method to analogically transfer motion trajectories between 3D scenes by partitioning scenes into object-centric clusters, using hierarchical smooth map prediction with 3D foundation model features, combinatorial assembly of per-cluster maps, and refinement to produce semantically consistent, collision-free transfers. It is training-free, fast (~0.6s), and outperforms LLM/VLM/scene graph baselines; with applications in AR/VR, robotics, and content creation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable analogical spatial reasoning by translating motion trajectories across semantically similar but geometrically different 3D scenes. Challenges include variations in object placement, scale, and layout that cause collisions or distortions when naive mappings are attempted, and a large search space for point-wise correspondences that preserve semantics and functionality.

Method: 1) Partition scenes into object-centric clusters. 2) Estimate cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth map prediction using 3D foundation model features that encode context from object and open-space arrangements. 3) Combinatorially assemble per-cluster maps into an initial transfer. 4) Refine to remove collisions and distortions, yielding a spatially coherent trajectory.

Result: No-training approach with fast runtime (~0.6 seconds). Empirically outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene graph matching. Demonstrates applications in virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer.

Conclusion: The proposed spatially decomposed, hierarchically mapped, and refined transfer framework yields semantically consistent and spatially coherent trajectory transfers without training, with broad applicability to AR/VR and robotics.

Abstract: We study analogical trajectory transfer, where the goal is to translate motion trajectories in one 3D environment to a semantically analogous location in another. Such a capacity would enable machines to perform analogical spatial reasoning, with applications in AR/VR co-presence, content creation, and robotics. However, even semantically similar scenes can still differ substantially in object placement, scale, and layout, so naively matching semantics leads to collisions or geometric distortions. Furthermore, finding where each trajectory point should transfer to has a large search space, as the mapping must preserve semantics and functionality without tearing the trajectory apart or causing collisions. Our key insight is to decompose the problem into spatially segregated subproblems and merge their solutions to produce semantically consistent and spatially coherent transfers. Specifically, we partition scenes into object-centric clusters and estimate cross-scene mappings via hierarchical smooth map prediction, using 3D foundation model features that encode contextual information from object and open-space arrangements. We then combinatorially assemble the per-cluster maps into an initial transfer and refine the result to remove collisions and distortions, yielding a spatially coherent trajectory. Our method does not require training, attains a fast runtime around 0.6 seconds, and outperforms baselines based on LLMs, VLMs, and scene graph matching. We further showcase applications in virtual co-presence, multi-trajectory transfer, camera transfer, and human-to-robot motion transfer, which indicates the broad applicability of our work to AR/VR and robotics.

</details>


### [42] [SceneForge: Structured World Supervision from 3D Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14399)
*Jizhizi Li,Jiayang Ao,Danny Wicks,Petru-Daniel Tudosiu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SceneForge is an intervention-driven framework that derives structured supervision from editable 3D world states to enable consistent multimodal learning across edits and views; it yields counterfactuals, multi-view observations, and effect-aware signals by propagating explicit interventions through scene dependencies, instantiated with Infinigen and Blender to create a large, licensing-clean indoor dataset; it improves object/scene removal performance under matched budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Observation-level datasets lack explicit, consistent supervision that reflects how changes propagate through a scene; to support intervention-aware multimodal learning, supervision must be grounded in the underlying world state and its dependencies.

Method: Represent each scene as a persistent world with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies. Apply explicit interventions (e.g., object removal, camera changes) and propagate their effects through the dependency graph to render supervision that is consistent with object structure and scene-level effects. Render counterfactual observations, multi-view views, and signals such as shadows/reflections from the shared world state. Instantiate using Infinigen and Blender to produce a large, licensing-clean indoor supervision resource with thousands of counterfactual pairs and aligned annotations from >2K scenes.

Result: With matched training budgets, SceneForge supervision improves object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks, supported by both quantitative and qualitative evaluations.

Conclusion: Modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds provides a practical, scalable foundation for intervention-consistent multimodal learning.

Abstract: Many multimodal learning tasks require supervision that remains consistent across edits, viewpoints, and scene-level interventions. However, such supervision is difficult to obtain from observation-level datasets, which do not expose the underlying scene state or how changes propagate through it. We present SceneForge, an intervention-driven framework that generates structured supervision from editable 3D world states. SceneForge represents each scene as a persistent world with semantic, geometric, and physical dependencies. By applying explicit interventions (e.g., object removal or camera variation) and propagating their effects through scene dependencies, SceneForge renders supervision that remains consistent with object structure and scene-level effects. This produces aligned outputs including counterfactual observations, multi-view observations, and effect-aware signals such as shadows and reflections, all derived from a shared world state rather than post hoc image-space processing. We instantiate SceneForge using Infinigen and Blender to construct a licensing-clean indoor supervision resource with a large number of counterfactual pairs and aligned annotations from over 2K scenes, covering both diverse single-view and registered multi-view settings. Under matched training budgets, incorporating SceneForge supervision improves both object removal and scene removal performance across multiple benchmarks in both quantitative and qualitative evaluation. These results indicate that modeling supervision as structured state transitions in editable worlds provides a practical and scalable foundation for intervention-consistent multimodal learning.

</details>


### [43] [DermAgent: A Self-Reflective Agentic System for Dermatological Image Analysis with Multi-Tool Reasoning and Traceable Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14403)
*Yize Liu,Siyuan Yan,Ming Hu,Lie Ju,Xieji Li,Feilong Tang,Wei Feng,Zongyuan Ge*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DermAgent is a multi-tool, plan-execute-reflect system for dermatology that uses seven vision-language modules, external retrieval from 413k images and 3,199 guideline chunks, and a deterministic critic to provide stepwise, grounded diagnostic reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art results on five benchmarks and outperforming GPT-4o in skin-disease diagnosis and captioning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dermatology requires fine-grained visual perception plus expert clinical knowledge; existing Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) lack domain-specific grounding and are prone to hallucinations, hindering reliable diagnosis and reporting.

Method: A collaborative multi-tool agent (DermAgent) with a Plan-Execute-Reflect loop and seven specialized vision-language modules. Visual perception tools generate morphological descriptions, dermoscopic concept annotations, and disease diagnoses. A dual-modality retrieval module anchors predictions to external evidence from 413,210 diagnosed image cases and 3,199 clinical guideline chunks. A deterministic critic module performs post-hoc auditing using confidence, coverage, and conflict gates to detect inter-source disagreements and trigger targeted self-correction.

Result: On five dermatology benchmarks, DermAgent consistently surpasses state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical agents in zero-shot fine-grained disease diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning. It achieves a 17.6% absolute improvement in skin-disease diagnostic accuracy over GPT-4o and a 3.15% improvement in captioning ROUGE-L.

Conclusion: DermAgent demonstrates that a structured multi-tool, evidence-grounded, and auditable reasoning framework can substantially improve dermatology tasks while mitigating hallucinations; code is publicly available.

Abstract: Dermatological diagnosis requires integrating fine-grained visual perception with expert clinical knowledge. Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) facilitate interactive medical image analysis, their application in dermatology is hindered by insufficient domain-specific grounding and hallucinations. To address these issues, we propose DermAgent, a collaborative multi-tool agent that orchestrates seven specialized vision and language modules within a Plan-Execute-Reflect framework. DermAgent delivers stepwise, traceable diagnostic reasoning through three core components. First, it employs complementary visual perception tools for comprehensive morphological description, dermoscopic concept annotation, and disease diagnosis. Second, to overcome the lack of domain prior, a dual-modality retrieval module anchors every prediction in external evidence by cross-referencing 413,210 diagnosed image cases and 3,199 clinical guideline chunks. To further mitigate hallucinations, a deterministic critic module conducts strict post-hoc auditing via confidence, coverage, and conflict gates, automatically detecting inter-source disagreements to trigger targeted self-correction. Extensive experiments on five dermatology benchmarks demonstrate that DermAgent consistently outperforms state-of-the-art MLLMs and medical agent baselines across zero-shot fine-grained disease diagnosis, concept annotation, and clinical captioning tasks, exceeding GPT-4o by 17.6% in skin disease diagnostic accuracy and 3.15% in captioning ROUGE-L. Our code is available at https://github.com/YizeezLiu/DermAgent.

</details>


### [44] [Think When Needed: Adaptive Reasoning-Driven Multimodal Embeddings with a Dual-LoRA Architecture](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14448)
*Longxiang Zhang,Weilong Dai,Guanghao Zhang,Hao Jiang,Pipei Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Think When Needed (TWN): a unified multimodal embedding framework with adaptive reasoning; uses dual-LoRA adapters on a frozen backbone; self-supervised routing gate to decide CoT; embedding-guided RL; achieves SOTA embedding quality on MMEB-V2; parameter-efficient and reduces reasoning tokens; gating to skip CoT for simple inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal embedding pipelines based on large language models often rely on chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning, which increases model size and inference cost. Separate reasoner and embedder incur parameter overhead and CoT is produced even when unnecessary, potentially hurting performance on simple inputs. There is a need for a unified, parameter-efficient framework that adaptively applies reasoning.

Method: A dual-LoRA architecture attaches reasoning and embedding adapters to a shared frozen backbone, with gradients detached at their interface to mitigate conflicts. An adaptive think mechanism uses a self-supervised routing gate to decide per input whether to generate CoT. Embedding-guided reinforcement learning explores CoT quality beyond supervised training. Evaluated on 78 tasks from MMEB-V2.

Result: TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality while being substantially more efficient than existing generative methods: only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens compared to full generative mode.

Conclusion: Adaptive, selective reasoning within a unified, parameter-efficient framework can maintain or improve embedding quality while reducing inference cost, demonstrating the practicality of adaptive CoT in multimodal embeddings.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have emerged as a powerful backbone for multimodal embeddings. Recent methods introduce chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning into the embedding pipeline to improve retrieval quality, but remain costly in both model size and inference cost. They typically employ separate reasoner and embedder with substantial parameter overhead, and generate CoT indiscriminately for every input. However, we observe that for simple inputs, discriminative embeddings already perform well, and redundant reasoning can even mislead the model, degrading performance. To address these limitations, we propose Think When Needed (TWN), a unified multimodal embedding framework with adaptive reasoning. TWN introduces a dual-LoRA architecture that attaches reasoning and embedding adapters to a shared frozen backbone, detaching gradients at their interface to mitigate gradient conflicts introduced by joint optimization while keeping parameters close to a single model. Building on this, an adaptive think mechanism uses a self-supervised routing gate to decide per input whether to generate CoT, skipping unnecessary reasoning to reduce inference overhead and even improve retrieval quality. We further explore embedding-guided RL to optimize CoT quality beyond supervised training. On the 78 tasks of MMEB-V2, TWN achieves state-of-the-art embedding quality while being substantially more efficient than existing generative methods, requiring only 3-5% additional parameters relative to the backbone and up to 50% fewer reasoning tokens compared to the full generative mode.

</details>


### [45] [ClickRemoval: An Interactive Open-Source Tool for Object Removal in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14461)
*Ledun Zhang,Yatu Ji,Xufei Zhuang,Xinying Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ClickRemoval provides an open-source, click-guided object removal tool built on pretrained Stable Diffusion that localizes targets and restores background without masks or prompts by modulating self-attention during denoising; competitive results and open-sourcing under Apache-2.0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce reliance on manual masks or text prompts for object removal in complex scenes and improve removal precision and background consistency for non-experts.

Method: Interactive, click-based localization integrated with pretrained Stable Diffusion. No additional training, masks, or text descriptions required. The background is restored via self-attention modulation during the denoising process.

Result: Experiments demonstrate competitive performance on quantitative metrics and supportive user studies. The authors release a complete software package under Apache-2.0 at GitHub.

Conclusion: The approach offers a user-friendly, effective alternative to traditional removal tools, highlighting the viability of click-guided diffusion for object removal and encouraging adoption via open-source release.

Abstract: Existing object removal tools often rely on manual masks or text prompts, making precise removal difficult for non-expert users in complex scenes and often leading to incomplete removal or unnatural background completion. To address this issue, we present ClickRemoval, an open-source interactive object removal tool built on pretrained Stable Diffusion models and driven solely by user clicks. Without additional training, hand-drawn masks, or text descriptions, ClickRemoval localizes target objects and restores the background through self-attention modulation during denoising. Experiments show that ClickRemoval achieves competitive results across quantitative metrics and user studies. We release a complete software package at https://github.com/zld-make/ClickRemoval under the Apache-2.0 license.

</details>


### [46] [Real2Sim in HOI: Toward Physically Plausible HOI Reconstruction from Monocular Videos](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14462)
*Yubo Zhao,Yujin Chai,Yunao Dong,Chengfeng Zhao,Zijiao Zeng,Yuan Liu,Chi-Keung Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HA-HOI introduces a human-first, object-follow 4D HOI reconstruction pipeline from monocular video that yields physically plausible, simulation-ready human–object interactions. It aligns and refines the object relative to the human action and uses the resulting trajectory as a teacher for physics-based humanoid–object rollout, improving alignment, contact consistency, stability, and simulation readiness over prior monocular HOI methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular HOI reconstruction typically yields visually plausible but physically implausible trajectories lacking stable contact and functional manipulation, leading to poor performance in simulation and manipulation tasks. A dedicated interaction-aware representation is needed to recover the relation between human and object, not just their trajectories.

Method: Proposes a human-first, object-follow formulation where the human motion is recovered as the interaction anchor and the object is reconstructed with alignment/refinement relative to the human action. The resulting 4D HOI trajectory is projected into a physics-based humanoid–object simulation, where it serves as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout.

Result: Empirical evaluation on benchmark and in-the-wild videos shows improved human–object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness compared to prior monocular HOI reconstruction methods.

Conclusion: Moving from visually plausible trajectory recovery to physically grounded interaction animation, HA-HOI enables scalable demonstration of humanoid–object behavior from monocular videos and provides a path toward more robust, physics-aware HOI reconstruction.

Abstract: Recovering 4D human-object interaction (HOI) from monocular video is a key step toward scalable 3D content creation, embodied AI, and simulation-based learning. Recent methods can reconstruct temporally coherent human and object trajectories, but these trajectories often remain visual artifacts while failing to preserve stable contact, functional manipulation, or physical plausibility when used as reference motions for humanoid-object simulation. This reveals a fundamental interaction gap: HOI reconstruction should not stop at tracking a human and an object, but should recover the relation that makes their motion a coherent interaction. We introduce $\textbf{HA-HOI}$, a framework for reconstructing physically plausible 4D HOI animation from in-the-wild monocular videos. Instead of treating the human and object as independent entities in an ambiguous monocular 3D space, we propose a $\textit{human-first, object-follow}$ formulation. The human motion is recovered as the interaction anchor, and the object is reconstructed, aligned, and refined relative to the human action. The resulting kinematic trajectory is then projected into a physics-based humanoid-object simulation, where it acts as a teacher trajectory for stable physical rollout. Across benchmark and in-the-wild videos, $\textbf{HA-HOI}$ improves human-object alignment, contact consistency, temporal stability, and simulation readiness over prior monocular HOI reconstruction methods. By moving beyond visually plausible trajectory recovery toward physically grounded interaction animation, our work takes a step toward turning general monocular HOI videos into scalable demonstrations for humanoid-object behavior. Project page: https://knoxzhao.github.io/real2sim_in_HOI/

</details>


### [47] [GeoVista: Visually Grounded Active Perception for Ultra-High-Resolution Remote Sensing Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14475)
*Jiashun Zhu,Ronghao Fu,Jiasen Hu,Nachuan Xing,Xu Na,Xiao Yang,Zhiwen Lin,Weipeng Zhang,Lang Sun,Zhiheng Xue,Haoran Liu,Weijie Zhang,Bo Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GeoVista introduces planning-driven active perception for ultra-high-resolution remote sensing images, employing a global exploration plan with branch-wise local inspections and a deduplicated cross-region evidence state, achieving state-of-the-art on RS-VQA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current remote sensing vision-language models rely on single-path exploration (one-shot or sequential trajectories), which can miss global context, leave scattered tiny evidence unvisited, and duplicate evidence. A planning-driven, multi-region strategy with explicit evidence aggregation addresses these issues for large-scale UHR scenes.

Method: GeoVista builds a global exploration plan and verifies multiple candidate regions via branch-wise local inspection, maintaining an explicit evidence state for cross-region aggregation and de-duplication. It introduces APEX-GRO (cold-start supervised trajectory corpus) to reformulate UHR tasks as Global-Region-Object interactive reasoning with a scale-invariant spatial representation. An Observe-Plan-Track mechanism enables global observation, adaptive region inspection, and evidence tracking, and it adopts a GRPO-based strategy with step-wise rewards for planning, localization, and final answer correctness.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on RSHR-Bench, XLRS-Bench, and LRS-VQA. Code and dataset are publicly available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: The work demonstrates the effectiveness of planning-driven active perception for UHR remote sensing interpretation, enabling multi-region exploration with deduplication and improved global-context understanding; it offers reproducible resources for the community (code/dataset).

Abstract: Interpreting ultra-high-resolution (UHR) remote sensing images requires models to search for sparse and tiny visual evidence across large-scale scenes. Existing remote sensing vision-language models can inspect local regions with zooming and cropping tools, but most exploration strategies follow either a one-shot focus or a single sequential trajectory. Such single-path exploration can lose global context, leave scattered regions unvisited, and revisit or count the same evidence multiple times. To this end, we propose GeoVista, a planning-driven active perception framework for UHR remote sensing interpretation. Instead of committing to one zooming path, GeoVista first builds a global exploration plan, then verifies multiple candidate regions through branch-wise local inspection, while maintaining an explicit evidence state for cross-region aggregation and de-duplication. To enable this behavior, we introduce APEX-GRO, a cold-start supervised trajectory corpus that reformulates diverse UHR tasks as Global-Region-Object interactive reasoning processes with a unified, scale-invariant spatial representation. We further design an Observe-Plan-Track mechanism for global observation, adaptive region inspection, and evidence tracking, and align the model with a GRPO-based strategy using step-wise rewards for planning, localization, and final answer correctness. Experiments on RSHR-Bench, XLRS-Bench, and LRS-VQA show that GeoVista achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code and dataset are available at https://github.com/ryan6073/GeoVista

</details>


### [48] [Reduce the Artifacts Bias for More Generalizable AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14486)
*Yiheng Li,Yang Yang,Zichang Tan,Gao Li,Zhen Lei,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A detector for AI-generated images using SEF (Separate Expert Fusion): it combines two aligned fake-image domains—reconstruction-based artifacts and GAN-upsampled artifacts—via LoRA-adapted domain-specific experts on a frozen foundation model and a gating network for decoupled fusion, improving generalization across 13 benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The misuse of AI-generated images creates a need for robust, generalizable detectors. Reconstruction-based artifacts alone are limited and may not capture diverse generative patterns (e.g., GANs). Domain shift between reconstruction- and upsampling-based fakes motivates a method that leverages diverse artifact cues while avoiding inter-domain interference.

Method: Introduce a GAN-based upsampling pipeline to generate diverse aligned fake patterns; propose Separate Expert Fusion (SEF): train domain-specific experts via LoRA adaptation on a frozen foundation model, then perform decoupled fusion with a gating network to adaptively combine expert features while preserving specialization.

Result: SEF yields strong detection performance across 13 diverse benchmarks; code released for reproducibility.

Conclusion: Separating and fusing domain-specific experts with adaptive gating enhances the decision boundary's robustness and generalization, enabling effective detection across broader generative methods beyond reconstruction-based artifacts.

Abstract: As the misuse of AI-generated images grows, generalizable image detection techniques are urgently needed. Recent state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods adopt aligned training datasets to reduce content, size, and format biases, empowering models to capture robust forgery cues. A common strategy is to employ reconstruction techniques, e.g., VAE and DDIM, which show remarkable results in diffusion-based methods. However, such reconstruction-based approaches typically introduce limited and homogeneous artifacts, which cannot fully capture diverse generative patterns, such as GAN-based methods. To complement reconstruction-based fake images with aligned yet diverse artifact patterns, we propose a GAN-based upsampling approach that mimics GAN-generated fake patterns while preserving content, size, and format alignment. This naturally results in two aligned but distinct types of fake images. However, due to the domain shift between reconstruction-based and upsampling-based fake images, direct mixed training causes suboptimal results, where one domain disrupts feature learning of the other. Accordingly, we propose a Separate Expert Fusion (SEF) framework to extract complementary artifact information and reduce inter-domain interference. We first train domain-specific experts via LoRA adaptation on a frozen foundational model, then conduct decoupled fusion with a gating network to adaptively combine expert features while retaining their specialized knowledge. Rather than merely benefiting GAN-generated image detection, this design introduces diverse and complementary artifact patterns that enable SEF to learn a more robust decision boundary and improve generalization across broader generative methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method yields strong results across 13 diverse benchmarks. Codes are released at: https://github.com/liyih/SEF_AIGC_detection.

</details>


### [49] [Head Forcing: Long Autoregressive Video Generation via Head Heterogeneity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14487)
*Jiahao Tian,Yiwei Wang,Gang Yu,Chi Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free Head Forcing for autoregressive video diffusion: assigns tailored KV-cache strategies to attention heads (local/anchor/memory) with a hierarchical memory and RoPE re-encoding to mitigate error accumulation and context loss; yields minute-level long-horizon generation and multi-prompt synthesis without extra training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AR video diffusion suffers from error accumulation and context loss over long horizons and from treating attention heads uniformly. The paper seeks to align per-head roles with per-head memory strategies to improve long-range consistency and efficiency.

Method: Classifies AR video diffusion transformer heads into three types (local, anchor, memory). Applies per-head KV-cache strategies: local/anchor keep only essential tokens; memory uses a hierarchical episodic memory with dynamic updates. Introduces head-wise RoPE re-encoding to keep positional encodings within pretrained range. All training-free (no new training).

Result: Generation duration extends from ~5 seconds to minute-level; supports multi-prompt interactive synthesis; outperforms baselines on reported metrics.

Conclusion: A training-free, head-aware KV-cache strategy with hierarchical memory and per-head RoPE re-encoding can substantially extend long-horizon AR video synthesis and improve stability without additional training.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time synthesis but suffer from error accumulation and context loss over long horizons. We discover that attention heads in AR video diffusion transformers serve functionally distinct roles as local heads for detail refinement, anchor heads for structural stabilization, and memory heads for long-range context aggregation, yet existing methods treat them uniformly, leading to suboptimal KV cache allocation. We propose Head Forcing, a training-free framework that assigns each head type a tailored KV cache strategy: local and anchor heads retain only essential tokens, while memory heads employ a hierarchical memory system with dynamic episodic updates for long-range consistency. A head-wise RoPE re-encoding scheme further ensures positional encodings remain within the pretrained range. Without additional training, Head Forcing extends generation from 5 seconds to minute-level duration, supports multi-prompt interactive synthesis, and consistently outperforms existing baselines. Project Page: https://jiahaotian-sjtu.github.io/headforcing.github.io/.

</details>


### [50] [HASTE: Training-Free Video Diffusion Acceleration via Head-Wise Adaptive Sparse Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14513)
*Xuzhe Zheng,Yuexiao Ma,Jing Xu,Xiawu Zheng,Rongrong Ji,Fei Chao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free, head-wise adaptive sparse-attention framework for diffusion-based video generation that reuses temporal masks and calibrates per-head sparsity under a global budget, yielding up to 1.93x speedups at 720p with competitive quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The quadratic complexity of full attention limits diffusion-based video generation. Training-free sparse attention is attractive but current online top-p sparse methods incur mask-prediction costs and apply shared thresholds, ignoring strong head-level heterogeneity; these factors constrain speed-quality trade-offs.

Method: A head-wise adaptive framework with two plug-in components: (1) Temporal Mask Reuse—skips unnecessary mask prediction based on query-key drift; (2) Error-guided Budgeted Calibration—assigns per-head top-p thresholds by minimizing measured model-output error under a global sparsity budget.

Result: On Wan2.1-1.3B and Wan2.1-14B, the approach consistently improves XAttention and SVG2, achieving up to 1.93× speedup at 720P while maintaining competitive video quality and similarity metrics.

Conclusion: The proposed head-wise adaptive framework remedies overlooked factors in training-free sparse attention, enabling faster, quality-preserving sparse attention for diffusion-based video generation without retraining across large Video DiTs.

Abstract: Diffusion-based video generation has advanced substantially in visual fidelity and temporal coherence, but practical deployment remains limited by the quadratic complexity of full attention. Training-free sparse attention is attractive because it accelerates pretrained models without retraining, yet existing online top-$p$ sparse attention still spends non-negligible cost on mask prediction and applies shared thresholds despite strong head-level heterogeneity. We show that these two overlooked factors limit the practical speed-quality trade-off of training-free sparse attention in Video DiTs. To address them, we introduce a head-wise adaptive framework with two plug-in components: Temporal Mask Reuse, which skips unnecessary mask prediction based on query-key drift, and Error-guided Budgeted Calibration, which assigns per-head top-$p$ thresholds by minimizing measured model-output error under a global sparsity budget. On Wan2.1-1.3B and Wan2.1-14B, our method consistently improves XAttention and SVG2, achieving up to 1.93 times speedup at 720P while maintaining competitive video quality and similarity metrics.

</details>


### [51] [ArcGate: Adaptive Arctangent Gated Activation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14518)
*Avik Bhattacharya,Siddhant Dnyanesh Gole,Subhasis Chaudhuri,Alejandro C. Frery,Biplab Banerjee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ArcGate: adaptable arctangent-based gated activation with seven learnable per-layer parameters; yields improved accuracy and robustness on remote sensing benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard fixed activations (ReLU, GELU, SiLU) may not optimally shape non-linearities across layers or data distributions; an adaptive activation could better support feature learning and robustness.

Method: A three-stage non-linear transformation defines ArcGate with seven learnable parameters per layer. Implemented on ResNet-50 and ViT-B/16; evaluated on PatternNet, UC Merced, and EuroSAT (13-band MSI); includes analysis of depth-dependent gating.

Result: ArcGate consistently outperforms baselines; peak overall accuracy 99.67% on PatternNet; 26.65% performance lead over ReLU under moderate Gaussian noise (σ=0.1). Learned parameters show depth-dependent evolution with stronger gating in deeper layers.

Conclusion: ArcGate offers a robust and adaptive activation well-suited for high-resolution earth observation tasks, enabling autonomous tuning of non-linearity to data and hierarchy.

Abstract: Activation functions are central to deep networks, influencing non-linearity, feature learning, convergence, and robustness. This paper proposes the Adaptive Arctangent Gated Activation (ArcGate) function, a flexible formulation that generates a broad spectrum of activation shapes via a three-stage non-linear transformation. Unlike conventional fixed-shape activations such as ReLU, GELU, or SiLU, ArcGate uses seven learnable parameters per layer, allowing the neural network to autonomously optimize its non-linearity to the specific requirements of the feature hierarchy and data distribution. We evaluate ArcGate using ResNet-50 and Vision Transformer (ViT-B/16) architectures on three widely used remote sensing benchmarks: PatternNet, UC Merced Land Use, and the 13-band EuroSAT MSI multispectral dataset. Experimental results show that ArcGate consistently outperforms standard baselines, achieving a peak overall accuracy of 99.67% on PatternNet. Most notably, ArcGate exhibits superior structural resilience in noisy environments, maintaining a 26.65% performance lead over ReLU under moderate Gaussian noise (standard deviation 0.1). Analysis of the learned parameters reveals a depth-dependent functional evolution, where the model increases gating strength in deeper layers to enhance signal propagation. These findings suggest that ArcGate is a robust and adaptive general node activation function for high-resolution earth observation tasks.

</details>


### [52] [From Sparse to Dense: Spatio-Temporal Fusion for Multi-View 3D Human Pose Estimation with DenseWarper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14525)
*Ling Li,Changjie Chen,Yuyan Wang,Jiaqing Lyu,Kenglun Chang,Yiyun Chen,Zhidong Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A sparse interleaved multi-view input method (DenseWarper) for 3D human pose estimation that uses interleaved views at different times and epipolar geometry to exchange spatio-temporal heatmaps, achieving higher effective frame rates and lower redundancy, with state-of-the-art results on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional multi-view 3D pose estimation relies on simultaneous views and strong temporal assumptions, often neglecting temporal dependencies across frames. The goal is to increase temporal resolution and efficiency by exploiting sparse, interleaved temporal-view inputs.

Method: Introduce sparse interleaved input: images from different camera views at different times (e.g., View 1 at t, View 2 at t+δ). Propose DenseWarper that leverages epipolar geometry to exchange dense spatio-temporal heatmaps efficiently.

Result: Experiments on Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP show that using sparse interleaved inputs yields superior performance compared with traditional dense multi-view inputs and achieves state-of-the-art results.

Conclusion: The approach can theoretically increase the output pose frame rate by a factor of N for N cameras, reduces data redundancy via sparsity, and improves temporal-resolution while maintaining or enhancing accuracy.
