Abstract: In multi-view 3D human pose estimation, models typically rely on images captured simultaneously from different camera views to predict a pose at a specific moment. While providing accurate spatial information, this traditional approach often overlooks the rich temporal dependencies between adjacent frames. We propose a novel 3D human pose estimation input method: the sparse interleaved input to address this. This method leverages images captured from different camera views at various time points (e.g., View 1 at time $t$ and View 2 at time $t+δ$), allowing our model to capture rich spatio-temporal information and effectively boost performance. More importantly, this approach offers two key advantages: First, it can theoretically increase the output pose frame rate by N times with N cameras, thereby breaking through single-view frame rate limitations and enhancing the temporal resolution of the production. Second, using a sparse subset of available frames, our method can reduce data redundancy and simultaneously achieve better performance. We introduce the DenseWarper model, which leverages epipolar geometry for efficient spatio-temporal heatmap exchange. We conducted extensive experiments on the Human3.6M and MPI-INF-3DHP datasets. Results demonstrate that our method, utilizing only sparse interleaved images as input, outperforms traditional dense multi-view input approaches and achieves state-of-the-art performance. The source code for this work is available at: https://github.com/lingli1724/DenseWarper-ICLR2026

</details>


### [53] [Mitigating Mask Prior Drift and Positional Attention Collapse in Large Diffusion Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14530)
*Sujung Hong,Chanyong Yoon,Seongjae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Diagnoses why long-form generation in LDVLMs repeats and lacks grounding; offers two decoding-time fixes—Mask Prior Suppression and Monotonic RoPE Scaling—training-free and architecture-agnostic.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LDVLMs enable parallel decoding and bidirectional context but exhibit repetition and weak visual grounding in long-form outputs; need practical, training-free remedies.

Method: Identify two root causes: (1) mask-prior drift due to initialization of generation tokens as masks; (2) misalignment between positional attention bias and iterative unmasking. Propose Mask Prior Suppression and Monotonic RoPE Scaling as decoding-time strategies; no additional training required.

Result: Empirical results on general multimodal benchmarks and visual grounding tasks show improvements over baselines; robust gains on long-form description tasks; method is plug-and-play and generalizes across LDVLM architectures.

Conclusion: Decoding-time interventions can alleviate repetitive generation and degraded grounding in LDVLMs without retraining, suggesting practical, broadly applicable improvements for long-form multimodal generation.

Abstract: Large diffusion vision-language models (LDVLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models, enabling parallel decoding for efficient inference and leveraging bidirectional attention for global context. Despite these advances, their behavior under long-form generation remains underexplored. In this work, we show that existing LDVLMs suffer from repetitive generation and degraded visual grounding, and identify two underlying causes. First, repetitive generation originates from a mask token prior: since generation tokens are initialized as mask tokens, their hidden representations progressively drift toward a shared prior direction over generation steps. Second, a fundamental misalignment between the positional attention bias and the iterative unmasking process suppresses attention toward informative visual tokens, degrading visual grounding. Based on these insights, we propose a training-free approach, introducing Mask Prior Suppression and Monotonic RoPE Scaling to mitigate mask prior drift and positional attention collapse during decoding. Experiments on general multimodal benchmarks and visual grounding tasks demonstrate improvements over baseline LDVLMs, with robust gains on long-form description benchmarks. Our results show that these failures can be effectively addressed with a lightweight, plug-and-play strategy that requires no additional training and generalizes across diverse LDVLM architectures.

</details>


### [54] [PROVE: A Perceptual RemOVal cohErence Benchmark for Visual Media](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14534)
*Fuhao Li,Shaofeng You,Jiagao Hu,Yu Liu,Yuxuan Chen,Zepeng Wang,Fei Wang,Daiguo Zhou,Jian Luan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes RC (Removal Coherence): a pair of perception-aligned metrics for evaluating object removal in images/videos (RC-S for spatial consistency, RC-T for temporal consistency) and PROVE-Bench benchmarks (PROVE-M with motion-augmented paired videos and PROVE-H challenging no-ground-truth subset) to enable perceptual benchmarking; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics for object removal are misaligned with human perception: full-reference metrics favor copy-paste, no-reference metrics bias toward blur, and global temporal metrics miss localized artifacts. A perceptual evaluation framework is needed.

Method: Introduce RC-S (spatial coherence) via sliding-window feature comparison between masked and background regions; RC-T (temporal coherence) via distribution tracking in shared restored regions across adjacent frames. Build PROVE-Bench: PROVE-M (80-video paired dataset with motion augmentation) and PROVE-H (100-video challenging subset without ground-truth). Public code.

Result: RC metrics show substantially stronger alignment with human judgments across diverse image and video benchmarks compared to existing evaluation protocols.

Conclusion: RC and PROVE-Bench provide a perceptually aligned, community-benchmarked framework for evaluating object removal, with public code to foster adoption and reproducibility.

Abstract: Evaluating object removal in images and videos remains challenging because the task is inherently one-to-many, yet existing metrics frequently disagree with human perception. Full-reference metrics reward copy-paste behaviors over genuine erasure; no-reference metrics suffer from systematic biases such as favoring blurry results; and global temporal metrics are insensitive to localized artifacts within edited regions. To address these limitations, we propose RC (Removal Coherence), a pair of perception-aligned metrics: RC-S, which measures spatial coherence via sliding-window feature comparison between masked and background regions, and RC-T, which measures temporal consistency via distribution tracking within shared restored regions across adjacent frames. To validate RC and support community benchmarking, we further introduce PROVE-Bench, a two-tier real-world benchmark comprising PROVE-M, an 80-video paired dataset with motion augmentation, and PROVE-H, a 100-video challenging subset without ground truth. Together, RC metrics and PROVE-Bench form the PROVE (Perceptual RemOVal cohErence) evaluation framework for visual media. Experiments across diverse image and video benchmarks demonstrate that RC achieves substantially stronger alignment with human judgments than existing evaluation protocols. The code for RC metrics and PROVE-Bench are publicly available at: https://github.com/xiaomi-research/prove/.

</details>


### [55] [Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network for Robust Gait Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14548)
*Xiaoyun Wang,Cunrong Li,Wu Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A lightweight dual-branch Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network (LSTCN) for gait recognition that injects temporal modeling into 2D CNNs via Global Bidirectional Spatial Pooling and Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional layers, with asymmetric kernels to attend to temporal, spatial, and spatiotemporal domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current gait recognition approaches either rely on static appearance features from silhouettes or use heavy sequential models (e.g., LSTM, 3D CNNs) that are computationally demanding and require complex training. There is a need for a simpler, efficient architecture that captures intrinsic motion patterns under covariates like viewpoint, clothing, and carrying conditions.

Method: Introduce a dual-branch architecture named LSTCN that enables standard 2D CNNs to capture temporal information. Propose Global Bidirectional Spatial Pooling (GBSP) to decompose spatial features into horizontal/vertical strip-based local representations, reducing gait tensors' dimensionality and allowing temporal convolution within 2D frameworks. Develop Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional (LSTC) layers that jointly process temporal and spatial dimensions to learn strip-based gait motion patterns. Extend with asymmetric convolution kernels that independently attend to temporal, spatial, and joint spatiotemporal domains to enrich feature representations.

Result: The abstract presents the architectural concepts and expected benefits (efficiency, ability to capture temporal patterns using 2D CNNs) but does not provide empirical quantitative results or datasets within the abstract.

Conclusion: The proposed LSTCN framework offers a simple yet effective approach to incorporate temporal dynamics into 2D CNNs for gait recognition, with GBSP and LSTC layers plus asymmetric convolutions enabling richer, motion-aware representations while remaining computationally efficient.

Abstract: Gait recognition, as a promising biometric technology, identifies individuals through their unique walking patterns and offers distinctive advantages including non-invasiveness, long-range applicability, and resistance to deliberate disguise. Despite these merits, capturing the intrinsic motion patterns concealed within consecutive video frames remains challenging due to the complexity of video data and the interference of external covariates such as viewpoint changes, clothing variations, and carrying conditions. Existing approaches predominantly rely on either static appearance features extracted from individual silhouette frames or employ complex sequential models (\eg, LSTM, 3D convolutions) that demand substantial computational resources and sophisticated training strategies. To address these limitations, we propose a Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional Network (LSTCN), a structurally simple yet highly effective dual-branch architecture that endows standard two-dimensional convolutional networks with the capacity to extract temporal information. Specifically, we introduce a Global Bidirectional Spatial Pooling (GBSP) mechanism that reduces the dimensionality of gait tensors by decomposing spatial features into horizontal and vertical strip-based local representations, enabling the temporal dimension to participate in standard 2D convolution operations. Building upon this, we design a Local Spatiotemporal Convolutional (LSTC) layer that jointly processes temporal and spatial dimensions, allowing the network to adaptively learn strip-based gait motion patterns. We further extend this formulation with asymmetric convolution kernels that independently attend to the temporal, spatial, and joint spatiotemporal domains, thereby enriching the extracted feature representations.

</details>


### [56] [LiWi: Layering in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14552)
*Yu He,Fang Li,Haoyang Tong,Lichen Ma,Xinyuan Shan,Jingling Fu,Dong Chen,Luohang Liu,Junshi Huang,Yan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces an Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) pipeline to synthesize layered in-the-wild data, yielding LiWi-100k, and a shadow-guided + degradation-restoration framework for improved photometric fidelity and alpha boundary accuracy in natural image decomposition, achieving SoTA on RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address two bottlenecks in natural image decomposition: (1) scarcity of scalable layered data for real-world scenes and (2) limited modeling of object interactions, including illumination and boundary structure, in natural images.

Method: (1) ADD pipeline that coordinates agents and tools to synthesize layered data without manual intervention, producing LiWi-100k (100k+ layered, in-the-wild images); (2) a decomposition framework combining shadow-guided learning to model illumination effects and a degradation-restoration objective that supervises boundary corrections by reconstructing a clean foreground from a degraded input.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate SoTA performance on natural image decomposition, outperforming existing models in RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics.

Conclusion: Code and LiWi-100k dataset will be released, enabling further advances in natural image decomposition and layered editing of real-world images.

Abstract: Recent advances in generative models have empowered impressive layered image generation, yet their success is largely confined to graphic design domains. The layering of in-the-wild images remains an underexplored problem, limiting fine-grained editing and applications of images in real-world scenarios. Specifically, challenges remain in scalable layered data and the modeling of object interaction in natural images, such as illumination effects and structural boundary. To address these bottlenecks, we propose a novel framework for high-fidelity natural image decomposition. First, we introduce an Agent-driven Data Decomposition (ADD) pipeline that orchestrates agents and tools to synthesize layered data without manual intervention. Utilizing this pipeline, we construct a large-scale dataset, named LiWi-100k, with over 100,000 high-quality layered in-the-wild images. Second, we present a novel framework that jointly improves photometric fidelity and alpha boundary accuracy. Specifically, shadow-guided learning explicitly models the illumination effects, and degradation-restoration objective provides boundary-correction supervision by recovering clean foreground image from degraded one. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art (SoTA) performance in natural image decomposition, outperforming existing models in RGB L1 and Alpha IoU metrics. We will soon release our code and dataset.

</details>


### [57] [SpectraFlow: Unifying Structural Pretraining and Frequency Adaptation for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14566)
*Zhiquan Chen,Haitao Wang,Guowei Zou,Hejun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage framework for low-data medical image segmentation that pretrains a structure-aware encoder via Mixed-Domain MeanFlow with latent transport guidance and Dispersive Loss, then fine-tunes with a boundary-focused lightweight decoder using Direct Attentional Fusion and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution, achieving improved robustness and sharper boundaries across ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome poor generalization and ambiguous boundaries in low-data medical segmentation by moving beyond texture-biased self-supervised pretraining toward geometry-aware representations that preserve topological structure and boundaries.

Method: Stage-1: Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space using latent transport regression; masks serve as conditional structural guidance rather than explicit prediction targets; Dispersive Loss is introduced to stabilize training and prevent representation collapse. Stage-2: Pretrained encoder is fine-tuned with a lightweight decoder comprising Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution to refine high-frequency boundary information under appearance variation.

Result: Demonstrates consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data scenarios and sharper boundary delineation on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS datasets.

Conclusion: Coupling structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding yields more accurate, geometry-consistent segmentation under data scarcity and appearance variation, suggesting strong potential for generalization to other medical imaging modalities.

Abstract: Medical image segmentation remains challenging in low-data regimes, where scarce annotations often yield poor generalization and ambiguous boundaries with missing fine structures. Recent self-supervised pretraining has improved transferability, but it often exhibits a texture bias. In contrast, accurate segmentation is inherently geometry-aware and depends on both topological consistency and precise boundary preservation. To address this problem, we propose a two-stage framework that couples structure-aware encoder pretraining with boundary-oriented decoding. In Stage-1, we aim to learn structure-aware representations for downstream segmentation in low-data regimes. To this end, we propose Mixed-Domain MeanFlow Pretraining, which aligns images and binary masks in a shared latent space through latent transport regression, where masks act as conditional structural guidance rather than prediction targets, making the pretraining task-agnostic. To further improve training stability under scarce supervision, we incorporate a lightweight Dispersive Loss to prevent representation collapse. In Stage-2, we fine-tune the pretrained encoder with a lightweight decoder that combines Direct Attentional Fusion for adaptive cross-scale gating and Frequency-Directional Dynamic Convolution for high-frequency boundary refinement under appearance variation. Experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, and GlaS demonstrate consistent gains over state-of-the-art methods, with improved robustness in low-data settings and sharper boundary delineation.

</details>


### [58] [Bridging Brain and Semantics: A Hierarchical Framework for Semantically Enhanced fMRI-to-Video Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14569)
*Yujie Wei,Chenglong Ma,Jianxiong Gao,Chenhui Wang,Shiwei Zhang,Biao Gong,Shuai Tan,Hangjie Yuan,Hongming Shan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CineNeuron presents a two-stage fMRI-to-video reconstruction framework with a bottom-up semantic enrichment mapping fMRI to a rich, multimodal embedding, and a top-down memory integration using a Mixture-of-Memories to fuse prior memories, achieving state-of-the-art on two benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and rich video content by leveraging richer, video-relevant cues (actions, objects) and prior knowledge, inspired by dual-pathway brain processing.

Method: Bottom-up stage: maps fMRI to a rich embedding space capturing textual semantics, image contents, action concepts, and object categories. Top-down stage: Mixture-of-Memories dynamically selects relevant past memories and fuses them with the fMRI embedding to refine video reconstruction.

Result: Extensive experiments on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks show CineNeuron outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple metrics.

Conclusion: A two-stage, semantically enriched, memory-guided architecture effectively narrows the semantic gap in fMRI-to-video reconstruction and yields superior video reconstructions, with potential for broader multimodal neuromonitoring applications.

Abstract: Reconstructing dynamic visual experiences as videos from functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) is pivotal for advancing the understanding of neural processes. However, current fMRI-to-video reconstruction methods are hindered by a semantic gap between noisy fMRI signals and the rich content of videos, stemming from a reliance on incomplete semantic embeddings that neither capture video-specific cues (e.g., actions) nor integrate prior knowledge. To this end, we draw inspiration from the dual-pathway processing mechanism in human brain and introduce CineNeuron, a novel hierarchical framework for semantically enhanced video reconstruction from fMRI signals with two synergistic stages. First, a bottom-up semantic enrichment stage maps fMRI signals to a rich embedding space that comprehensively captures textual semantics, image contents, action concepts, and object categories. Second, a top-down memory integration stage utilizes the proposed Mixture-of-Memories method to dynamically select relevant "memories" from previously seen data and fuse them with the fMRI embedding to refine the video reconstruction. Extensive experimental results on two fMRI-to-video benchmarks demonstrate that CineNeuron surpasses state-of-the-art methods across various metrics.

</details>


### [59] [Med-DisSeg: Dispersion-Driven Representation Learning for Fine-Grained Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14579)
*Zhiquan Chen,Haitao Wang,Guowei Zou,Hejun Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Med DisSeg introduces a dispersion-driven loss with adaptive attention to improve representation learning and multi-scale decoding, achieving state-of-the-art results across multiple medical imaging datasets and tasks with low overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Segmentation under heterogeneous appearance, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability suffers from representation collapse during encoding and insufficient fine-grained multi-scale decoding; similar intensity patterns blur activations and hinder separation.

Method: Propose Med DisSeg combining Dispersive Loss (treats in-batch hidden representations as negative pairs to enlarge inter-sample margins and produce boundary-aware embeddings with negligible overhead) with adaptive attention for fine-grained structure segmentation. The encoder strengthens structure-sensitive responses; the decoder performs adaptive multi-scale calibration to preserve complementary local texture and global shape information.

Result: Extensive experiments on five datasets across three imaging modalities show consistent state-of-the-art performance; competitive results on multi-organ CT segmentation, indicating robustness and cross-task applicability.

Conclusion: Dispersive embeddings plus adaptive attention yield improved segmentation robustness and accuracy with minimal overhead, demonstrated across diverse datasets and imaging modalities.

Abstract: Accurate medical image segmentation is fundamental to precision medicine, yet robust delineation remains challenging under heterogeneous appearances, ambiguous boundaries, and large anatomical variability. Similar intensity and texture patterns between targets and surrounding tissues often lead to blurred activations and unreliable separation. We attribute these failures to representation collapse during encoding and insufficient fine grained multi scale decoding. To address these issues, we propose Med DisSeg, a dispersion driven medical image segmentation framework that jointly improves representation learning and anatomical delineation. Med DisSeg combines a lightweight Dispersive Loss with adaptive attention for fine grained structure segmentation. The Dispersive Loss enlarges inter sample margins by treating in batch hidden representations as negative pairs, producing well dispersed and boundary aware embeddings with negligible overhead. Based on these enhanced representations, the encoder strengthens structure sensitive responses, while the decoder performs adaptive multi scale calibration to preserve complementary local texture and global shape information. Extensive experiments on five datasets spanning three imaging modalities demonstrate consistent state of the art performance. Moreover, Med DisSeg achieves competitive results on multi organ CT segmentation, supporting its robustness and cross task applicability.

</details>


### [60] [A Picture is Worth a Thousand Words? An Empirical Study of Aggregation Strategies for Visual Financial Document Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14581)
*Ho Hung Lim,Yi Yang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Single-vector aggregation of patch-based visual representations in Visual RAG obscures semantic changes in financial documents; patch-level analyses reveal critical distinctions lost during aggregation; global texture dominance drives the failure, posing risks for retrieval accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether aggregating hundreds of patch tokens into a single vector preserves essential semantic information in financial documents, where small changes (e.g., in digits) can alter meaning.

Method: Construct a diagnostic benchmark using financial documents with digit-level perturbations; compare patch-level representations to single-vector aggregations across models, embeddings, and mitigation strategies; analyze the role of global texture; evaluate consistency across model scales and retrieval-optimized embeddings.

Result: Single-vector representations collapse distinct documents into nearly identical vectors; patch-level features detect semantic changes that aggregation hides; this behavior persists across model scales and embedding types; mitigation strategies tested do not fully resolve the issue; global texture dominance identified as the root cause.

Conclusion: Caution is warranted against single-vector visual document retrieval for financial applications. Patch-level or multi-vector strategies may be necessary to preserve critical information; further work needed to develop robust aggregation or alternative indexing schemes.

Abstract: Visual RAG has offered an alternative to traditional RAG. It treats documents as images and uses vision encoders to obtain vision patch tokens. However, hundreds of patch tokens per document create retrieval and storage challenges in a vector database. Practical deployment requires aggregating them into a single vector. This raises a critical question: does single-vector aggregation lose key information in financial documents? We develop a diagnostic benchmark using financial documents where changes in single digits can lead to significant semantic shifts. Our experiments show that single-vector aggregation collapses different documents with almost identical vectors. Metrics show that the patch level detects semantic changes, and confirm that aggregation obscures these details. We identify global texture dominance as the root cause. Our findings are consistent across model scales, retrieval-optimized embeddings, and multiple mitigation strategies, highlighting significant risks for single-vector visual document retrieval in financial applications.

</details>


### [61] [FedStain: Modeling Higher-Order Stain Statistics for Federated Domain Generalization in Computational Pathology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14590)
*Fengyi Zhang,Junya Zhang,Wenzhuo Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FedStain: a federated domain-generalization (FedDG) framework for robust whole-slide image analysis that models higher-order stain statistics (skewness and kurtosis) in a privacy-preserving manner, coupled with a contrastive cross-site aggregation strategy; achieves state-of-the-art improvements on Camelyon17 and a new MvMidog-Fed benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-institutional stain heterogeneity in pathology images causes performance drops. Existing FedL/FedDG methods rely on low-order (Gaussian-like) statistics and pixel-level data sharing is restricted by privacy regulations. Higher-order, non-Gaussian stain characteristics (skewness, kurtosis) are underexplored but critical for robust generalization.

Method: FedStain exchanges compact descriptors capturing higher-order stain moments (skewness and kurtosis) across sites during federated optimization, avoiding pixel-data transmission. It also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations, enabling a global model to capture non-Gaussian variability in staining.

Result: Empirical evaluation on Camelyon17 and the newly introduced MvMidog-Fed benchmark shows FedStain improves accuracy relative to strong FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to 3.9 percentage points, indicating better handling of non-Gaussian stain variations.

Conclusion: FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling more robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology.

Abstract: Robust whole-slide image (WSI) analysis under strict data-governance remains challenging due to substantial cross-institutional stain heterogeneity. Domain generalization (DG) mitigates these shifts but typically requires centralized data, conflicting with privacy regulations. Federated learning (FedL) provides a decentralized alternative; however, existing FedL and federated DG (FedDG) approaches rely almost exclusively on low-order statistics, assuming Gaussian-like stain distributions. In contrast, real-world staining processes often produce asymmetric, heavy-tailed color distributions due to biochemical diffusion and scanner nonlinearity. Consequently, current methods fail to model the higher-order, non-Gaussian characteristics dominating real-world stain variability. To address this, we propose FedStain, a stain-aware FedDG framework explicitly incorporating higher-order stain moments--skewness and kurtosis--as compact statistical descriptors exchanged during federated optimization. These descriptors require no pixel-level data transmission, preserving strict privacy and communication efficiency, while enabling the global model to capture stain variability missed by low-order statistics. FedStain also employs a contrastive, cross-site parameter aggregation strategy to promote stain-invariant representations without relaxing data constraints. Extensive experiments on Camelyon17 and our new MvMidog-Fed benchmark show FedStain yields consistent improvements, outperforming state-of-the-art FedL, DG, and FedDG baselines by up to +3.9% absolute accuracy. To our knowledge, FedStain is the first FedDG approach to explicitly model higher-order stain statistics, enabling robust cross-institutional deployment in computational pathology.

</details>


### [62] [TOPOS: High-Fidelity and Efficient Industry-Grade 3D Head Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14594)
*Bojun Xiong,Zoubin Bi,Xinghui Peng,Yunmu Wang,Junchen Deng,Jun Liang,Jing Li,Bowen Cai,Huan Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: TOPOS: a single-image conditioned 3D head generation framework that enforces a fixed studio topology, using TOPOS-VAE with a Perceiver Resampler to map diverse input topologies to a reference topology, a rectified flow transformer TOPOS-DiT for high-fidelity mesh generation, and TOPOS-Texture for relightable UV maps; achieves state-of-the-art results with consistent vertex correspondence for production-ready digital humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial pipelines require a fixed, production-friendly topology for rigging, skinning, and reuse. General 3D generative models produce inconsistent topology and excessive vertex counts, hindering semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse. A single-image conditioned pipeline with topology consistency is needed.

Method: TOPOS-VAE is a variational autoencoder that uses a Perceiver Resampler to convert input point clouds from diverse topologies into the target reference topology, aligning geometry for a unified latent space. Built on TOPOS-VAE, TOPOS-DiT is a rectified flow transformer that generates high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. TOPOS-Texture produces relightable UV texture maps by fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model, with textures spatially aligned to the mesh. The framework emphasizes a fixed topology for vertex-level correspondence across outputs and production-oriented rigging compatibility.

Result: Empirical results claim state-of-the-art performance in 3D head generation, outperforming classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, with extensive experiments validating the approach for high-fidelity digital human creation.

Conclusion: TOPOS provides an end-to-end, topology-consistent 3D head generation pipeline conditioned on a single image, enabling asset-level reuse, precise vertex correspondence, and relightable textures suitable for film, animation, and games.

Abstract: High-fidelity 3D head generation plays a crucial role in the film, animation and video game industries. In industrial pipelines, studios typically enforce a fixed reference topology across all head assets, as such a clean and uniform topology is a prerequisite for production-level rigging, skinning and animation. In this paper, we present TOPOS, a framework tailored for single image conditioned 3D head generation that jointly recovers geometry and appearance under such an industry-standard topology. In contrast to general 3D generative models which produce triangle meshes with inconsistent topology and numerous vertices, hindering semantic correspondence and asset-level reuse, TOPOS generates head meshes with a fixed, studio-style topology, enabling consistent vertex-level correspondence across all generated heads. To model heads under this unified topology, we proposed a novel variational autoencoder structure, termed TOPOS-VAE. Inspired by multi-model large language models (MLLMs), our TOPOS-VAE leverages the Perceiver Resampler to convert input pointclouds sampled from head meshes of diverse topologies into the target reference topology. Building upon TOPOS-VAE's structured latent space, we train a rectified flow transformer, TOPOS-DiT, to efficiently generate high-fidelity head meshes from a single image. We further present TOPOS-Texture, an end-to-end module that produces relightable UV texture maps from the same portrait image via fine-tuning a multimodal image generative model. The generated textures are spatially aligned with the underlying mesh geometry and faithfully preserve high-frequency appearance details. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TOPOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D head generation, surpassing both classical face reconstruction methods and general 3D object generative models, highlighting its effectiveness for digital human creation.

</details>


### [63] [VMU-Diff: A Coarse-to-fine Multi-source Data Fusion Framework for Precipitation Nowcasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14597)
*Chunlei Shi,Hao Li,Yufeng Zhu,Boyu Liu,Yongchao Feng,Zengliang Zang,Hongbin Wang,Yanlan Yang,Dan Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A two-stage coarse-to-fine precipitation nowcasting framework (VMU-Diff) that combines deterministic coarse motion estimation from multi-source data with probabilistic diffusion-based refinement, achieving improved short-term accuracy on Jiangsu SWAN data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of single-source radar with deterministic blurring and probabilistic diffusion artifacts/inefficiency; exploit multi-source data and a coarse-to-fine strategy to better capture global dynamics and fine details.

Method: Two-stage model: 1) deterministic coarse stage using radar + multi-band satellite data with spatial-temporal attention and Vision Mamba state-space blocks to predict global echo dynamics; 2) residual diffusion-based fine stage using a spatio-temporal refine generator to reconstruct residuals conditioned on coarse predictions and ground truth, via a residual conditional diffusion model with a Mamba state-space module.

Result: Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets show improvements over state-of-the-art methods, especially for short-term forecasts.

Conclusion: The proposed VMU-Diff framework effectively fuses multi-source data and employs a coarse-to-fine strategy to reduce blurring/artifacts and improve accuracy and efficiency in precipitation nowcasting.

Abstract: Precipitation nowcasting is a vital spatio-temporal prediction task for meteorological applications but faces challenges due to the chaotic property of precipitation systems. Existing methods predominantly rely on single-source radar data to build either deterministic or probabilistic models for extrapolation. However, the single deterministic model suffers from blurring due to MSE convergence. The single probabilistic model, typically represented by diffusion models, can generate fine details but suffers from spurious artifacts that compromise accuracy and computational inefficiency. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a novel coarse-to-fine Vision Mamba Unet and residual Diffusion (VMU-Diff) based precipitation nowcasting framework. It realizes precipitation nowcasting through a two-stage process, i.e., a deterministic model-based coarse stage to predict global motion trends and a probabilistic model-based fine stage to generate fine prediction details. In the coarse prediction stage, rather than single-source radar data, both radar and multi-band satellite data are taken as input. A spatial-temporal attention block and several Vision mamba state-space blocks realize multi-source data fusion, and predict the future echo global dynamics. The fine-grained stage is realized by a spatio-temporal refine generator based on residual conditional diffusion models. It first obtains spatio-temporal residual features based on coarse prediction and ground truth, and further reconstructs the residual via conditional Mamba state-space module. Experiments on Jiangsu SWAN datasets demonstrate the improvements of our method over state-of-the-art methods, particularly in short-term forecasts.

</details>


### [64] [Towards Accurate Single Panoramic 3D Detection: A Semantic Gaussian Centric Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14601)
*Kanglin Ning,Yiran Zhao,Wenrui Li,Shaoru Sun,Xingtao Wang,Xiaopeng Fan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PanoGSDet uses continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations for monocular panoramic 3D detection, combining a panoramic depth estimation module with a semantic Gaussian lifting/optimization pipeline to predict 3D bounding boxes, showing strong results on Structured3D.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods project 2D panorama features onto discrete 3D grids, which breaks geometric continuity and reduces representation efficiency. A continuous 3D semantic Gaussian representation can better preserve geometry and efficiency for panoramic 3D detection.

Method: A two-branch framework: (1) panoramic depth estimation to extract equirectangular semantic and depth features from the monocular panorama; (2) semantic Gaussian component comprising (a) semantic Gaussian lifting to project spherical features into 3D semantic Gaussians, (b) semantic Gaussian optimization to refine Gaussians, and (c) a Gaussian-guided prediction head to produce 3D bounding boxes from the optimized Gaussians.

Result: Experiments on Structured3D show that the proposed method significantly outperforms existing methods.

Conclusion: Continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations enable effective monocular panoramic 3D detection by preserving geometric continuity and improving representation efficiency, demonstrated by strong results on Structured3D.

Abstract: Three-dimensional object detection in panoramic imagery is crucial for comprehensive scene understanding, yet accurately mapping 2D features to 3D remains a significant challenge. Prevailing methods often project 2D features onto discrete 3D grids, which break geometric continuity and limit representation efficiency. To overcome this limitation, this paper proposes PanoGSDet, a monocular panoramic 3D detection framework built upon continuous semantic 3D Gaussian representations. The proposed framework comprises a panoramic depth estimation component and a semantic Gaussian component. The panoramic depth estimation component extracts the equirectangular semantic and depth features from the monocular panorama input. The semantic Gaussian component includes a semantic Gaussian lifting module that projects spherical features into 3D semantic Gaussians, a semantic Gaussian optimization module that refines these semantic Gaussians, and a Gaussian guided prediction head that generates 3D bounding boxes from optimized Gaussian representations. Extensive experiments on the Structured3D dataset demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing methods.

</details>


### [65] [MambaRain: Multi-Scale Mamba-Attention Framework for 0-3 Hour Precipitation Nowcasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14606)
*Chunlei Shi,Cui Wu,Xiang Xu,Hao Li,Ni Fan,Xue Han,Yongchao Feng,Yufeng Zhu,Boyu Liu,Zengliang Zang,Hongbin Wang,Yanlan Yang,Dan Niu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A hybrid MambaRain model combines linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with explicit spatial self-attention to extend radar-based nowcasting from 0-2 hours up to 2-3 hours, aided by a spectral loss to reduce blur; it substantially outperforms deterministic baselines in 0-3 hour forecasts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deterministic nowcasting methods struggle beyond short horizons (≈90 minutes) due to inability to capture long-range spatiotemporal dependencies in radar data; extending the forecast horizon while preserving detail is essential for disaster mitigation.

Method: A multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture where Mamba blocks perform selective state-space modeling for global temporal dynamics over long sequences, complemented by spatial self-attention modules that capture explicit correlations within precipitation fields; includes a spectral loss to mitigate blurring artifacts.

Result: Empirical validation shows substantial improvements over existing deterministic methods in 0-3 hour nowcasting, with particularly strong gains in the 2-3 hour window.

Conclusion: The hybrid temporal-spatial design extends nowcasting horizons effectively, and the spectral loss helps preserve fine-scale motion details, indicating the proposed approach successfully addresses long-range spatiotemporal dependencies in precipitation forecasting.

Abstract: Accurate precipitation nowcasting over extended horizons (0-3 hours) is essential for disaster mitigation and operational decision-making, yet remains a critical challenge in the field. Existing deterministic approaches are predominantly constrained to shorter prediction windows (0-2 hours), exhibiting severe performance degradation beyond 90 minutes owing to their inherent difficulty in capturing long-range spatiotemporal dependencies from radar-derived observations. To address these fundamental limitations, we propose MambaRain, a novel multi-scale encoder-decoder architecture that synergistically integrates Mamba's linear-complexity long-range temporal modeling with self-attention mechanisms for explicit spatial correlation capture. The core innovation lies in a hybrid design paradigm wherein Mamba blocks leverage selective state space mechanisms to model global temporal dynamics across extended sequences with computational efficiency, while self-attention modules explicitly characterize spatial correlations within precipitation fields - a capability inherently absent in Mamba's sequential processing paradigm. This complementary synergy enables comprehensive spatiotemporal representation learning, effectively extending the viable forecasting horizon to 2-3 hours with substantial accuracy improvements. Furthermore, we introduce a spectral loss formulation to mitigate blurring artifacts characteristic of chaotic precipitation systems, thereby preserving fine-scale motion details critical for nowcasting accuracy. Experimental validation demonstrates that MambaRain substantially outperforms existing deterministic methodologies in 0-3 hour nowcasting tasks, with particularly pronounced performance gains in the challenging 2-3 hour prediction range.

</details>


### [66] [ViMU: Benchmarking Video Metaphorical Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14607)
*Qi Li,Xinchao Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ViMU is a new benchmark to evaluate subtext understanding in video models, beyond literal content, using multimodal grounding with open-ended and MCQ questions, all hint-free.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video content conveys implicit meanings (humor, irony, social meanings) that current models struggle to capture; need a standardized benchmark to assess subtext inference across cultures and contexts.

Method: Design of ViMU benchmark with two question types (open-ended and multiple-choice) grounded in multimodal evidence; hint-free prompts; evaluation of frontier models' ability to infer implicit meaning.

Result: Introduces ViMU benchmark and evaluation protocol as a contribution; establishes tasks to assess subtext understanding, with emphasis on grounding in multimodal cues.

Conclusion: ViMU enables systematic assessment of models' subtext understanding; progress toward models that interpret metaphor, irony, and social meaning in videos beyond literal perception; dataset prompts careful evaluation without hint leakage.

Abstract: Any new medium, once it emerges, is used for more than the transmission of overt content alone. The information it carries typically operates on two levels: one is the content directly presented, while the other is the subtext beneath it-the implicit ideas and intentions the creator seeks to convey through the medium. Likewise, since video technologies became widely adopted, video has served not only as a powerful tool for recording and communicating visual information, but also as a vehicle for emotions, attitudes, and social meanings that are often difficult to articulate explicitly. Thus, the true meaning of many videos does not reside solely in what is shown on screen; it is often embedded in context, style of expression, and the viewer's social experience. Some forms of such video subtext are humorous, while others carry irony, mockery, or criticism. These implicit meanings can also be interpreted very differently across cultural backgrounds and social groups. However, most existing video understanding models still focus primarily on literal visual comprehension, such as recognizing objects, actions, or temporal relations, and lack a systematic ability to understand the metaphorical, ironic, and social meanings embedded in videos. To bridge this gap, we introduce ViMU, the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the subtext understanding capabilities of frontier models in videos. ViMU assesses whether video understanding models can go beyond literal perception to infer implicit meaning while grounding their interpretations in multimodal evidence and answering both open-ended and multiple-choice questions. Importantly, all questions are designed to be hint-free, ensuring that no key evidence is disclosed to models before answering.

</details>


### [67] [Deep Image Segmentation via Discriminant Feature Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14609)
*Adam Dawid Sztamborski,Raül Pérez-Gonzalo,Antonio Agudo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA), a differentiable discriminant-based loss for segmentation that improves boundary sharpness and confidence without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard segmentation losses (Cross-Entropy, Dice) often overlook discriminative structure in learned features, leading to blurred or uncertain boundaries; a discriminant-oriented loss could enforce better feature separability.

Method: DDA is a differentiable, architecture-agnostic loss that embeds classical discriminant principles by maximizing between-class variance and minimizing within-class variance to produce compact, separable feature distributions during training, without increasing inference cost.

Result: On the DIS5K benchmark, DDA consistently improves segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across multiple architectures.

Conclusion: Incorporating discriminant analysis via a differentiable loss provides a simple, effective route to more robust segmentation models without additional inference overhead.

Abstract: Accurate image segmentation remains challenging, particularly in generating sharp, confident boundaries. While modern architectures have advanced the field, many of them still rely on standard loss functions like Cross-Entropy and Dice, which often neglect the discriminative structure of learned features, leading to inaccurate boundaries. This work introduces Deep Discriminant Analysis (DDA), a differentiable, architecture-agnostic loss function that embeds classical discriminant principles for network training. DDA explicitly maximizes between-class variance while minimizing within-class one, promoting compact and separable feature distributions without increasing inference cost. Evaluations on the DIS5K benchmark demonstrate that DDA consistently improves segmentation accuracy, boundary sharpness, and model confidence across various architectures. Our results show that integrating discriminant analysis offers a simple, effective path for building more robust segmentation models.

</details>


### [68] [CalibAnyView: Beyond Single-View Camera Calibration in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14615)
*Boying Li,Cheng Zhang,Weirong Chen,Daniel Cremers,Ian Reid,Hamid Rezatofighi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CalibAnyView unifies single- and multi-view camera calibration by modeling cross-view geometric consistency with a multi-view transformer and a geometric optimization, backed by a large-scale multi-view dataset, improving intrinsics and gravity estimation in-the-wild.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Calibrating cameras reliably in arbitrary, in-the-wild conditions is challenging; existing methods are either single-view or lack cross-view geometric consistency across multiple views, reducing accuracy and robustness.

Method: Introduce CalibAnyView: a framework that handles any number of views (N≥1) by enforcing cross-view geometric consistency. Build a large-scale multi-view video dataset with diverse cameras and distortions. Develop a multi-view transformer that predicts dense perspective fields, which are integrated into a geometric optimization to jointly estimate intrinsics and gravity direction.

Result: Empirical results show CalibAnyView outperforms state-of-the-art methods, remains robust under single-view usage, and further gains from multi-view inference; enables improved downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in real-world scenarios.

Conclusion: A unified, scalable calibration framework that leverages cross-view consistency, supported by a comprehensive dataset and a multi-view transformer, providing a robust foundation for in-the-wild calibration and downstream perception tasks.

Abstract: Camera calibration is a fundamental prerequisite for reliable geometric perception, yet classical approaches rely on controlled acquisition setups that are impractical for in-the-wild imagery. Recent learning-based methods have shown promising results for single-view calibration, but inherently neglect geometric consistency across multiple views. We introduce CalibAnyView, a unified formulation that supports an arbitrary number of input views ($N \geq 1$) by explicitly modeling cross-view geometric consistency. To facilitate this, we construct a large-scale multi-view video dataset covering diverse real-world scenarios, including multiple camera models, dynamic scenes, realistic motion trajectories, and heterogeneous lens distortions. Building on this dataset, we develop a multi-view transformer that predicts dense perspective fields, which are further integrated into a geometric optimization framework to jointly estimate camera intrinsics and gravity direction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CalibAnyView consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieves strong robustness under single-view settings, and further improves with multi-view inference, providing a reliable foundation for downstream tasks such as 3D reconstruction and robotic perception in the wild.

</details>


### [69] [Do We Really Need External Tools to Mitigate Hallucinations? SIRA: Shared-Prefix Internal Reconstruction of Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14621)
*Tian Qin,Junzhe Chen,Yuqing Shi,Tianshu Zhang,Qiang Ju,Lijie Wen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIRA is a training-free internal contrastive decoding method for LVLMs that creates an internal counterfactual reference within the model by masking image-token attention in later transformer layers, reducing hallucinations without extra forward passes or perturbed inputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs often hallucinate when language priors dominate weak or ambiguous visual evidence. External two-pass contrastive methods rely on perturbed inputs and external references, which can cause off-manifold artifacts and incur high compute costs.

Method: During decoding, SIRA forms a shared multimodal state by allowing image and text tokens to interact through a common prefix. It then forks a counterfactual branch in later layers with attention to image-token positions masked, preserving context but losing fine-grained visual access. Token-level contrast is performed by suppressing tokens that rely on the late visual pathway and favoring predictions that depend on full visual access.

Result: Empirical evaluation on POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5 shows consistent hallucination reduction while maintaining descriptive coverage, and with lower overhead than two-pass contrastive decoding.

Conclusion: SIRA is training-free, requires no external verifier or perturbed inputs, and applies to open-weight LVLMs with white-box inference access, offering a scalable and efficient remedy to vision-language hallucinations.

Abstract: Large vision-language models (LVLMs) often hallucinate when language priors dominate weak or ambiguous visual evidence. Existing contrastive decoding methods mitigate this problem by comparing predictions from the original image with those from externally perturbed visual inputs, but such references can introduce off-manifold artifacts and require costly extra forward passes. We propose SIRA, a training-free internal contrastive decoding framework that constructs a counterfactual reference inside the same LVLM by exploiting the staged information flow of multimodal transformers. Instead of removing visual information from the input, SIRA first lets image and text tokens interact through a shared prefix, forming an aligned multimodal state that preserves prompt interpretation, decoding history, positional structure, and early visual grounding. It then forks a counterfactual branch in later transformer layers, where attention to image-token positions is masked. This branch retains the shared multimodal context but lacks continued access to fine-grained visual evidence, yielding a language-prior-dominated internal reference for token-level contrast. During decoding, SIRA suppresses tokens that remain strong without late visual access and favors predictions whose advantage depends on the full visual pathway. Experiments on POPE, CHAIR, and AMBER with Qwen2.5-VL and LLaVA-v1.5 show that SIRA consistently reduces hallucinations while preserving descriptive coverage and incurring lower overhead than two-pass contrastive decoding. SIRA requires no training, external verifier, or perturbed input, and applies to open-weight LVLMs with white-box inference access.

</details>


### [70] [UniTriGen: Unified Triplet Generation of Aligned Visible-Infrared-Label for Few-Shot RGB-T Semantic Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14626)
*Ping Zhou,Haoyu Wang,Mengmeng Zheng,Lei Zhang,Wei Wei,Chen Ding,Fei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: UniTriGen introduces a unified diffusion-based triplet generation framework for RGB-T semantic segmentation that jointly encodes VIS, IR, and Label into a shared latent space, with modality-specific adapters and balanced few-shot sampling to produce high-quality, spatially aligned triplets from limited data, boosting segmentation performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generative augmentation methods rely on cascaded local conditioning, often breaking cross-modal spatial structure and semantic consistency. Aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets are scarce in real data, hindering robust RGB-T segmentation.

Method: A unified triplet generation mechanism that jointly encodes VIS, IR, and Label into a shared latent space and models them with a diffusion process to enforce global cross-modal consistency. Modality-specific residual adapters handle imaging characteristics and output formats. A scene-balanced and class-aware few-shot sampling strategy mitigates bias from limited paired triplets, enhancing scene and class diversity.

Result: Experiments show UniTriGen can generate high-quality, spatially aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets from limited real data and yields consistent performance improvements across multiple RGB-T semantic segmentation models.

Conclusion: UniTriGen provides a principled approach to cross-modal triplet synthesis with text guidance, achieving aligned, diverse triplets and improving RGB-T segmentation, by combining unified diffusion-based generation with modality adapters and balanced sampling.

Abstract: RGB-T semantic segmentation requires strictly aligned VIS-IR-Label triplets; however, such aligned triplet data are often scarce in real-world scenarios. Existing generative augmentation methods usually adopt cascaded generation paradigms, decomposing joint triplet generation into local conditional processes. As a result, consistency among VIS, IR, and Label in spatial structure, semantic content, and cross-modal details cannot be reliably maintained. To address this issue, we propose UniTriGen, a unified triplet generation framework that directly generates spatially aligned, semantically consistent, and modality complementary VIS-IR-Label triplets under the guidance of text prompts. UniTriGen first introduces a unified triplet generation mechanism, where VIS, IR, and Label are jointly encoded into a shared latent space and modeled with a diffusion process to enforce global cross-modal consistency. Lightweight modality-specific residual adapters are further integrated into this mechanism to accommodate modality-specific imaging characteristics and output formats. To mitigate generation bias caused by imbalanced scene and class distributions in limited paired triplets, UniTriGen also employs a scene-balanced and class-aware few-shot sampling strategy, which induces a more balanced sampling distribution and enhances the scene and class diversity of generated triplets. Experiments show that UniTriGen generates high-quality aligned triplets from limited real paired data, thereby achieving consistent performance improvements across various RGB-T semantic segmentation models.

</details>


### [71] [How to Evaluate and Refine your CAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14641)
*Luca Domeniconi,Alessandra Stramiglio,Michele Lombardi,Samuele Salti*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a synthetic ground-truth dataset for CAM evaluation, proposes ARCC as a composite fidelity metric, and presents RefineCAM for high-resolution CAMs via multi-layer aggregation, achieving superior performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating class attribution maps is challenging due to lack of ground-truth explanations, and many CAMs yield low-resolution maps that hinder detailed interpretability.

Method: 1) Create a synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions for rigorous CAM metric evaluation. 2) Analyze existing CAM evaluation metrics and introduce ARCC, a composite metric for identifying faithful explanations. 3) Develop RefineCAM, a high-resolution CAM by aggregating CAMs across multiple network layers to enhance spatial detail.

Result: ARCC more reliably identifies faithful explanations on the synthetic evaluation. RefineCAM produces higher-resolution attribution maps and consistently outperforms existing CAM methods according to the proposed evaluation.

Conclusion: The study provides a principled evaluation framework for CAMs via a ground-truth dataset and introduces ARCC, improving CAM fidelity assessment, while RefineCAM addresses resolution limitations and yields superior interpretability.

Abstract: Class attribution maps (CAMs) provide local explanations for the decisions of convolutional neural networks. While widely used in practice, the evaluation of CAMs remains challenging due to the lack of ground-truth explanations, making it difficult to evaluate the soundness of existing metrics. Independently, most commonly used CAM methods produce low-resolution attribution maps, which limits their usefulness for detailed interpretability.
  To address the evaluation challenge, we introduce a synthetic dataset with ground-truth attributions that enables a rigorous comparison of CAM evaluation metrics. Using this dataset, we analyze existing metrics and propose ARCC, a new composite metric that more reliably identifies faithful explanations. To address the low resolution issue, we introduce RefineCAM, a method that produces high-resolution attribution maps by aggregating CAMs across multiple network layers. Our results show that RefineCAM consistently outperforms existing methods according to the proposed evaluation.

</details>


### [72] [Vision-Based Water Level and Flow Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14645)
*ZhiXin Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: An integrated, vision-based framework combines SOTA vision models with statistical filtering and physical priors to estimate water level and river surface velocity, with code to be released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional sensing and pure vision approaches—environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and calibration complexity—by introducing physics-informed priors and robust filtering for accurate, interpretable river measurements.

Method: Use state-of-the-art vision models to extract cues about water level and surface motion, then fuse these with statistical models under physical priors (e.g., hydrostatics, conservation) and robust filtering to estimate water level and flow velocity.

Result: The abstract claims improved accuracy from the integration but provides no quantitative metrics or datasets.

Conclusion: A physics-informed, vision-based estimation framework is proposed for more accurate and robust water level and river flow estimation, with code availability indicated.

Abstract: With the rapid evolution of computer vision, vision-based methodologies for water level and river surface velocity estimation have reached significant maturity. Compared to traditional sensing, these techniques offer superior interpretability, automated data archiving, and enhanced system robustness. However, challenges such as environmental sensitivity, limited precision, and complex site calibration persist. This work proposes an integrated framework that synergizes state-of-the-art (SOTA) vision models with statistical modeling. By leveraging physical priors and robust filtering strategies, we improve the accuracy of water level detection and flow estimation. Code will be available at https://github.com/sunzx97/Vision_Based_Water_Level_and_Flow_Estimation.git

</details>


### [73] [TERRA-CD: Multi-Temporal Framework for Multi-class and Semantic Change Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14651)
*Omkar Oak,Rukmini Nazre,Rujuta Budke,Suraj Sawant*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new urban vegetation change-detection benchmark, TERRA-CD, with 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs across 232 cities (USA/Europe) and three annotation schemes, enabling evaluation of multiple DL approaches for vegetation and semantic change detection; dataset and code available on GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the scarcity of comprehensive, labeled datasets for urban vegetation monitoring and change detection across temporal pairs; provide multi-scheme annotations to support diverse tasks (land-cover classification, vegetation change, and semantic land-cover transitions).

Method: Assembled 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024 covering 232 cities. Annotated with three schemes: (i) 4-class land-cover masks, (ii) 3-class vegetation change masks, and (iii) 13-class semantic change masks capturing land-cover transitions. Evaluated diverse deep learning models for two tasks: multi-class vegetation change detection and semantic change detection, including Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies.

Result: Provided a comparative benchmark and baseline evaluations across multiple architectures on TERRA-CD for vegetation and semantic change detection, demonstrating dataset effectiveness and establishing a standard for future work. Specific quantitative results are not given in the abstract.

Conclusion: TERRA-CD fills a gap in available datasets for urban vegetation change detection by offering multiple annotation schemes and a sizable, geographically diverse collection; the dataset and methods are publicly available at the linked GitHub repository for benchmarking future work.

Abstract: Urban vegetation monitoring plays a vital role in understanding environmental changes, yet comprehensive datasets for this purpose remain limited. To address this gap, we present the Temporal Remote-sensing Repository for Analyzing Change Detection (TERRA-CD), a benchmark dataset comprising 5,221 Sentinel-2 image pairs from 2019 and 2024, covering 232 cities across the USA and Europe. The dataset features three distinct annotation schemes: 4-class land cover mapping masks, 3-class vegetation change masks, and 13-class semantic change masks capturing all possible land cover transitions. Using various deep learning approaches including Siamese networks, STANet variants, Bi-SRNet, Changemask, Post-Classification Comparison, and HRSCD strategies, we evaluated the dataset's effectiveness for both vegetation Multi-class Change Detection as well as Semantic Change Detection. The proposed dataset and methods are available at https://github.com/omkarsoak/TERRA-CD.

</details>


### [74] [Beyond Instance-Level Self-Supervision in 3D Multi-Modal Medical Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14654)
*Tan Pan,Shuhao Mei,Yixuan Sun,Kaiyu Guo,Chen Jiang,Zhaorui Tan,Mengzhu Li,Limei Han,Xiang Zou,Yuan Cheng,Mahsa Baktashmotlagh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Leverages cross-instance topological consistency as supervision for self-supervised pretraining in multi-modal medical imaging, using intra-instance pixel-level topology-preserving triplets and inter-instance pseudo-correspondences to align partial neighborhoods across modalities; yields improved segmentation and classification and greater robustness to missing modalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard self-supervised learning in medical imaging treats each instance in isolation and often ignores stable anatomical topology. Anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals, offering a supervisory signal to improve representation learning and cross-modal generalization.

Method: Two alignment regimes are proposed: (i) intra-instance with pixel-level correspondences via a cross-modal triplet objective to preserve local topology; (ii) inter-instance without explicit correspondences, deriving pseudo-correspondences to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities.

Result: Validated on 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% in segmentation and 5.94% in classification, and demonstrating significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

Conclusion: Cross-instance topological consistency is an effective supervisory signal for self-supervised pretraining in multi-modal medical imaging, improving task performance and robustness to modality missingness.

Abstract: Self-supervised pre-training methods in medical imaging typically treat each individual as an isolated instance, learning representations through augmentation-based objectives or masked reconstruction. They often do not adequately capitalize on a key characteristic of physiological features: anatomical structures maintain consistent spatial relationships across individuals (instances), such as the thalamus being medial to the basal ganglia, regardless of variations in brain size, shape, or pathology. We propose leveraging this cross-instance topological consistency as a supervisory signal. The challenge arises from the inherent variability in medical imaging, which can differ significantly across instances and modalities. To tackle this, we focus on two alignment regimes. (i) Intra-instance: with pixel-level correspondences available, a cross-modal triplet objective explicitly preserves local neighborhood topology. (ii) Inter-instance: without such supervision, we derive pseudo-correspondences to control partial neighborhood alignment and prevent topology collapse across modalities. We validate our approach across 7 downstream multi-modal tasks, achieving average improvements of 1.1% and 5.94% in segmentation and classification tasks, respectively, and demonstrating significantly better robustness when modalities are missing at test time.

</details>


### [75] [MiVE: Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14664)
*Tong Wang,Meng Zou,Chengjing Wu,Xiaochao Qu,Luoqi Liu,Xiaolin Hu,Ting Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MiVE leverages multiscale Vision-Language Model features for reference-guided video editing, using a unified Diffusion Transformer and hierarchical Qwen3-VL features to overcome modality gaps and loss of spatial detail, achieving state-of-the-art human preference performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a fundamental trade-off in reference-guided video editing between decoupled encoders (which suffer from modality gaps) and unified vision-language encoders (which lose fine-grained spatial information). The paper hypothesizes that VLM layers contain complementary information across scales: early layers encode local spatial details, while deeper layers encode global semantics, suggesting a multiscale feature extraction strategy.

Method: Extract hierarchical features from a Vision-Language Model (Qwen3-VL) at multiple layers and feed them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer (MiVE). This avoids cross-attention between modalities, mitigating modality mismatch and preserving detailed editing cues while respecting instructions.

Result: Empirical results show state-of-the-art performance with highest human preference rankings, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

Conclusion: Multiscale Vision-Language features, when integrated into a diffusion-based editing framework, effectively address modality gaps and preserve spatial detail for reference-guided video editing, leading to superior human-perceived quality.

Abstract: Reference-guided video editing takes a source video, a text instruction, and a reference image as inputs, requiring the model to faithfully apply the instructed edits while preserving original motion and unedited content. Existing methods fall into two paradigms, each with inherent limitations: decoupled encoders suffer from modality gaps when processing instructions and visual content independently, while unified vision-language encoders lose fine-grained spatial details by relying solely on final-layer representations. We observe that VLM layers encode complementary information hierarchically -- early layers capture localized spatial details essential for precise editing, while deeper layers encode global semantics for instruction comprehension. Building on this insight, we present MiVE (Multiscale Vision-language features for reference-guided video Editing), a framework that repurposes VLMs as multiscale feature extractors. MiVE extracts hierarchical features from Qwen3-VL and integrates them into a unified self-attention Diffusion Transformer, eliminating the modality mismatch inherent in cross-attention designs. Experiments demonstrate that MiVE achieves state-of-the-art performance by ranking highest in human preference, outperforming both academic methods and commercial systems.

</details>


### [76] [Are Candidate Models Really Needed for Active Learning?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14689)
*Harshini Mridula Mohan,Maanya Manjunath,Vipul Arya,S. H. Shabbeer Basha,Nitin Cheekatla*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes active learning without candidate models by using randomly initialized CNNs/transformers and confidence-based sampling (HC, LC, HCLC); low-confidence sampling often yields best performance, enabling efficient labeling without pre-trained models and showing robustness across datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce labeling cost and remove dependence on initial candidate models in active learning by leveraging randomly initialized networks and simple confidence-based sampling rules.

Method: Evaluate three sampling strategies (high confidence, low confidence, and a hybrid of early HC and later LC) on CNNs and transformers with random initialization; compare against traditional active learning that relies on candidate models; perform extensive experiments to assess robustness.

Result: Low-confidence sampling (LC) frequently yields the best performance, achieving results comparable to model-based active learning approaches; the approach is robust across datasets and settings.

Conclusion: Candidate-model-free active learning via LC (and related strategies) offers a streamlined, efficient, and flexible alternative with broad applicability, reducing computational and labeling overhead.

Abstract: Deep learning has profoundly impacted domains such as computer vision and natural language processing by uncovering complex patterns in vast datasets. However, the reliance on extensive labeled data poses significant challenges, including resource constraints and annotation errors, particularly in training Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and transformers due to a larger number of parameters. Active learning offers a promising solution to reduce labeling burdens by strategically selecting the most informative samples for annotation. However, the current active learning frameworks are time-intensive which select the samples iteratively with the help of initial candidate models. This study investigates the feasibility of using CNNs and transformers with randomly initialized weights, eliminating the need for initial candidate models while achieving results comparable to active learning frameworks that depend on such candidate models. We evaluate three confidence-based sampling strategies: high confidence (HC), low confidence (LC), and a combination of high confidence in the early stages of training and low confidence at later stages of training (HCLC). Among these, mostly LC demonstrated the best performance in our experiments, showcasing its effectiveness as an active learning strategy without the need for candidate models. Further, extensive experiments verify the robustness of the proposed active learning methods. By challenging traditional frameworks, the proposed work introduces a streamlined approach to active learning, advancing efficiency and flexibility across diverse datasets and domains.

</details>


### [77] [Generating HDR Video from SDR Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14703)
*SaiKiran Tedla,Francesco Banterle,Trevor Canham,Karanpreet Raja,David B. Lindell,Kiriakos N. Kutulakos,Jiacheng Li,Feiran Li,Daisuke Iso*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes MEVM and VMM to convert casual SDR video into HDR by predicting exposure-bracketed SDR sequences from one SDR input and merging them into HDR, enabling HDR synthesis on top of existing SDR generative models and applications to in-the-wild videos and films.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HDR upconversion of legacy SDR video remains unresolved; leverage large-scale generative video models to synthesize HDR content from a single SDR input; address dynamic range and detail preservation in shadows/highlights; applicable to consumer footage and cinema.

Method: Introduce Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that predicts exposure-bracketed linear SDR sequences from a single nonlinear SDR input; propose a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) to fuse these sequences into a high-quality HDR video while preserving details; pipeline integrates with existing SDR generative video models; evaluation includes quantitative metrics, qualitative analysis, and user studies.

Result: Extensive experiments, both quantitative and qualitative; user study; robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild consumer videos and iconic films; supplementary webpage for viewing outputs; compatibility with existing SDR models.

Conclusion: Framework enables HDR synthesis from SDR using large-scale generative video models; MEVM and VMM provide end-to-end HDR upconversion; practical for real-world videos; potential to integrate into HDR pipelines.

Abstract: The high dynamic range (HDR) video ecosystem is approaching maturity, but the problem of upconverting legacy standard dynamic range (SDR) videos persists without a convincing solution. We propose a framework for HDR video synthesis from casual SDR footage by leveraging large-scale generative video models. We introduce a Multi-Exposure Video Model (MEVM) that can predict exposure-bracketed linear SDR video sequences from a single nonlinear SDR video input. We further propose a learnable Video Merging Model (VMM) that merges the predicted exposure-bracketed video into a high-quality HDR sequence while preserving detail in both shadows and highlights. Extensive experiments, quantitative and qualitative evaluation, and a user study demonstrate that our approach enables robust HDR conversion for in-the-wild examples from casual consumer videos and even iconic films. Finally, our model can support HDR synthesis pipelines built upon existing SDR generative video models. Output HDR videos can be viewed on our supplementary webpage: sdr2hdrvideo.github.io

</details>


### [78] [Towards Continuous Sign Language Conversation from Isolated Signs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14705)
*Youngmin Kim,Kyobin Choo,Jiwoo Park,Minseo Kim,Chanyoung Kim,Junhyeok Kim,Seong Jae Hwang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes SignaVox: large-scale, dataset-driven sign-to-sign dialogue by composing continuous sign conversations from isolated signs; introduces SignaVox-W/U, a retrieval-guided translator, and BRAID diffusion Transformer to align duration and inpaint co-articulations; demonstrates improved motion quality and semantic alignment in sign-based conversation without spoken language.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the data bottleneck in sign-language dialog systems: sentence-level sign video data are scarce, leading to limited vocabulary coverage and poor open-domain generalization; aim to enable direct sign-to-sign conversation with scalable, signer-centered output by recomposing isolated signs into continuous utterances.
