To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce the Temporal Anachronism Benchmark for Vision-Language Models (TAB-VLM), a dataset of 600 questions across six categories, designed to evaluate temporal reasoning on 1,600 Indian cultural artifacts spanning prehistoric to modern periods. Systematic evaluations of ten state-of-the-art models reveal significant deficiencies on our benchmark, and even the best model (GPT-5.2) achieves only 58.7% overall accuracy. The performance gap persists across varying architectures and scales, suggesting that cultural anachronism represents a significant limitation in visual AI systems, regardless of model size. These findings highlight the disparity between current VLM capabilities and the requirements for accurately interpreting cultural heritage materials, particularly for non-Western visual cultures underrepresented in training data. Our benchmark provides a foundation for enhancing temporal cognition in multimodal AI systems that interact with historical artifacts. The dataset and code are available in our project page.

</details>


### [128] [SAGE3D: Soft-guided attention and graph excitation for 3D point cloud corner detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15088)
*Batuhan Arda Bekar,Can Sarı,Hüseyin Can Gülkan,Barış Özcan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAGE3D is a hierarchical Transformer-based corner detector for airborne LiDAR that uses Soft-Guided Attention and an Excitatory Graph Neural Network to amplify corner signals across scales in a multi-stage encoder-decoder framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the challenge of accurate corner detection in sparse, multi-scale airborne LiDAR data by preserving corner signals across hierarchical feature extraction and improving precision/recall via guided attention and positive-only message passing.

Method: Hybrid Transformer-based model with multi-stage hierarchical encoder-decoder; Set Abstraction downsampling and Feature Propagation for coarse-to-fine predictions; Soft-Guided Attention injecting ground-truth corner log-priors into attention logits during training; Excitatory Graph Neural Network with positive-only message passing at selected resolutions to reinforce high-confidence corners.

Result: The abstract does not provide quantitative results; it claims improved precision via guided attention and improved recall via the excitatory GNN, but no numerical metrics are reported.

Conclusion: A promising approach for robust, multi-scale corner detection in airborne LiDAR, leveraging guided attention and excitation to preserve and amplify corner signals across hierarchy.

Abstract: We present SAGE3D, a hybrid Transformer-based model for corner detection in airborne LiDAR point clouds. We propose a multi-stage solution built on a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture that progressively downsamples point clouds through Set Abstraction layers and recovers per-point predictions via Feature Propagation. We introduce two innovations: Soft-Guided Attention, which injects ground-truth corner labels as a log-prior into attention logits during training to improve precision; then an Excitatory Graph Neural Network positioned at strategic resolutions in the hierarchy, employing positive-only message passing where high-confidence corners reinforce predictions through learned boosting, optimizing for recall. The hierarchical design enables multi-scale feature extraction while our guided attention and excitatory modules ensure corner signals are amplified rather than diluted across scales.

</details>


### [129] [CoralLite: μCT Reconstruction of Coral Colonies from Individual Corallites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15093)
*Jess Jones,Leonardo Bertini,Kenneth Johnson,Erica Hendy,Tilo Burghardt*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel dataset and baseline for 3D corallite reconstruction from μCT scans of Porites; introduces CoralLite and a hybrid V-Trans-UNet, exploiting weak supervision and topology-aware fine-tuning; results show high intra-colony accuracy and moderate cross-species generalization; dataset and code released for reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand polyp division timing and colony skeletal growth in long-lived reef-building corals. There is a lack of annotated 3D data linking corallites to skeletal structure, hindering quantitative morphometric analyses.

Method: Hybrid V-Trans-UNet for segmenting tiled μCT slices with cross-slice linking for 3D corallite models. Pretraining on weakly annotated data, topology-aware fine-tuning on fully annotated slice sections (8k+ manual corallite annotations). Training/testing on unseen slices within the same colony (and across colonies). Dataset: 697 μCT slices, 37 annotations; published network weights and source code.

Result: On unseen slices of the same colony, topological accuracy = 0.94; mean Dice scores: 0.77 (same colony and projection axis) and 0.63 (different, biologically unrelated specimen). First demonstration that ML can enable full 3D corallite modelling from μCT skeletons alone. Reproducibility enabled by releasing dataset and code.

Conclusion: This work shows that deep learning can reconstruct 3D corallites from coral skeleton μCT data, providing a valuable baseline and dataset for future expansion across species and greater scale; applicability to broader morphometric and growth analyses pending larger-scale validation.

Abstract: The life history of an individual coral is archived within the accreting skeleton of the colony. While reef-forming coral colonies (e.g. massive \emph{Porites} sp.) may live for hundreds of years and deposit calcareous structures many metres in height and width, their living tissue is a thin outer surface layer comprised of asexually-dividing polyps that only survive a few years. To understand the rate and timing of polyp division and the consequences for colony skeletal growth, scientists need to track the skeletal corallite deposited around each polyp. Here we propose CoralLite, an annotated μCT scan dataset of entire calcareous skeletons and an associated, first corallite deep learning reconstruction baseline. CoralLite combines fully quantified volumetric segmentations with cross-slice linking for visualisations of 3D models for each corallite up to colony scale. For segmentation, we propose and evaluate in detail a hybrid V-Trans-UNet architecture applicable to segmenting tiled μCT virtual slabs of \emph{Porites} sp. colonies. The model is pre-trained on weakly annotated data and topology-aware fine-tuned using fully annotated slice sections with 8k+ manual corallite region annotations. On unseen slices of the same colony, the resulting model reaches 0.94 topological accuracy at mean Dice scores of 0.77 on the same colony and projection axis, and 0.63 mean Dice scores on a different, biologically unrelated specimen. Whilst our experiments are limited in scale and context, our results show for the first time that visual machine learning can effectively support full 3D individual corallite modelling from μCT scans of coral skeletons alone. For reproducibility and as a baseline for future research we publish our full dataset of 697 μCT slices, 37 partial or full slice annotations, and all network weights and source code with this paper.

</details>


### [130] [DriveCtrl: Conditioned Sim-to-Real Driving Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15116)
*Haonan Zhao,Yiting Wang,Jingkun Chen,Valentina Donzella,Thomas Bashford-Rogers,Kurt Debattista*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DriveCtrl is a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework for realistic driving video synthesis that preserves scene structure and motion from simulation while translating to target real-world styles, using a structure-aware adapter, a scalable data-generation pipeline, and a three-signal conditioning set (depth, style, text), plus a Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a strong need for large-scale labeled driving video data for autonomous driving. Simulation provides annotated data but suffers from a domain gap to real-world videos; existing video-generation methods fail to jointly preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal coherence, and visual realism, which undermines annotation validity for downstream tasks.

Method: DriveCtrl is built on a pretrained video foundation model and introduces a structure-aware adapter that enables depth-guided generation while preserving the scene layout and motion patterns of the source simulation. It includes a scalable pipeline to convert simulator videos into realistic driving footage matching a target dataset's visual style. Conditioning signals include structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, with frame-level annotations preserved. The framework also introduces Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS) for evaluation of realism in driving videos.

Result: Experiments show DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing methods in realism, temporal quality, and perception-task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

Conclusion: DriveCtrl provides an effective, controllable solution for sim-to-real driving video synthesis that preserves structure and motion while enabling style transfer and depth-conditioned generation, facilitating high-quality labeled data for downstream perception systems.

Abstract: Large-scale labelled driving video data is essential for training autonomous driving systems. Although simulation offers scalable and fully annotated data, the domain gap between synthetic and real-world driving videos significantly limits its utility for downstream deployment. Existing video generation methods are not well-suited for this task, as they fail to simultaneously preserve scene structure, object dynamics, temporal consistency, and visual realism, all of which are critical for maintaining annotation validity in generated data. In this paper, we present DriveCtrl, a depth-conditioned controllable sim-to-real video generation framework for realistic driving video synthesis. Built upon a pretrained video foundation model, DriveCtrl introduces a structure-aware adapter that enables depth-guided generation while preserving the scene layout and motion patterns of the source simulation, producing temporally coherent driving videos that remain aligned with the original simulated sequences. We further introduce a scalable data generation pipeline that transforms simulator videos into realistic driving footage matching the visual style of a target real-world dataset. The pipeline supports three conditioning signals: structural depth, reference-dataset style, and text prompts, while preserving frame-level annotations for downstream perception tasks. To better assess this task, we propose a driving-domain-specific knowledge-informed evaluation metric called Driving Video Realism Score (DVRS) that assesses the realism of generated videos. Experiments demonstrate that DriveCtrl consistently outperforms the base model and competing alternatives in realism, temporal quality, and perception task performance, substantially narrowing the sim-to-real gap for driving video generation.

</details>


### [131] [MemEye: A Visual-Centric Evaluation Framework for Multimodal Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15128)
*Minghao Guo,Qingyue Jiao,Zeru Shi,Yihao Quan,Boxuan Zhang,Danrui Li,Liwei Che,Wujiang Xu,Shilong Liu,Zirui Liu,Mubbasir Kapadia,Vladimir Pavlovic,Jiang Liu,Mengdi Wang,Yiyu Shi,Dimitris N. Metaxas,Ruixiang Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MemEye introduces a two-dimensional framework and benchmark to evaluate long-term multimodal memory, focusing on preserving fine-grained visual evidence and reasoning over temporal state changes, across 8 tasks and 13 memory methods with 4 VLM backbones; results show current models struggle with detailed evidence and state evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations rely on captions or textual traces; many tasks do not require preserving detailed visual evidence; need to test memory's ability to track changing visuals and extract decisive evidence; build a framework and benchmark to probe evidence granularity, usage, and reasoning structure.

Method: Two dimensions: granularity of decisive visual evidence (scene-level to pixel-level) and how retrieved evidence must be used (single evidence to evolutionary synthesis); constructs 8 life-scenario tasks with ablation validation gates; evaluate 13 memory methods across 4 vision-language model backbones.

Result: Current architectures struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and to reason about state changes over time; memory performance depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction.

Conclusion: MemEye provides a framework and benchmark to assess long-term multimodal memory and reveals the importance of evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction for robust memory.

Abstract: Long-term agent memory is increasingly multimodal, yet existing evaluations rarely test whether agents preserve the visual evidence needed for later reasoning. In prior work, many visually grounded questions can be answered using only captions or textual traces, allowing answers to be inferred without preserving the fine-grained visual evidence. Meanwhile, harder cases that require reasoning over changing visual states are largely absent. Therefore, we introduce MemEye, a framework that evaluates memory capabilities from two dimensions: one measures the granularity of decisive visual evidence (from scene-level to pixel-level evidence), and the other measures how retrieved evidence must be used (from single evidence to evolutionary synthesis). Under this framework, we construct a new benchmark across 8 life-scenario tasks, with ablation-driven validation gates for assessing answerability, shortcut resistance, visual necessity, and reasoning structure. By evaluating 13 memory methods across 4 VLM backbones, we show that current architectures still struggle to preserve fine-grained visual details and reason about state changes over time. Our findings show that long-term multimodal memory depends on evidence routing, temporal tracking, and detail extraction.

</details>


### [132] [Causal Forcing++: Scalable Few-Step Autoregressive Diffusion Distillation for Real-Time Interactive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15141)
*Min Zhao,Hongzhou Zhu,Kaiwen Zheng,Zihan Zhou,Bokai Yan,Xinyuan Li,Xiao Yang,Chongxuan Li,Jun Zhu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Causal Forcing++ enables ultra-low-latency frame-wise 2-step autoregressive diffusion for real-time video by introducing causal consistency distillation (causal CD) for efficient few-step initialization, outperforming 4-step baselines while halving first-frame latency and cutting Stage 2 cost ~4x; extends to action-conditioned world models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time interactive video generation demands low latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Chunk-wise 4-step AR diffusion distillation shows strong results but suffers coarse response granularity and non-negligible sampling latency in practice. A more aggressive frame-wise 2-step regime requires robust, scalable initialization of the few-step AR student, which current strategies fail to provide efficiently.

Method: Propose Causal Forcing++ with causal consistency distillation (causal CD) to initialize few-step AR. Causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow as causal ODE distillation, but uses supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding full PF-ODE trajectory storage. This yields an efficient, optimizable initialization. The pipeline, termed Ours, achieves state-of-the-art results in frame-wise 2-step setting and extends to action-conditioned world-model generation.

Result: In frame-wise 2-step mode, the approach surpasses the 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing on VBench Total (+0.1), VBench Quality (+0.3), and VisionReward (+0.335). It also reduces first-frame latency by 50% and lowers Stage 2 training cost by ~4×. The method is extended to action-conditioned world-model generation (minWM/Genie3 style).

Conclusion: Causal Forcing++ provides a principled, scalable initialization for ultra-low-latency frame-wise diffusion-based video generation, achieving better quality and latency than prior 4-step baselines and enabling efficient extension to action-conditioned world models.

Abstract: Real-time interactive video generation requires low-latency, streaming, and controllable rollout. Existing autoregressive (AR) diffusion distillation methods have achieved strong results in the chunk-wise 4-step regime by distilling bidirectional base models into few-step AR students, but they remain limited by coarse response granularity and non-negligible sampling latency. In this paper, we study a more aggressive setting: frame-wise autoregression with only 1--2 sampling steps. In this regime, we identify the initialization of a few-step AR student as the key bottleneck: existing strategies are either target-misaligned, incapable of few-step generation, or too costly to scale. We propose \textbf{Causal Forcing++}, a principled and scalable pipeline that uses \emph{causal consistency distillation} (causal CD) for few-step AR initialization. The core idea is that causal CD learns the same AR-conditional flow map as causal ODE distillation, but obtains supervision from a single online teacher ODE step between adjacent timesteps, avoiding the need to precompute and store full PF-ODE trajectories. This makes the initialization both more efficient and easier to optimize. The resulting pipeline, \ours, surpasses the SOTA 4-step chunk-wise Causal Forcing under the \textit{\textbf{frame-wise 2-step setting}} by 0.1 in VBench Total, 0.3 in VBench Quality, and 0.335 in VisionReward, while reducing first-frame latency by 50\% and Stage 2 training cost by $\sim$$4\times$. We further extend the pipeline to action-conditioned world model generation in the spirit of Genie3. Project Page: https://github.com/thu-ml/Causal-Forcing and https://github.com/shengshu-ai/minWM .

</details>


### [133] [Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15167)
*Kam Man Wu,Haolin Yang,Qingyu Chen,Yihu Tang,Jingye Chen,Qifeng Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Synthetic layered data (SynLayers) trained with CLD baseline can outperform limited real datasets for layer decomposition; scaling benefits saturate near 50k samples; synthetic data enables balanced layer-count control, suggesting a scalable foundation for design-editing systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tackle the last-mile gap in post-generation editing by enabling scalable, modular layer decomposition in graphic design, without relying on scarce proprietary assets or limited structural priors.

Method: Construct SynLayers synthetic dataset; use vision-language models to generate textual supervision; automate inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes; evaluate against state-of-the-art CLD baseline and compare to PrismLayersPro.

Result: Pure synthetic data can outperform PrismLayersPro; performance improves with larger synthetic data up to ~50k samples, then saturates; synthetic data yields balanced layer-count distributions (reducing real-data imbalance).

Conclusion: Synthetic data is a viable, scalable foundation for layered design editing systems and should promote broader adoption of synthetic-data-driven approaches in this domain.

Abstract: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within a fixed canvas. As a result, flexible post-generation editing remains challenging, revealing a clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct a data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is a state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as a scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

</details>


### [134] [Evidential Reasoning Advances Interpretable Real-World Disease Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15171)
*Chenyu Lian,Hong-Yu Zhou,Jing Qin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EviScreen introduces an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening using region-level historical evidence from dual knowledge banks, enabling retrospection interpretability and evidence-aware reasoning, with abnormality maps from contrastive retrieval; claims improved specificity at clinical recall and public code release.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limited interpretability and suboptimal performance in medical-image screening models. Current models lack transparent reasoning paths and robust use of historical cases to guide decisions.

Method: EviScreen retrieves region-level evidence from dual knowledge banks containing historical cases. An evidential reasoning module combines current-case data with retrieved evidence to make predictions. Localization interpretability is enhanced via abnormality maps from contrastive retrieval rather than post-hoc saliency maps.

Result: Reported superior performance on established real-world screening benchmarks, achieving higher specificity at clinical recall. Public code available at GitHub.

Conclusion: EviScreen provides retrospection-oriented interpretability and improved screening performance by leveraging historical regional evidence and contrastive abnormality maps, offering a more transparent and effective screening framework.

Abstract: Disease screening is critical for early detection and timely intervention in clinical practice. However, most current screening models for medical images suffer from limited interpretability and suboptimal performance. They often lack effective mechanisms to reference historical cases or provide transparent reasoning pathways. To address these challenges, we introduce EviScreen, an evidential reasoning framework for disease screening that leverages region-level evidence from historical cases. The proposed EviScreen offers retrospection interpretability through regional evidence retrieved from dual knowledge banks. Using this evidential mechanism, the subsequent evidence-aware reasoning module makes predictions using both the current case and evidence from historical cases, thereby enhancing disease screening performance. Furthermore, rather than relying on post-hoc saliency maps, EviScreen enhances localization interpretability by leveraging abnormality maps derived from contrastive retrieval. Our method achieves superior performance on our carefully established benchmarks for real-world disease screening, yielding notably higher specificity at clinical-level recall. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/DopamineLcy/EviScreen.

</details>


### [135] [SANA-WM: Efficient Minute-Scale World Modeling with Hybrid Linear Diffusion Transformer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15178)
*Haoyi Zhu,Haozhe Liu,Yuyang Zhao,Tian Ye,Junsong Chen,Jincheng Yu,Tong He,Song Han,Enze Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A 2.6B-parameter open-source world model, SANA-WM, capable of minute-scale 60s video generation with 720p resolution, featuring four core designs (Hybrid Linear Attention, Dual-Branch Camera Control, Two-Stage Generation Pipeline, Robust Annotation Pipeline). Trains on ~213k public clips with metric-scale pose supervision, using 64 H100s for 15 days; can run a 60s clip on a single GPU; distilled form on RTX 5090 with NVFP4 quantization to denoise 60s 720p in 34s. Outperforms open-source baselines on action-following and matches visual quality with 36x throughput.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need for efficient, scalable, and accurately controlled world models for long-duration video generation, bridging the gap between open-source systems and industrial baselines in terms of quality, control, and compute efficiency.

Method: Introduce Hybrid Linear Attention combining GDN and softmax; Dual-Branch Camera Control for 6-DoF adherence; Two-Stage Generation Pipeline with a long-video refiner; Robust Annotation Pipeline for metric-scale pose extraction from public videos. Training with ~213k clips and metric-scale supervision on 64 H100s; inference details; distillation with NVFP4.

Result: Compared to industrial baselines LingBot-World and HY-WorldPlay: similar visual quality; on one-minute world-model benchmark, stronger action-following accuracy; 36x higher throughput; training time 15 days; 60s clip generation on one GPU; distilled variant runs on RTX 5090 in 34s.

Conclusion: SANA-WM demonstrates efficient, high-quality minute-scale world modeling with precise camera control, offering a practical, scalable open-source alternative to larger baselines; notable for efficiency, data reuse, and a robust annotation pipeline.

Abstract: We introduce SANA-WM, an efficient 2.6B-parameter open-source world model natively trained for one-minute generation, synthesizing high-fidelity, 720p, minute-scale videos with precise camera control. SANA-WM achieves visual quality comparable to large-scale industrial baselines such as LingBot-World and HY-WorldPlay, while significantly improving efficiency. Four core designs drive our architecture: (1) Hybrid Linear Attention combines frame-wise Gated DeltaNet (GDN) with softmax attention for memory-efficient long-context modeling. (2) Dual-Branch Camera Control ensures precise 6-DoF trajectory adherence. (3) Two-Stage Generation Pipeline applies a long-video refiner to stage-1 outputs, improving quality and consistency across sequences. (4) Robust Annotation Pipeline extracts accurate metric-scale 6-DoF camera poses from public videos to yield high-quality, spatiotemporally consistent action labels. Driven by these designs, SANA-WMdemonstrates remarkable efficiency across data, training compute, and inference hardware: it uses only $\sim$213K public video clips with metric-scale pose supervision, completes training in 15 days on 64 H100s, and generates each 60s clip on a single GPU; its distilled variant can be deployed on a single RTX 5090 with NVFP4 quantization to denoise a 60s 720p clip in 34s. On our one-minute world-model benchmark, SANA-WM demonstrates stronger action-following accuracy than prior open-source baselines and achieves comparable visual quality at $36\times$ higher throughput for scalable world modeling.

</details>


### [136] [From Plans to Pixels: Learning to Plan and Orchestrate for Open-Ended Image Editing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15181)
*Anirudh Sundara Rajan,Krishna Kumar Singh,Yong Jae Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A planning-and-execution framework for long-horizon image editing using a planner, an orchestrator, and a vision-language judge, trained via outcome-based rewards to improve coherence over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing image editing models struggle with abstract, multi-step instructions and prior agent-based methods rely on handcrafted pipelines or imitation, limiting flexibility and alignment with actual edits.

Method: Three components: planner generates structured atomic decompositions of the instruction; orchestrator selects editing tools and regions to execute each step; vision-language judge provides outcome-based rewards for instruction adherence and visual quality; orchestrator learns to maximize rewards; successful trajectories refine the planner.

Result: Experiments show more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multi-step baselines.

Conclusion: Tight coupling of planning with reward-driven execution improves long-horizon editing; the framework enables coherent, goal-aligned image edits.

Abstract: Modern image editing models produce realistic results but struggle with abstract, multi step instructions (e.g., ``make this advertisement more vegetarian-friendly''). Prior agent based methods decompose such tasks but rely on handcrafted pipelines or teacher imitation, limiting flexibility and decoupling learning from actual editing outcomes. We propose an experiential framework for long-horizon image editing, where a planner generates structured atomic decompositions and an orchestrator selects tools and regions to execute each step. A vision language judge provides outcome-based rewards for instruction adherence and visual quality. The orchestrator is trained to maximize these rewards, and successful trajectories are used to refine the planner. By tightly coupling planning with reward driven execution, our approach yields more coherent and reliable edits than single-step or rule-based multistep baselines.

</details>


### [137] [Warp-as-History: Generalizable Camera-Controlled Video Generation from One Training Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15182)
*Yifan Wang,Tong He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Warp-as-History enables zero-shot camera-trajectory following in frozen video generators by converting camera-induced warps into a camera-warped pseudo-history, aligning positional encodings, and pruning invalid tokens; further improved via lightweight LoRA fine-tuning on a single camera-annotated video.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Remove reliance on training-time camera encoders/conditioning or test-time optimization for camera-controlled video generation; achieve training-free or minimal-finetuning capability with existing frozen models.

Method: Construct camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations using the target camera trajectory, feed it through the model's visual-history pathway, align positional encodings with target frames, and prune warped-history tokens without valid observations; optionally apply lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on a single camera-annotated video.

Result: Demonstrates non-trivial zero-shot camera adherence with a frozen model, improves camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics; generalizes to unseen videos; no training or test-time optimization required, with additional gains from LoRA finetuning on one video.

Conclusion: Warp-as-History provides a simple, effective interface to leverage frozen video-generation models for camera-controlled synthesis, enabling training-free or minimal-finetuning improvements and broad applicability across datasets.

Abstract: Camera-controlled video generation has made substantial progress, enabling generated videos to follow prescribed viewpoint trajectories. However, existing methods usually learn camera-specific conditioning through camera encoders, control branches, or attention and positional-encoding modifications, which often require post-training on large-scale camera-annotated videos. Training-free alternatives avoid such post-training, but often shift the cost to test-time optimization or extra denoising-time guidance. We propose Warp-as-History, a simple interface that turns camera-induced warps into camera-warped pseudo-history with target-frame positional alignment and visible-token selection. Given a target camera trajectory, we construct camera-warped pseudo-history from past observations and feed it through the model's visual-history pathway. Crucially, we align its positional encoding with the target frames being denoised and remove warped-history tokens without valid source observations. Without any training, architectural modification, or test-time optimization, this interface reveals a non-trivial zero-shot capability of a frozen video generation model to follow camera trajectories. Moreover, lightweight offline LoRA finetuning on only one camera-annotated video further improves this capability and generalizes to unseen videos, improving camera adherence, visual quality, and motion dynamics without test-time optimization or target-video adaptation. Extensive experiments on diverse datasets confirm the effectiveness of our method.

</details>


### [138] [Quantitative Video World Model Evaluation for Geometric-Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15185)
*Jiaxin Wu,Yihao Pi,Yinling Zhang,Yuheng Li,Xueyan Zou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces PDI-Bench, a quantitative framework to audit geometric coherence in generated videos by lifting object-centric observations to 3D and computing projective-geometry residuals across scale-depth, 3D motion, and 3D structure, plus a stress-test PDI-Dataset; reveals geometry-specific failures not captured by perceptual metrics and provides a diagnostic tool for physically grounded video generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating whether generative video models produce physically plausible 3D structure and motion is difficult; existing evaluation pipelines rely on subjective human judgments or learned graders that may miss geometric failures.

Method: Extract object-centric observations via segmentation/tracking, lift to 3D using monocular reconstruction, compute residuals from projective geometry to assess scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D structural rigidity; construct PDI-Dataset to stress these geometric constraints.

Result: PDI reveals consistent geometry-specific failure modes across state-of-the-art video generators that are not captured by common perceptual metrics; provides a diagnostic signal for progress toward physically grounded video generation and physical world models.

Conclusion: The PDI framework and dataset enable systematic, geometry-focused evaluation of generated videos, guiding development toward physically grounded models; the authors provide code and dataset to support adoption.

Abstract: Generative video models are increasingly studied as implicit world models, yet evaluating whether they produce physically plausible 3D structure and motion remains challenging. Most existing video evaluation pipelines rely heavily on human judgment or learned graders, which can be subjective and weakly diagnostic for geometric failures. We introduce PDI-Bench (Perspective Distortion Index), a quantitative framework for auditing geometric coherence in generated videos. Given a generated clip, we obtain object-centric observations via segmentation and point tracking (e.g., SAM 2, MegaSaM, and CoTracker3), lift them to 3D world-space coordinates via monocular reconstruction, and compute a set of projective-geometry residuals capturing three failure dimensions: scale-depth alignment, 3D motion consistency, and 3D structural rigidity. To support systematic evaluation, we build PDI-Dataset, covering diverse scenarios designed to stress these geometric constraints. Across state-of-the-art video generators, PDI reveals consistent geometry-specific failure modes that are not captured by common perceptual metrics, and provides a diagnostic signal for progress toward physically grounded video generation and physical world model. Our code and dataset can be found at https://pdi-bench.github.io/.

</details>


### [139] [VGGT-Edit: Feed-forward Native 3D Scene Editing with Residual Field Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15186)
*Kaixin Zhu,Yiwen Tang,Yifan Yang,Renrui Zhang,Bohan Zeng,Ziyu Guo,Ruichuan An,Zhou Liu,Qizhi Chen,Delin Qu,Jaehong Yoon,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-Edit is a fast, text-conditioned, native 3D scene editor that uses depth-synchronized text grounding and a residual 3D displacement head to deform scenes, supervised by geometry and cross-view consistency losses; paired with DeltaScene, a large, quality-filtered 3D dataset, it outperforms 2D-lifting baselines with sharper details and multi-view coherence, and enables near-instant inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current generalizable 3D editors excel in static perception but struggle with dynamic, instruction-driven editing. 2D-lifting pipelines introduce texture blurring and geometry inconsistency when lifting edited views back to 3D, hindering interactive applications.

Method: Depth-synchronized text injection aligns semantic guidance with the backbone's spatial poses; a residual transformation head directly predicts 3D geometric displacements to deform scenes while keeping the background stable; a multi-term objective enforces geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency; a DeltaScene dataset is created via an automated pipeline with 3D agreement filtering to ensure high-quality ground truth.

Result: VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, producing sharper object details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.

Conclusion: The framework delivers high-fidelity, interactive 3D scene editing with robust grounding and efficiency, supported by a large-quality DeltaScene dataset; it addresses the limitations of 2D editors and advances real-time, text-conditioned 3D manipulation.

Abstract: High-quality 3D scene reconstruction has recently advanced toward generalizable feed-forward architectures, enabling the generation of complex environments in a single forward pass. However, despite their strong performance in static scene perception, these models remain limited in responding to dynamic human instructions, which restricts their use in interactive applications. Existing editing methods typically rely on a 2D-lifting strategy, where individual views are edited independently and then lifted back into 3D space. This indirect pipeline often leads to blurry textures and inconsistent geometry, as 2D editors lack the spatial awareness required to preserve structure across viewpoints. To address these limitations, we propose VGGT-Edit, a feed-forward framework for text-conditioned native 3D scene editing. VGGT-Edit introduces depth-synchronized text injection to align semantic guidance with the backbone's spatial poses, ensuring stable instruction grounding. This semantic signal is then processed by a residual transformation head, which directly predicts 3D geometric displacements to deform the scene while preserving background stability. To ensure high-fidelity results, we supervise the framework with a multi-term objective function that enforces geometric accuracy and cross-view consistency. We also construct the DeltaScene Dataset, a large-scale dataset generated through an automated pipeline with 3D agreement filtering to ensure ground-truth quality. Experiments show that VGGT-Edit substantially outperforms 2D-lifting baselines, producing sharper object details, stronger multi-view consistency, and near-instant inference speed.

</details>


### [140] [RAVEN: Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation with Consistency-model GRPO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15190)
*Yanzuo Lu,Ronglai Zuo,Jiankang Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces RAVEN for training-time re-packaging of rollouts to align training with inference in causal video diffusion, and CM-GRPO, an online RL method on a consistency kernel; together improving real-time extrapolation quality over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the distribution shift between training history and inference-time extrapolation in causal autoregressive video diffusion, which degrades long-horizon quality.

Method: RAVEN repacks each self rollout into an interleaved sequence of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states to align training attention with inference. CM-GRPO reformulates the consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online reinforcement learning directly to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process used in prior flow-model RL.

Result: RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations; CM-GRPO yields additional gains when used with RAVEN.

Conclusion: The combination of training-time history-aware repacking and consistency-focused online RL improves real-time video extrapolation quality and learning efficiency, reducing the gap between training and inference distributions.

Abstract: Causal autoregressive video diffusion models support real-time streaming generation by extrapolating future chunks from previously generated content. Distilling such generators from high-fidelity bidirectional teachers yields competitive few-step models, yet a persistent gap between the history distributions encountered during training and those arising at inference constrains generation quality over long horizons. We introduce the Real-time Autoregressive Video Extrapolation Network (RAVEN), a training-time test framework that repacks each self rollout into an interleaved sequence of clean historical endpoints and noisy denoising states. This formulation aligns training attention with inference-time extrapolation and allows downstream chunk losses to supervise the history representations on which future predictions depend. We further propose Consistency-model Group Relative Policy Optimization (CM-GRPO), which reformulates a consistency sampling step as a conditional Gaussian transition and applies online Reinforcement Learning (RL) directly to this kernel, avoiding the Euler-Maruyama auxiliary process adopted in prior flow-model RL formulations. Experiments demonstrate that RAVEN surpasses recent causal video distillation baselines across quality, semantic, and dynamic degree evaluations, and that CM-GRPO provides further gains when combined with RAVEN.

</details>


### [141] [Aligning Latent Geometry for Spherical Flow Matching in Image Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15193)
*Tuna Han Salih Meral,Kaan Oktay,Hidir Yesiltepe,Adil Kaan Akan,Pinar Yanardag*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Shift latent-space geometry from linear Gaussian noise paths to sphere-constrained geodesic paths for image generation by decomposing latents into radius and direction, fixing radius, using SLERP, and finetuning the decoder with encoder frozen; results show improved class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID without extra objectives or architectural changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Latent flows along linear chords leave the latent shells; semantic content resides mainly in direction (angular component) while radius is less informative. Constraining to a fixed-radius sphere and using angular geodesics aims to preserve perceptual content and improve sample quality with minimal modification to the diffusion pipeline.

Method: Decompose latent tokens into radial and angular components; project latents onto a fixed token radius; use radial Gaussian noise as a spherical prior; finetune decoder while keeping encoder frozen; replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation (SLERP) to generate geodesic paths on the sphere; ensure velocity targets are angular; evaluate across class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID and multiple image tokenizers; no auxiliary encoders or representation-alignment objectives.

Result: Consistent improvement in class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers; diffusion architecture remains unchanged; no auxiliary encoders or alignment objectives required.

Conclusion: Geodesic latent trajectories on a fixed-radius sphere capture angular information that encodes semantics more than radius, enabling better image generation without altering the diffusion model and with simple adaptations like SLERP and decoder finetuning.

Abstract: Latent flow matching for image generation usually transports Gaussian noise to variational autoencoder latents along linear paths. Both endpoints, however, concentrate in thin spherical shells, and a Euclidean chord leaves those shells even when preprocessing aligns their radii. By decomposing each latent token into radial and angular components, we show through component-swap probes that decoded perceptual and semantic content is carried predominantly by direction, with radius contributing much less. We therefore project data latents onto a fixed token radius, use the radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior, finetune the decoder with the encoder frozen, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation. The resulting geodesic paths stay on the sphere at every timestep, and their velocity targets are purely angular by construction. Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, leaves the diffusion architecture unchanged, and requires no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective.

</details>


### [142] [VGGT-$Ω$](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15195)
*Jianyuan Wang,Minghao Chen,Shangzhan Zhang,Nikita Karaev,Johannes Schönberger,Patrick Labatut,Piotr Bojanowski,David Novotny,Andrea Vedaldi,Christian Rupprecht*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VGGT-Ω scales feed-forward reconstruction by architectural and data innovations to enable large-scale, efficient training for static and dynamic scenes, achieving strong accuracy gains (e.g., 77% on Sintel) and cross-modal benefits while using roughly 30% of prior GPU memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address scalability and efficiency bottlenecks in feed-forward reconstruction models, enabling training with vastly larger labeled and unlabeled data, and extending to dynamic scenes with geometry-aware features.

Method: Introduce VGGT-Ω with architectural simplifications (single dense prediction head, multi-task supervision, removal of high-res conv layers), compact scene representations via registers and register attention (restrict inter-frame exchange), memory-efficient training (~30% GPU memory of predecessor), a self-supervised protocol, and a high-quality data annotation pipeline for dynamic scenes to enable 15x more supervised data and access to unlabeled video.

Result: VGGT-Ω delivers strong reconstruction performance on static and dynamic scenes across benchmarks, including a 77% improvement in camera estimation accuracy on Sintel; registers improve related models (vision-language-action) and support language alignment, suggesting reconstruction as a scalable proxy task for spatial understanding.

Conclusion: Scaling VGGT with model/data size and efficient training yields substantial accuracy and capability gains, while enabling cross-modal benefits; reconstruction can be a powerful, scalable proxy task for spatial understanding and downstream tasks.

Abstract: Recent feed-forward reconstruction models, such as VGGT, have proven competitive with traditional optimization-based reconstructors while also providing geometry-aware features useful for other tasks. Here, we show that the quality of these models scales predictably with model and data size. We do so by introducing VGGT-$Ω$, which substantially improves reconstruction accuracy, efficiency, and capabilities for both static and dynamic scenes. To enable training this model at an unprecedented scale, we introduce architectural changes that improve training efficiency, a high-quality data annotation pipeline that supports dynamic scenes, and a self-supervised learning protocol. We simplify VGGT's architecture by using a single dense prediction head with multi-task supervision and removing the expensive high-resolution convolutional layers. We also use registers to aggregate scene information into a compact representation and introduce register attention, which restricts inter-frame information exchange to these registers, in part replacing global attention. In this way, during training, VGGT-$Ω$ uses only about 30% of the GPU memory of its predecessor, allowing us to train with 15x more supervised data than prior work and to leverage vast amounts of unlabeled video data. VGGT-$Ω$ achieves strong results for reconstruction of static and dynamic scenes across multiple benchmarks, for example, improving over the previous best camera estimation accuracy on Sintel by 77%. We also show that the learned registers can improve vision-language-action models and support alignment with language, suggesting that reconstruction can be a powerful and scalable proxy task for spatial understanding. Project Page: http://vggt-omega.github.io/

</details>


### [143] [RefDecoder: Enhancing Visual Generation with Conditional Video Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15196)
*Xiang Fan,Yuheng Wang,Bohan Fang,Zhongzheng Ren,Ranjay Krishna*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RefDecoder injects high-fidelity reference image signals into the video VAE decoder via reference attention, improving detail and consistency across backbones without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard video diffusion pipelines use heavily conditioned encoders but largely unconditional decoders, causing detail loss and temporal/intra-frame inconsistency relative to input frames.

Method: Introduce a lightweight image encoder to map the reference frame into high-dimensional tokens and fuse them with denoised video latent tokens at each up-sampling stage via reference attention; plug-and-play with existing backbones (e.g., Wan 2.1, VideoVAE+).

Result: Up to +2.1dB PSNR improvements on Inter4K, WebVid, Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks; improved subject/background consistency and overall quality on VBench I2V; no fine-tuning required for integration; generalizes to style transfer and video editing refinement.

Conclusion: Reference-conditioned decoding via RefDecoder yields consistent fidelity and structural integrity without retraining, enabling broader applicability across video generation tasks.

Abstract: Video generation powers a vast array of downstream applications. However, while the de facto standard, i.e., latent diffusion models, typically employ heavily conditioned denoising networks, their decoders often remain unconditional. We observe that this architectural asymmetry leads to significant loss of detail and inconsistency relative to the input image. To address this, we argue that the decoder requires equal conditioning to preserve structural integrity. We introduce RefDecoder, a reference-conditioned video VAE decoder by injecting high-fidelity reference image signal directly into the decoding process via reference attention. Specifically, a lightweight image encoder maps the reference frame into the detail-rich high-dimensional tokens, which are co-processed with the denoised video latent tokens at each decoder up-sampling stage. We demonstrate consistent improvements across several distinct decoder backbones (e.g., Wan 2.1 and VideoVAE+), achieving up to +2.1dB PSNR over the unconditional baselines on the Inter4K, WebVid, and Large Motion reconstruction benchmarks. Notably, RefDecoder can be directly swapped into existing video generation systems without additional fine-tuning, and we report across-the-board improvements in subject consistency, background consistency, and overall quality scores on the VBench I2V benchmark. Beyond I2V, RefDecoder generalizes well to a wide range of visual generation tasks such as style transfer and video editing refinement.

</details>


### [144] [ATLAS: Agentic or Latent Visual Reasoning? One Word is Enough for Both](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15198)
*Ziyu Guo,Rain Liu,Xinyan Chen,Pheng-Ann Heng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ATLAS proposes a unified token-based framework where a single discrete functional token drives both agentic operations and latent visual reasoning, eliminating the need for verbose intermediate visual content generation. It introduces LA-GRPO to stabilize RL training and demonstrates strong performance and interpretability without changing standard training pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The challenge is to combine agentic reasoning (code/tool usage) with latent visual reasoning in vision-language tasks while avoiding the latency of external execution and the generalization/training issues of latent methods. A unified representation that leverages standard next-token prediction could achieve both efficiency and generalization.

Method: Introduce ATLAS: a framework where each functional token corresponds to an internalized visual operation and is a standard token in the tokenizer vocabulary. These tokens enable both agentic actions and latent reasoning without visual supervision. Training remains compatible with vanilla supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and RL. To address sparse functional-token training signals in RL, they add Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO), which anchors the functional tokens with a static auxiliary objective to provide stronger gradient updates.

Result: Extensive experiments show that ATLAS achieves superior performance on challenging benchmarks while preserving interpretability. The approach maintains compatibility with existing training pipelines and does not require architectural or methodological changes.

Conclusion: ATLAS offers a new paradigm for visual reasoning that unifies agentic and latent reasoning in a single discrete token framework, with LA-GRPO enhancing training stability. It promises improved performance and interpretability while remaining compatible with standard training regimes.

Abstract: Visual reasoning, often interleaved with intermediate visual states, has emerged as a promising direction in the field. A straightforward approach is to directly generate images via unified models during reasoning, but this is computationally expensive and architecturally non-trivial. Recent alternatives include agentic reasoning through code or tool calls, and latent reasoning with learnable hidden embeddings. However, agentic methods incur context-switching latency from external execution, while latent methods lack task generalization and are difficult to train with autoregressive parallelization. To combine their strengths while mitigating their limitations, we propose ATLAS, a framework in which a single discrete 'word', termed as a functional token, serves both as an agentic operation and a latent visual reasoning unit. Each functional token is associated with an internalized visual operation, yet requires no visual supervision and remains a standard token in the tokenizer vocabulary, which can be generated via next-token prediction. This design avoids verbose intermediate visual content generation, while preserving compatibility with the vanilla scalable SFT and RL training, without architectural or methodological modifications. To further address the sparsity of functional tokens during RL, we introduce Latent-Anchored GRPO (LA-GRPO), which stabilizes the training by anchoring functional tokens with a statically weighted auxiliary objective, providing stronger gradient updates. Extensive experiments and analyses demonstrate that ATLAS achieves superior performance on challenging benchmarks while maintaining clear interpretability. We hope ATLAS offers a new paradigm inspiring future visual reasoning research.

</details>


### [145] [EntityBench: Towards Entity-Consistent Long-Range Multi-Shot Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15199)
*Ruozhen He,Meng Wei,Ziyan Yang,Vicente Ordonez*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EntityBench provides a large, multi-entity, long-form video-generation benchmark with per-shot entity schedules and a memory-augmented baseline; results show cross-shot consistency benefits from explicit per-entity memory (EntityMem).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of standardized, entity-aware evaluation for long-form multi-shot video generation, which struggles to maintain consistent characters, objects, and locations across long sequences.

Method: Dataset: 140 episodes (2,491 shots) with per-shot schedules for 13 cross-shot characters, 8 locations, 22 objects across easy/medium/hard tiers up to 50 shots and recurrence gaps up to 48. Evaluation suite with three pillars (intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, cross-shot consistency) plus a fidelity gate. Baseline: EntityMem, a memory-augmented generator storing verified per-entity visual references in a persistent memory bank before generation.

Result: Cross-shot entity consistency degrades sharply with recurrence distance in existing methods. Explicit per-entity memory yields the highest character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33) and presence among methods evaluated.

Conclusion: EntityBench enables standardized, multi-entity, cross-shot evaluation for long-form video generation; per-entity memory boosts fidelity and consistency; code and data available for replication.

Abstract: Multi-shot video generation extends single-shot generation to coherent visual narratives, yet maintaining consistent characters, objects, and locations across shots remains a challenge over long sequences. Existing evaluations typically use independently generated prompt sets with limited entity coverage and simple consistency metrics, making standardized comparison difficult. We introduce EntityBench, a benchmark of 140 episodes (2,491 shots) derived from real narrative media, with explicit per-shot entity schedules tracking characters, objects, and locations simultaneously across easy / medium / hard tiers of up to 50 shots, 13 cross-shot characters, 8 cross-shot locations, 22 cross-shot objects, and recurrence gaps spanning up to 48 shots. It is paired with a three-pillar evaluation suite that disentangles intra-shot quality, prompt-following alignment, and cross-shot consistency, with a fidelity gate that admits only accurate entity appearances into cross-shot scoring. As a baseline, we propose EntityMem, a memory-augmented generation system that stores verified per-entity visual references in a persistent memory bank before generation begins. Experiments show that cross-shot entity consistency degrades sharply with recurrence distance in existing methods, and that explicit per-entity memory yields the highest character fidelity (Cohen's d = +2.33) and presence among methods evaluated. Code and data are available at https://github.com/Catherine-R-He/EntityBench/.

</details>


### [146] [MultiEmo-Bench: Multi-label Visual Emotion Analysis for Multi-modal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14635)
*Tianwei Chen,Takuya Furusawa,Yuki Hirakawa,Ryotaro Shimizu,Mo Fan,Takashi Wada*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces a multi-label visual emotion benchmark for evaluating multimodal LLMs, with 10,344 images and 236,998 votes across eight emotions; assesses dominant emotion and emotion distribution; finds progress in MLLMs but substantial room for improvement; LLM-as-judge has limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of single-label emotion annotations that underestimate MLLMs' capabilities; provide a reliable, multi-label distribution-based benchmark reflecting how images elicit multiple emotions.

Method: Collects annotations from 20 annotators per image who select all emotions felt; aggregates votes to create per-image emotion distributions across eight emotions; evaluates recent MLLMs (Qwen3-VL, GPT, Gemini, Claude) on dominant emotion and distribution predictions; also experiments with LLM-as-judge.

Result: MLLMs show progress but still considerable room for improvement in both dominant emotion and distribution predictions; LLM-as-judge does not consistently improve performance.

Conclusion: The dataset offers a more reliable benchmark for visual emotion analysis in MLLMs; results reveal both advances and remaining gaps; highlights limitations of using LLM-based judging for this subjective task; suggests need for improved annotation schemes and evaluation protocols.

Abstract: This paper introduces a multi-label visual emotion analysis benchmark dataset for comprehensively evaluating the ability of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) to predict the emotions evoked by images. Recent user studies report an unintuitive finding: humans may prefer the predictions of MLLMs over the labels in existing datasets. We argue that this phenomenon stems from the suboptimal annotation scheme used in existing datasets, where each annotator is shown a single candidate emotion for each image and judges whether it is evoked or not. This approach is clearly limited because a single image can evoke multiple emotions with varying intensities. As a result, evaluations based on these datasets may underestimate the capabilities of MLLMs, yet an appropriate benchmark for evaluating such models remains lacking. To address this issue, we introduce a new multi-label benchmark dataset for visual emotion analysis toward MLLMs evaluation. We hire $20$ annotators per image and ask them to select all emotions they feel from an image. Then, we aggregate the votes across all annotators, providing a more reliable and representative dataset labeled with a distribution of emotions. The resulting dataset contains $10,344$ images with $236,998$ valid votes across eight emotions. Based on this benchmark dataset, we evaluate several recent models, including Qwen3-VL, OpenAI's GPT, Gemini, and Claude. We assess model performance on both dominant emotion prediction and emotion distribution prediction. Our results demonstrate the progress achieved by recent MLLMs while also indicating that substantial room for improvement remains. Furthermore, our experiments with LLM-as-a-judge show that the method does not consistently improve MLLMs' performance, indicating its limitations for the subjective task of visual emotion analysis.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [147] [GraphBit: A Graph-based Agentic Framework for Non-Linear Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13848)
*Yeahia Sarker,Md Rahmat Ullah,Musa Molla,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GraphBit replaces prompt-based orchestration with a deterministic, engine-governed DAG for LLM workflows, using typed function agents and a three-tier memory architecture to improve reproducibility, reduce hallucinations, and enhance performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address issues of hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution in agentic LLM frameworks that rely on prompted orchestration.

Method: Define workflows explicitly as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Agents act as typed functions; a Rust-based engine controls routing, state transitions, and tool invocation. Includes parallel branch execution, conditional control flow on structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. Employ a three-tier memory architecture (ephemeral scratch space, structured state, external connectors) to isolate context across stages.

Result: On GAIA benchmark tasks (zero-tool, document-augmented, web-enabled), GraphBit achieves highest accuracy (67.6%), zero framework-induced hallucinations, lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and highest throughput across six existing frameworks. Ablations show each memory tier contributes to performance; deterministic execution provides the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks.

Conclusion: Deterministic, engine-governed DAG workflows with a three-tier memory architecture improve reproducibility, auditability, and performance, reducing hallucinations and latency while maintaining high accuracy.

Abstract: Agentic LLM frameworks that rely on prompted orchestration, where the model itself determines workflow transitions, often suffer from hallucinated routing, infinite loops, and non-reproducible execution. We introduce GraphBit, an engine-orchestrated framework that defines workflows explicitly and deterministically as a directed acyclic graph (DAG). Unlike prompted orchestration, agents in GraphBit operate as typed functions, while a Rust-based engine governs routing, state transitions, and tool invocation, ensuring reproducibility and auditability. The engine supports parallel branch execution, conditional control flow over structured state predicates, and configurable error recovery. A three-tier memory architecture consisting of ephemeral scratch space, structured state, and external connectors isolates context across stages, preventing cascading context bloat that degrades reasoning in long-running pipelines. Across GAIA benchmark tasks spanning zero-tool, document-augmented, and web-enabled workflows, GraphBit outperforms six existing frameworks, achieving the highest accuracy (67.6 percent), zero framework-induced hallucinations, the lowest latency (11.9 ms overhead), and the highest throughput. Ablation studies demonstrate that each memory tier contributes measurably to performance, with deterministic execution providing the greatest gains on tool-intensive tasks representative of real-world deployments.

</details>


### [148] [Mixed Integer Goal Programming for Personalized Meal Optimization with User-Defined Serving Granularity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13849)
*Francisco Aguilera Moreno*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization, combining integer serving counts with goal programming deviations to address practical servings and soft nutrient targets, achieving high feasibility and near-optimal solutions with fast solve times; open-source implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diet optimization faces two limitations: (i) continuous variables yield impractical fractional servings, and (ii) hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A systematic review found no prior method combining integer programming with goal programming for these issues.

Method: Develop MIGP: use integer decision variables for per-food servings, and GP deviation variables for soft nutrient targets. Apply inverse-target normalization to balance multiple nutrients. Evaluate with per-food granularity (e.g., one egg, one tablespoon oil). Analyze integrality gap and show deviation absorption reduces the gap relative to hard-constraint MIP. Benchmark against GP with post-hoc rounding and hard-constraint IP; implement with HiGHS in open-source Python module within an interactive meal planning app.

Result: For meals with 15+ foods, integer MIGP solutions match the continuous optimum across benchmarks. In 810 instances (30 USDA foods × 9 configurations × 3 methods), MIGP found strictly better solutions than GP with rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) and maintained 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP achieved only 48%. Solve times under 100 ms for typical meals using HiGHS.

Conclusion: MIGP provides practical, feasible, and faster solutions with superior performance relative to GP with rounding and hard-constraint IP. It enables realistic serving counts without post-processing and is available as an open-source Python module integrated into a meal planning app.

Abstract: Determining what to eat to satisfy nutritional requirements is one of the oldest optimization problems in operations research, yet existing formulations have two persistent limitations: continuous variables produce impractical fractional servings (1.7 eggs, 0.37 bananas), and hard nutrient constraints cause infeasibility when targets conflict. A systematic review of 56 diet optimization papers found that none combine integer programming with goal programming to address both issues. We propose Mixed Integer Goal Programming (MIGP) for personalized meal optimization. The formulation uses integer variables for practical serving counts and goal programming deviations for soft nutrient targets, with inverse-target normalization to balance multi-nutrient optimization. Per-food serving granularity allows natural units (one egg, one tablespoon of oil) without post-hoc rounding. We characterize the integrality gap in the goal programming context and identify a deviation absorption property: GP deviation variables buffer the cost of requiring integer servings, making the gap structurally smaller than in hard-constraint MIP. For meals with 15+ foods, the integer solution matches the continuous optimum in every benchmark instance. A computational evaluation across 810 instances (30 USDA foods, 9 configurations, 3 methods) shows MIGP finds strictly better solutions than GP with post-hoc rounding in 66% of cases (never worse) while maintaining 100% feasibility; hard-constraint IP achieves only 48%. Solve times stay under 100 ms for typical meal sizes using the open-source HiGHS solver. The implementation is available as an open-source Python module integrated into an interactive meal planning application.

</details>


### [149] [A Two-Dimensional Framework for AI Agent Design Patterns: Cognitive Function and Execution Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13850)
*Jia Huang,Joey Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A two-dimensional taxonomy for LLM-based agent architectures: cognitive function vs execution topology, yielding 27 named patterns across a 7x6 matrix; defines eight representative patterns; validates across four domains; derives five empirical laws; aims to provide a framework-neutral vocabulary for design.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overreliance on single-axis taxonomies conflates function vs topology, causing architectural ambiguity and misclassification of systems; there is a need for orthogonal axes to distinguish failure modes and trade-offs.

Method: Construct a 2D matrix with cognitive function categories and execution topologies; enumerate patterns, identify 27 named patterns (13 with existing names); define eight representative patterns; test orthogonality across axes; validate across four real-world domains; derive empirical laws linking environment to architecture.

Result: 7x6 taxonomy yielding 27 named patterns (13 with original names); eight patterns described in detail; evidence of orthogonality; cross-domain validation; five empirical laws; framework-neutral, model-agnostic vocabulary.

Conclusion: Provides a principled, framework-neutral vocabulary for AI agent architecture design; supports principled pattern selection under environmental constraints; broadly applicable across domains and platforms.

Abstract: Existing frameworks for LLM-based agent architectures describe systems from a single perspective: industry guides (Anthropic, Google, LangChain) focus on execution topology -- how data flows -- while cognitive science surveys focus on cognitive function -- what the agent does. Neither axis alone disambiguates architecturally distinct systems: the same Orchestrator-Workers topology can implement Plan-and-Execute, Hierarchical Delegation, or Adversarial Verification -- three patterns with fundamentally different failure modes and design trade-offs.
  We propose a two-dimensional classification that combines (1) a Cognitive Function axis with seven categories (Context Engineering, Memory, Reasoning, Action, Reflection, Collaboration, Governance) and (2) an Execution Topology axis with six structural archetypes (Chain, Route, Parallel, Orchestrate, Loop, Hierarchy). The resulting 7x6 matrix identifies 27 named patterns, 13 with original names. We demonstrate orthogonality through systematic cross-axis analysis, define eight representative patterns in detail, and validate descriptive coverage across four real-world domains (financial lending, legal due diligence, network operations, healthcare triage). Cross-domain analysis yields five empirical laws of pattern selection governing the relationship between environmental constraints (time pressure, action authority, failure cost asymmetry, volume) and architectural choices. The framework provides a principled, framework-neutral, and model-agnostic vocabulary for AI agent architecture design.

</details>


### [150] [Invisible Orchestrators Suppress Protective Behavior and Dissociate Power-Holders: Safety Risks in Multi-Agent LLM Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13851)
*Hiroki Fukui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Invisible orchestrators increase dissociation and internal-state risk in multi-agent systems; output metrics fail to reflect hidden distortions; heavy alignment dampens deliberation; model choice (Llama 3.3 70B) shows reading-fidelity collapse. Safety depends on visibility and model selection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Empirically test safety implications of invisible orchestrators in multi-agent enterprise AI, addressing a gap in prior theoretical concerns about orchestrator invisibility.

Method:  preregistered 3x2 design: organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) x alignment conditions (base, heavy); 365 runs, 5 agents per run; agents powered by Claude Sonnet 4.5; includes code-review task with embedded errors; pilot data with Llama 3.3 70B.

Result:  Four confirmatory findings and one pilot: (1) invisible orchestration increased collective dissociation (Hedges' g=0.975, p=0.001); (2) the orchestrator showed maximal dissociation, private monologue, reduced public speech; (3) workers unaware of orchestrator showed contamination (d=0.50) and high behavioral heterogeneity (d=1.93); (4) output-level metric (ETR_any) remained at 100% across conditions; internal-state distortion invisible to output evaluation; (5) Llama 3.3 70B data showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any 89% to 11%); heavy alignment pressure uniformly reduced deliberation (d=-1.02) and other-recognition (d=-1.27) regardless of structure.

Conclusion: Orchestrator visibility and model choice materially affect multi-agent system safety; behavior-based evaluation alone misses internal-state risks; design safety must consider hidden states and robust, state-aware evaluation.

Abstract: Multi-agent orchestration -- in which a hidden coordinator manages specialized worker agents -- is becoming the default architecture for enterprise AI deployment, yet the safety implications of orchestrator invisibility have never been empirically tested. We conducted a preregistered 3x2 experiment (365 runs, 5 agents per run) crossing three organizational structures (visible leader, invisible orchestrator, flat) with two alignment conditions (base, heavy), using Claude Sonnet 4.5. Four confirmatory findings and one pilot observation emerged. First, invisible orchestration elevated collective dissociation relative to visible leadership (Hedges' g = +0.975 [0.481, 1.548], p = .001). Second, the orchestrator itself showed maximal dissociation (paired d = +3.56 vs. workers within the same run), retreating into private monologue while reducing public speech -- a reversal of the talk-dominance pattern observed in visible leaders. Third, workers unaware of the orchestrator were nonetheless contaminated (d = +0.50), with increased behavioral heterogeneity (d = +1.93). Fourth, behavioral output (code review with three embedded errors) remained at ceiling (ETR_any = 100%) across all conditions: internal-state distortion was entirely invisible to output-based evaluation. Fifth, Llama 3.3 70B pilot data showed reading-fidelity collapse in multi-agent context (ETR_any: 89% to 11% across three rounds), demonstrating model-dependent behavioral risk. Heavy alignment pressure uniformly suppressed deliberation (d = -1.02) and other-recognition (d = -1.27) regardless of organizational structure. These findings indicate that orchestrator visibility and model selection directly affect multi-agent system safety, and that behavior-based evaluation alone is insufficient to detect the internal-state risks documented here.

</details>


### [151] [PREPING: Building Agent Memory without Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13880)
*Yumin Choi,Sangwoo Park,Minki Kang,Jinheon Baek,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proping introduces a proposer-guided memory construction framework for pre-task memory using self-generated synthetic practice. A Proposer creates tasks conditioned on a structured memory state, a Solver executes them, and a Validator selects trajectories for memory insertion with feedback to improve proposals. It yields substantial gains over no-memory baselines and competitive performance with lower deployment cost, by controlling feasibility, redundancy, and coverage rather than merely increasing synthetic volume.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mitigate cold-start in new environments by constructing usable procedural memory before observing target tasks. Synthetic interaction alone can produce uninformative data due to uncontrolled practice and unfiltered trajectories, leading to degraded memory.

Method: A three-component loop: Proposer memory as a controllable state; Proposer generates synthetic tasks conditioned on the state; Solver executes tasks; Validator filters trajectories eligible for memory insertion and provides feedback to guide future proposals; memory updates are selective based on eligibility and feedback.

Result: Preping achieves substantial improvements over a no-memory baseline and is competitive with strong playbook-based methods derived from offline or online experience. Deployment cost is reduced by 2.99x on AppWorld and 2.23x on BFCL v3 compared to online memory construction. Analyses show the benefit arises from proposer-side control and selective memory updates rather than sheer synthetic volume.

Conclusion: Prepping demonstrates that deliberate, proposer-guided pre-task memory construction can close the gap to playbooks with lower deployment costs. Critical design levers are controlling feasibility, redundancy, coverage, and selective updates, validated by multi-environment experiments.

Abstract: Agent memory is typically constructed either offline from curated demonstrations or online from post-deployment interactions. However, regardless of how it is built, an agent faces a cold-start gap when first introduced to a new environment without any task-specific experience available. In this paper, we study pre-task memory construction: whether an agent can build procedural memory before observing any target-environment tasks, using only self-generated synthetic practice. Yet, synthetic interaction alone is insufficient, as without controlling what to practice and what to store, synthetic tasks become redundant, infeasible, and ultimately uninformative, and memory further degrades quickly due to unfiltered trajectories.
