To overcome this, we present Preping, a proposer-guided memory construction framework. At its core is proposer memory, a structured control state that shapes future practice. A Proposer generates synthetic tasks conditioned on this state, a Solver executes them, and a Validator determines which trajectories are eligible for memory insertion while also providing feedback to guide future proposals. Experiments on AppWorld, BFCL v3, and MCP-Universe show that Preping substantially improves over a no-memory baseline and achieves performance competitive with strong playbook-based methods built from offline or online experience, with deployment cost $2.99\times$ lower on AppWorld and $2.23\times$ lower on BFCL v3 than online memory construction. Further analyses reveal that the main benefit does not come from synthetic volume alone, but from proposer-side control over feasibility, redundancy, and coverage, combined with selective memory updates.

</details>


### [152] [PolitNuggets: Benchmarking Agentic Discovery of Long-Tail Political Facts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14002)
*Yifei Zhu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Multilingual, agentic information synthesis benchmark PolitNuggets with a FactNet protocol; reveals gaps in fine-grained factuality and efficiency across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Assess long-tail, cross-source factual synthesis in agentic large reasoning models and standardize evaluation beyond static QA.

Method: Create political biographies of 400 global elites (~10k facts); deploy an optimized multi-agent evaluation framework; introduce FactNet, an evidence-conditional scoring protocol for discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency; evaluate across models and settings; use diagnostics to relate performance to model capabilities.

Result: Agents struggle with fine-grained details and show variable efficiency; short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use are critical determinants of performance.

Conclusion: Highlight the need to improve short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and tool-use reliability; provide PolitNuggets and FactNet to standardize evaluation of agentic information synthesis.

Abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) embedded in agentic frameworks have transformed information retrieval from static, long context question answering into open-ended exploration. Yet real world use requires models to discover and synthesize "long-tail" facts from dispersed sources, a capability that remains under-evaluated. We introduce PolitNuggets, a multilingual benchmark for agentic information synthesis via constructing political biographies for 400 global elites, covering over 10000 political facts. We standardize evaluation with an optimized multi agent system and propose FactNet, an evidence conditional protocol that scores discovery, fine-grained accuracy, and efficiency. Across models and settings, we find that current systems often struggle with fine-grained details, and vary substantially in efficiency. Finally, using benchmark diagnostics, we relate agent performance to underlying model capabilities, highlighting the importance of short-context extraction, multilingual robustness, and reliable tool use.

</details>


### [153] [Conditional Attribute Estimation with Autoregressive Sequence Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14004)
*Erica Stutz,Giacomo Marino,Daniella Meeker,Qiao Liu,Andrew J. Loza*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Conditional Attribute Transformers (CAT) to jointly estimate next-token probability and an attribute value conditioned on each candidate next token, enabling per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation in a single forward pass; achieves strong results on sparse rewards, improves token prediction with sufficient model size, and speeds attribute probability estimation and guided decoding across language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Next-token training focuses on local patterns, often neglecting global sequence structure and controllability. Existing methods for estimating/predicting attributes during generation rely on expensive sampling or downstream modifications. There is a need for a single-forward-pass framework that jointly models token likelihood and attribute value to enable efficient credit assignment and steering of generation.

Method: Proposes a framework that, for each potential next token, jointly computes P(next_token) and P(attribute | next_token). This enables per-token credit assignment, counterfactual analysis (attribute differences under alternative next tokens), and steerable generation (decoding via a combination of token and attribute likelihoods) in a single forward pass without modifying the input sequence.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on sparse reward tasks; improves next-token prediction with sufficiently large models; estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling; can guide decoding of autoregressive models on a range of language tasks.

Conclusion: Conditional Attribute Transformers enable efficient, end-to-end control of sequence-level attributes in autoregressive models, providing credit assignment, counterfactual analysis, and steerable generation in a single forward pass with broad applicability.

Abstract: Generative models are often trained with a next-token prediction objective, yet many downstream applications require the ability to estimate or control sequence-level properties. Next-token prediction can lead to overfitting of local patterns during training, underfitting of global structure, and requires significant downstream modifications or expensive sampling to guide or predict the global attributes of generated samples at inference time. Here, we introduce Conditional Attribute Transformers, a novel method for jointly estimating the next-token probability and the value of an attribute conditional on each potential next token selection. This framework enables three critical capabilities within a single forward pass, without modification of the input sequence: (1) per-token credit assignment across an entire sequence, by identifying how each token in a sequence is associated with an attribute's value; (2) counterfactual analysis, by quantifying attribute differences conditional on alternative next token choices; (3) steerable generation, by decoding sequences based on a combination of next-token and attribute likelihoods. Our approach achieves state of the art performance on sparse reward tasks, improves next-token prediction at sufficient model sizes, estimates attribute probabilities orders of magnitude faster than sampling, and can guide decoding of autoregressive sequence models on a range of language tasks.

</details>


### [154] [Sheaf-Theoretic Transport and Obstruction for Detecting Scientific Theory Shift in AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14033)
*David N. Olivieri,Roque J. Hernández*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Finite sheaf-theoretic diagnostic for detecting theory-shifts in AI agents via transport vs extension, using gluing-based coherence checks and obstruction ranking on a transition-card benchmark; includes a secondary kernel probe.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To go beyond fitting equations to data and determine when an existing representational framework remains transportable in a new regime or requires extension; provide a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents to identify transport failure and the next coherent move.

Method: Organize contexts into a local-to-global structure with source, overlap, target, and validation charts; perform fitting, restriction, and gluing tests; define obstruction measures (residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, representational cost); evaluate on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within source language from extension of that language; incorporate a constellation kernel as a secondary representational-similarity probe.

Result: Direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is typically the lowest-obstruction candidate; transition type is separable in the benchmark; the constellation kernel provides a secondary diagnostic probe.

Conclusion: The work isolates a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents to detect when representational transport fails and extension is the coherent next move; does not aim to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention.

Abstract: Scientific theory shift in AI agents requires more than fitting equations to data. An artificial scientific agent must detect whether an existing representational framework remains transportable into a new regime, or whether its language has become locally-to-globally obstructed and must be extended. This paper develops a finite sheaf-theoretic framework for detecting theory-shift candidates through transport and obstruction. Contexts are organized as a local-to-global structure in which source, overlap, target, and validation charts are fitted, restricted, and tested for gluing. Obstruction measures failure of coherence through residual fit, overlap incompatibility, constraint violation, limiting-relation failure, and representational cost. We evaluate the framework on a controlled transition-card benchmark designed to separate deformation within a source language from extension of that language. The main result is direct obstruction ranking: the intended deformation or extension is usually the lowest-obstruction candidate, and transition type is separated in the benchmark. A constellation kernel over the same signatures is included only as a secondary representational-similarity probe. The aim is not to reconstruct historical paradigm shifts or solve open-ended autonomous theory invention, but to isolate a finite diagnostic subproblem for AI agents: detecting when representational transport fails and extension becomes the coherent next move.

</details>


### [155] [Enhanced and Efficient Reasoning in Large Learning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14036)
*Leslie G. Valiant*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a two-stage reasoning framework for LLMs: first recodes data into a Unary Relational Integracode to explicit relationships, then learns via standard ML; claims polynomial-time learnability of relational rules and applicability to vision/actions; uses Robust Logic for principled chaining; aims to improve trust in generated content while preserving current hardware/software base.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM outputs lack a principled basis for trusting content; belief that principled reasoning is too computationally expensive; need to adapt reasoning methods to existing software/hardware while improving trust.

Method: Stage 1: preprocess data into a Unary Relational Integracode that explicitly encodes relationships among objects; Stage 2: apply standard ML to predict these relationships. Introduces Robust Logic for principled chaining on uncertain learned information. Claims applicability beyond natural language to vision and actions. The polynomial-time learnability of a core subset of relational rules depends on rule complexity.

Result: The approach provides theoretical justification that the recoding enables polynomial-time learnability of a core subset of world-relational rules, supporting sound reasoning within a single classifier call as well as across multiple calls. The paper emphasizes theoretical advantages and framework, rather than empirical results.

Conclusion: The proposed two-stage recoding and learning framework offers a principled, efficient path to robust reasoning in LLMs and other modalities, preserving existing hardware/software bases and enabling scalable, compositional reasoning via Robust Logic.

Abstract: In current Large Language Models we can trust the production of smoothly flowing prose on the basis of the principles of machine learning. However, there is no comparably principled basis to justify trust in the content of the text produced. It appears to be conventional wisdom that addressing this issue by adding more principled reasoning is not computationally affordable.
  Here we propose a principled method of reasoning that is efficient enough to be practical for large language models. Further, the method allows the retention of much of the currently used software and hardware base. Our method for improving the functioning of large language models consists of a first stage of preprocessing that recodes the data to a Unary Relational Integracode that is more explicit about the relationships among the objects described in the text, followed as a second stage by a standard but possibly streamlined machine learning process that then also learns to predict these relationships.
  The method may be viewed as realizing a world model and applying beyond natural language, to vision and actions, for example, where the multiple properties of an object referred to in an input are brought together explicitly, rather than remaining distributed in the various references to it in the input. We articulate its advantages in terms of Robust Logic, a system for performing principled chaining on learned, and hence uncertain, information. We show that this recoding has the surprising and fortuitous property that, while succinct, it makes the task of learning a core subset of relational rules that hold in the world described in the training data polynomial time learnable in a defined sense, the polynomial depending on the complexity of the rule. This gives support for sound reasoning within each single call of the learned classifier as well as between multiple calls.

</details>


### [156] [Model-Adaptive Tool Necessity Reveals the Knowing-Doing Gap in LLM Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14038)
*Yize Cheng,Chenrui Fan,Mahdi JafariRaviz,Keivan Rezaei,Soheil Feiz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a model-adaptive notion of tool-necessity for LLMs, showing large gaps between when a tool is needed and when it is actually used; analyzes cognition vs execution stages and reveals a knowing-doing gap that hinders reliable tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Because tool usage in LLMs is often treated as model-agnostic, but capability boundaries vary across models; a definition grounded in empirical performance is needed to reliably determine when to invoke tools.

Method: Define an empirical, model-adaptive tool-necessity; compare necessity to observed tool calls across four models on arithmetic and factual QA datasets; decompose tool use into internal cognition and execution stages; probe hidden states to assess decodability; analyze the trajectory of samples to locate where mismatches arise.

Result: Found substantial mismatches: arithmetic 26.5-54.0% and factual QA 30.8-41.8% mismatch between necessity and actual tool calls; both cognition and execution signals are linearly decodable, but their directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime; the majority of mismatch lies in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself.

Conclusion: Reveals a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use; improving reliability requires better recognition of tool necessity and better translation of that recognition into action (tool invocation).

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as autonomous agents that must decide when to answer directly vs. when to invoke external tools. Prior work studying adaptive tool use has largely treated tool necessity as a model-agnostic property, annotated by human or LLM judge, and mostly cover cases where the answer is obvious (e.g., fetching the weather vs. paraphrasing text). However, tool necessity in the wild is more nuanced due to the divergence of capability boundaries across models: a problem solvable by a strong model on its own may still require tools for a weaker one. In this work, we introduce a model-adaptive definition of tool-necessity, grounded in each model's empirical performance. Following this definition, we compare the necessity against observed tool-call behavior across four models on arithmetic and factual QA dataset, and find substantial mismatches of 26.5-54.0% and 30.8-41.8%, respectively. To diagnose the failure, we decompose tool use into two stages: an internal cognition stage that reflects whether a model believes a tool is necessary, and an execution stage that determines whether the model actually makes a tool-call action. By probing the LLM hidden states, we find that both signals are often linearly decodable, yet their probe directions become nearly orthogonal in the late-layer, last-token regime that drives the next-token action. By tracing the trajectory of samples in the two-stage process, we further discover that the majority of mismatch is concentrated in the cognition-to-action transition, not in cognition itself. These results reveal a knowing-doing gap in LLM tool-use: improving tool-use reliability requires not only better recognition of when tools are needed, but also better translation of that recognition into action.

</details>


### [157] [Network-Aware Bilinear Tokenization for Brain Functional Connectivity Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14048)
*Leo Milecki,Qingyu Hu,Bahram Jafrasteh,Mert R. Sabuncu,Qingyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NERVE introduces network-aware tokenization and bilinear embedding for resting-state FC, improving cross-cohort transferability and prediction of behavior/psychopathology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To align self-supervised FC representation learning with the brain's intrinsic modular organization, addressing limitations of region-centric/graph-based tokenization in MAEs.

Method: Partition FC matrices into intra- and inter-network blocks. Apply a structured bilinear factorization to embed patches, preserving network identity and reducing parameter scaling from quadratic to linear in the number of networks. Use anatomically grounded parcellation. Evaluate on ABCD, PNC, CCNP for behavior and psychopathology prediction, with ablations to assess components.

Result: NERVE yields more stable and transferable representations than structurally agnostic MAEs and graph-based baselines, especially in cross-cohort evaluation. Ablations confirm the necessity of bilinear embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation.

Conclusion: Incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning substantially improves functional connectomics representations and cross-cohort generalization.

Abstract: Masked autoencoders (MAEs) have recently shown promise for self-supervised representation learning of resting-state brain functional connectivity (FC). However, a fundamental question remains unresolved: how should FC matrices be tokenized to align with the intrinsic modular organization of large-scale brain networks? Existing approaches typically adopt region-centric or graph-based schemes that treat FC as structurally homogeneous elements and overlook the large-scale network brain organization. We introduce NERVE (Network-Aware Representations of Brain Functional Connectivity via Bilinear Tokenization), a self-supervised learning framework that redefines FC tokenization by partitioning FC matrices into patches of intra- and inter-network connectivity blocks. Unlike image-based MAE, where fixed-size patches share a common tokenizer, FC patches defined by network pairs are heterogeneous in size and correspond to distinct functional roles. To resolve this problem, NERVE embeds FC patches through a novel structured bilinear factorization. This formulation preserves network identity and reduces parameter complexity from quadratic to linear scaling in the number of networks. We evaluate NERVE across three large-scale developmental cohorts (ABCD, PNC, and CCNP) for behavior and psychopathology prediction. Compared to structurally agnostic MAE variants and graph-based self-supervised baselines, the proposed network-aware formulation yields more stable and transferable representations, particularly in cross-cohort evaluation. Ablation studies confirm that the proposed bilinear network embedding and anatomically grounded parcellation are critical for performance. These findings highlight the importance of incorporating domain-specific structural priors into self-supervised learning for functional connectomics.

</details>


### [158] [Bridging Legal Interpretation and Formal Logic: Faithfulness, Assumption, and the Future of AI Legal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14049)
*Olivia Peiyu Wang,Leilani H. Gilpin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic legal AI framework that fuses LLM reasoning with formal verification to provide grounded, verifiable legal conclusions, addressing hallucinations and unsupported inferences while reducing manual verification and preserving accountability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs promise high efficiency in legal tasks but exhibit hallucinations and unjustified inferences; legal practice requires rigorous, source-grounded, auditable reasoning with accountability.

Method: Propose a neuro-symbolic architecture that combines the expressive capabilities of large language models with formal verification techniques to ensure that conclusions are supported by source texts and verifiable; includes a loop between reasoning and verification to maintain accountability.

Result: A proposed framework, not yet empirically validated, intended to deliver capable and trustworthy AI-assisted legal reasoning and reduce verification burden.

Conclusion: Goal: establish a system where AI-assisted legal reasoning is both capable and trustworthy, balancing automation with rigorous accountability.

Abstract: The growing adoption of large language models in legal practice brings both significant promise and serious risk. Legal professionals stand to benefit from AI that can reason over contracts, draft documents, and analyze sources at scale, yet the high-stakes nature of legal work demands a level of rigor that current AI systems do not provide. The central problem is not simply that LLMs hallucinate facts and references; it is that they systematically draw inferences that go beyond what the source text actually supports, presenting assumption-laden conclusions as if they were logically grounded. This proposal presents a neuro-symbolic approach to legal AI that combines the expressive power of large language models with the rigor of formal verification, aiming to make AI-assisted legal reasoning both capable and trustworthy, thus reducing the burden of manual verification without sacrificing the accountability that legal practice demands.

</details>


### [159] [SPIN: Structural LLM Planning via Iterative Navigation for Industrial Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14051)
*Yusuke Ozaki,Dhaval Patel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SPIN: a DAG-based planning wrapper that ensures executable LLM plans via validation/repair and incremental prefix execution, improving efficiency and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial LLM agent systems often produce structurally invalid or overly long workflows; reducing wasted tool calls and brittleness by enforcing a DAG-based plan and controlled execution.

Method: Integrates validated DAG planning with repair prompting through _validate_plan_text, producing executable plans prior to execution, and evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix suffices to answer the query.

Result: On AssetOpsBench (261 scenarios), executed tasks drop from 1061 to 623; Accomplished score rises from 0.638 to 0.706; tool calls per run fall from 11.81 to 6.82. On MCP Bench, SPIN also improves planning, grounding, and dependency-related scores for GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

Conclusion: SPIN yields more reliable and cost-efficient planning-execution cycles in industrial LLM agent systems by enforcing a DAG contract and prefix-based evaluation.

Abstract: Industrial LLM agent systems often separate planning from execution, yet LLM planners frequently produce structurally invalid or unnecessarily long workflows, leading to brittle failures and avoidable tool and API cost. We propose \texttt{SPIN}, a planning wrapper that combines validated Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) planning with prefix based execution control. \texttt{SPIN} enforces a strict DAG contract through \texttt{\_validate\_plan\_text} and repair prompting, producing executable plans before downstream execution, and then evaluates DAG prefixes incrementally to stop when the current prefix is sufficient to answer the query. On AssetOpsBench, across 261 scenarios, \texttt{SPIN} reduces executed tasks from 1061 to 623 and improves \emph{Accomplished} from 0.638 to 0.706, while reducing tool calls from 11.81 to 6.82 per run. On MCP Bench, the same wrapper improves planning, grounding, and dependency related scores for both GPT OSS1 and Llama 4 Maverick.

</details>


### [160] [Bad Seeing or Bad Thinking? Rewarding Perception for Vision-Language Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14054)
*Haozhe Wang,Qixin Xu,Changpeng Wang,Taofeng Xue,Chong Peng,Wenhu Chen,Fangzhen Lin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A reinforcement learning framework (MoCA) for vision-language models that decouples perception and reasoning, uses Perception Verification (PV) and Structured Verbal Verification to reward perceptual fidelity, and applies modality-aware credit assignment to route errors to bad seeing or bad thinking, enabling robust, scalable perception–reasoning synergy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLMs face a perception–reasoning trade-off and a pervasive credit-assignment ambiguity: failures may stem from poor perception or poor reasoning. External agentic modules and static textual reasoning add cost without proportional gains, producing a seesaw effect between perception and reasoning.

Method: Decouple generation into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. Introduce Perception Verification (PV) via a blindfolded reasoning proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of outcomes. Adopt Structured Verbal Verification to replace high-variance LLM judgments with structured algorithmic checks. Integrate these components into a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) mechanism that assigns rewards to bad seeing or bad thinking, enabling a single VLM to improve across diverse tasks.

Result: The framework is designed to yield simultaneous improvements in perception and reasoning across a wide set of vision–language tasks, with scalable training across free-form VL tasks.

Conclusion: By explicitly disentangling perception and reasoning and aligning rewards with the true bottleneck via PV, Structured Verifications, and MoCA, the approach mitigates the perception–reasoning trade-off and enhances overall VLM performance without heavy external agents.

Abstract: Achieving robust perception-reasoning synergy is a central goal for advanced Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Recent advancements have pursued this goal via architectural designs or agentic workflows. However, these approaches are often limited by static textual reasoning or complicated by the significant compute and engineering burden of external agentic complexity. Worse, this heavy investment does not yield proportional gains, often witnessing a "seesaw effect" on perception and reasoning. This motivates a fundamental rethinking of the true bottleneck. In this paper, we argue that the root cause of this trade-off is an ambiguity in modality credit assignment: when a VLM fails, is it due to flawed perception ("bad seeing") or flawed logic ("bad thinking")? To resolve this, we introduce a reinforcement learning framework that improves perception-reasoning synergy by reliably rewarding the perception fidelity. We explicitly decompose the generation process into interleaved perception and reasoning steps. This decoupling enables targeted supervision on perception. Crucially, we introduce Perception Verification (PV), leveraging a "blindfolded reasoning" proxy to reward perceptual fidelity independently of reasoning outcomes. Furthermore, to scale training across free-form VL tasks, we propose Structured Verbal Verification, which replaces high-variance LLM judging with structured algorithmic execution. These techniques are integrated into a Modality-Aware Credit Assignment (MoCA) mechanism, which routes rewards to the specific source of error -- either bad seeing or bad thinking -- enabling a single VLM to achieve simultaneous performance gains across a wide task spectrum.

</details>


### [161] [MathAtlas: A Benchmark for Autoformalization in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14061)
*Nilay Patel,Noah Arias,Davit Babayan,Victoria Cochran,Timothy Libman,Hafsah Mahmood,Liam McCarty,Soli Munoz,Laurel Willey,Jeffrey Flanigan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MathAtlas is a large-scale, in-the-wild graduate-level math autoformalization benchmark with 52k items and a 178k-edge dependency graph, revealing extreme difficulty for current models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Graduate-level mathematical formalization is underexplored; existing benchmarks target Olympiad/undergrad; a realistic, dependency-aware dataset is needed to drive progress.

Method: Assemble MathAtlas from 103 graduate textbooks, extract theorems/definitions/exercises/examples/proofs (~52k items), construct a ~178k-edge dependency graph, and evaluate state-of-the-art models on theorem statements and definitions, including a MA-Hard subset with deepest dependency trees.

Result: MathAtlas is high-quality but extremely challenging: best baselines achieve 9.8% correctness on theorems and 16.7% on definitions; in MA-Hard, best model drops to 2.6% correctness; demonstrates sensitivity to dependency depth.

Conclusion: MathAtlas is released as a community benchmark to enable large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics and to support development of dependency-aware systems.

Abstract: Current autoformalization benchmarks are largely focused on olympiad or undergraduate mathematics, while graduate and research-level mathematics remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce MathAtlas, the first large-scale autoformalization benchmark of in the wild graduate-level mathematics, containing ~52k theorems, definitions, exercises, examples, and proofs extracted from 103 graduate mathematics textbooks. MathAtlas is enriched with a mathematical dependency graph containing ~178k relations, and is the first autoformalization benchmark to include such relations, facilitating evaluation and development of dependency-aware autoformalization systems. Our extensive experiments show that MathAtlas is high quality but extremely challenging: strong baselines achieve at most 9.8% correctness on theorem statements and 16.7% on definitions. Furthermore, we find performance of state-of-the-art models degrades substantially with dependency depth: on MA-Hard, a subset of 700 entities with the deepest dependency trees, the best model achieves only 2.6% correctness for autoformalization on this challenging dataset. We release MathAtlas to the community as a benchmark set for large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics in the wild.

</details>


### [162] [Know When To Fold 'Em: Token-Efficient LLM Synthetic Data Generation via Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14062)
*Anjir Ahmed Chowdhury,Syed Zawad,Feng Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MSIFR is a training-free framework that performs early, in-flight rejection of low-quality LLM outputs by sequentially applying fast validators at intermediate checkpoints, reducing token waste while preserving accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses substantial token waste in synthetic data generation when quality filters are applied post hoc to full generations; aims to terminate faulty trajectories early to save compute.

Method: Decomposes generation into sequential stages; uses fast rule-based validators to detect arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucinations, and formatting violations; formalizes in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process; proves non-trivial discard policies reduce expected token use; shows conditional utility estimates form a martingale ensuring no bias to retained samples.

Result: MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11–77% as a standalone method and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit techniques, across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks; maintains or improves evaluation accuracy.

Conclusion: MSIFR offers a practical, training-free mechanism to improve efficiency in LLM-based synthetic data generation by early rejection of low-quality generations without compromising performance.

Abstract: While synthetic data generation with large language models (LLMs) is widely used in post-training pipelines, existing approaches typically generate full outputs before applying quality filters, leading to substantial token waste on samples that are ultimately discarded. To address this, we propose Multi-Stage In-Flight Rejection (MSIFR), a lightweight, training-free framework that detects and terminates low-quality generation trajectories at intermediate checkpoints before they reach full completion. MSIFR decomposes the generation process into sequential stages and applies fast rule-based validators to identify arithmetic inconsistencies, hallucination patterns, and formatting violations, enabling early rejection of faulty samples. We formalize in-flight rejection as a sequential decision process and show that any non-trivial discard policy reduces expected token consumption, with stage-wise savings increasing when rejection occurs earlier in the generation pipeline. We further demonstrate that conditional utility estimates form a martingale, ensuring that early, in-flight rejection does not bias the expected utility of retained samples. Across five instruction-tuned models and seven reasoning benchmarks, MSIFR reduces token consumption by 11%-77% as a standalone method, and up to 78.2% when combined with early-exit methods, while preserving or improving evaluation accuracy. These results confirm that MSIFR provides a practical mechanism for improving the efficiency of LLM-based synthetic data generation without additional training or architectural changes.

</details>


### [163] [SkillFlow: Flow-Driven Recursive Skill Evolution for Agentic Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14089)
*Mingda Zhang,Tiesunlong Shen,Haoran Luo,Wenjin Liu,Zikai Xiao,Erik Cambria,Xiaoying Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SkillFlow introduces a trainable supervisor with a flow-based orchestration framework that uses Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB) to align trajectories with reward, enables transparent credit assignment via a backward flow, and implements a recursive skill evolution mechanism for growth; it outperforms baselines across 14 datasets in QA, math reasoning, code generation, and interactive decision tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based task orchestration methods suffer from strategy collapse under reward maximization, high gradient variance with opaque credit assignment, and unguided skill evolution. A principled, trainable flow-based framework can address these core challenges.

Method: A flow-based framework with a trainable Supervisor, a structured environment featuring a dynamic skill library and a frozen executor, and multi-turn interaction. It introduces Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward. The same flow objective yields a jointly learned backward policy for per-step credit assignment at zero extra inference cost. It further adds a recursive skill evolution mechanism to decide when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and where decision gaps lie.

Result: Experimental results on 14 datasets show SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines across question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks.

Conclusion: SkillFlow provides a principled, scalable approach to task orchestration with transparent credit assignment and continuous skill growth, improving performance and avoiding strategy collapse by preserving diverse orchestration modes.

Abstract: In recent years, a variety of powerful LLM-based agentic systems have been applied to automate complex tasks through task orchestration. However, existing orchestration methods still face key challenges, including strategy collapse under reward maximization, high gradient variance with opaque credit assignment, and unguided skill evolution whose decisions are typically made by directly prompting an LLM to judge rather than derived from principled training signals. To address these challenges, we propose SkillFlow, a flow-based framework that takes a trainable Supervisor as the agent and a structured environment with dynamic skill library and frozen executor, automating task orchestration through multi-turn interaction. SkillFlow employs Tempered Trajectory Balance (TTB), a regression-based flow-matching loss that samples trajectories proportional to reward, preserving diverse orchestration strategies rather than collapsing to a single mode. The same flow objective yields a jointly learned backward policy that provides transparent per-step credit assignment at zero additional inference cost. Building on these flow diagnostics, a recursive skill evolution mechanism determines when to evolve, what skills to create or prune, and where decision gaps lie -- closing the loop from training signal to autonomous capability growth. Experimental results on 14 datasets show that SkillFlow significantly outperforms baselines across question answering, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and real-world interactive decision making tasks. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SkillFlow-E850.

</details>


### [164] [ChromaFlow: A Negative Ablation Study of Orchestration Overhead in Tool-Augmented Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14102)
*Tarun Mittal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Tool-augmented autonomous reasoning (ChromaFlow) with planner-directed execution shows that more aggressive orchestration can degrade performance and increase operational noise; stable evaluation requires bounded escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address failure modes in autonomous agents that are not captured by final accuracy, by evaluating telemetry, tool usage, and run governance during planning and execution.

Method: A tool-augmented autonomous framework (ChromaFlow) using planner-directed execution, specialized tools, and telemetry-driven evaluation; evaluated on GAIA 2023 Level-1 validation tasks under clean evaluation constraints; compared a frozen Level-1 baseline to a recovery/configuration with expanded orchestration; added smoke evaluations to probe stability.

Result: Baseline: 29/53 correct (54.72%). Recovery/config: 27/53 correct (50.94%), with more tracebacks, timeouts, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log cost estimates. Two 20-task smoke tests: 12/20 (60%) and 11/20 (55%). Conclusion: more aggressive orchestration did not improve full-set performance and increased operational noise; small diagnostic gains were unstable across samples.

Conclusion: For reliable autonomous-agent evaluation, prioritize bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates as first-order requirements; telemetry and run governance are essential to reveal and manage failure modes beyond final accuracy.

Abstract: Autonomous language-model agents increasingly combine planning, tool use, document processing, browsing, code execution, and verification loops. These capabilities make agent systems more useful, but they also introduce operational failure modes that are not visible from final accuracy alone. This report presents ChromaFlow, a tool-augmented autonomous reasoning framework built around planner-directed execution, specialized tool use, and telemetry-driven evaluation. We analyze ChromaFlow on GAIA 2023 Level-1 validation tasks under clean evaluation constraints. A frozen full Level-1 baseline achieved 29/53 correct answers, or 54.72%. A later recovery configuration with expanded orchestration achieved 27/53 correct answers, or 50.94%, while increasing tracebacks, timeout events, tool-failure mentions, token-line calls, and campaign-log cost estimates. Two randomized 20-task smoke evaluations produced 12/20 and 11/20 correct answers, showing that small diagnostic gains can be unstable across samples. The central result is therefore a negative ablation: more aggressive orchestration did not improve full-set performance and increased operational noise. The report argues that bounded planner escalation, deterministic extraction, evidence reconciliation, and explicit run gates should be treated as first-order requirements for reliable autonomous agent evaluation.

</details>


### [165] [Modeling Bounded Rationality in Drug Shortage Pharmacists Using Attention-Guided Dynamic Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14111)
*Yaniv Eliyahu Amiri,Noah Chicoine,Jacqueline Griffin,Stacy Marsella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Attention-guided, bounded-rational planning for drug decisions; two agents (Expert and Learner) allocate cognitive effort to high-cost vs low-cost reasoning, improving stability under uncertainty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pharmacists face high-stakes, time-pressured decisions amid drug shortages and uncertainty; cognitive load is a concern; need to understand how attention allocation affects decision quality.

Method: Formalize a bounded-rational framework decomposing drugs into a high-cost reasoning subset and a low-cost monitoring subset; implement Expert Agent with fixed attention weights from interviews; train Learner Agent to adapt attention over time; test across simulated horizons.

Result: Attention-guided planning yields stable decision-making without requiring full state reasoning; both agents achieve good performance; primary decision is allocation of cognitive effort; satisficing strategies reduce complexity while maintaining performance.

Conclusion: Attention-guided satisficing is effective for high-stakes, time-pressured decisions under uncertainty, suggesting cognitive effort allocation is central to decision quality and robustness.

Abstract: Hospital pharmacists make high-stakes decisions to mitigate drug shortages under uncertainty, time pressure, and patient risk. Interviews revealed that pharmacists focus attention on a small subset of drugs, limiting cognitive effort to the most urgent cases. Motivated by these findings, we formalize a bounded-rational, attention-guided decision framework that dynamically decomposes drugs into a subset for high-cost reasoning and a complementary subset for low-cost monitoring. We develop two agents: an Expert Agent that applies attention weights derived from pharmacist interviews, and a Learner Agent that adapts attention allocation over time through experience. Across simulated scenarios spanning short to long horizons, we show that attention-guided planning supports stable decision-making without complete state reasoning. These results suggest that a primary decision is not what action to take, but where to allocate cognitive effort, and that attention-guided, satisficing strategies can reduce problem complexity while maintaining stable performance.

</details>


### [166] [ClawForge: Generating Executable Interactive Benchmarks for Command-Line Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14133)
*Yuxiang Lai,Peng Xia,Haonian Ji,Kaiwen Xiong,Kaide Zeng,Jiaqi Liu,Fang Wu,Jike Zhong,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A generator-backed benchmark framework (ClawForge) for executable CLI workflows under state conflict, enabling reproducible, stepwise evaluation on persistent surfaces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome limitations of hand-authored tasks and static prompts by testing agents' handling of pre-existing, stale, or conflicting artifacts in persistent state.

Method: Compile scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specs; evaluate agents step-by-step over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching.

Result: ClawForge-Bench (17 scenarios, 6 ability categories) evaluated on seven frontier models; best model achieves 45.3% strict accuracy; wrong-state replacement < 17% across models; the widest model separation (17% to 90%) arises from whether agents inspect existing state before acting; partial-credit and step-efficiency analyses reveal near-miss closures and qualitatively different failure styles under state conflict.

Conclusion: State-aware benchmarking reveals brittleness under pre-existing artifacts and enables nuanced evaluation beyond exact trajectories; the framework offers reproducible, scalable tasks and exposes key failure modes tied to state exploration behavior.

Abstract: Interactive agent benchmarks face a tension between scalable construction and realistic workflow evaluation. Hand-authored tasks are expensive to extend and revise, while static prompt evaluation misses failures that only appear when agents operate over persistent state. Existing interactive benchmarks have advanced agent evaluation significantly, but most initialize tasks from clean state and do not systematically test how agents handle pre-existing partial, stale, or conflicting artifacts. We present \textbf{ClawForge}, a generator-backed benchmark framework for executable command-line workflows under state conflict. The framework compiles scenario templates, grounded slots, initialized state, reference trajectories, and validators into reproducible task specifications, and evaluates agents step by step over persistent workflow surfaces using normalized end state and observable side effects rather than exact trajectory matching. We instantiate this framework as the ClawForge-Bench (17 scenarios, 6 ability categories). Results across seven frontier models show that the best model reaches only 45.3% strict accuracy, wrong-state replacement remains below 17\% for all models, and the widest model separation (17% to 90%) is driven by whether agents inspect existing state before acting. Partial-credit and step-efficiency analyses further reveal that many failures are near-miss closures rather than early breakdowns, and that models exhibit qualitatively different failure styles under state conflict.

</details>


### [167] [Distribution-Aware Algorithm Design with LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14141)
*Saharsh Koganti,Priyadarsi Mishra,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Tomer Galanti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Learns executable solver code via reusable solver hints compiled from samples; provides both correctness and runtime guarantees and shows strong empirical benefits across combinatorial problem classes using LLM-based solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Correctness alone is insufficient for solvers; runtime and deployment efficiency matter when multiple solvers agree on solutions. The work targets learning specialized, faster solver code tailored to a task distribution.

Method: Introduce solver hints: reusable structures inferred from samples and compiled into specialized solver code. Prove that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and that identifiable hints can be recovered from polynomially many samples. Empirically instantiate with LLM code agents over 21 structured combinatorial-optimization distributions across seven problem classes, synthesizing solvers that leverage distribution-specific computation.

Result: Theoretical guarantees: generalization in both correctness and runtime for the fastest sample-consistent solver; recoverability of hints from polynomial samples. Empirical results show high-quality solvers: mean normalized quality 0.971, large improvements over heuristics and a quality-based baseline, and massive speedups (hundreds-fold) over quality-best heuristic, Gurobi, and a time-limited exact backend. On PACE 2025 Dominating Set instances, solvers are valid on all graphs and about 100× faster than top competitors, with moderate quality gaps. Gains largely come from replacing ambient exponential search with compiled, distribution-specific computation.

Conclusion: Compiler-generated solver hints enable robust generalization and substantial practical speedups by embedding distribution-specific computation into executable solver code, transforming problem-solving from generic optimization to specialized, efficient inference.

Abstract: We study learning when the learned object is executable solver code rather than a predictor. In this setting, correctness is not enough: two solvers may both return valid solutions on the deployment distribution while differing substantially in runtime. Given samples from an unknown task distribution, the learner returns code evaluated on fresh instances by both solution quality and execution time. Our central abstraction is a \emph{solver hint}: reusable structure inferred from samples and compiled into specialized solver code. We prove that the empirically fastest sample-consistent solver from a fixed library generalizes in both correctness and runtime, and that statistically identifiable hints can be recovered and compiled from polynomially many samples.
  Empirically, we instantiate the framework with LLM code agents on \(21\) structured combinatorial-optimization target distributions across seven problem classes. The synthesized solvers reach mean normalized quality \(0.971\), improve by \(+0.224\) over the average heuristic pool and by \(+0.098\) over the highest-quality heuristic, and are \(336.9\times\), \(342.8\times\), and \(16.1\times\) faster than the quality-best heuristic, Gurobi, and the selected time-limited exact backend, respectively. On released PACE 2025 Dominating Set private instances, the synthesized solver is valid on all \(100\) graphs and runs about two orders of magnitude faster than top competition solvers, with a moderate quality gap. Inspection shows that many gains come from changing the computational scale: replacing ambient exponential search or general-purpose optimization with compiled distribution-specific computation.

</details>


### [168] [Agentic Systems as Boosting Weak Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14163)
*Varun Sunkaraneni,Pierfrancesco Beneventano,Riccardo Neumarker,Tomaso Poggio,Tomer Galanti*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Verifier-backed committee search can boost reasoning LM performance beyond a single weak model by combining multiple proposals with critics/comparators and local soundness checks; while sampling increases proposal coverage, reliable gains require local verification; empirically, eight proposals with critics reach near the strongest models, with remaining failures due to coverage gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore whether a committee of weak reasoning agents, aided by verifier-backed selection, can match or approach the performance of strong models at inference time. Formalize how coverage, identifiability, progress, and diversity interact, and identify conditions under which sampling and local checks lead to reliable improvement.

Method: Theoretically decompose committee performance into four components: proposal coverage, local identifiability, progress, and diversity. Prove that sampling can amplify coverage but cannot by itself produce reliable critics/comparators; introduce local soundness signals (execution, proof checking, type checking, tests, constraint solving) to enable reliable amplification. Derive rank-based bounds on how local selection errors propagate and derive the proposer-side ceiling (oracle best-of-k converges to the mass of task slices with nonzero useful probability). Empirically evaluate on SWE-bench Verified comparing a GPT-5.4 nano single proposal vs. critic--comparator ensembles with k proposals.

Result: Theoretical results show coverage amplification requires a local soundness signal to be useful; local errors can compound; the proposer’s best-of-k upper bound equals the total measure of task slices where the proposal has nonzero probability of good solutions. Empirically, a single GPT-5.4 nano solves 67.0% of tasks; with an 8-proposal critic--comparator system, 76.4% is achieved, matching the standalone performance of Gemini 3 Pro and Claude Opus 4.5 Thinking and approaching the oracle best-of-8 (79.0%). Many correct patches exist within weak-model proposals; the remaining failures are largely coverage-related rather than selection faults.

Conclusion: Many correct patches reside in weak-model proposal pools; the main challenge is effective selection. Strengthening coverage alone cannot close all gaps; reliable gains require local verification signals. The study quantifies when such signals enable reliable inference-time boosting and provides empirical evidence that modest-size ensembles with proper selection approach strong-model performance.

Abstract: Can a committee of weak reasoning-model calls reach the performance of much stronger models? We study verifier-backed committee search as inference-time boosting for reasoning language models. The mechanism is not simply that ``more agents help'': samples expose latent correct solutions, while critics and comparators must recover them without access to the hidden verifier. We formalize this view by separating proposal coverage, local identifiability, progress, and diversity. We prove that coverage can be amplified by repeated sampling, but cannot by itself create useful critics or comparators; reliable amplification requires an additional local soundness signal, such as execution, proof checking, type checking, tests, or constraint solving. We give rank-based bounds showing when local selection errors compose into reliable trajectories, and characterize the proposer-side ceiling: oracle best-of-\(k\) converges only to the mass of task slices on which the proposal system assigns nonzero useful probability. Empirically, on SWE-bench Verified, a single \texttt{GPT-5.4 nano} proposal solves \(67.0\%\) of tasks. Using the same nano model, our critic--comparator orchestration reaches \(76.4\%\) with \(k=8\) proposals, matching the standalone performance of \texttt{Gemini 3 Pro} and \texttt{Claude Opus 4.5} Thinking and approaching the \(79.0\%\) oracle best-of-\(8\) upper bound. Thus, many correct patches are already present in weak-model proposal pools; the main challenge is selecting them. The remaining failures are mostly proposal-coverage failures, indicating shared blind spots that stronger selection alone cannot close.

</details>


### [169] [Unsteady Metrics and Benchmarking Cultures of AI Model Builders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14164)
*Stefan Baack,Christo Buschek,Maty Bohacek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A dataset of 231 highlighted benchmarks across 139 2025 model releases from 11 builders; reveals a fragmented benchmarking landscape with limited cross-model comparability; introduces a taxonomy and open dataset; argues benchmarks function more as narrative tools than standardized measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate how publicly highlighted benchmarks shape perceptions of AI capabilities and cross-model comparability and address potential misalignment between marketing and scientific evaluation; provide a unified framework for interpreting benchmark claims.

Method: Assemble Benchmarking-Cultures-25: 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 releases in 2025 from 11 builders; open-source dataset and interactive exploration tool; descriptive analyses of cross-builder usage; qualitative mapping of terminology to a unified signals framework; taxonomy development.

Result: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks are used by a single builder; 38.5% appear in only one release; few benchmarks achieve wide adoption (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025); develop a unified taxonomy to map divergent terminology to common measured signals; many benchmarks emphasize general knowledge/reasoning and STEM subjects, with a tendency to de-emphasize construct validity and frame results as progress toward AGI.

Conclusion: Highlighted benchmarks function more as narrative devices and marketing signals than standardized measurement tools; dataset and interactive tool provided to support transparent analysis; calls for careful interpretation and efforts toward cross-model comparability and methodological standardization.

Abstract: The primary way to establish and compare competencies in foundation and generative AI models has shifted from peer-reviewed literature to press releases and company blog posts, where model builders highlight results on selected benchmarks. These artifacts now largely define the state of the art for researchers and the public. Despite their prominence, which benchmarks model builders choose to highlight, and what they communicate through this selection, is underexamined. To investigate, we introduce and open-source Benchmarking-Cultures-25, a dataset of 231 benchmarks highlighted across 139 model releases in 2025 from 11 major AI builders, alongside an interactive tool to explore the data. Our analysis reveals a fragmented evaluation landscape with limited cross-model comparability: 63.2% of highlighted benchmarks are used by a single builder, and 38.5% appear in just one release. Few achieve widespread use (e.g., GPQA Diamond, LiveCodeBench, AIME 2025). Moreover, benchmarks are attributed different competencies by different builders, depending on their narrative. To disentangle these conflicting presentations, we develop a unified taxonomy mapping diverging terminology to a shared framework of measured signals based on what benchmark authors claim to measure. "General knowledge application" is the second most popular, yet vaguely defined, category. Qualitative analysis shows many such benchmarks deemphasize construct validity, instead framing results as indicators of progress toward AGI. Their authors claim to measure knowledge or reasoning broadly, yet mostly evaluate STEM subjects (especially math). We argue that highlighted benchmarks function less as standardized measurement tools and more as flexible narrative devices prioritizing market positioning over scientific evaluation. Data: https://hf.co/datasets/matybohacek/benchmarking-cultures-25; tool: https://bench-cultures.net.

</details>


### [170] [The Evaluation Trap: Benchmark Design as Theoretical Commitment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14167)
*Theodore J Kalaitzidis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Epistematics provides a meta-evaluative framework to audit benchmarks by deriving evaluation criteria directly from claimed capabilities and testing whether benchmarks discriminate genuine capability from proxies; applied to Dupoux et al. (2026) to show evaluation criteria reproduce architectural assumptions, thereby entrenching limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Benchmarks embed and stabilize dominant paradigms by encoding unexamined assumptions; without auditing, evaluation risk becomes self-reinforcing and blind to genuine progress across capabilities.

Method: Introduce a three-part audit toolkit: an audit procedure, a failure-mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria; conduct a worked audit of a contemporary proposal to demonstrate how claimed capabilities can be masked by evaluation criteria.

Result: Demonstrates that the proposal reproduces the dominant paradigm’s architectural assumptions within its evaluation, thus entrenching the very constraint it aims to overcome; provides a generalizable audit framework for capability-evaluation coherence.

Conclusion: Evaluation frameworks can create blind spots and misrepresent progress; Epistematics offers a meta-evaluative method to align benchmarks with technical claims and to expose proxy behaviors that inflate perceived capabilities.

Abstract: Every AI benchmark operationalizes theoretical assumptions about the capability it claims to assess. When assumptions function as unexamined commitments, benchmarks stabilize the dominant paradigm by narrowing what counts as progress. Over time, narrow evaluation reorganizes capability concepts: architectures and definitions are selected for benchmark legibility until evaluation ceases to track an independent object and instead produces a version of the target defined by its own operational assumptions. The result is a trap: evaluation frameworks treat self-reinforcing assessments as valid, both creating and obscuring structural limits on what the current paradigm can accomplish. We introduce Epistematics, a methodology for deriving evaluation criteria directly from technical capability claims and auditing whether proposed benchmarks can discriminate the claimed capability from proxy behaviors. The contribution is meta-evaluative: an audit procedure, a failure mode taxonomy, and benchmark-design criteria for evaluating capability-evaluation coherence. We demonstrate the procedure through a worked audit of Dupoux et al. (2026), a proposal that revises the dominant paradigm's theoretical assumptions at the architectural level while reproducing them in its evaluation criteria, thereby entrenching the constraint it seeks to overcome in a form the evaluation cannot detect.

</details>


### [171] [Grounded Continuation: A Linear-Time Runtime Verifier for LLM Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14175)
*Qisong He,Yi Dong,Xiaowei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A runtime verifier for LLM dialogues uses an explicit dependency graph to ensure that each continuation is supported by prior evidence; it tracks updates via eight operations from four formalisms, enabling graph-based checks with linear per-turn cost and a formal conflict-free guarantee, yielding accuracy gains over baselines and a soundness-faithfulness decomposition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long, multi-turn conversations can drift beyond previously established premises; attackers exploit gaps where plausible but unsupported continuations are produced. A runtime verifier is needed to ensure continuations remain grounded in evidence and prior discourse.

Method: A runtime verifier maintains a dependency graph. An LLM classifies each turn into eight update operations drawn from four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation). A symbolic engine records which claims depend on which evidence. Verifying a continuation becomes a graph walk; retraction propagates through the same graph to flag conclusions that lose support, with linear per-turn cost and a formal conflict-free guarantee.

Result: Empirical results show improved accuracy over baselines: LongMemEval-KU (n=78) verifier 89.7% vs LLM-only 88.5% (+1.3pp) and transcript-RAG 87.2% (+2.6pp); wins among disagreements are abstentions where the baseline confabulates. On LoCoMo’s 60 QA items, verifier is competitive with retrieval-augmented baselines. Additional tests (multi-agent and grounding) yield 100% accuracy on a 15-item stale-premise subset and demonstrate a soundness-faithfulness decomposition (structural check is sound by construction; LLM extraction faithfulness is empirical). Retraction checks operate in microseconds; history-replay grows linearly with conversation length.

Conclusion: The approach provides a sound structural check with a scalable retraction mechanism, achieving practical defense against context-manipulation in long conversations. Faithfulness depends on LLM extraction across models, supporting a soundness-faithfulness perspective.

Abstract: In long conversations, an LLM can produce a next utterance that sounds plausible but rests on premises the conversation has already abandoned. Context-manipulation attacks against deployed agents now actively exploit this gap. We close it with a runtime verifier that maintains an explicit dependency graph: an LLM classifies each turn into one of 8 update operations drawn from four formalisms (dynamic epistemic logic, abductive reasoning, awareness logic, argumentation), and a symbolic engine records which claims depend on which evidence. Checking whether a continuation is supported reduces to a graph walk; retraction propagates through the same graph to flag exactly the conclusions that lose support, with linear per-turn cost and a formal conflict-free guarantee. On LongMemEval-KU oracle (n=78), the verifier reaches 89.7% accuracy vs. 88.5% for the LLM-only baseline (+1.3pp) and 87.2% for a transcript-RAG baseline matched on retrieval budget (+2.6pp); wins among disagreements are correct abstentions where the baseline confabulates. On LoCoMo's 60 official QA items the verifier is competitive with retrieval-augmented baselines. Beyond external benchmarks, we construct two multi-agent scenarios and a 50-item grounding test: on the 15-item stale-premise subset, the verifier reaches 100% accuracy vs. 93.3% (+6.7pp). These instantiate a soundness-faithfulness decomposition: the structural check is sound by construction, and per-deployment LLM extraction faithfulness is the empirical question we measure across four LLM families. The retraction check plateaus at microseconds while history-replay grows linearly with conversation length.

</details>


### [172] [SimPersona: Learning Discrete Buyer Personas from Raw Clickstreams for Grounded E-Commerce Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14205)
*Zahra Zanjani Foumani,Alberto Castelo,Shuang Xie,Ted Chaiwachirasak,Han Li,Lingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SimPersona learns discrete buyer types from historical clickstream data via a behavior-aware VQ-VAE and uses persona tokens to tailor LLM-based web agents. It preserves merchant-specific buyer distributions, enables population-level simulation, and achieves strong alignment with real behavior (78% conversion-rate) with interpretable variation and favorable efficiency, plus open-source tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitation of LLM-based web agents collapsing to an average buyer policy, by capturing heterogeneous, distributional buyer behavior at scale without brittle hand-crafted prompts.

Method: Train a behavior-aware VQ-VAE on raw clickstreams to derive a discrete buyer-type codebook. Map each type to a persona token in the LLM agent vocabulary and fine-tune agents with these tokens on real browsing traces. At inference, assign synthetic buyers to a learned type via a single encoder pass. For population simulation, sample types from each merchant’s empirical distribution over the codebook and instantiate agents accordingly.

Result: Evaluated on 8.37M buyers across 42 held-out storefronts, achieving 78% conversion-rate alignment with real buyers. Demonstrates interpretable variation across types and outperforms a larger-parameter baseline by 8x on goal-oriented shopping tasks.

Conclusion: Public release of an open-source data pipeline for converting raw e-commerce logs into buyer representations and agent-training traces, enabling scalable population-level simulation with LLM-based agents.

Abstract: LLM-based web agents can navigate live storefronts, yet they often collapse to a single "average buyer" policy, failing to capture the heterogeneous and distributional nature of real buyer populations. Existing personalization methods rely on hand-crafted prompt-based personas that are brittle, difficult to scale, context-inefficient, and unable to faithfully represent population-level behavior. We introduce SimPersona, a novel framework that learns discrete buyer types from historical traffic and exposes them to LLM-based web agents as compact persona tokens. Given raw clickstreams, a behavior-aware VQ-VAE induces a discrete buyer-type space that captures the statistical structure of real buyer behavior and merchant-specific buyer population distributions. To provide behavior-specific guidance to LLM-based web agents, SimPersona maps each learned buyer type to a dedicated persona token in the LLM agent vocabulary and fine-tunes the agent with these tokens on real browsing traces. At inference, each synthetic buyer is assigned to a learned buyer type with a single encoder forward pass, requiring no retraining or store-specific prompt engineering. For population-level simulation, SimPersona samples buyer types from each merchant's empirical distribution over the learned VQ-VAE codebook and instantiates agents with the corresponding persona tokens, preserving merchant-specific buyer population distributions. Evaluated on $8.37$M buyers across $42$ held-out live storefronts, SimPersona achieves $78\%$ conversion-rate alignment with real buyers, exhibits interpretable behavioral variation across buyer types, and outperforms a baseline with $8\times$ more parameters on goal-oriented shopping tasks. We further release an open-source data pipeline that converts raw e-commerce event logs into buyer representations and agent-training traces.

</details>


### [173] [ASH: Agents that Self-Hone via Embodied Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14211)
*Benjamin Schneider,Xavier Schneider,Victor Zhong,Sun Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ASH is a self-improving agent that learns embodied policies from unlabeled internet video without rewards, using an inverse dynamics model and long-term memory to achieve sustained long-horizon performance, surpassing baselines in two games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon embodied tasks are hard due to reliance on hand-crafted rewards or labeled demonstrations, which do not scale. A scalable solution requires self-supervision from unlabeled data and mechanisms to maintain long-horizon memory.

Method: ASH introduces a self-improvement loop: when stuck, it trains an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) on its own trajectories and uses the IDM to extract supervision from relevant internet video. It also performs unsupervised moment discovery to identify key events in large-scale video and stores these as long-term memory. Evaluation is on two long-horizon tasks: Pokemon Emerald (turn-based RPG) and The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap (real-time action-adventure). Baselines include behavioral cloning, retrieval-augmented, and zero-shot foundation-model approaches.

Result: ASH attains an average of 11.2/12 milestones in Pokemon Emerald and 9.9/12 in Zelda, while the strongest baselines reach 6.5/12 and 6.0/12 respectively. The evaluation spans 8 hours, with ASH maintaining progression where baselines plateau.

Conclusion: Self-improving agents provide a scalable path for long-horizon embodied learning; ASH demonstrates that combining IDM-based self-supervision, unsupervised video mining, and long-term memory yields sustained progress beyond strong non-self-improving baselines.

Abstract: Long-horizon embodied tasks remain a fundamental challenge in AI, as current methods rely on hand-engineered rewards or action-labeled demonstrations, neither of which scales. We introduce ASH, an agentic system that learns an embodied policy from unlabeled, noisy internet video, without reward shaping or expert annotation. ASH follows a self-improvement loop; when it gets stuck, ASH learns an Inverse Dynamics Model (IDM) from its own trajectories, and uses its IDM to extract supervision from relevant internet video. ASH uses unsupervised learning to identify key moments from large-scale internet video and retains them as long-term memory -- allowing it to tackle long-horizon problems. We evaluate ASH on two complementary environments demanding multi-hour planning: Pokemon Emerald, a turn-based RPG, and The Legend of Zelda: The Minish Cap, a real-time action-adventure game. In both games, behavioral cloning, retrieval-augmented and zero-shot foundation-model baselines plateau, while ASH sustains progression across our 8-hour evaluation. ASH reaches an average of $11.2/12$ milestones in Pokemon Emerald and $9.9/12$ in Legend of Zelda, while the strongest baseline gets stuck in both environments at an average of $6.5/12$ and $6.0/12$ milestones, respectively. We demonstrate that self-improving agents are a scalable recipe for long-horizon embodied learning.

</details>


### [174] [MetaAgent-X : Breaking the Ceiling of Automatic Multi-Agent Systems via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14212)
*Yaolun Zhang,Yujie Zhao,Nan Wang,Yiran Wu,Jiayu Chang,Yizhao Chen,Qingyun Wu,Jishen Zhao,Huazheng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: End-to-end reinforcement learning framework MetaAgent-X jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution, enabling stagewise co-evolution of designer and executor; achieves up to 21.7% gains and end-to-end trainability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the frozen-executor ceiling and the lack of end-to-end training in automatic MAS; existing approaches either rely on training-free test-time search or fix downstream executors, hindering full adaptability.

Method: MetaAgent-X employs script-based MAS generation, execution rollout collection, and credit assignment for both designer and executor trajectories; introduces Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout and Stagewise Co-evolution to stabilize training and illuminate designer-executor dynamics.

Result: Outperforms baselines with up to 21.7% gains; ablations show simultaneous improvement of designer and executor during training; results support that effective automatic MAS learning follows a stagewise co-evolution process.

Conclusion: End-to-end trainable automatic MAS is a practical paradigm for self-designing and self-executing agentic models, with stagewise co-evolution mitigating training instability and exposing designer-executor dynamics.

Abstract: Automatic multi-agent systems aim to instantiate agent workflows without relying on manually designed or fixed orchestration. However, existing automatic MAS approaches remain only partially adaptive: they either perform training-free test-time search or optimize the meta-level designer while keeping downstream execution agents frozen, which creating a frozen-executor ceiling and leaving the end-to-end training of self-designing and self-executing agentic models unexplored. To address this, we introduce MetaAgent-X, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes automatic MAS design and execution. MetaAgent-X enables script-based MAS generation, execution rollout collection, and credit assignment for both designer and executor trajectories. To support stable and scalable optimization, we propose Executor Designer Hierarchical Rollout and Stagewise Co-evolution to improve training stability and expose the dynamics of designer-executor co-evolution. MetaAgent-X consistently outperforms existing automatic MAS baselines, achieving up to 21.7% gains. Comprehensive ablations show that both designer and executor improve throughout training, and that effective automatic MAS learning follows a stagewise co-evolution process. These results establish end-to-end trainable automatic MAS as a practical paradigm for building self-designing and self-executing agentic models.

</details>


### [175] [GenCircuit-RL: Reinforcement Learning from Hierarchical Verification for Genetic Circuit Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14215)
*Noah Flynn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes GenCircuit-RL and SynBio-Reason benchmark to improve code-generation for SBOL genetic circuit design using hierarchical verification rewards and curriculum learning; achieves 14–16 percentage-point gains on functional reasoning tasks and demonstrates generalization and recovery of canonical designs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Genetic circuit design is an expert-driven, labor-intensive process. Automated design with formal SBOL representations and automated verification is needed. Existing code-generation approaches lack structured, multi-fidelity reasoning and robust generalization.

Method: Introduce GenCircuit-RL, a reinforcement learning framework with hierarchical verification rewards decomposed into five levels (from code execution to topology checks) and a four-stage curriculum that shifts optimization pressure toward functional reasoning. Create SynBio-Reason, a benchmark of 4,753 circuits across six canonical circuit types and nine tasks, with held-out biological parts for out-of-distribution evaluation.

Result: Hierarchical verification improves task success on functional reasoning tasks by 14–16 percentage points over binary rewards. Curriculum learning is required for strong design performance. The models generate topologically correct circuits, generalize to novel parts, and rediscover canonical designs from the literature.

Conclusion: Demonstrates that combining hierarchical verification rewards with curriculum learning enables robust, generalizable automatic SBOL circuit design. SynBio-Reason provides a large, out-of-distribution benchmark to evaluate future models and promotes automated verification and discovery of canonical designs.

Abstract: Genetic circuit design remains a laborious, expert-driven process despite decades of progress in synthetic biology. We study this problem through code generation: models produce Python code in pysbol3 to construct genetic circuits in the Synthetic Biology Open Language (SBOL), a formal representation that supports automated verification. We introduce GenCircuit-RL, a reinforcement learning framework built around hierarchical verification rewards that decompose correctness into five levels, from code execution to task-specific topological checks, and a four-stage curriculum that shifts optimization pressure from code generation to functional reasoning. We also introduce SynBio-Reason, a benchmark of 4,753 circuits spanning six canonical circuit types and nine tasks from code repair to de novo design, with held-out biological parts for out-of-distribution evaluation. Hierarchical verification improves task success on functional reasoning tasks by 14 to 16 percentage points over binary rewards, and curriculum learning is required for strong design performance. The resulting models generate topologically correct circuits, generalize to novel biological parts, and rediscover canonical designs from the synthetic biology literature.

</details>


### [176] [Fusion-fission forecasts when AI will shift to undesirable behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14218)
*Neil F. Johnson,Frank Yingjie Huo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new, model-agnostic signal forecasts when ChatGPT-like AI behavior may shift from desirable to undesirable by modeling group dynamics between conversation history and competing behavioral basins; validated across multiple models and corpora with high accuracy and offered as a real-time warning below the safety stack.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
