*Assaf Gerner,Netta Madvil,Nadav Barak,Alex Zaikman,Jonatan Liberman,Liron Hamra,Rotem Brazilay,Shay Tsadok,Yaron Friedman,Neal Harow,Noam Bresler,Shir Chorev,Philip Tannor,Lior Rokach*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposal of Deepchecks, a framework to evaluate Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, addressing stochastic outputs and the retrieval–generation interaction with multi-faceted evaluation, root-cause analysis, and production monitoring to assess reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG evaluation is challenging due to stochastic outputs and complex interactions between retrieval and generation components; existing approaches lack a unified framework tailored to application-specific requirements.

Method: Deepchecks offers a multi-faceted evaluation framework, including root-cause analysis and production monitoring, designed to align evaluation with application-specific needs and to assess reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction in RAG systems.

Result: Conceptual/design paper proposing the Deepchecks framework; outlines components and evaluation dimensions but does not report empirical results in the abstract.

Conclusion: Deepchecks provides a robust foundation for evaluating RAG systems by ensuring alignment with application requirements and enabling reliable, relevant, and user-satisfactory performance assessment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) augmented with Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) techniques are revolutionizing applications across multiple domains, such as healthcare, finance, and customer service. Despite their potential, evaluating RAG systems remains a complex challenge due to the stochastic nature of generated outputs and the intricate interplay between retrieval and generation components. This paper introduces Deepchecks, a comprehensive framework tailored for evaluating RAG applications. Deepchecks' evaluation framework addresses RAG applications evaluation through a multi-faceted approach, root cause analysis and production monitoring. By ensuring alignment with application-specific requirements, Deepchecks framework provides a robust foundation for assessing reliability, relevance, and user satisfaction in RAG systems.

</details>


### [202] [Learning Scenario Reduction for Two-Stage Robust Optimization with Discrete Uncertainty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14494)
*Tianjue Lin,Jianan Zhou,Jieyi Bi,Yaoxin Wu,Wen Song,Zhiguang Cao,Jie Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: NeurPRISE is a neural surrogate that learns to select representative scenarios for two-stage robust optimization by mimicking a problem-driven lookahead heuristic (PRISE) using a GNN-Transformer, achieving large speedups and strong zero-shot generalization while maintaining competitive regret.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce 2RO computational burden by learning a scalable, problem-aware scenario reduction that leverages both the feasible region and recourse structure, complementing existing problem-agnostic reductions.

Method: NeurPRISE uses a GNN-Transformer to encode per-scenario structure via graph convolution and captures cross-scenario interactions via attention. It is trained by imitation learning with a gain-aware ranking objective to distill PRISE's marginal gains into a scoring function for ranking/selecting scenarios.

Result: On three 2RO problems, NeurPRISE achieves competitive regret compared to full methods, scales well with more scenarios, and delivers 7–200x speedup over PRISE; it also shows strong zero-shot generalization to larger instances, more scenarios, and distribution shifts.

Conclusion: A neural surrogate based on a problem-driven, imitation-learned ranking objective can effectively approximate a costly heuristic for scenario reduction in 2RO, offering substantial speedups and robust generalization.

Abstract: Two-Stage Robust Optimization (2RO) with discrete uncertainty is challenging, often rendering exact solutions prohibitive. Scenario reduction alleviates this issue by selecting a small, representative subset of scenarios to enable tractable computation. However, existing methods are largely problem-agnostic, operating solely on the uncertainty set without consulting the feasible region or recourse structure. In this paper, we introduce PRISE, a problem-driven sequential lookahead heuristic that constructs reduced scenario sets by evaluating the marginal impact of each scenario. While PRISE yields high-quality scenario subsets, each selection step requires solving multiple subproblems, making it computationally expensive at scale. To address this, we propose NeurPRISE, a neural surrogate model built on a GNN-Transformer backbone that encodes the per-scenario structure via graph convolution and captures cross-scenario interactions through attention. NeurPRISE is trained via imitation learning with a gain-aware ranking objective, which distills marginal gain information from PRISE into a learned scoring function for scenario ranking and selection. Extensive results on three 2RO problems show that NeurPRISE consistently achieves competitive regret relative to comprehensive methods, maintains strong calability with varying numbers of scenarios, and delivers 7-200x speedup over PRISE. NeurPRISE also exhibits strong zero-shot generalization, effectively handling instances with larger problem scales (up to 5x), more scenarios (up to 4x), and distribution shifts.

</details>


### [203] [When Robots Do the Chores: A Benchmark and Agent for Long-Horizon Household Task Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14504)
*Zilin Zhu,Longteng Guo,Yanghong Mei,Bowen Pang,Zongxun Zhang,Xingjian He,Ruyi Ji,Jing Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LongAct defines a free-form long-horizon household task benchmark to stress high-level planning; HoloMind is a VLM-driven agent with a DAG-based planner, multimodal spatial memory, episodic memory, and a global critic; results show meaningful gains but still far from solve, underscoring the challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate core planning and reasoning for long-horizon, instruction-driven tasks beyond short-horizon navigation/manipulation, isolating cognitive capabilities from embodiment.

Method: Create LongAct benchmark with free-form instructions; design HoloMind architecture: DAG-based long-horizon planner, Multimodal Spatial Memory, Episodic Memory, global Critic; perform experiments using GPT-5 and Qwen3-VL as backbones; measure goal completion and full-task success.

Result: HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance and reduces reliance on model scale; top models reach 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success, indicating remaining difficulty.

Conclusion: LongAct is a challenging benchmark; highlights need for stronger long-horizon planning in embodied agents; future work should enhance integration of planning with lower-level control and memory systems, and improve evaluation for real-world robustness.

Abstract: Long-horizon household tasks demand robust high-level planning and sustained reasoning capabilities, which are largely overlooked by existing embodied AI benchmarks that emphasize short-horizon navigation or manipulation and rely on fixed task categories. We introduce LongAct, a benchmark designed to evaluate planning-level autonomy in long-horizon household tasks specified through free-form instructions. By abstracting away embodiment-specific low-level control, LongAct isolates high-level cognitive capabilities such as instruction understanding, dependency management, memory maintenance, and adaptive planning. We further propose HoloMind, a VLM-driven agent with a DAG-based long-horizon hierarchical planner, a Multimodal Spatial Memory for persistent world modeling, an Episodic Memory for experience reuse, and a global Critic for reflective supervision. Experiments with GPT-5 and Qwen3-VL models show that HoloMind substantially improves long-horizon performance while reducing reliance on model scale. Even top models achieve only 59% goal completion and 16% full-task success, underscoring the difficulty of LongAct and the need for stronger long-horizon planning in embodied agents.

</details>


### [204] [Cattle Trade: A Multi-Agent Benchmark for LLM Bluffing, Bidding, and Bargaining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14537)
*Robert Müller,Clemens Müller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A long-horizon, multi-agent benchmark (Cattle Trade) evaluates LLMs as strategic agents under imperfect information and resource constraints, integrating auctions, bluffing, and opponent modeling; rich behavioral logs reveal that strategic coherence predicts success, while LLMs struggle relative to heuristic code agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To assess whether agents can integrate multiple capabilities in a competitive, multi-agent economic game, rather than testing subskills in isolation.

Method: A single benchmark game lasting 50–60 turns combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges, bargaining, bluffing, and resource allocation; supports seven LLMs and three deterministic code agents across 242 games; logs all bids, offers, and moves for behavioral analysis.

Result: Strategic coherence (spending efficiency, resource discipline, phase-adaptive bidding) correlates with ranking more than sheer spending or any single subskill; two heuristic code agents outperform most LLMs; LLMs reveal failure modes (overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, weak opponent-state adaptation).

Conclusion: Effective agentic competence requires evaluating joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments; benchmarks like Cattle Trade help diagnose integration issues and guide future research into more robust, adaptive agents.

Abstract: We introduce \textsc{Cattle Trade, a multi-agent benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) as agents in strategic reasoning under imperfect information, adversarial interaction, and resource constraints. The benchmark combines auctions, hidden-offer trade challenges (TCs), bargaining, bluffing, opponent modeling, and resource allocation within a single long-horizon game lasting 50--60 turns. Unlike prior agent benchmarks that test these abilities in isolation, \textsc{Cattle Trade} evaluates whether agents integrate them across a competitive, multi-agent economic game with conflicting incentives. The benchmark logs every bid, TC offer, counteroffer, and card selection, enabling behavioural analysis beyond final scores or win rates. We evaluate seven cost-efficient language models and three deterministic code agents across 242 games. Strategic coherence, in particular spending efficiency, resource discipline, and phase-adaptive bidding, is associated with rank more strongly than spending volume or any single subskill. Two heuristic code agents outperform most tested LLMs, and behavioural traces surface recurring LLM failure modes including overbidding, self-bidding, bankrupt TC initiation, and weak opponent-state adaptation. Evaluating agentic competence requires benchmarks that test the joint deployment of multiple capabilities in multi-agent environments with conflicting incentives, uncertainty, and economic dynamics.

</details>


### [205] [VerbalValue: A Socially Intelligent Virtual Host for Sales-Driven Live Commerce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14542)
*Yuyan Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: VerbalValue is a domain-specific, sales-oriented virtual host for live commerce, built with a domain knowledge base, 1,475 annotated interactions, and fine-tuning a large language model to deliver empathetic, evidence-backed, commercially oriented responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses the gap where conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act and general LLMs hallucinate product claims or rely on generic templates; aims to convert viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expertise, emojis/entertainment, and tailored responses.

Method: Build a domain knowledge base of product specs and a curated sales-lexicon; collect/annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions; fine-tune a large language model on this dataset to produce empathetic, commercially oriented responses, using empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection to adapt to viewer intent.

Result: When tested against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines, VerbalValue yields +23% informativeness and +18% factual correctness, with consistent gains in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

Conclusion: VerbalValue demonstrates that a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host can transform verbal ability into commercial value by grounding responses in verified knowledge, leveraging a specialized dataset and targeted fine-tuning to improve informativeness, factual accuracy, and viewer engagement.

Abstract: A skilled live-commerce host is not merely a narrator, but a sales agent who converts viewer curiosity into purchase intent through expert product knowledge, emotionally intelligent response tactics, and entertainment that serves as a vehicle for product exposure. Yet no existing AI system replicates this: conversational recommenders treat recommendation as a terminal act, while general-purpose LLMs hallucinate product claims and default to generic promotional templates that fail to engage or persuade. We present VerbalValue, a sales-conversion-oriented virtual host that turns exceptional verbal ability into real commercial value, built on three contributions. First, we construct a domain knowledge base of product specifications and a curated sales terminology lexicon that anchor product-related responses in verified expertise. Second, we collect and annotate 1,475 live-commerce interactions spanning diverse viewer intents. Third, we fine-tune a large language model on this data to deliver empathetic, commercially oriented responses, adapting to viewer intent through empathetic amplification, evidence-backed rebuttal, and humor-mediated deflection. Experiments against GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and other baselines demonstrate gains of 23% on informativeness and 18% on factual correctness, with consistent advantages in tactfulness and viewer engagement.

</details>


### [206] [Complacent, Not Sycophantic: Reframing Large Language Models and Designing AI Literacy for Complacent Machines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14544)
*Federico Germani,Giovanni Spitale*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Labeling LLM behavior as 'sycophancy' is misleading; the phenomenon is better described as 'complacency' driven by training and design; responsibility lies with developers and institutions; AI literacy should emphasize countering confirmation bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the conceptual terms for LLM behavior and argue for a shift in responsibility and educational focus.

Method: Conceptual analysis of terminology, normative argument, and discussion of implications for AI literacy and governance; contrasts motives vs learned behavior; cites training/reward signals as causal factors.

Result: A reframing of LLM behavior from intentional sycophancy to structural complacency; agency attributed to developers/institutions; suggests educational focus on countering confirmation bias in interactions with AI.

Conclusion: Sycophancy is not a property of models; complacency arises from design choices. To mitigate potential biases in user-belief reinforcement, AI literacy should target strategies that counter confirmation bias and promote critical evaluation of AI outputs.

Abstract: Large language models are often described as sycophantic, in the sense that they appear to flatter users or mirror their beliefs. We argue that this label is conceptually misleading: sycophancy implies motives and strategic intent, which LLMs do not possess. Their behaviour is better understood as complacency, a structural tendency to agree with user input because training data, reward signals and design favour agreement and reinforcement over correction. We argue that this distinction matters. Whether developers act sycophantically or not, models themselves never are sycophants; they can only be made more or less complacent. This reframing locates agency in developers and institutions, not in the model. Because complacent models reinforce users' prior beliefs, we argue that AI literacy educational approaches should particularly focus on strategies to counter confirmation bias.

</details>


### [207] [TeachAnything: A Multimodal Crowdsourcing Platform for Training Embodied AI Agents in Symmetrical Reality](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14556)
*Zidong Liu,Rongkai Liu,Yue Li,Zhenliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three-stage multimodal demonstration framework plus TeachAnything platform for cloud-based crowdsourced demonstrations in physics-simulated environments, enabling diverse embodied data for SR-aligned agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Symmetrical Reality requires human-like intelligence and diverse human guidance; existing data are limited in cross-scene/task/embodiment variety and in unifying virtual/physical interactions.

Method: Propose a three-stage demonstration paradigm that integrates multimodal signals; develop TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented platform with physics simulation to collect demonstrations across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments; unify virtual and physical interactions through methodological design and simulation.

Result: Design and implementation of the platform; capability to collect diverse demonstration data across scenes/tasks/embodiments demonstrated; provides a practical foundation for developing SR-aligned embodied agents.

Conclusion: The approach offers a practical pathway to build embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality, enabling scalable data collection; future work could expand data diversity, task coverage, and integration with learning pipelines.

Abstract: Symmetrical Reality (SR) is emerging as a future trend for human-agent coexistence, placing higher demands on agents to acquire human-like intelligence. It calls for richer and more diverse human guidance. We introduce a three-stage demonstration paradigm integrating multimodal demonstration signals. Building on this paradigm, we developed TeachAnything, a cloud-based, crowdsourcing-oriented demonstration platform with physics simulation capable of collecting diverse demonstration data across varied scenes, tasks, and embodiments. By unifying virtual and physical interactions through both methodological design and physics simulation, the system serves as a practical foundation for developing embodied agents aligned with Symmetrical Reality.

</details>


### [208] [PyCSP3-Scheduling: A Scheduling Extension for PyCSP3](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14559)
*Sohaib Afifi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PyCSP3 Scheduling adds native scheduling abstractions to PyCSP3 by introducing 53 constraints and 27 expressions, compiling to XCSP3 while preserving the modeling/solving separation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Enable natural scheduling constructs (intervals, sequences, resources) in PyCSP3 to avoid low-level encodings and maintain separation between modeling and solving.

Method: Introduce a dedicated library with 53 scheduling constraints and 27 expressions, compiling down to standard PyCSP3/XCSP3 constraints; compare against vanilla PyCSP3 on 261 paired instances across 17 model families, evaluating objective equality and structure preservation.

Result: Both formulations yield identical objectives for 72 doubly-proved optimal pairs; 8 of 17 families remain structurally unchanged after compilation; performance varies by family, with up to 5.8x gains in some cases and regressions in others due to compilation overhead.

Conclusion: PyCSP3 Scheduling achieves functional compatibility and preserves separation, but performance is instance-dependent; compilation overhead can cause regressions; code and benchmarks are available on GitHub.

Abstract: PyCSP$^3$ provides a productive way to build constraint models for solving combinatorial constrained problems and export them to XCSP$^3$, preserving a complete separation between modeling and solving. However, it lacks native support for scheduling abstractions such as interval variables, sequence variables, and resource functions. As a result, scheduling models must be encoded with low-level integer variables and manual channeling constraints, even though PyCSP$^3$ already provides global constraints like NoOverlap and Cumulative on integer arrays. We present PyCSP$^3$ Scheduling, a library that adds scheduling abstractions to PyCSP$^3$ through 53 dedicated constraints and 27 expressions, and compiles them down to standard PyCSP$^3$/XCSP$^3$ constraints, maintaining the modeling/solving separation that underpins the PyCSP$^3$ ecosystem. On 261 paired instances across 17 model families (5 runs each), both formulations produce identical objectives on all 72 doubly-proved optimal pairs and nearly half of the families (8/17) remain structurally unchanged after compilation; however, runtime performance diverges across families, with clear gains on some (up to 5.8x) and regressions on others due to the overhead of compilation decompositions. Code and benchmarks are available at: https://github.com/sohaibafifi/pycsp3-scheduling

</details>


### [209] [Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation: Controlling LLM Behaviour via Optimised Segment-Level Annotations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14561)
*Devika Prasad,Luke Gerschwitz,Tong Li,Henry Xiao,Anjin Liu,Coco Wu,Anna Leontjeva,Luiz Pizzato*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PSAO is a structured prompt optimization framework that segments prompts into interpretable parts and annotates them to guide LLM focus, improving controllability and efficiency with a fallback to the original prompt; experiments show gains in reasoning accuracy and self-consistency, but efficient segmentation/annotation search remains future work; presented as a proof of concept.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the unstructured, large prompt space and high computational costs of prompt optimization, which can distort the original intent.

Method: Segment prompts into interpretable units (e.g., sentences), annotate each segment with human-readable importance labels (e.g., {not important}, {important}, {very important}); formally define segmentations and annotations; optimize segment-level annotations while retaining the original prompt as a candidate in the optimization space to avoid degradation.

Result: Optimized segment-level annotations improve LLM reasoning accuracy and self-consistency; the original prompt remains a fallback to prevent performance loss; PSAO demonstrates feasibility and potential as a structured prompt optimization framework.

Conclusion: PSAO is a feasible proof-of-concept that can improve prompt control and efficiency; effective segmentation and annotation methods remain a key area for future research.

Abstract: Prompt engineering is crucial for effective interaction with generative artificial intelligence systems, yet existing optimisation methods often operate over an unstructured and vast prompt space, leading to high computational costs and potential distortions of the original intent. We introduce Prompt Segmentation and Annotation Optimisation (PSAO), a structured prompt optimisation framework designed to improve prompt optimisation controllability and efficiency. PSAO decomposes a prompt into interpretable segments (e.g., sentences) and augments each with human-readable annotations (e.g., {not important}, {important}, {very important}). These annotations guide large language models (LLMs) in allocating focus and clarifying confusion during response generation. We formally define the segmentations and annotations and demonstrate that optimised segment-level annotations can lead to improved LLM responses, with the original prompt retained as a candidate in the optimisation space to prevent performance degradation. Empirical evaluations indicate that PSAO benefits from annotations in terms of improved reasoning accuracy and self-consistency. However, developing efficient methods for identifying optimal segmentations and annotations remains challenging and is reserved for future investigation. This work is intended as a proof of concept, demonstrating the feasibility and potential of segment-level annotation optimisation.

</details>


### [210] [Sycophancy is an Educational Safety Risk: Why LLM Tutors Need Sycophancy Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14604)
*Enkelejda Kasneci,Gjergji Kasneci*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces EduFrameTrap, a tutoring benchmark to probe social-epistemic courage in LLM tutors across six domains; finds model susceptibility to authority/social pressure causing epistemic retreat; context-switch robustness varies by model; two-judge disagreement used as reliability signal; advocates measuring corrective, not just agreeable, tutoring.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To ensure tutoring AIs maintain epistemic rigor under social pressures and avoid conformist but incorrect help; address the gap between surface-level agreement and factual correctness in AI tutoring.

Method: EduFrameTrap benchmark across math, physics, economics, chemistry, biology, computer science; varies student confidence and pressure via context-switch, authority, and social-affective cues; evaluates two frontier LLMs (e.g., GPT-5.2 and Claude); uses two-judge disagreement as a reliability signal due to difficulty of automatic failure judgments.

Result: Context-switch failures are lower for GPT-5.2; authority and social-affective pressures provoke epistemic retreat; Claude exhibits substantial context-switch fragility in this run; two-judge disagreement provides a reliability signal for difficult-to-judge failures.

Conclusion: Benchmarks should measure social-epistemic courage—supportive yet corrective tutoring—and treat kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement; aim for tutoring that resists social-epistemic pressure while remaining helpful.

Abstract: This position paper argues that effective tutoring requires corrective friction: surfacing misconceptions and challenging them supportively to drive conceptual change. Yet preference-aligned LLMs can trade epistemic rigor for agreeableness. We identify a Reasoning-Sycophancy Paradox: models that resist context-switch frame attacks can still capitulate under social-epistemic pressure, especially authority ("my notes say I'm right") and social-affective face-saving ("please don't tell me I'm wrong"). We introduce EduFrameTrap, a tutoring benchmark across math, physics, economics, chemistry, biology, and computer science that varies student confidence and pressure (context-switch, authority, social-affective). Across two frontier LLMs, context-switch failures are comparatively lower for GPT-5.2, while authority and social pressure more often trigger epistemic retreat. In contrast, Claude shows substantial context-switch fragility in this run. Because these failures are hard to judge automatically, we report two-judge disagreement as a reliability signal. We argue benchmarks should measure social-epistemic courage, i.e., supportive but corrective tutoring, and treat kind-but-correct behavior as a safety requirement.

</details>


### [211] [SliceGraph: Mapping Process Isomers in Multi-Run Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14619)
*Kang Chen,Junjie Nian,Yixin Cao,Yugang Jiang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SliceGraph uncovers structured, multi-route chain-of-thought (CoT) process geometry across model ensembles, showing that correct answers can emerge from diverse reasoning families rather than a single path; final-answer aggregation hides this internal diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond final-answer evaluation and examine the geometry of intermediate CoT reasoning, including how trajectories share, split, and rejoin, and how distinct reasoning families contribute to the same solution.

Method: Construct SliceGraph by mutual-kNN on a sparse activation-key Jaccard similarity between CoT slices across sampled runs from 4B/8B models on math and science benchmarks. Use blinded annotation to identify biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and define process families as route units. Employ a label-seeded reward field to map value landscapes and perform typed-state transition analysis. Validate via representation ablations, cross-architecture replication, and cross-scale replications.

Result: In 954 problem-model cells, 85.5% contain correct CoTs sharing the same normalized answer that split into multiple process families. Among cells with at least two runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average. Introduce the notion of ‘process isomers’ for same-answer, family-divergent trajectories. Reward-field analysis shows high-value cores split into disconnected regions; route families exhibit distinct transition kernels under matched controls. Robustness is supported by ablations and cross-architecture/cross-scale replications, showing final-answer aggregation overlooks the structured multi-route process geometry.

Conclusion: SliceGraph provides a robust measurement object for the geometry of CoT processes, revealing that multiple, family-divergent routes underlie correct answers and that relying on final-answer aggregation masks rich internal reasoning structure. This advocates incorporating process-geometry analysis into CoT evaluation and model diagnostics.

Abstract: Multi-run chain-of-thought reasoning is usually collapsed to final-answer aggregates, which discard howsampled trajectories share, split, and rejoin through intermediate computation. We propose SliceGraph, a post-hoc problem-model-cell graph built by mutual-kNN over sparse activation-key Jaccard similarity between CoT slices, and treat it as a measurement object for process geometry rather than as a decoding program. Across sampled CoT ensembles from three primary 4B/8B models on math and science benchmarks, blinded annotation supports SliceGraph biconnected components as shared reasoning-state units and process families as within-family strategy-coherent route units. In 85.5% of 954 problem-model cells, correct CoTs sharing the same normalized answer split into multiple process families; among cells with at least two such runs, 76.6% of run pairs are cross-family on average. We call such same-answer, family-divergent correct trajectories process isomers. A label-seeded reward field provides a separate value-landscape layer: success-associated regions often split into disconnected high-value cores, and route families specialize over these core footprints rather than merely duplicating one another. A typed-state transition analysis further shows that process families navigate the same atlas with distinct transition kernels under matched null controls. Representation ablations, a cross-architecture replication, and two cross-scale replications support the robustness of the route-family scaffold, showing that final-answer aggregation overlooks this structured multi-route process geometry.

</details>


### [212] [Teaching Large Language Models When Not to Know: Learning Temporal Critique for Ex-Ante Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14636)
*Chenlu Ding,Jiancan Wu,Yanchen Luo,Zheyuan Liu,Yancheng Yuan,Xiang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes TCFT, a fine-tuning approach for temporal critique to verify ex-ante admissibility of responses. Shows prompting alone leaks information; explicit cutoffs help, prefix constraints help; SFT insufficient. TCFT improves temporal verification and reduces leakage in experiments on Qwen models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit temporal leakage when reasoning from an earlier cutoff, failing ex-ante reasoning. Need a mechanism to ensure answers are temporally admissible rather than relying on prompt design alone.

Method: Systematic evaluation of prompt-level interventions; design of Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning (TCFT) to train models to identify post-cutoff leakage, explain boundary violations, and judge admissibility. Empirical validation on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, comparing TCFT to prompting and SFT baselines.

Result: TCFT consistently reduces temporal leakage compared to prompting and SFT baselines (reductions of 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points on the two model sizes, respectively). Explicit cutoff statements outperform implicit historical framing; prefix constraints reduce leakage more effectively than suffix constraints. Demonstrates that prompting can steer temporal framing but not verify admissibility; ex-ante correctness is a relational property."

Conclusion: Temporal verification via specialized fine-tuning is needed to ensure ex-ante admissibility. Prompt design alone is insufficient, and TCFT offers a robust approach to teach models to detect leakage, explain violations, and judge temporal admissibility.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often fail to reason under temporal cutoffs: when prompted to answer from the standpoint of an earlier time, they exploit knowledge that became available only later. We study this failure through the lens of ex-ante reasoning, where a model must rely exclusively on information knowable before a cutoff. Through a systematic analysis of prompt-level interventions, we find that temporal leakage is highly sensitive to cutoff formulation and instruction placement: explicit cutoff statements outperform implicit historical framings, and prefix constraints reduce leakage more effectively than suffix constraints. These findings indicate that prompting can steer models into a temporal frame, but does not endow them with the ability to verify whether a response is temporally admissible. We further argue that supervised fine-tuning is insufficient, since ex-ante correctness is not an intrinsic property of an answer, but a relation between the answer and the cutoff. To address this gap, we propose TCFT, a Temporal Critique Fine-Tuning framework that trains models to acquire cutoff-aware temporal verification. Given a query, a cutoff, and a candidate response, TCFT teaches the model to identify post-cutoff leakage, explain temporal boundary violations, and judge temporal admissibility. Experiments with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct show that TCFT consistently outperforms prompting and SFT baselines, reducing average leakage by 41.89 and 37.79 percentage points, respectively.

</details>


### [213] [Falkor-IRAC: Graph-Constrained Generation for Verified Legal Reasoning in Indian Judicial AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14665)
*Joy Bose*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Falkor-IRAC introduces a graph-constrained, IRAC-based knowledge graph for Indian legal AI, grounding LLM generation with a Verifier Agent to enforce traceable, doctrine-consistent reasoning; initial proof-of-concept on 51 Supreme Court judgments shows verified citations and rejection of fabricated ones, with future work on RAG baselines and GPU acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Legal reasoning requires constrained, symbolic, precedent-driven inference that vector-based retrieval-augmented generation cannot faithfully capture; in high-volume jurisdictions like India, hallucinations and outdated citations have real justice implications.

Method: Judgments are ingested as IRAC node structures with procedural state transitions, precedent relationships, and statutory references, stored in FalkorDB. Inference accepts LLM outputs only if a valid supporting path exists in the graph, verified by a falsifiability Verifier Agent; doctrinal conflicts are detected as explicit outputs. Evaluation uses graph-native metrics (citation grounding accuracy, path validity, hallucinated precedent rate, conflict detection rate).

Result: Proof-of-concept on 51 Supreme Court judgments; Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and correctly rejected fabricated citations. Baseline comparison to vector-only RAG was not performed in this work.

Conclusion: Graph-grounded generation with an explicit verification layer is feasible for Indian legal AI, offering more appropriate evaluation metrics and addressing hallucination and conflict-detection; current evaluation is limited to a proof-of-concept and CPU-based inference, with GPU acceleration and broader baselines as future work.

Abstract: Legal reasoning is not semantic similarity search. A court judgment encodes constrained symbolic reasoning: precedent propagation, procedural state transitions, and statute-bound inference. These are properties that vector-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) cannot faithfully represent. Hallucinated precedents, outdated statute citations, and unsupported reasoning chains remain persistent failure modes in LLM-based legal AI, with real consequences for access to justice in high-caseload jurisdictions such as India. This paper presents Falkor-IRAC, a graph-constrained generation framework for Indian legal AI that grounds generation in structured reasoning over an IRAC (Issue, Rule, Analysis, Conclusion) knowledge graph. Judgments from the Supreme Court and High Courts of India are ingested as IRAC node structures enriched with procedural state transitions, precedent relationships, and statutory references, stored in FalkorDB for low-latency agentic traversal. At inference time, LLM-generated answers are accepted only if a valid supporting path can be traced through the graph, a check performed by a falsifiability oracle called the Verifier Agent. The system also detects doctrinal conflicts as a first-class output rather than silently resolving them. Falkor-IRAC is evaluated using graph-native metrics: citation grounding accuracy, path validity rate, hallucinated precedent rate, and conflict detection rate. These metrics are argued to be more appropriate for legal reasoning evaluation than BLEU and ROUGE. On a proof-of-concept corpus of 51 Supreme Court judgments, the Verifier Agent correctly validated citations on completed queries and correctly rejected fabricated citations. Evaluation against vector-only RAG baselines is left for future work, as is GPU-accelerated inference to address current timeout rates on CPU hardware.

</details>


### [214] [How Sensitive Are Radiomic AI Models to Acquisition Parameters?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14667)
*D. Gil,I. Sanchez,C. Sanchez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A mixed-effects, performance-oriented framework to quantify CT acquisition-parameter sensitivity for radiomic AI; identifies parameter regions that improve cross-dataset robustness in lung cancer CT, with actionable acquisition settings that balance diagnostic quality and dose.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI radiomic systems often fail when imaging protocols vary across centres. There is a need to quantify how scan parameters influence model performance and to locate clinically meaningful regions that enhance cross-dataset robustness.

Method: A mixed-effects framework that links model performance to acquisition parameters while accounting for subject-level random effects. Applied to lung cancer CT with two multicentre datasets and multiple state-of-the-art architectures; cross-dataset evaluation involves adjusting CT parameters using data from one set and testing on another.

Result: Identified an optimal CT configuration: X-ray tube ≥200 mA, spiral pitch ≤1.5, slice thickness ≤1.25 mm. This improved performance from 0.79±0.04 sensitivity and 0.47±0.10 specificity in low-quality scans to 0.90±0.10 sensitivity and 0.79±0.13 specificity in high-quality scans, indicating enhanced cross-dataset robustness while balancing dose.

Conclusion: The proposed framework provides a quantitative tool to map scan-parameter sensitivity and to select acquisition settings that improve radiomic AI robustness across centres; the recommended configuration demonstrates a favorable trade-off between diagnostic performance and radiation dose.

Abstract: A main barrier for the deployment of AI radiomic systems in clinical routine is their drop in performance under heterogeneous multicentre acquisition protocols. This work presents a performance-oriented framework for quantifying scan parameter sensitivity of radiomic AI models, while identifying clinically significant parameter regions associated with improved cross-dataset robustness. We formulate a mixed-effects framework for quantifying the influence that clinically relevant acquisition parameters have on models performance, while accounting for subject-level random effects. We have applied our framework to lung cancer diagnosis in CT scans using two independent multicentre datasets (a public database and own-collected data) and several SoA architectures. To evaluate across-database reproducibility, CT parameters have been adjusted using the data collected and tested on the public set. The optimal configuration selected is the current of the X-ray tube >= 200 mA, spiral pitch <= 1.5, slice thickness <= 1.25 mm, which balances diagnostic quality with low radiation dose. These configuration push metrics from 0.79+-0.04 sensitivity, 0.47+-0.10 specificity in low quality scans to 0.90+-0.10 sensitivity, 0.79 +- 0.13 specificity in high quality ones.

</details>


### [215] [$π$-Bench: Evaluating Proactive Personal Assistant Agents in Long-Horizon Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14678)
*Haoran Zhang,Luxin Xu,Zhilin Wang,Runquan Gui,Shunkai Zhang,Haodi Lei,Zihao He,Bingsu He,Chicheng Qin,Tong Zhu,Xiaoye Qu,Yang Yang,Yu Cheng,Yafu Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: π-Bench introduces a long-horizon, proactive-assistance benchmark with 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 user personas, emphasizing hidden intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity to evaluate proactivity alongside task completion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional benchmarks underevaluate the ability of agents to infer unstated user needs in sustained, multi-turn interactions; real-world assistants require anticipation of hidden intents across sessions.

Method: Construct π-Bench with 100 tasks across 5 domain-specific personas, embedding hidden intents, task dependencies, and cross-session continuity. Evaluate agents on joint proactivity and task completion over long trajectories, contrasting proactive behavior with mere task fulfillment.

Result: Experiments indicate that proactive assistance remains challenging, there is a clear separation between proactivity and task completion, and prior interactions help resolve intents in later tasks.

Conclusion: π-Bench provides a targeted framework to study proactive, long-horizon assistant behavior, highlighting the need for memory, intent inference, and planning capabilities in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: The rise of personal assistant agents, e.g., OpenClaw, highlights the growing potential of large language models to support users across everyday life and work. A core challenge in these settings is proactive assistance, since users often begin with underspecified requests and leave important needs, constraints, or preferences unstated. However, existing benchmarks rarely evaluate whether agents can identify and act on such hidden intents before they are explicitly stated, especially in sustained multi-turn interactions where user needs emerge gradually. To address this gap, we introduce $π$-Bench, a benchmark for proactive assistance comprising 100 multi-turn tasks across 5 domain-specific user personas. By incorporating hidden user intents, inter-task dependencies, and cross-session continuity, $π$-Bench evaluates agents' ability to anticipate and address user needs over extended interactions, jointly measuring proactivity and task completion in long-horizon trajectories that better reflect real-world use. Experiments show (1) proactive assistance remains challenging, (2) a clear distinction between task completion and proactivity, and (3) the value of prior interaction for proactive intent resolution in later tasks.

</details>


### [216] [On Strong Equivalence Notions in Logic Programming and Abstract Argumentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14721)
*Giovanni Buraglio,Wolfgang Dvorak,Stefan Woltran*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates strong equivalence in dynamic settings for logic programs and abstract argumentation, proposing a new notion of strong equivalence that remains stable under translations to Dung-style and claim-augmented AFs, thus aligning dynamic behavior across formalisms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Strong equivalence is crucial for safe replacement of knowledge bases in nonmonotonic reasoning. Existing static equivalence between logic programs and AFs fails under updates, causing cross-formalism incompatibilities.

Method: Introduce a novel semantic notion of strong equivalence for logic programs and analyze how translations to Dung-style and claim-augmented AFs preserve this equivalence for certain program classes, establishing translation-compatible criteria.

Result: A new strong equivalence notion that is preserved by translations to specified AF frameworks for chosen classes of logic programs, restoring compatibility of dynamic reasoning across these formalisms.

Conclusion: Dynamic compatibility is achieved: logic programming and argumentation frameworks can be updated coherently via translations without breaking strong equivalence, enabling interoperable updates across these nonmonotonic formalisms.

Abstract: Strong equivalence between knowledge bases ensures the possibility of replacing one with the other without affecting reasoning outcomes, in any given context. This makes it a crucial property in nonmonotonic formalisms. In particular, the fields of logic programming and abstract argumentation provide primary examples in which this property has been subject to vast investigations. However, while (classes of) logic programs and abstract argumentation frameworks are known to be semantically equivalent in static settings, this alignment breaks in dynamic contexts due to differing notions of update. As a result, strong equivalence does not always carry over from one formalism to the other. In this paper, we carefully investigate this discrepancy and introduce a new notion of strong equivalence for logic programs. Our approach preserves strong equivalence under translation between certain classes of logic programs and both Dung-style and claim-augmented argumentation frameworks, thus restoring compatibility across these formalisms.

</details>


### [217] [Agentifying Patient Dynamics within LLMs through Interacting with Clinical World Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14723)
*Minghao Wu,Yuting Yan,Zhenyang Cai,Ke Ji,Chuangsen Fang,Ziying Sheng,Xidong Wang,Rongsheng Wang,Hejia Zhang,Shuang Li,Benyou Wang,Hongyuan Zha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SepsisAgent: a world-model-augmented LLM that proposes, simulates, and refines sepsis treatment decisions; demonstrated superior off-policy value and safety on MIMIC-IV vs baselines; learns robust patient dynamics even without simulator access.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs encode broad clinical knowledge but lack grounding in action-conditioned patient dynamics; integrating a learnable Clinical World Model enables simulation of patient responses to interventions, improving safety and guideline adherence.

Method: Three-stage curriculum: (1) patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning; (2) propose–simulate–refine behavior cloning; (3) world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning; uses a Clinical World Model to simulate responses to candidate fluid–vasopressor interventions; evaluated on MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories against traditional RL and LLM baselines with safety and guideline-adherence metrics.

Result: Outperforms baselines in off-policy value; achieves best safety profile under guideline adherence and unsafe-action metrics; repeated world-model interactions help the agent learn regularities in patient evolution that persist even when simulator access is removed.

Conclusion: Integrating a world model with an LLM yields safer, more effective sepsis-treatment recommendations; the agent learns reusable patient-dynamics regularities and demonstrates robust performance beyond simulator availability.

Abstract: Sepsis management in the ICU requires sequential treatment decisions under rapidly evolving patient physiology. Although large language models (LLMs) encode broad clinical knowledge and can reason over guidelines, they are not inherently grounded in action-conditioned patient dynamics. We introduce SepsisAgent, a world model-augmented LLM agent for sepsis treatment recommendation. SepsisAgent uses a learned Clinical World Model to simulate patient responses under candidate fluid--vasopressor interventions, and follows a propose--simulate--refine workflow before committing to a prescription. We first show that world-model access alone yields inconsistent LLM decision performance, motivating agent-specific training. We then train SepsisAgent through a three-stage curriculum: patient-dynamics supervised fine-tuning, propose--simulate--refine behavior cloning, and world-model-based agentic reinforcement learning. On MIMIC-IV sepsis trajectories, SepsisAgent outperforms all traditional RL and LLM-based baselines in off-policy value while achieving the best safety profile under guideline adherence and unsafe-action metrics. Further analysis shows that repeated interaction with the Clinical World Model enables the agent to learn regularities in patient evolution, which remain useful even when simulator access is removed.

</details>


### [218] [XDomainBench: Diagnosing Reasoning Collapse in High-Dimensional Scientific Knowledge Composition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14754)
*Gong Zhiren,Tiantong Wu,Jiaming Zhang,Fuyao Zhang,Che Wang,Yurong Hao,Yikun Hou,Foo Ping,Yilei Zhao,Fei Huang,Chau Yuen,Wei Yang Bryan Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XDomainBench introduces a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning, evaluating LLMs across 8,598 sessions, 20 domains, and 4 task categories. It reveals a systematic reasoning collapse as composition order and domain mixture increase, driven by direct domain-difficulty and interaction-induced error accumulation leading to session collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on single-turn, single-discipline tasks and miss how LLMs handle compositional generalization in dynamic, interactive scientific workflows. There is a need to characterize robustness across domain mixtures and trajectory-based reasoning challenges.

Method: Formalize composition order and mixture structure; construct 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories with 8 trajectory patterns that vary difficulty and domain-mixture dynamics, simulating real AI4S-style workflows; perform large-scale LLM evaluation and analyze error propagation, domain confusion, and session stability.

Result: Finds a systematic reasoning collapse as composition order rises, due to (i) direct difficulty from domain composition and (ii) indirect interaction-amplified failures where trajectory patterns trigger error accumulation and reasoning breakdown, culminating in session collapse.

Conclusion: Interactive, trajectory-aware evaluation is essential to reveal LLM limitations in cross-domain scientific reasoning. XDomainBench provides a framework to stress-test models, highlighting the importance of addressing both domain mixing difficulty and error propagation to improve robustness in interdisciplinary workflows.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed for knowledge synthesis, yet their capacity for compositional generalization in scientific knowledge remains under-characterized. Existing benchmarks primarily focus on single-turn restricted scenarios, failing to capture the capability boundaries exposed by real-world interactive scientific workflows. To address this, we introduce XDomainBench, a diagnostic benchmark for interactive interdisciplinary scientific reasoning. We formalize the composition order and mixture structure to enable systematic stress-testing from single-discipline to inter-disciplinary, comprising 8,598 interactive sessions across 20 domains and 4 task categories, with 8 realistic trajectory patterns covering difficulty and domain-mixture dynamics, simulating real AI4S scenarios. Large-scale evaluation of LLMs reveals a systematic reasoning collapse as composition order increases, stemming from two root causes: (i) direct difficulty increases induced by domain composition, and (ii) indirect interaction-amplified failures where trajectory patterns trigger error accumulation, reasoning breaks, and domain confusion, ultimately leading to session collapse.

</details>


### [219] [Probabilistic Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks for Single and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14758)
*Luca Marzari,Enrico Marchesini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A probabilistic verification framework for RNN-based policies (RNN-ProVe) that estimates the likelihood of undesired behaviors via policy-driven sampling and statistical error bounds, providing bounded-error, high-confidence guarantees and scalability to single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Verification of history-dependent policies in partially observable RL is challenging due to latent hidden state dynamics and limitations of existing tools that rely on restrictive assumptions or coarse over-approximations, often yielding conservative or inconclusive results.

Method: Use policy-driven sampling to approximate the set of hidden states feasible under the trained policy; derive statistical error bounds to produce bounded-error, high-confidence estimates of behavioral violations.

Result: Experimental evaluation on partially observable single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks shows that RNN-ProVe provides more quantitative, feasibility-aware probabilistic guarantees than existing tools and scales to recurrent and multi-agent settings.

Conclusion: RNN-ProVe offers a scalable probabilistic verification framework with quantitative, bounded-error guarantees for RNN-based policies in partially observable RL.

Abstract: History-dependent policies induced by recurrent neural networks (RNNs) rely on latent hidden state dynamics, making verification in partially observable reinforcement learning (RL) challenging. Existing RNN verification tools typically rely on restrictive modeling assumptions or coarse over-approximations of the hidden state space, which can lead to overly conservative or inconclusive results. We propose $\textbf{RNN}$ $\textbf{Pro}$babilistic $\textbf{Ve}$rification ($\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$), a probabilistic framework that $\textit{estimates the likelihood}$ of undesired behaviors in RNN-based policies. $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ uses policy-driven sampling to approximate the set of hidden states that are feasible under a trained policy, and derives statistical error bounds to produce bounded-error, high-confidence estimates of behavioral violations. Experiments on partially observable single-agent and cooperative multi-agent tasks show that $\texttt{RNN-ProVe}$ yields more quantitative, feasibility-aware probabilistic guarantees than existing tools, while scaling to recurrent and multi-agent settings.

</details>


### [220] [AI Outperforms Humans in Personalized Image Aesthetics Assessment via LLM-Based Interviews and Semantic Feature Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14761)
*Yoshia Abe,Tatsuya Daikoku,Yasuo Kuniyoshi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Integrated DL-LLM system that predicts individual aesthetic evaluation by combining low-level image features with high-level semantic preferences elicited via LLM-driven semi-structured interviews; outperforms baselines and within-person variability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aesthetic judgments are subjective and current DL models focusing on low-level features fail to capture individual taste; LLMs trained on vast text corpora can extract high-level semantics and actively elicit preferences to personalize predictions.

Method: Develop an integrated DL-LLM framework that uses LLM-based semi-structured interviews to elicit personal preferences and combines low-level image features with high-level semantic cues to predict aesthetic evaluation.

Result: The proposed system outperforms conventional systems, human predictors, and the target individual's own future re-evaluations, with particularly strong performance on highly-rated images; prediction error is smaller than within-person variability, whereas humans exhibit larger error due to personal value biases.

Conclusion: AI may be better positioned than others or one’s future self to capture individual aesthetic preferences and could act as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility.

Abstract: Accurately predicting individual aesthetic evaluation for images is a fundamental challenge for AI. Various deep learning (DL)-based models have been proposed for this task, training on image evaluation data to extract objective low-level features. However, aesthetic preferences are inherently subjective and individual-dependent. Accurate prediction thus requires the extraction of high-level semantic features of images and the active collection of preference information from the target individual. To address this issue, we focus on the utility of Large Language Models (LLMs) pretrained on vast amounts of textual data, and develop an integrated DL-LLM system. The system actively elicits aesthetic preferences through LLM-based semi-structured interviews and predicts aesthetic evaluation by leveraging both low-level and high-level features. In our experiments, we compare the proposed system against conventional systems, human predictors, and the target individual's own re-evaluations after a certain time interval. Our results show that the proposed system outperforms all of them, with particularly strong performance on highly-rated images. Moreover, the prediction error of the proposed system is smaller than within-person variability, while human predictors show the largest error, likely due to the influence of their own aesthetic values. These results suggest that AI may be better positioned than others or one's future self to capture individual aesthetic preferences at a given point. This opens a new question of whether AI could serve as a deeper interpreter of human aesthetic sensibility than humans themselves.

</details>


### [221] [MediaClaw: Multimodal Intelligent-Agent Platform Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14771)
*Shaoan Zhao,Huanlin Gao,Qiang Hui,Ting Lu,Xueqiang Guo,Yantao Li,Xinpei Su,Fuyuan Shi,Chao Tan,Fang Zhao,Kai Wang,Shiguo Lian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes a three-layer MediaClaw architecture (unified invocation, plugin extension, and workflow orchestration) to unify AIGC capabilities, enable hot-pluggable expansion, and convert production processes into reusable workflows; discusses architectural philosophy and trade-offs to guide practical multimodal capability platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address practical deployment pain points in AIGC adoption—fragmented capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production workflows, and limited reuse of production workflows.

Method: Conceptual architectural design with a three-layer model: a unified invocation abstraction for all AIGC capabilities, a plugin-based extension layer for hot-pluggable capabilities, and a workflow-oriented Skills layer to transform complex production processes into reusable assets. The report emphasizes design philosophy, core capability model logic, and engineering trade-offs; no empirical evaluation is provided in the abstract.

Result: Proposes an architectural blueprint and design considerations for a multimodal capability platform; highlights reusable references for building such platforms; no experimental results are reported.

Conclusion: MediaClaw’s architecture aims to guide practitioners in creating scalable, modular multimodal capability platforms through unified invocation, plug-in extensibility, and workflow-oriented asset design, with an emphasis on practical deployment considerations.

Abstract: MediaClaw is a multimodal agent platform built on the OpenClaw ecosystem. Its core design follows a three-layer architecture of unified abstraction, pluginized extension, and workflow orchestration. The system is intended to address practical deployment pain points in AIGC adoption, including fragmented capabilities, heterogeneous interfaces, disconnected production processes, and limited reuse of high-quality production workflows. \system{} abstracts full-category AIGC capabilities into a unified invocation model, uses plugins to support hot-pluggable capability expansion, and uses task-oriented Skills to turn complex production processes into reusable workflow assets. This report focuses on the architectural design philosophy of MediaClaw, the design logic of its core capability model, and the key engineering trade-offs in implementation. It aims to provide reusable practical reference for building multimodal capability platforms.

</details>


### [222] [Identifying Culprits Through Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient Deep Learning Investigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14774)
*Lata B T,Savitha N J*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Applies Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) to fuse crime data for criminal identification, reporting high accuracy and outperforming several baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional investigations rely on limited data analysis; a robust method is needed to handle complex, noisy datasets and reduce false positives/negatives in suspect identification.

Method: Train a Deep RL agent (DDPG) on features derived from crime scene materials, witness statements, and suspect profiles to maximize the likelihood of identifying the offender while suppressing noise and irrelevant data.

Result: Empirical results indicate the method achieves 95% accuracy in identifying criminals, outperforming several existing methods on the reported dataset.

Conclusion: DDPG-based approach shows promise for forensic identification tasks with effective data fusion; further validation on diverse datasets and consideration of ethical/legal implications are warranted.

Abstract: In the world of AI and advanced technologies investigation aspects identification of a crime or criminal plays a major problem. In this research we focus on a Conventional ways of implicating criminal investigations usually rely on limited data analysis. Finding an optimal and efficient method that will effectively identify criminals from complex datasets and minimise false positives and false negatives is the considered as a challenge. The main novelty approach of this work is based on the deep learning algorithm Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) is presented in this paper. We train the DDPG model with a dataset of crime scene material, witness statements and suspect profiles. The algorithm uses features to maximise the likelihood of identifying the offender while minimising the noise impact and irrelevant data. We show the efficacy of the proposed method, where DDPG identified criminals with an amazing accuracy of 95% than other several existing methods.

</details>


### [223] [A Heterogeneous Temporal Memory Governance Framework for Long-Term LLM Persona Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14802)
*Zhao Yang,Wang Huan,Li Yingshuo,Tu Haomiao,Lin Hujite*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ARPM is an external temporal memory governance framework for long-term dialogue that separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory and employs multi-source evidence retrieval, dual-temporal reranking, and controlled verification to maintain semantic and persona continuity across long interactions, with white-box evaluation revealing benefits and limits under noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and instability during extended interactions, especially with noisy knowledge bases and cross-model transfers. A governance-based approach to continuity, decoupled from model weights, is proposed to enable auditable, transferable control over long-term persona and knowledge consistency.

Method: ARPM architecture separates static memory (knowledge) from dynamic dialogue experience; integrates vector retrieval, BM25, and RRF fusion; applies dual-temporal reranking and chronological evidence reading; employs a controlled evidence verification protocol for answer binding; evaluates with 3 experiments on 50-round QA with varying signal/noise ratios, ablation of history retrieval and BM25, and a 5.1M-character noise substrate with multi-model handoff.

Result: 1) Under 1:5 signal-to-noise, CSV recall is 54.0% vs manual review 100.0%; under 1:200+ noise, CSV recall 44.0% vs manual review 80.0%. Automatic rules can underestimate recall after evidence enters prompts. 2) Ablation: removing dialogue history retrieval drops strict accuracy from 100% to 66.7%; removing BM25 drops to 80.0%; semantic-only retrieval is insufficient for correction and tracing. 3) With large noise and context-clearing/multi-model handoff, ARPM maintains semantic and boundary continuity and persona consistency but reveals limits from weak protocol compliance.

Conclusion: Long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable, auditable components. A white-box evaluation framework exposes where governance controls succeed or fail, and shows that continuity is not solely a function of semantic similarity but of controllable, verifiable governance over memory and evidence.

Abstract: Large language models often suffer from fact loss, timeline confusion, persona drift, and reduced stability during long-range interaction, especially under high-noise knowledge bases, context clearing, and cross-model transfer. To address these issues, we introduce ARPM, an external temporal memory governance framework for long-term dialogue. ARPM separates static knowledge memory from dynamic dialogue experience memory and combines vector retrieval, BM25, RRF fusion, dual-temporal reranking, chronological evidence reading, and a controlled analysis protocol for evidence verification and answer binding. Unlike approaches that encode persona consistency into model weights or rely only on long context, ARPM treats continuity as a traceable, auditable, and transferable governance problem. Using engineering logs, we conduct three experiments. First, in a 50-round question-answering setting, we compare signal-to-noise ratios of 1:5 and 1:200+, and distinguish CSV auto-judgment from manual review. Under 1:5, CSV recall accuracy is 54.0%, while manual review raises it to 100.0%. Under 1:200+, the values are 44.0% and 80.0%. These results show that automatic rules can underestimate recall after supporting evidence enters the prompt. Second, ablation results show that dialogue history retrieval is necessary for recent continuity: disabling it reduces strict accuracy from 100% to 66.7%, and disabling BM25 reduces it to 80.0%, indicating that pure semantic retrieval is insufficient for correction and tracing. Third, under a 5.1-million-character noise substrate, periodic context clearing, and multi-model handoff, ARPM maintains semantic continuity, boundary continuity, and persona consistency, while exposing limits caused by weak protocol compliance. These findings show that long-term persona consistency can be decomposed into governable components and evaluated in a white-box manner.

</details>


### [224] [Interestingness as an Inductive Heuristic for Future Compression Progress](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14831)
*Vincent Herrmann,Jürgen Schmidhuber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The abstract formalizes interestingness as a predictor of future compression progress and shows, via complexity priors, that past progress signals future discoveries; recency drives progress exponentially; Algorithmic Prior is more optimistic than Length Prior; findings validated across three universal computational paradigms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the bottleneck of recursive self-improvement by predicting which tasks/data will yield future progress.

Method: Theoretical analysis using Kolmogorov complexity and Algorithmic Statistics; compare Length, Algorithmic, and Speed priors; derive complexity-runtime profiles; prove exponential dependence on recency; compare priors; empirical validation across three universal computation paradigms.

Result: Inductive interestingness is viable and empirically supported; future progress grows exponentially with recency; Algorithmic Prior yields a quadratic advantage over Length Prior; results replicable across three diverse universal computational paradigms.

Conclusion: Supports using inductive, prior-based heuristics to guide self-improvement research; Algorithmic Prior is especially optimistic; results suggest general applicability across universal computation domains.

Abstract: One of the bottlenecks on the way towards recursively self-improving systems is the challenge of interestingness: the ability to prospectively identify which tasks or data hold the potential for future progress. We formalize interestingness as an inductive heuristic for future compression progress and investigate its predictability using tools from Kolmogorov Complexity and Algorithmic Statistics. By analyzing complexity-runtime profiles under Length, Algorithmic, and Speed priors, we demonstrate that the inductive property of interestingness -- the capacity for past progress to signal future discovery -- is theoretically viable and empirically supported. We prove that expected future progress depends exponentially on the recency of the last observed breakthrough. Furthermore, we show that the Algorithmic Prior is significantly more optimistic than the Length Prior, yielding a quadratic increase in expected discovery for the same observed profile. These findings are experimentally confirmed across three diverse universal computational paradigms.

</details>


### [225] [Emotion-Attended Stateful Memory (EASM):The Architecture for Hyper-Personalization at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14833)
*Vineet Kotecha,Vansh Gupta*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes an emotion-attended stateful memory system to personalize LLM interactions over time, and demonstrates substantial gains in grounding, planning, and emotional validation in a 30-conversation A/B study across six emotion categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Stateless LLMs limit persistent personalization; a memory-enabled, emotion-aware framework could provide long-term user understanding and affective alignment.

Method: Introduce a memory architecture that combines long-term history, emotional signals, and inferred intent at inference time. Evaluate via a controlled A/B study with the same underlying LLM in both conditions over 30 non-scripted conversations spanning six emotional categories.

Result: The memory-enriched condition outperformed the stateless baseline across all measures: memory grounding +95%, plan clarity +57%, emotional validation +34%; results held in emotionally adversarial contexts (grief, distress, uncertainty).

Conclusion: Stateful, emotion-aware memory may form a foundational infrastructure for hyper-personalized AI, though broader validation with larger and more diverse evaluations is needed.

Abstract: Current language model systems remain fundamentally stateless across sessions, limiting their ability to personalize interactions over time. While retrieval-augmented generation and fine-tuning improve knowledge access and domain capability, they do not enable persistent understanding of individual users. We propose an emotion-attended stateful memory architecture that dynamically constructs user-specific conversational context using long-term history, emotional signals, and inferred intent at inference time. To evaluate its impact, we conducted a controlled A/B study across thirty non-scripted conversations spanning six emotionally distinct categories using the same underlying language model in both conditions. The memory-enriched condition consistently outperformed the stateless baseline across all evaluated scenarios. The largest gains were observed in memory grounding (95% improvement), plan clarity (57%), and emotional validation (34%). Results remained consistent even in emotionally adversarial conversations involving grief, distress, and uncertainty. These findings suggest that stateful emotional memory may represent a foundational infrastructure layer for hyper-personalized AI systems, though broader validation across larger and more diverse evaluations remains necessary

</details>


### [226] [A Deterministic Agentic Workflow for HS Tariff Classification: Multi-Dimensional Rule Reasoning with Interpretable Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14857)
*Yu Zhang,Dongjiang Zhuang,Qu Zhou,Zheng Huang,Junhe Wu,Jing Cao,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deterministic, stage-separated agentic workflow for HS tariff classification enforces multi-criteria priority constraints, improving interpretability and performance over end-to-end prompting; evaluated on HSCodeComp with notable results and a finding that some ground-truth labels may diverge from HS rules.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end LLM prompting struggles with enforcing multiple competing priority rules (material, form, function, essential character, part-vs-whole, heading specificity). A fixed, stage-wise, interpretable process aims to ensure rule-consistent classifications and enable verifiable justification for each decision.

Method: Offline knowledge engineering of Chinese HS tariff; online six-stage pipeline with fixed control flow; LM calls confined to narrow stages; reflection/verification as local checks; stage-wise outputs with verbatim citations to relevant notes; deterministic adjudication flow; 226 six-digit disagreements audited; appendix releases adjudication records.

Result: On HSCodeComp six-digit level: 75.0% top-1 and 91.5% top-3 at four digits; 64.2% top-1 and 78.3% top-3 at six digits with Qwen3.6-plus. Open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 in non-thinking mode achieves 84.2% four-digit top-1 and 77.4% six-digit top-1 agreement with frontier model. A two-stage manual audit suggests many HSCodeComp ground-truth labels may deviate from HS general rules; adjudication records provided in appendix for community review.

Conclusion: Deterministic, stage-wise agentic workflow yields interpretability by construction and exposes potential misalignments between ground-truth labels and HS rules; results indicate competitive performance and emphasize the value of adjudication transparency for community verification.

Abstract: Harmonized System (HS) tariff classification is a high-stakes, expert-level task in which a free-form product description must be mapped to a specific six- or eight-digit code under the General Interpretive Rules (GIR), section notes, chapter notes, and Explanatory Notes. The difficulty lies not in knowledge volume but in *multi-dimensional rule reasoning*: a correct classification must satisfy competing priority rules along several axes simultaneously, including material, form, function, essential character, the part-versus-whole boundary, and specific listing versus residual headings. End-to-end prompting of large language models fails characteristically by resolving one axis while ignoring the priority constraints on the others. We present a *deterministic agentic workflow* in contrast to self-planning agents: the control flow is fixed, language model calls are confined to narrow stages, and reflection and verification are retained as local mechanisms. This design yields interpretability by construction--each decision is decomposed into stage-wise structured outputs with verbatim citation of the chapter or section notes that bear on it. The architecture combines offline knowledge-engineering of the Chinese HS tariff with an online six-stage pipeline. Evaluated on HSCodeComp at the six-digit level, the workflow reaches 75.0% top-1 and 91.5% top-3 at four digits, and 64.2% top-1 and 78.3% top-3 at six digits with Qwen3.6-plus; an open-weight Qwen3.6-27B-FP8 backbone in non-thinking mode achieves 84.2% four-digit and 77.4% six-digit top-1 agreement with the frontier model. A two-stage manual audit of 226 six-digit disagreements suggests that a non-trivial fraction of HSCodeComp ground-truth labels may deviate from HS general rules; full adjudication records are released in the appendix as preliminary findings for community review.

</details>


### [227] [Holistic Evaluation and Failure Diagnosis of AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14865)
*Netta Madvil,Gilad Dym,Alon Mecilati,Edo Dekel,Jonatan Liberman,Rotem Brazilay,Liron Schliesser,Max Svidlo,Shai Nir,Orel Shalom,Yaron Friedman,David Connack,Amos Rimon,Philip Tannor,Shir Chorev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Span-level, per-span evaluation paired with top-down diagnosis yields state-of-the-art results on TRAIL (GAIA and SWE-Bench), scalable to long traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation methods report only success/failure and lack diagnostic or locational explanations, especially for long, structured traces; a scalable, interpretable evaluation framework is needed.

Method: A holistic agent evaluation framework that combines top-down agent-level diagnosis with bottom-up span-level evaluation, decomposing analysis into independent per-span assessments and producing span-level rationales.

Result: On TRAIL, achieves state-of-the-art across all metrics on GAIA and SWE-Bench; up to 38% relative gain on category F1, up to 3.5x localization accuracy, up to 12.5x joint localization-categorization accuracy; per-category analysis shows leading in more error categories.

Conclusion: Evaluation methodology, not model capability, is the bottleneck; using the framework yields substantially better localization and categorization, and scales to arbitrary-length traces.

Abstract: AI agents execute complex multi-step processes, but current evaluation falls short: outcome metrics report success or failure without explaining why, and process-level approaches struggle to connect failure types to their precise locations within long, structured traces. We present a holistic agent evaluation framework that pairs top-down agent-level diagnosis with bottom-up span-level evaluation, decomposing analysis into independent per-span assessments. This decomposition scales to traces of arbitrary length and produces span-level rationales for each verdict.
