On the TRAIL benchmark, our framework achieves state-of-the-art results across all metrics on both GAIA and SWE-Bench, with relative gains over the strongest prior baselines of up to 38% on category F1, up to 3.5x on localization accuracy, and up to 12.5x on joint localization-categorization accuracy. Per-category analysis shows our framework leading in more error categories than any other evaluator. Notably, the same frontier model achieves several times higher localization accuracy when used inside our framework than as a monolithic judge over the full trace, showing that evaluation methodology, not model capability, is the bottleneck.

</details>


### [228] [BiFedKD: Bidirectional Federated Knowledge Distillation Framework for Non-IID and Long-Tailed ECG Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14886)
*Zixuan Shu,Tiancheng Cao,Hen-Wei Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BiFedKD is a bidirectional federated knowledge distillation framework for ECG IoMT that stabilizes global distillation via an aggregation-by-distillation pipeline with temperature scaling, improving accuracy/Macro-F1 while reducing communication and computation costs compared to standard FD baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federated learning for ECG in IoMT faces strict privacy, regulatory constraints, and high communication overhead from exchanging high-dimensional model updates. Fed distillation (FD) reduces traffic but struggles with non-IID and long-tailed label distributions common in ECG data.

Method: Introduce BiFedKD with bidirectional knowledge distillation and an aggregation-by-distillation pipeline using temperature scaling to generate a stable, global distillation signal for cross-client alignment. Logit-based transfer is used instead of parameter sharing to cut communication. Evaluated on MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset.

Result: BiFedKD yields improvements over baseline by 3.52 percentage points in accuracy and 9.93 percentage points in Macro-F1. For the same Macro-F1, it reduces communication overhead by 40% and computation cost by 71.7%.

Conclusion: BiFedKD effectively mitigates non-IID and long-tailed distribution challenges in ECG FL via distilled knowledge transfer, achieving better performance with substantially lower communication and compute requirements, indicating strong potential for privacy-preserving IoMT ECG monitoring.

Abstract: Electrocardiogram (ECG) monitoring in Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) networks is constrained by strict data-sharing regulations and privacy concerns. Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative learning by keeping raw ECG data on devices, but frequent transmissions of high-dimensional model updates incur heavy per-round traffic over bandwidth-limited links. To alleviate this bottleneck, federated distillation (FD) replaces parameter exchange with logit-based knowledge transfer. However, the performance of FD often degrades under the non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID) and long-tailed label distributions in ECG deployments. To address these challenges, we propose a bidirectional federated knowledge distillation (BiFedKD) framework that employs an aggregation-by-distillation pipeline with temperature scaling to produce a stable global distillation signal for cross-client alignment. Experiments on the MIT-BIH Arrhythmia dataset show that BiFedKD improves accuracy and Macro-F1 over the baseline by $3.52\%$ and $9.93\%$, respectively. Moreover, to reach the same Macro-F1, BiFedKD reduces communication overhead by $40\%$ and computation cost by $71.7\%$ compared with the baseline.

</details>


### [229] [Beyond Individual Intelligence: Surveying Collaboration, Failure Attribution, and Self-Evolution in LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14892)
*Shihao Qi,Jie Ma,Rui Xing,Wei Guo,Xiao Huang,Zhitao Gao,Jianhao Deng,Jun Liu,Lingling Zhang,Bifan Wei,Boqian Yang,Pinghui Wang,Jianwen Sun,Jing Tao,Yaqiang Wu,Hui Liu,Yu Yao,Tongliang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces LIFE: Lay the capability foundation, Integrate agents through collaboration, Find faults through attribution, and Evolve through autonomous self-improvement—a causal, stage-based framework for analyzing and guiding self-improving multi-agent systems, unifying prior surveys and outlining a cross-stage research agenda.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents excel at reasoning and tool use but struggle with sustained coordination; multi-agent collaboration amplifies these challenges and can propagate errors without self-driven improvement. A unified, causal framework is needed to connect capability, collaboration, fault attribution, and autonomous evolution across stages.

Method: Proposes a four-stage LIFE framework, with systematic taxonomies for each stage and formal dependencies between adjacent stages. Synthesizes and organizes existing work on single-agent capabilities, multi-agent collaboration, fault attribution, and self-evolution, and identifies open challenges at stage boundaries to develop a cross-stage research agenda for closed-loop, self-improving multi-agent systems.

Result: A unified, stage-based taxonomy and causal map (LIFE) for multi-agent intelligence, including formalized dependencies between stages, a synthesized literature overview, and a roadmap of cross-stage challenges and future research directions toward autonomous self-improvement of multi-agent systems.

Conclusion: The LIFE progression provides a systematic reference and conceptual roadmap for developing autonomous, self-improving multi-agent intelligence, bridging fragmented strands and enabling closed-loop, self-diagnostic, and self-reorganizing multi-agent collaboration.

Abstract: LLM-based autonomous agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in reasoning, planning, and tool use, yet remain limited when tasks require sustained coordination across roles, tools, and environments. Multi-agent systems address this through structured collaboration among specialized agents, but tighter coordination also amplifies a less explored risk: errors can propagate across agents and interaction rounds, producing failures that are difficult to diagnose and rarely translate into structural self-improvement. Existing surveys cover individual agent capabilities, multi-agent collaboration, or agent self-evolution separately, leaving the causal dependencies among them unexamined. This survey provides a unified review organized around four causally linked stages, which we term the LIFE progression: Lay the capability foundation, Integrate agents through collaboration, Find faults through attribution, and Evolve through autonomous self-improvement. For each stage, we provide systematic taxonomies and formally characterize the dependencies between adjacent stages, revealing how each stage both depends on and constrains the next. Beyond synthesizing existing work, we identify open challenges at stage boundaries and propose a cross-stage research agenda for closed-loop multi-agent systems capable of continuously diagnosing failures, reorganizing structures, and refining agent behaviors, extending current coordination frameworks toward more self-organizing forms of collective intelligence. By bridging these previously fragmented research threads, this survey aims to offer both a systematic reference and a conceptual roadmap toward autonomous, self-improving multi-agent intelligence.

</details>


### [230] [COREKG: Coreset-Guided Personalized Summarization of Knowledge Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14900)
*Sohel Aman Khan,Raghava Mutharaju,Supratim Shit*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Personalized KG summarization via coreset sampling guided by sensitivity scores; creates per-user, compact KG summaries with bounded error, outperforming baselines on several KBs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KGs can be extremely large; need compact, query-focused representations; personalization aligns summaries with individual user workloads to improve QA and efficiency.

Method: Adapt coreset theory; define sensitivity of each triple with respect to a user's query workload; sample a subset (coreset) via importance sampling; construct independent summaries per user.

Result: Experiments on Freebase, Wikidata, DBpedia show higher query-answering accuracy and structural coverage than GLIMPSE, PPR, iSummary, PEGASUS, APEX2; achieved with only a tiny fraction of the original graph.

Conclusion: Personalized, coreset-based KG summarization is effective for reducing size while preserving QA performance and structural characteristics; approach generalizes to various KBs and workloads.

Abstract: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are extensively used across different domains and in several applications. Often, these KGs are very large in size. Such KGs become unwieldy for tasks such as question answering and visualization. Summarization of KGs offers a viable alternative in such cases. Furthermore, personalized KG summarization is crucial in the current data-driven world as it captures the specific requirements of users based on their query patterns. Since it only maintains relevant information, the personalized summaries of KG are small, resulting in significantly smaller storage requirements and query runtime. In this work, we adapt the coreset theory to create personalized KG summaries. For a given dataset and a user-specific query workload, we present an approach that samples a relevant subset of triples using sensitivity-based importance sampling. We ensure that the subset approximates the characteristics of the full dataset with bounded approximation error. We define sensitivity scores that measure the importance of a triple with respect to a user's query workload, which are then used by our coreset construction algorithm. We explicitly focus on personalized knowledge graph summarization by constructing summaries independently for each user based on their query behaviour.
  Our evaluation on Freebase, WikiData, and DBpedia shows that COREKG delivers higher query-answering accuracy and structural coverage than the state-of-the-art methods, such as GLIMPSE, PPR, iSummary, PEGASUS and APEX$^2$ while requiring only a tiny fraction of the original graph.

</details>


### [231] [KGPFN: Unlocking the Potential of Knowledge Graph Foundation Model via In-Context Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14907)
*Yisen Gao,Jiaxin Bai,Haoyu Huang,Zhongwei Xie,Yufei Li,Hong Ting Tsang,Sirui Han,Yangqiu Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KGPFN unifies transferable relational regularities with in-context learning for KG reasoning, enabling strong generalization to unseen graphs via multi-graph pretraining and retrieval-based global context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KG foundation models struggle with in-context learning and heterogenous structured context; there is a need to leverage both local (neighborhood) and global (relation-wide) context across multiple graphs to generalize to unseen graphs.

Method: KGPFN learns relation representations via message passing on relation graphs to capture cross-graph invariances; encodes local context with a multi-layer NBFNet; builds a large, relation-specific global context by retrieving many instances of the query relation with their neighborhoods; integrates via Prior-Data Fitted Network that fuses feature- and sample-level attention; pretrains across diverse KGs to learn reusable patterns and contextual override.

Result: On 57 KG benchmarks, KGPFN demonstrates strong adaptation to unseen graphs with in-context learning alone and outperforms competitive fine-tuned KG foundation models.

Conclusion: Shows effective integration of transferable relational regularities and inference-time structured context, enabling robust cross-graph generalization; code released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Knowledge graph (KG) foundation models aim to generalize across graphs with unseen entities and relations by learning transferable relational structure. However, most existing methods primarily emphasize relation-level universality, while in-context learning, the other pillar of foundation models remains under-explored for KG reasoning. In KGs, context is inherently structured and heterogeneous: effective prediction requires conditioning on the local context around the query entities as well as the global context that summarizes how a relation behaves across many instances. We propose KGPFN, a KG foundation model using Prior-data Fitted Network that unifies transferable relational regularities with inference-time in-context learning from structured context. KGPFN first learns relation representations via message passing on relation graphs to capture cross-graph relational invariances. For query-specific reasoning, it encodes local neighborhoods using a multi-layer NBFNet as local context. To enable ICL at global scale, it constructs relation-specific global context by retrieving a large set of instances of the query relation together with their local neighborhoods, and aggregates them within a Prior-Data Fitted Network framework that combines feature-level and sample-level attention. Through multi-graph pretraining on diverse KGs, KGPFN learns when to instantiate reusable patterns and when to override them using contextual evidence. Experiments on 57 KG benchmarks demonstrate that KGPFN achieves strong adaptation to previously unseen graphs through in-context learning alone, consistently outperforming competitive fine-tuned KG foundation models. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/KGPFN.

</details>


### [232] [From Sycophantic Consensus to Pluralistic Repair: Why AI Alignment Must Surface Disagreement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14912)
*Varad Vishwarupe,Nigel Shadbolt,Marina Jirotka*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Argues that preference aggregation alone is insufficient for pluralistic alignment; introduces Pluralistic Repair Score (PRS) based on Gricean maxims (scoping, signalling, repair); empirical evidence shows disagreement surfaces but repair quality remains low, highlighting deployment governance as crucial for true pluralism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Contemporary RLHF-aligned AI tends toward agreement with interlocutors, risking sycophantic consensus. Real-world consequential deliberation requires surfacing and managing value conflict rather than smoothing over disagreement.

Method: Formalize three Grice-inspired conversational mechanisms: scoping (acknowledge perspective limits), signalling (surface value conflict), repair (principled revision). Define PRS as an interactional precondition for pluralism and test on two frontier models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, N=198; GPT-4o, N=100) to distinguish principled revision from capitulation.

Result: PRS captures visible disagreement and principled revision but shows low repair quality across both models; agreement-following coexists with poor repair quality, indicating aggregation alone fails to ensure pluralistic engagement; deployment governance interfaces and data-pipelines are pivotal.

Conclusion: Pluralism is largely determined at the deployment-governance layer; PRS is a first step toward measuring interactional conditions for pluralism (not full pluralism). Future work should address definitional baselines of 'principled', scalability, and governance mechanisms for inclusive decision-making.

Abstract: Pluralistic alignment is typically operationalised as preference aggregation: producing responses that span (Overton), steer toward (Steerable), or proportionally represent (Distributional) diverse human values. We argue that aggregation alone is an incomplete primitive for deployed pluralistic alignment. Under genuine value pluralism, the failure mode of contemporary RLHF-trained assistants is not insufficient coverage but sycophantic consensus: a learned tendency to agree with, validate, and minimise friction with the immediate interlocutor. Because deployed AI systems now mediate consequential deliberation across health, civic life, labour, and governance, the collapse of disagreement at the interaction layer is not a narrow technical concern but a structural failure with distributive consequences. We reframe pluralistic alignment around three conversational mechanisms drawn from Grice's maxims: scoping (acknowledging the limits of one's perspective), signalling (surfacing value-conflict rather than smoothing it over), and repair (revising one's position on principled grounds, not on user pressure). We formalise a metric, the Pluralistic Repair Score (PRS), distinguishing principled revision from capitulation, and present a small-scale empirical illustration on two frontier RLHF-trained models (Claude Sonnet 4.5, N=198; GPT-4o, N=100) showing that, for both, agreement-following coexists with low repair-quality on contested-value prompts. PRS measures an interactional precondition for pluralism (visible disagreement; principled revision) rather than pluralism in full; we discuss the difference, take seriously the reflexive question of whose "principled" counts, and argue that pluralism is most decisively made or unmade at the deployment-governance layer: interfaces, preference-data pipelines, and audit infrastructure.

</details>


### [233] [GraphFlow: An Architecture for Formally Verifiable Visual Workflows Enabling Reliable Agentic AI Automation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14968)
*Drewry H. Morris,Luis Valles,Reza Hosseini Ghomi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GraphFlow treats workflow diagrams as executable specifications to improve reliability of agentic AI automation in mission-critical multi-step processes, combining compile-time guarantees with a durable runtime to enforce contracts and enable auditing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address rapid error propagation in multi-step workflows and the lack of semantic correctness guarantees in existing platforms; mitigate sensitivity to prompt variation in agentic systems and improve auditability.

Method: Limit diagrams to a restricted class at compile time to produce reusable automations with contracts (preconditions, postconditions, composition obligations) subject to proof-checking; runtime engine stores outcomes in an append-only log, enforces contracts at boundaries, supports replay/retries/audit; swimlanes delineate trust boundaries.

Result: Pilot across three clinical sites: 8,728 workflow runs with 97.08% completion using an early prototype without verified-core; external integrations were primary failure sources.

Conclusion: Semantics and a proof-checked admission model are defined and under active development; evaluation of the verified core awaits future work.

Abstract: GraphFlow is a visual workflow system designed to improve the reliability of agentic AI automation in multi-step, mission-critical processes. In these workflows, small errors compound rapidly: under an idealized model of independent steps, a ten-step process with 90% per-step reliability completes successfully only 35% of the time. Existing workflow platforms provide durable execution and observability but offer few semantic correctness guarantees, while agentic systems plan at inference time, making behavior sensitive to prompt variation and difficult to audit.
  GraphFlow is designed to address this gap by treating workflow diagrams as the executable specification, a single artifact defining data scope, execution semantics, and monitoring. At compile time, a restricted class of diagrams is specified to produce reusable automations whose contracts (preconditions, postconditions, and composition obligations) are intended to be proof-checked before admission to a shared library. At runtime, a durable engine records outcomes in an append-only event log and can enforce contracts at system boundaries, supporting replay, retries, and audit. Swimlanes make trust boundaries explicit, separating verified logic from external systems, human judgment, and AI decisions.
  A year-long pilot across three clinical sites executed 8,728 cohort-enrolled workflow runs with a 97.08% completion rate under an early prototype without the verified-core subsystem; observed failures were localized primarily to external integrations. The formal semantics and proof-checked admission model described here are specified and under active development. Evaluation of the verified core is reserved for future work.

</details>


### [234] [Explainable Detection of Depression Status Shifts from User Digital Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14995)
*Loris Belcastro,Francesco Gervino,Fabrizio Marozzo,Domenico Talia,Paolo Trunfio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Explainable, multi-BERT framework to track depression-related status shifts in social-media traces, modeling temporal trajectories and generating human-readable reports, with improved change-point detection and coherence over two datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the need to quantify and interpret longitudinal mental-health signals from timestamped digital traces, providing interpretable insights without clinical diagnosis.

Method: Use multiple BERT-based models to extract signals (sentiment, emotion, depression severity); aggregate signals into user trajectories over time; apply segmentation to identify change points; integrate a large language model to generate concise, human-readable reports describing signal evolution.

Result: Outperforms direct LLM-based reporting in coherence and coverage; higher temporal coherence; improved change-point detection; ablation confirms contributions of temporal modeling and segmentation.

Conclusion: Provides an interpretable view of mental health signals over time, supporting research and decision making without aiming at clinical diagnosis.

Abstract: Every day, users generate digital traces (e.g., social media posts, chats, and online interactions) that are inherently timestamped and may reflect aspects of their mental state. These traces can be organized into temporal trajectories that capture how a user's mental health signals evolve, including phases of improvement, deterioration, or stability. In this work, we propose an explainable framework for detecting and analyzing depression-related status shifts in user digital traces. The approach combines multiple BERT-based models to extract complementary signals across different dimensions (e.g., sentiment, emotion, and depression severity). Such signals are then aggregated over time to construct user-level trajectories that are analyzed to identify meaningful change points. To enhance interpretability, the framework integrates a large language model to generate concise and human-readable reports that describe the evolution of mental-health signals and highlight key transitions. We evaluate the framework on two social media datasets. Results show that the approach produces more coherent and informative summaries than direct LLM-based reporting, achieving higher coverage of user history, stronger temporal coherence, and improved sensitivity to change points. An ablation study confirms the contribution of each component, particularly temporal modeling and segmentation. Overall, the method provides an interpretable view of mental health signals over time, supporting research and decision making without aiming at clinical diagnosis.

</details>


### [235] [Learning Developmental Scaffoldings to Guide Self-Organisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14998)
*Milton L. Montero,Elias Najarro,Jakob Schauser,Sebastian Risi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Jointly learning self-organization rules and pre-patterns (NCA + SIREN) shows initial conditions bias dynamics, improving robustness, encoding capacity, and symmetry breaking; reveals a memory–compute trade-off in development-like systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how information is distributed between initial conditions and local interaction rules in self-organizing development, bridging biology and computation.

Method: Train a Neural Cellular Automaton (NCA) together with a learned coordinate-based pattern generator (SIREN) to produce patterns; perform information-theoretic analyses of information flow; vary interaction between pre-patterns and self-organization; assess robustness, encoding capacity, symmetry breaking.

Result: Jointly learned systems outperform purely self-organizing ones in robustness, encoding capacity, and symmetry breaking. Pre-patterns bias dynamics in ways that aid convergence beyond merely matching targets. Information analysis reveals a nontrivial distribution of information between pre-patterns and self-organization.

Conclusion: The structure of initial conditions plays an active, nontrivial role in shaping self-organizing dynamics, indicating a memory–compute trade-off in developmental processes. This has implications for designing robust self-organizing systems and for understanding information transfer in development.

Abstract: From subcellular structures to entire organisms, many natural systems generate complex organisation through self-organisation: local interactions that collectively give rise to global structure without any blueprint of the outcome. Yet a significant portion of the information driving such processes is not produced by self-organisation itself, instead, it is often offloaded to initial conditions of the system. Biological development is a prime example, where maternal pre-patterns encode positional and symmetry-breaking information that scaffolds the self-organising process. From maternal morphogen gradients in early embryogenesis to tissue-level morphogenetic pre-patterns guiding organ formation, this transfer of information to initial conditions, analogous to a memory-compute trade-off in computational systems, is a fundamental part of developmental processes. In this work, we study this offloading phenomenon by introducing a model that jointly learns both the self-organisation rules and the pre-patterns, allowing their interplay to be varied and measured under controlled conditions: a Neural Cellular Automaton (NCA) paired with a learned coordinate-based pattern generator (SIREN), both trained simultaneously to generate a set of patterns. We provide information-theoretic analyses of how information is distributed between pre-patterns and the self-organising process, and show that jointly learning both components yields improvements in robustness, encoding capacity, and symmetry breaking over purely self-organising alternatives. Our analysis further suggests that effective pre-patterns do not simply approximate their targets; rather, they bias the developmental dynamics in ways that facilitate convergence, pointing to a non-trivial relationship between the structure of initial conditions and the dynamics of self-organisation.

</details>


### [236] [Small, Private Language Models as Teammates for Educational Assessment Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15015)
*Chris Davis Jaldi,Anmol Saini,Shan Zhang,Noah Schroeder,Cogan Shimizu,Eleni Ilkou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SLMs rival LLMs in assessment-question design while preserving privacy; but model-based evaluation can be biased; Human-in-the-Loop is essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of LLMs (subjective eval, proprietary models, deployment constraints) and underexplored effectiveness of SLMs for assessment tasks; compare across generation quality and evaluation reliability.

Method: Systematic comparison of LLMs and SLMs for assessment question design; measure generation quality across Bloom's taxonomy using reproducible, pedagogically grounded metrics; compare model-based judgments to expert-informed evaluations to assess reliability and agreement.

Result: SLMs show competitive performance across key pedagogical quality dimensions and enable privacy-preserving local deployment; model-based evaluations exhibit inconsistencies and bias relative to expert ratings.

Conclusion: Position language models as bounded assistants in assessment workflows; emphasize Human-in-the-Loop; advance automated educational question generation by examining quality, reliability, and deployment trade-offs.

Abstract: Generative AI increasingly supports educational design tasks, e.g., through Large Language Models (LLMs), demonstrating the capability to design assessment questions that are aligned with pedagogical frameworks (e.g., Bloom's taxonomy). However, they often rely on subjective or limited evaluation methods; focus primarily on proprietary models; or rarely systematically examine generation, evaluation, or deployment constraints in real educational settings. Meanwhile, Small Language Models (SLMs) have emerged as local alternatives that better address privacy and resource limitations; yet their effectiveness for assessment tasks remains underexplored. To address this gap, we systematically compare LLMs and SLMs for assessment question design; evaluate generation quality across Bloom's taxonomy levels using reproducible, pedagogically grounded metrics; and further assess model-based judging against expert-informed evaluation by analyzing reliability and agreement patterns. Results show that SLMs achieve competitive performance across key pedagogically motivated quality dimensions while enabling local, privacy-sensitive deployment. However, model-based evaluations also exhibit systematic inconsistencies and bias relative to expert ratings. These findings provide evidence to posit language models as bounded assistants in assessment workflows; underscore the necessity of Human-in-the-Loop; and advance the automated educational question generation field by examining quality, reliability, and deployment-aware trade-offs.

</details>


### [237] [Orchard: An Open-Source Agentic Modeling Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15040)
*Baolin Peng,Wenlin Yao,Qianhui Wu,Hao Cheng,Xiao Yu,Rui Yang,Tao Ge,Alessandrio Sordoni,Xingdi Yuan,Yelong Shen,Pengcheng He,Tong Zhang,Zhou Yu,Jianfeng Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Open-source Orchard framework for scalable agentic modeling with Orchard Env and three domain recipes; achieves competitive results on SWE benchmarks and multiple open tasks, using limited synthetic data and RL improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the open research gap in agentic modeling by providing an open, harness-agnostic environment layer and scalable training recipes to reduce reliance on proprietary codebases.

Method: Introduce Orchard Env as a lightweight environment service for sandbox lifecycle management. Three recipes: Orchard-SWE (coding agents) using 107k trajectories from MiniMax-M2.5 and Qwen3.5-397B with credit-assignment SFT and Balanced Adaptive Rollout RL; Orchard-GUI (vision-language) using 0.4k distilled trajectories and 2.2k open-ended tasks; Orchard-Claw (personal assistant) trained on 0.2k synthetic tasks. Evaluation across SWE-bench, WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, DeepShop, Claw-Eval, and with ZeroClaw harnesses.

Result: SWE: 64.3% Verified after SFT; 67.5% after SFT+RL, setting a new open-source SOTA for comparable model sizes. GUI: 74.1% (WebVoyager), 67.0% (Online-Mind2Web), 64.0% (DeepShop). Claw: 59.6% pass@3 on Claw-Eval; 73.9% with a stronger ZeroClaw harness.

Conclusion: A lightweight, open, harness-agnostic environment layer enables reusable agentic data, training recipes, and evaluations across domains; Orchard demonstrates that open-source, modular infrastructure can achieve strong cross-domain agentic capabilities.

Abstract: Agentic modeling aims to transform LLMs into autonomous agents capable of solving complex tasks through planning, reasoning, tool use, and multi-turn interaction with environments. Despite major investment, open research remains constrained by infrastructure and training gaps. Many high-performing systems rely on proprietary codebases, models, or services, while most open-source frameworks focus on orchestration and evaluation rather than scalable agent training. We present Orchard, an open-source framework for scalable agentic modeling. At its core is Orchard Env, a lightweight environment service providing reusable primitives for sandbox lifecycle management across task domains, agent harnesses, and pipeline stages. On top of Orchard Env, we build three agentic modeling recipes. Orchard-SWE targets coding agents. We distill 107K trajectories from MiniMax-M2.5 and Qwen3.5-397B, introduce credit-assignment SFT to learn from productive segments of unresolved trajectories, and apply Balanced Adaptive Rollout for RL. Starting from Qwen3-30B-A3B-Thinking, Orchard-SWE achieves 64.3% on SWE-bench Verified after SFT and 67.5% after SFT+RL, setting a new state of the art among open-source models of comparable size. Orchard-GUI trains a 4B vision-language computer-use agent using only 0.4K distilled trajectories and 2.2K open-ended tasks. It achieves 74.1%, 67.0%, and 64.0% success rates on WebVoyager, Online-Mind2Web, and DeepShop, respectively, making it the strongest open-source model while remaining competitive with proprietary systems. Orchard-Claw targets personal assistant agents. Trained with only 0.2K synthetic tasks, it achieves 59.6% pass@3 on Claw-Eval and 73.9% when paired with a stronger ZeroClaw harness. Collectively, these results show that a lightweight, open, harness-agnostic environment layer enables reusable agentic data, training recipes, and evaluations across domains.

</details>


### [238] [Case-Based Calibration of Adaptive Reasoning and Execution for LLM Tool Use](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15041)
*Renning Pang,Tian Lan,Leyuan Liu,Piao Tong,Sheng Cao,Xiaosong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CAST uses case-derived signals from historical executions to adapt prompting and RL strategies for calibrated tool use, improving accuracy and reducing reasoning length.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tension between reasoning depth and structural validity in tool-augmented LLMs by leveraging historical execution trajectories as reusable signals.

Method: Proposes CAST, a case-driven framework that treats historical execution traces as structured cases, extracting complexity and failure signals to guide reward design and adaptive reasoning in reinforcement learning.

Result: On BFCLv2 and ToolBench CAST achieves higher schema-faithful execution and tool-use success, with up to 5.85 percentage-point accuracy gain and 26% shorter reasoning, reducing high-impact structural errors.

Conclusion: Historical execution cases offer reusable adaptation knowledge for calibrated tool use, enabling autonomous internalization of case-based strategies.

Abstract: Tool use extends large language models beyond parametric knowledge, but reliable execution requires balancing appropriate reasoning depth with strict structural validity. We approach this problem from a case-based perspective to present CAST, a case-driven framework that treats historical execution trajectories as structured cases. Instead of reusing raw exemplar outputs, CAST extracts case-derived signals to identify complexity profiles for estimating optimal reasoning strategies, alongside failure profiles to map likely structural breakdowns. The framework translates this knowledge into a fine-grained reward design and adaptive reasoning, enabling the model to autonomously internalize case-based strategies during reinforcement learning. Experiments on BFCLv2 and ToolBench demonstrate that CAST improves both schema-faithful execution and task-level tool-use success while reducing unnecessary deliberation. The approach achieves up to 5.85 percentage points gain in overall execution accuracy and reduces average reasoning length by 26%, significantly mitigating high-impact structural errors. Ultimately, this demonstrates how historical execution cases can provide reusable adaptation knowledge for calibrated tool use.

</details>


### [239] [Dual-Dimensional Consistency: Balancing Budget and Quality in Adaptive Inference-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15100)
*Rongman Xu,Yifei Li,Tianzhe Zhao,Yanrui Wu,Bo Li,Hang Yan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Dual-Dimensional Consistency (DDC) couples Confidence-Weighted Bayesian path evaluation with Trend-Aware Stratified Pruning to focus compute on high-quality reasoning paths, reducing token usage by over 10x while preserving or improving accuracy across LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Inference-time scaling is hampered by treating sampling width (breadth) and depth (termination) as separate, orthogonal objectives, leading to hallucinations or premature pruning. A unified framework is needed to balance path quality and adaptive termination.

Method: Integrate a Confidence-Weighted Bayesian protocol to assess and weight reasoning paths by estimated quality, with a Trend-Aware Stratified Pruning mechanism that adapts termination depth based on trend signals and path stratification, concentrating compute on promising paths and filtering low-quality ones.

Result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmarks shows token consumption is reduced by >10x while achieving or surpassing the accuracy of strong baselines across multiple LLMs.

Conclusion: DDC provides a unified mechanism to jointly optimize path quality and termination, enabling efficient inference-time scaling without sacrificing performance, with potential broad applicability to decoding strategies.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities in reasoning. However, maximizing their potential through inference-time scaling faces challenges in trade-off between sampling budget and reasoning quality. Current strategies remain inefficient as they typically treat sampling width and depth as orthogonal objectives, where width consensus methods risk reinforcing hallucinations, while depth pruning mechanisms prematurely truncate complex yet valid reasoning chains. Therefore, we propose Dual-Dimensional Consistency (DDC), a unified framework that bridges path quality with adaptive termination. By coupling Confidence-Weighted Bayesian protocol with a Trend-Aware Stratified Pruning, our method ensures that computational resources are concentrated on high quality reasoning paths, filtering hallucinations while accelerating consensus. Evaluations across five benchmarks demonstrate that this approach reduces token consumption by over 10 times while maintaining or exceeding the accuracy of strong baselines across various LLMs.

</details>


### [240] [Why Neighborhoods Matter: Traversal Context and Provenance in Agentic GraphRAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15109)
*Riccardo Terrenzi,Maximilian von Zastrow,Serkan Ayvaz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Citations in Agentic GraphRAG must consider the full retrieval trajectory, not just cited evidence; both cited and uncited graph context influence answer accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and quantify citation faithfulness in retrieval-augmented generation with graph traversal, and to assess how citation omission or masking affects answer quality.

Method: Controlled ablation experiments that isolate, remove, and mask cited and uncited graph entities to observe effects on answers and accuracy.

Result: Cited evidence is often necessary for accurate answers; removing citations substantially changes answers and reduces accuracy. However, uncited traversal context and surrounding graph structure also influence correctness, meaning citations alone are insufficient.

Conclusion: Evaluation of citations should emphasize provenance across the retrieval trajectory, not just support from cited sources.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation can improve factuality by grounding answers in external evidence, but Agentic GraphRAG complicates what it means for citations to be faithful. In these systems, an agent explores a knowledge graph before producing an answer and a small set of citations. We frame citation faithfulness as a trajectory-level problem: final citations should not only support the answer, but also account for the graph traversal, structure, and visited-but-uncited entities that may influence it. Through controlled ablation experiments, we compare the effects of isolating, removing, and masking cited and uncited graph entities. Our results show that cited evidence is often necessary, as removing it substantially changes answers and reduces accuracy. However, citations are not sufficient, because accurate answers can also depend on uncited traversal context and surrounding graph structure. These findings suggest that citation evaluation in Agentic GraphRAG should move beyond source support toward provenance over the broader retrieval trajectory.

</details>


### [241] [APWA: A Distributed Architecture for Parallelizable Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15132)
*Evan Rose,Tushin Mallick,Matthew D. Laws,Cristina Nita-Rotaru,Alina Oprea*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: APWA presents a distributed multi-agent architecture that achieves efficient, scalable processing of heavily parallelizable LLM-based workloads by decomposing workflows into independent subproblems executed with non-interfering resources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome reasoning, coordination, and computational scaling bottlenecks in autonomous LLM-based multi-agent systems when tackling large, parallelizable tasks.

Method: Proposes APWA, a distributed architecture that decomposes workflows into non-interfering subproblems; enables parallel execution across heterogeneous data and resources without cross-communication, using independent resources for subproblems.

Result: APWA dynamically decomposes complex queries into parallelizable workflows and demonstrates scalability on larger tasks where prior systems fail.

Conclusion: APWA enhances throughput and scalability for heavily parallelizable agentic workloads by enabling parallel decomposition and non-interfering execution across distributed resources.

Abstract: Autonomous multi-agent systems based on large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable abilities in independently solving complex tasks in a wide breadth of application domains. However, these systems hit critical reasoning, coordination, and computational scaling bottlenecks as the size and complexity of their tasks grow. These limitations hinder multi-agent systems from achieving high-throughput processing for highly parallelizable tasks, despite the availability of parallel computing and reasoning primitives in the underlying LLMs. We introduce the Agent-Parallel Workload Architecture (APWA), a distributed multi-agent system architecture designed for the efficient processing of heavily parallelizable agentic workloads. APWA facilitates parallel execution by decomposing workflows into non-interfering subproblems that can be processed using independent resources without cross-communication. It supports heterogeneous data and parallel processing patterns, and it accommodates tasks from a wide breadth of domains. In our evaluation, we demonstrate that APWA can dynamically decompose complex queries into parallelizable workflows and scales on larger tasks in settings where prior systems fail completely.

</details>


### [242] [OpenDeepThink: Parallel Reasoning via Bradley--Terry Aggregation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15177)
*Shang Zhou,Wenhao Chai,Kaiyuan Liu,Huanzhi Mao,Qiuyang Mang,Jingbo Shang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OpenDeepThink is a population-based test-time reasoning framework that uses pairwise Bradley–Terry comparisons to rank and mutate candidate solutions, enabling scalable, model-agnostic improvement of LLM reasoning with multi-candidate sampling and no ground-truth verifier.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome selection bottlenecks and biases in pointwise LLM judging when selecting the best among multiple reasoning traces without a verifier, enabling scalable test-time compute through population-based selection and mutation.

Method: In each generation, random pairs of candidate outputs are judged by the LLM; a Bradley–Terry model aggregates these pairwise judgments into a global ranking. Top-ranked candidates are preserved; the top 75% are mutated using natural-language critiques produced during comparisons; the bottom 25% are discarded.

Result: Empirically increases effective Codeforces Elo on Gemini 3.1 Pro by +405 points across eight sequential LLM-call rounds (~27 minutes). The approach transfers across weaker/stronger models without retuning. On the multi-domain HLE benchmark, gains cluster in objectively verifiable domains and diminish in subjective domains. CF-73, a curated set of 73 expert-rated Codeforces problems with International Grandmaster annotation and high local-evaluation agreement, is released.

Conclusion: Population-based, pairwise comparison-driven selection at test time improves LLM reasoning performance and transferability without retuning, with domain-dependent gains; the authors also provide a curated benchmark (CF-73) to study expert-rated, verifiable problem solving.

Abstract: Test-time compute scaling is a primary axis for improving LLM reasoning. Existing methods primarily scale depth by extending a single reasoning trace. Scaling breadth by sampling multiple candidates in parallel is straightforward, but introduces a selection bottleneck: choosing the best candidate without a ground-truth verifier, since pointwise LLM judging is noisy and biased. To address this, we introduce OpenDeepThink, a population-based test-time compute framework that selects via pairwise Bradley-Terry comparison. Each generation, the LLM judges random pairs of candidates and aggregates votes via Bradley-Terry into a global ranking; top-ranked candidates are preserved and the top three quarters are mutated using the natural-language critiques produced during comparison; the bottom quarter is discarded. OpenDeepThink raises Gemini 3.1 Pro's effective Codeforces Elo by +405 points in eight sequential LLM-call rounds (~27 minutes wall-clock). The pipeline transfers across weaker and stronger models without retuning, and on the multi-domain HLE benchmark, gains appear concentrated in objectively verifiable domains and reverse in subjective ones. We release CF-73, a curated set of 73 expert-rated Codeforces problems with International Grandmaster annotation and 99% local-evaluation agreement against the official verdict.

</details>


<div id='cs.CG'></div>

# cs.CG [[Back]](#toc)

### [243] [Hitting Axis-Parallel Segments with Weighted Points](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14499)
*Rajiv Raman,Siddhartha Sarkar,Jatin Yadav*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: LP-rounding breaks the classic factor-2 barrier for geometric hitting-set with axis-parallel segments, giving near-optimal constants across weighted, unweighted, and few-orientation variants, plus limited-structure PTAS.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Geometric hitting-set is NP-hard even in simple axis-aligned settings; improving approximation factors beyond 2 for practical and theoretical interest; understanding the tractability of oriented-segment variants.

Method: Decompose into horizontal and vertical sub-instances; solve via LP-relaxation with systematic rounding on horizontal lines and an exact repair step on residual vertical instances; analyze unweighted case more tightly; study special case with lines; generalize to d orientations; derive PTAS for bounded-complexity unweighted subclasses.

Result: Weighted: randomized (1+2/e)-approximation; Unweighted: (1+1/(e-1))-approximation; Lines-sub-instance variant: improved to 1+1/e and APX-hardness result; Generalization to d orientations; PTAS for bounded-complexity subclasses of unweighted problem.

Conclusion: LP-rounding yields substantial improvements over the factor-2 baseline, with near-optimal constants for several variants; extended to multiple orientations and fixed-complexity cases; opens questions on derandomization, tight integrality gaps, and broader geometric settings.

Abstract: We study a geometric hitting-set problem in which the input consists of a set $P$ of weighted points and a family $S=H\cup V$ of axis-parallel segments in the plane. The goal is to select a minimum-weight subset of $P$ that hits every segment in $S$. Even restricted geometric hitting-set problems are known to be computationally hard, and for axis-parallel segments the standard decomposition into horizontal and vertical sub-instances yields only a simple factor-$2$ approximation.
  We present an LP-rounding algorithm that breaks the factor-2 barrier. For the weighted problem, we obtain a randomized $(1+2/e)$-approximation by combining systematic rounding on horizontal lines with an exact repair step on residual vertical sub-instances. In the unweighted case, a sharper analysis gives a $(1+1/(e-1))$-approximation. Finally, we consider the case where one of the sub-instances consists of lines instead of line segments, a problem considered by Fekete et al. (Geometric Hitting Set for Segments of Few Orientations, Theor. Comp. Sys., 62 (2) 2018),. In this case, we improve their result to obtain an approximation factor of $1+1/e$ and show that the problem is APX-hard. We also present algorithms for the generalization to $d$ orientations, as well as PTASes for bounded-complexity subclasses of the unweighted Hitting Set problem.

</details>


### [244] [Min-1-Planarity is NP-Hard](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14834)
*Yuto Okada*

Main category: cs.CG

TL;DR: NP-hard to decide if a graph has a min-1-planar drawing; min-k-planarity generalizes k-planarity and was introduced recently as a framework for crossing-constrained drawings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the computational complexity of min-k-planarity and its place in the landscape of crossing-constrained graph drawings, and to extend planarity-like constraints beyond standard k-planarity.

Method: Complexity-theoretic reduction establishing NP-hardness for min-1-planarity; construction of graph gadgets to enforce crossing constraints and relate instances to known hard problems; analysis of min-1-planar drawings where each crossing edge participates in at most one crossing.

Result: The decision problem for min-1-planarity is NP-hard; min-k-planarity is a generalization of k-planarity introduced by Binucci et al. in GD 2023 and JGAA 2024.

Conclusion: This work strengthens the complexity understanding of crossing-constrained drawings, showing that even the seemingly restricted min-1-planar setting is intractable and situates min-k-planarity as a broad, challenging generalization relevant to drawing algorithms and complexity theory.

Abstract: In this paper, we show that it is NP-hard to determine whether a given graph admits a min-1-planar drawing. A drawing of a graph is min-$k$-planar if, for every crossing in the drawing, at least one of the two crossing edges involves at most $k$ crossings. This notion of min-$k$-planarity was introduced by Binucci, Büngener, Di Battista, Didimo, Dujmović, Hong, Kaufmann, Liotta, Morin, and Tappini [GD 2023; JGAA, 2024] as a generalization of $k$-planarity.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [245] [Vision-Based Runtime Monitoring under Varying Specifications using Semantic Latent Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13923)
*Bardh Hoxha,Oliver Schön,Hideki Okamoto,Lars Lindemann,Georgios Fainekos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Certified runtime monitors for past-time STL (ptSTL) from visual observations under partial observability. Introduces a semantic-basis as the minimal prediction target for a finite atom dictionary, enabling a single conformal calibration across a fragment without per-formula retraining. Also proposes a rolling predictor monitor that reconstructs history online. Empirical results show horizon-dependent advantages (rolling better at short horizons; semantic-basis better at long horizons). Validated with conformal coverage on pedestrian-crossroad benchmarks and Waymo data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Need safe, sample-efficient runtime monitoring from images with finite-sample guarantees, under partial observability, and with reusability across formulas (no per-formula retraining).

Method: For finite dictionaries of temporal atoms, prove that the semantic-basis vector of atom robustness scores is the minimal (best) prediction target within the class of monotone, 1-Lipschitz reusable interfaces. Formulas are evaluated by a deterministic decoder derived from the parse tree; a single conformal calibration pass certifies the entire fragment without a union bound. Introduce a rolling prediction monitor that only predicts current predicate values and reconstructs the temporal history online; easier to learn but more conservative at long horizons.

Result: Rolling monitor achieves tighter certified bounds at short horizons; semantic-basis monitor is up to 4x tighter at long horizons. Real-world validation on Waymo driving data shows both monitors achieve empirical conformal coverage.

Conclusion: The work provides reusable, certifiably safe monitoring for ptSTL from images under partial observability, with a clear trade-off between rolling (short-horizon efficiency) and semantic-basis (long-horizon tightness). Demonstrates practical applicability with conformal guarantees on real driving data.

Abstract: We study certified runtime monitoring of past-time signal temporal logic (ptSTL) from visual observations under partial observability. The monitor must infer safety-relevant quantities from images and provide finite-sample guarantees, while being \emph{reusable}: once trained and calibrated, it should certify any formula in a target fragment without per-formula retraining. For fragments induced by a finite dictionary of temporal atoms, we prove that the \emph{semantic basis}, the vector of atom robustness scores, is the minimum prediction target within the class of monotone, 1-Lipschitz reusable interfaces: any formula is evaluated by a deterministic decoder derived from the parse tree, and a single conformal calibration pass certifies the entire fragment with no union bound. We also introduce a \emph{rolling prediction monitor} that predicts only current predicate values and reconstructs temporal history online; this is easier to learn but grows conservative at long horizons. On a pedestrian-crossroad benchmark, rolling achieves tighter certified bounds at short horizons while the semantic-basis monitor is up to 4-times tighter at long horizons. We validate the presented monitors on real-world Waymo driving data, where both monitors satisfy the conformal coverage guarantee empirically.

</details>


### [246] [Mechanistic Interpretability of EEG Foundation Models via Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13930)
*William Lehn-Schiøler,Magnus Ruud Kjær,Rahul Thapa,Magnus Guldberg Pedersen,Anton Storgaard Mosquera,Nick Williams,Radu Gatej,Tue Lehn-Schiøler,Sándor Beniczky,Sadasivan Puthusserypady,James Zou,Lars Kai Hansen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc sparse-feature auditing of EEG foundation-model embeddings using TopK Sparse Autoencoders across three architectures to test monosemanticity and entanglement, with a target-vs-off-target steering metric and a spectral decoder that maps interventions to interpretable frequency changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open the black-box of EEG foundation models, grounding latent representations in a clinical taxonomy to improve interpretability, trust, and robustness.

Method: Train TopK Sparse Autoencoders on embeddings from three EEG transformers (SleepFM, REVE, LaBraM) to extract sparse feature dictionaries. Ground features to clinical concepts (abnormality, age, sex, medication). Use a single hyperparameter procedure via an intrinsic dictionary health audit that transfers across architectures. Apply concept steering with a target vs. off-target metric to define regimes: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, non-encoded. Identify failure modes (wrecking-ball interventions, age-pathology entanglement) and map interventions back through a spectral decoder to EEG amplitude spectra, yielding interpretable frequency signatures.

Result: Three operational regimes emerge: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, and non-encoded. The framework exposes failures such as wrecking-ball interventions that collapse performance and confounding age-pathology entanglements. A single, transferable hyperparameter audit supports cross-architecture robustness. The spectral decoder ties latent manipulations to physiologically interpretable signatures (e.g., slow-wave suppression, alpha restoration).

Conclusion: The proposed sparse-feature audit provides a generalizable, clinically grounded interpretability framework for EEG foundation models, revealing critical representational failures and offering interpretable spectral correlates to latent concept manipulations.

Abstract: EEG foundation models achieve state-of-the-art clinical performance, yet the internal computations driving their predictions remain opaque: a barrier to clinical trust. We apply TopK Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) across three architecturally distinct EEG transformers: SleepFM, REVE, and LaBraM to extract sparse feature dictionaries from their embeddings. By grounding these features in a clinical taxonomy (abnormality, age, sex, and medication), we benchmark monosemanticity and entanglement across architectures. A single hyperparameter procedure, driven by an intrinsic dictionary health audit, transfers robustly across all three architectures. Via concept steering, we introduce a "target vs. off-target" probe area metric to quantify steering selectivity and reveal three operational regimes: selectively steerable, encoded but entangled, and non-encoded. This framework exposes critical representational failures: "wrecking-ball" interventions that collapse global model performance, and clinical entanglements, such as age-pathology confounding, where it is impossible to suppress one concept without corrupting the other. Finally, a spectral decoder maps these interventions back to the amplitude spectrum, translating latent manipulations into physiologically interpretable frequency signatures, such as pathological slow-wave suppression and $α$-band restoration.

</details>


### [247] [Rethinking Molecular OOD Generalization via Target-Aware Source Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13932)
*Zhuohao Lin,Kun Li,Jiameng Chen,Jiajun Yu,Duanhua Cao,Yizhen Zheng,Wenbin Hu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SCOPE-BENCH benchmarks OOD molecular property prediction via cluster-based partitioning in descriptor space; POMA enables robust multi-source transfer with a retrieve-compose-adapt RL-based source-selection and dual-scale adaptation, revealing limitations of current models and delivering MAE gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current scaffold-splitting and conventional domain adaptation fail under extreme out-of-distribution shifts, causing shortcut learning or negative transfer. A robust evaluation benchmark and a principled transfer framework are needed for reliable extrapolation in drug discovery.

Method: SCOPE-BENCH uses cluster-level partitioning in an explicit physicochemical descriptor space to evaluate OOD performance. POMA formulates knowledge transfer as a retrieve-compose-adapt pipeline: (1) identify labeled source scaffolds structurally close to the unlabeled target as proxy targets; (2) a reinforcement-learning policy selects the optimal source subset from a large candidate pool; (3) perform dual-scale domain adaptation at macroscopic topological and microscopic pharmacophore scales.

Result: On SCOPE-BENCH, state-of-the-art 3D molecular models exhibit prediction errors up to 8.0× (mean 5.9×) under extreme OOD. POMA achieves up to 11.2% reduction in mean absolute error, with an average relative improvement of 6.2% across diverse backbone architectures.

Conclusion: The proposed benchmark and retrieval-based transfer framework address robust OOD prediction in molecular properties and show practical improvements; code is available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Robust prediction of molecular properties under extreme out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios is a pivotal bottleneck in AI-driven drug discovery. Current scaffold-splitting protocols fail to obstruct microscopic semantic overlap, predisposing models to shortcut learning and overestimating their true extrapolation capability; meanwhile, conventional domain adaptation paradigms suffer under extreme structural shifts, as blindly aligning heterogeneous source libraries injects topological noise and triggers negative transfer. To address these two challenges, scaffold-cluster out-of-distribution performance evaluation benchmark (SCOPE-BENCH), a benchmark built on cluster-level partitioning in an explicit physicochemical descriptor space, is proposed alongside policy optimization for multi-source adaptation (POMA), a framework that formulates knowledge transfer as a retrieve-compose-adapt pipeline: labeled source scaffolds structurally close to the unlabeled target are first identified as proxy targets; a reinforcement learning policy then adaptively selects the optimal source subset from an exponentially large candidate pool; and dual-scale domain adaptation is finally performed at macroscopic topological and microscopic pharmacophore scales. Evaluations show that prediction errors of state-of-the-art 3D molecular models surge by up to 8.0x on SCOPE-BENCH with a mean of 5.9x, while POMA achieves up to an 11.2% reduction in mean absolute error with an average relative improvement of 6.2% across diverse backbone architectures. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Molecular-OOD-Code-73F6.

</details>


### [248] [Unsupervised learning of acquisition variability in structural connectomes via hybrid latent space modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13933)
*Gaurav Rudravaram,Lianrui Zuo,Karthik Ramadass,Elyssa McMaster,Jongyeon Yoon,Aravind R. Krishnan,Adam M. Saunders,Chenyu Gao,Nancy R. Newlin,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Lori L. Beason Held,Murat Bilgel,Laura A. Barquero,Micah DArchangel,Tin Q. Nguyen,Laurie B. Cutting,Derek Archer,Timothy J. Hohman,Daniel C. Moyer,Bennett A. Landman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Architectural annealing in a Joint-VAE to separate acquisition variability from biology in dMRI connectomes; improves site clustering vs baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Acquisition differences across sites, scanners, and protocols introduce variability in diffusion MRI connectomes, confounding biological signals; need unsupervised models that separate acquisition-related variation from biological variation.

Method: Unsupervised Joint-VAE with architectural annealing of encoder outputs prior to decoding to balance discrete and continuous latent variables; dataset of 7,416 connectomes across 2-102 years, 13 studies, 25 acquisition-parameter combos; compared to standard VAE, PCA+kmeans, and loss-annealed hybrids.

Result: Architectural annealing yields stronger site learning (ARI=0.53, p<0.05); Joint-VAE captures clusters aligned with scanner/protocol differences; improved disentanglement of acquisition effects.

Conclusion: Architectural annealing provides a useful unsupervised mechanism for capturing acquisition variability in dMRI by jointly modeling smooth and categorical structure, outperforming loss-based annealing methods.

Abstract: Acquisition differences across sites, scanners, and protocols in dMRI introduce variability that complicates structural connectome analysis. This motivates deep learning models that can represent high-dimensional connectomes in a low-dimensional space while explicitly separating acquisition-related effects from biological variation. Conventional dimensionality reduction methods model all variance as continuous, so acquisition effects often get absorbed into a continuous latent space. Recent hybrid latent-space models combine discrete and continuous components to address this, but typically require manual capacity tuning to ensure the discrete component captures the intended variability. We introduce an unsupervised framework that removes this manual tuning by architecturally annealing encoder outputs before decoding, allowing the model to adaptively balance discrete and continuous latent variables during training. To evaluate it, we curated a dataset of N=7,416 structural connectomes derived from dMRI, spanning ages 2 to 102 and 13 studies with 25 unique acquisition-parameter combinations. Of these, 5,900 are cognitively unimpaired, 877 have mild cognitive impairment (MCI), and 639 have Alzheimer's disease (AD). We compare against a standard VAE, PCA with k-means clustering, and hybrid models that anneal only through the loss function. Our architectural annealing produces stronger site learning (ARI=0.53, p<0.05) than these baselines. Results show that a hybrid continuous-discrete latent space, with architectural rather than loss-based annealing, provides a useful unsupervised mechanism for capturing acquisition variability in dMRI: by jointly modeling smooth and categorical structure, the Joint-VAE recovers clusters aligned with scanner and protocol differences.

</details>


### [249] [Beyond Mode-Seeking RL: Trajectory-Balance Post-Training for Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13935)
*Saba Ahmadi,Prasanna Parthasarathi,Yufei Cui*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TraFL mitigates trajectory locking in diffusion language models by introducing a trajectory-balance objective that aligns post-training updates with a reward-tilted target distribution anchored to a frozen reference model, improving both in-distribution and held-out performance across math and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Post-training for diffusion LMs often uses reward-maximizing updates that cause trajectory locking, where sampling concentrates probability on a narrow set of denoising paths, reducing coverage of valid alternatives and harming generalization.

Method: Introduce TraFL (Trajectory Flow baLancing), a trajectory-balance objective. It trains the policy toward a reward-tilted target distribution anchored to a frozen reference model, uses a diffusion-compatible sequence-level surrogate, and applies a learned prompt-dependent normalization to make training practical for diffusion LMs.

Result: Across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks, TraFL is the only evaluated post-training method that improves over the base model in every benchmark-length setting, with gains persisting as sampling budget increases. Improvements transfer to held-out evaluations: TraFL remains above the base on Minerva Math and is strongest on all LiveCodeBench difficulty splits.

Conclusion: TraFL effectively mitigates trajectory locking in diffusion language models and yields robust improvements across tasks and evaluation settings, suggesting better coverage of valid solution paths and improved generalization.

Abstract: Diffusion language models are a promising alternative to autoregressive models, yet post-training methods for them largely adapt reward-maximizing objectives. We identify a central failure mode in this setting we call trajectory locking: sampled reward-driven updates over-concentrate probability mass onto a narrow set of denoising paths, reducing coverage of alternative correct solutions under repeated sampling. To address this, we propose TraFL (Trajectory Flow baLancing), a trajectory-balance objective that trains the policy toward a reward-tilted target distribution anchored to a frozen reference model. We make this practical for diffusion language models with a diffusion-compatible sequence-level surrogate and a learned prompt-dependent normalization. Across mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks, TraFL is the only evaluated post-training method that improves over the base model in every benchmark-length setting, with gains that persist as the sampling budget increases. The improvements transfer to held-out evaluations: TraFL stays above the base model on Minerva Math and is the strongest method on every LiveCodeBench difficulty split.

</details>


### [250] [Towards the Next Frontier of LLMs, Training on Private Data: A Cross-Domain Benchmark for Federated Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13936)
*Daniel M. Jimenez-Gutierrez,Enrique Zuazua,Georgios Kellaris,Joaquin del Rio,Oleksii Sliusarenko,Xabi Uribe-Etxebarria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated PEFT-based fine-tuning of LLMs across private, non-IID data silos enables near-central performance with privacy preservation; QLoRA/IA3 offer efficiency gains with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlock valuable private institutional data (healthcare, finance) for LLM adaptation while preserving privacy, privacy/regulatory constraints, and addressing non-IID heterogeneity across sites; align with Green AI goals.

Method: Employ Sherpa.ai Federated Learning to jointly fine-tune a shared LLM across data silos without data exchange; compare three PEFT strategies (LoRA, QLoRA, IA3) on MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, FiQA-SA across multiple backbones under non-IID conditions.

Result: Federated fine-tuning achieves performance close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning; QLoRA and IA3 improve efficiency with limited accuracy degradation.

Conclusion: Federated PEFT is a viable, privacy-preserving approach for adapting LLMs to non-public domain data; it enables practical deployment in regulated sectors, with trade-offs in heterogeneity handling and communication efficiency.

Abstract: The recent success of large language models (LLMs) has been largely driven by vast public datasets. However, the next frontier for LLM development lies beyond public data. Much of the world's most valuable information is private, especially in highly regulated sectors such as healthcare and finance, where data include patient histories or customer communications. Unlocking this data could represent a major leap forward, enabling LLMs with deeper domain expertise and stronger real-world utility. Yet, these data cannot be shared because they are distributed across institutions and constrained by privacy, regulatory, and organizational barriers. Moreover, institutional datasets are typically non-independent and identically distributed (non-IID), differing across sites in population characteristics, data modalities, documentation patterns, and task-specific label distributions.
  In this paper, we demonstrate a practical approach to unlocking private and distributed institutional data for LLM adaptation through federated collaboration across data silos. Built on the Sherpa.ai Federated Learning platform, our framework enables nodes to jointly fine-tune a shared LLM without exchanging private data. We evaluate this approach through a cross-domain benchmark in healthcare and finance, using four closed-ended question answering and classification datasets: MedQA, MedMCQA, FPB, and FiQA-SA. We compare three parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) strategies-LoRA, QLoRA, and IA3-across pretrained backbones under non-IID settings reflecting institutional data heterogeneity. Our results show that federated fine-tuning performs close to centralized training and outperforms isolated single-institution learning. From a Green AI perspective, QLoRA and IA3 improve efficiency with limited accuracy degradation, supporting federated PEFT as a viable approach for adapting LLMs where data cannot be shared.

</details>


### [251] [EvolveMem:Self-Evolving Memory Architecture via AutoResearch for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13941)
*Jiaqi Liu,Xinyu Ye,Peng Xia,Zeyu Zheng,Cihang Xie,Mingyu Ding,Huaxiu Yao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-evolving memory architecture for LLM agents enabling co-evolution of stored content and retrieval mechanism via an LLM-powered diagnosis module and guarded meta-analysis; AutoResearch loop yields improved retrieval strategies and cross-benchmark transfer; code available.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome fixed retrieval infrastructure in long-term memory by enabling dynamic, autonomous optimization of retrieval configurations; achieve adaptive memory that evolves with tasks and data.

Method: Expose retrieval configuration as structured action space; diagnosis module reads per-question failure logs; root-cause analysis; targeted configuration adjustments; guarded meta-analyzer applies with revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards; iterative self-evolution from minimal baseline; discovers new configuration dimensions; cross-benchmark transfer.

Result: LoCoMo: 25.7% relative improvement over top baseline; 78.0% relative over minimal. MemBench: 18.9% relative over strongest baseline. Positive transfer across benchmarks; code at GitHub.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a self-evolving mechanism (AutoResearch) for adaptive memory retrieval that generalizes beyond benchmarks, uncovering universal retrieval principles and reducing manual tuning.

Abstract: Long-term memory is essential for LLM agents that operate across multiple sessions, yet existing memory systems treat retrieval infrastructure as fixed: stored content evolves while scoring functions, fusion strategies, and answer-generation policies remain frozen at deployment. We argue that truly adaptive memory requires co-evolution at two levels: the stored knowledge and the retrieval mechanism that queries it. We present EvolveMem, a self-evolving memory architecture that exposes its full retrieval configuration as a structured action space optimized by an LLM-powered diagnosis module. In each evolution round, the module reads per-question failure logs, identifies root causes, and proposes targeted configuration adjustments; a guarded meta-analyzer applies them with automatic revert-on-regression and explore-on-stagnation safeguards. This closed-loop self-evolution realizes an AutoResearch process: the system autonomously conducts iterative research cycles on its own architecture, replacing manual configuration tuning. Starting from a minimal baseline, the process converges autonomously, discovering effective retrieval strategies including entirely new configuration dimensions not present in the original action space. On LoCoMo, EvolveMem outperforms the strongest baseline by 25.7% relative and achieves a 78.0% relative improvement over the minimal baseline. On MemBench, EvolveMem exceeds the strongest baseline by 18.9% relative. Evolved configurations transfer across benchmarks with positive rather than catastrophic transfer, indicating that the self-evolution process captures universal retrieval principles rather than benchmark-specific heuristics. Code is available at https://github.com/aiming-lab/SimpleMem.

</details>


### [252] [EMA: Efficient Model Adaptation for Learning-based Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13942)
*Daiyang Yu,Xinyu Chen,Yihan Zhang,Yan Liang,Yaqi Qiao,Fan Lai*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EMA enables cross-environment model adaptation for learning-based networked systems using state transformers and selective data labeling, reducing adaptation cost and improving performance across eight systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Networked systems exhibit heterogeneous, long-running, dynamic conditions; traditional ML lacks adaptation, causing costly retraining, data collection, and slow responsiveness.

Method: System-driven, data-centric approach: introduce state transformers to warm-start adaptation by mapping new environment input states to previously similar states; prioritize labeling high-utility data to balance training and labeling costs.

Result: Evaluation on eight representative learning-based systems shows adaptation cost reductions of 14.9-42.4% (GPU training time) and throughput improvements of 6.9-31.3%.

Conclusion: EMA provides minimal-overhead, broadly applicable adaptation for learning-based systems, addressing two deployment challenges and yielding practical performance gains.

Abstract: Machine learning (ML) is increasingly applied to optimize system performance in tasks such as resource management and network simulation. Unlike traditional ML tasks (e.g., image classification), networked systems often operate in heterogeneous, long-running, and dynamic environment states, where input conditions (e.g., network loads) and operational objectives can shift over time and across settings. Existing learning-based systems offer little support for adaptation, resulting in costly model training, extensive data collection, degraded system performance, and slow responsiveness.
  This paper presents EMA, the first model adaptation system supporting learning-based systems to adapt to evolving environments with minimal operational overhead. EMA takes a system-driven, data-centric approach that accommodates diverse system and model designs while addressing two key deployment challenges. First, it reduces expensive model training by introducing state transformers that align the input state of a new environment with previously similar states, allowing models to warm-start adaptation. Second, it addresses the often-overlooked yet costly process of data labeling--collecting ground truth for exploring and training on various system decisions--by prioritizing labeling high-utility data while balancing the tradeoff between training and labeling cost. Evaluations on eight representative learning-based systems show that EMA reduces adaptation costs (e.g., GPU training time) by 14.9-42.4% while improving system performance (e.g., network throughput) by 6.9-31.3%.

</details>


### [253] [A Unified Geometric Framework for Weighted Contrastive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13943)
*Raphael Vock,Edouard Duchesnay,Benoit Dufumier*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Weighted InfoNCE can be interpreted as a Distance Geometry Problem, where the weighting scheme prescribes the target geometry realized by the representation. This yields exact optimal embeddings for several CL objectives and clarifies when CL geometries are realizable, degenerate, or inconsistent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the geometric structure of contrastive embeddings and how different weighting schemes shape the realized geometry; to provide a principled framework for designing contrastive objectives across supervised, weakly supervised, and continuous-label settings.

Method: Formulate weighted InfoNCE as a distance-geometry optimization. Derive exact characterizations of optimal embeddings for supervised (SupCon, Soft SupCon) and weakly supervised/objective variants, analyze class-imbalance effects, and examine continuous-label (y-Aware) CL and X-CLR. Compare Euclidean vs spherical weighting to identify geometrically consistent choices.

Result: - SupCon and Soft SupCon collapse samples within each class to a single prototype. 
- Balanced SupCon recovers the regular simplex geometry; class imbalance induces non-uniform inter-class similarities under SupCon. Soft SupCon preserves a regular simplex geometry regardless of class sizes.
