- In continuous-label settings, y-Aware CL cannot reach its entropic optimum unless labels lie on a hypersphere, revealing a mismatch between Euclidean label weights and spherical latent similarity. Geometrically consistent choices (Euclidean-Euclidean weighting or X-CLR) admit unique optimal embeddings.

Conclusion: The choice of weighting dictates whether contrastive learning is geometrically realizable, degenerate, or inconsistent. This framework provides principled guidelines for designing CL objectives and explains observed phenomena (e.g., class-imbalance effects, y-aware limitations).

Abstract: Contrastive learning (CL) aims to preserve relational structure between samples by learning representations that reflect a similarity graph. Yet, the geometry of the resulting embeddings remains poorly understood. Here we show that weighted InfoNCE objectives can be interpreted as Distance Geometry Problems, where the weighting scheme specifies the target geometry to be realized by the representation. This viewpoint yields exact characterizations of the optimal embeddings for several supervised and weakly supervised objectives. In supervised classification, both SupCon and Soft SupCon (a dense relaxation of it where pairs from distinct classes have small non-zero similarity) collapse samples within each class to a single prototype. However, while balanced SupCon recovers the classical regular simplex geometry, class imbalance breaks this symmetry: SupCon induces non-uniform inter-class similarities depending on class sizes, whereas Soft SupCon preserves a regular simplex geometry regardless of class imbalance. In continuous-label settings, our framework reveals a different failure mode: y-Aware CL generally cannot attain its entropic optimum unless the labels lie on a hypersphere, exposing a mismatch between Euclidean label weights and spherical latent similarity. By contrast, geometrically consistent choices such as Euclidean-Euclidean weighting or X-CLR admit unique optimal embeddings. Our results show that the choice of weighting scheme determines whether contrastive learning is geometrically realizable, degenerate, or inconsistent, providing a principled framework for designing contrastive objectives.

</details>


### [254] [Collider-Bench: Benchmarking AI Agents with Particle Physics Analysis Reproduction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13950)
*Darius A. Faroughy,Sofia Palacios Schweitzer,Ian Pang,Siddharth Mishra-Sharma,David Shih*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Collider-Bench introduces a benchmark to test whether LLM agents can reproduce LHC experimental analyses using only public papers and open software, turning analyses into executable simulation pipelines and evaluating predictions with histogram metrics; results show average agents do not outperform a physicist-in-the-loop baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing long-horizon tool-use benchmarks miss the nuanced, real scientific workflow of collider analyses and reproducibility challenges arising from opaque internal toolchains; a public, executable task suite is needed to stress-test reasoning, coding, and trial-and-error under physics constraints.

Method: Provide a set of tasks derived from LHC searches; supply a containerized sandbox and open event-simulation tools; require agents to convert published analyses into executable simulation-and-selection pipelines to predict signal-region yields; evaluate with histogram-based metrics; report per-task compute cost; use an LLM judge to detect fabrications/duplications; test across general-purpose coding agents.

Result: On average, no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop solution; initial task set released with open tooling; evaluation across a capability ladder of agents demonstrates the challenge of faithfully recreating collider analyses without internal software.

Conclusion: Collider-Bench establishes a rigorous, reproducible benchmark for long-horizon scientific reasoning in particle physics, highlighting current limitations of autonomous agents and guiding future improvements in tool integration, evaluation protocols, and reproducibility-focused design.

Abstract: Autonomous language-model agents are increasingly evaluated on long-horizon tool-use tasks, but existing benchmarks rarely capture the complexity and nuance of real scientific work. To address this gap, we introduce Collider-Bench, a benchmark for evaluating whether LLM agents can reproduce experimental analyses from the Large Hadron Collider (LHC) using only public papers and open scientific software. Such analyses are often difficult to reproduce because the public toolchain only approximates the software used internally by the experimental collaborations, while the published papers inevitably omit implementation details needed for a faithful reconstruction. Agents must therefore rely on physical reasoning, domain knowledge, and trial-and-error to fill these gaps. Each task requires the agent to turn a published analysis into an executable simulation-and-selection pipeline and submit predicted collision event yields in specified signal regions. These predictions are evaluated with standard histogram metrics that provide continuous fidelity scores without a hand-written rubric. We also report the computational cost incurred by each agent per task. Finally, we evaluate the codebase and full session trace using an LLM judge to catch qualitative failure modes such as fabrications, hallucinations and duplications. We release an initial set of tasks drawn from LHC searches, together with a containerized sandbox and event simulation tools. We evaluate across a capability ladder of general purpose coding agents. Our results show that on average no agent reliably beats the physicist-in-the-loop solution.

</details>


### [255] [WarmPrior: Straightening Flow-Matching Policies with Temporal Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13959)
*Sinjae Kang,Chanyoung Kim,Kaixin Wang,Li Zhao,Kimin Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Replacing the Gaussian source with WarmPrior (an action-history–driven temporally grounded prior) yields consistent gains in diffusion/flow-based visuomotor policies and prior-space RL; the source distribution strongly shapes policy performance, with straighter probability paths echoing optimal-transport couplings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify how the choice of source distribution in generative policies affects robotic control performance and explore whether a history-based prior can improve success and learning efficiency.

Method: Introduce WarmPrior as the source distribution for diffusion/flow matching. Constructed from recent action history, WarmPrior is compared against the standard Gaussian source across robotic manipulation tasks. Examine probability-path geometry (path straightness) and link to Rectified Flow. Evaluate behavior cloning and prior-space RL to assess exploration, sample efficiency, and final performance.

Result: WarmPrior consistently improves success rates in manipulation tasks; probability paths become markedly straighter, resembling optimal-transport couplings in Rectified Flow. In RL, WarmPrior reshapes exploration, boosting sample efficiency and final performance.

Conclusion: The source distribution is a critical and underexplored design axis for generative robot control. WarmPrior demonstrates that leveraging temporally grounded action history as a prior can enhance both planning-like and learning-based robot control.

Abstract: Generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching have become a dominant paradigm for visuomotor robotic control. We show that replacing the standard Gaussian source distribution with WarmPrior, a simple temporally grounded prior constructed from readily available recent action history, consistently improves success rates on robotic manipulation tasks. We trace this gain to markedly straighter probability paths, echoing the effect of optimal-transport couplings in Rectified Flow. Beyond standard behavior cloning, WarmPrior also reshapes the exploration distribution in prior-space reinforcement learning, improving both sample efficiency and final performance. Collectively, these results identify the source distribution as an important and underexplored design axis in generative robot control.

</details>


### [256] [Towards Resource-Efficient LLMs: End-to-End Energy Accounting of Distillation Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13981)
*Katherine Lambert,Sasha Luccioni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes end-to-end energy accounting framework for distillation pipelines in LLMs, revealing complete energy costs including teacher-side workloads; provides measurements for logit-based KD and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning; yields energy-quality Pareto frontiers and design rules; releases open-source harness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rising GPU demand and electricity concerns in datacenters due to large AI workloads; distillation claimed to save energy but often ignores full pipeline costs; need standardized, reproducible end-to-end energy accounting.

Method: Stage-wise GPU power measurement across distillation pipeline; log energy across phases; evaluate two distillation paradigms (logit-based KD and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning); construct energy-quality Pareto frontiers; deliver open-source measurement harness and protocol.

Result: Quantified energy and emissions for two distillation methods; identified Pareto-frontiers showing trade-offs; derived practical design rules for method selection and hyperparameters under energy budgets.

Conclusion: End-to-end energy accounting is essential for energy-aware ML deployment; the framework enables comparable, reproducible distillation studies; encourages transparent accounting of complete pipeline energy impact.

Abstract: The rise in deployment of large language models has driven a surge in GPU demand and datacenter scaling, raising concerns about electricity use, grid stress, and the impacts of modern AI workloads. Distillation is often promoted as one of the most effective paths to obtain cheaper, more efficient models, yet these claims rarely account for the full end-to-end energy and resource costs, including crucial teacher-side workloads such as data generation, logit caching, and evaluation. We present a comprehensive energy accounting framework that measures the complete computational cost of distillation pipelines via detailed stage-wise tracking of GPU device power consumption. In our experiments, we separate and log empirical energy use across distinct phases and systematically measure the energy and emissions of two common distillation methods: the classic logit-based knowledge distillation and synthetic-data supervised fine-tuning, constructing energy-quality Pareto frontiers that expose the previously ignored costs. From these measurements and analyses, we derive practical design rules for selecting distillation methods and hyperparameters under energy and budget constraints, and release an open-source measurement harness and accounting protocol to provide a standardized foundation for comparable, reproducible distillation research, explicitly accountable for complete pipeline energy impact.

</details>


### [257] [TabPFN-3: Technical Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13986)
*Léo Grinsztajn,Klemens Flöge,Oscar Key,Felix Birkel,Philipp Jund,Brendan Roof,Mihir Manium,Shi Bin,Hoo,Magnus Bühler,Anurag Garg,Dominik Safaric,Jake Robertson,Benjamin Jäger,Simone Alessi,Adrian Hayler,Vladyslav Moroshan,Lennart Purucker,Philipp Singer,Alan Arazi,Julien Siems,Jan Hendrik Metzen,Georg Grab,Nick Erickson,Siyuan Guo,Eliott Kalfon,Simon Bing,David Salinas,Clara Cornu,Lilly Charlotte Wehrhahn,Diana Kriuchkova,Kursat Kaya,Lydia Sidhoum,Marie Salmon,Jerry Chen,Madelon Hulsebos,Yann LeCun,Samuel Müller,Bernhard Schölkopf,Sauraj Gambhir,Noah Hollmann,Frank Hutter*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TabPFN-3 is a scalable tabular foundation model that achieves state-of-the-art performance and speed on large datasets (up to 1M rows) across tabular, relational, and tabular-text data, outperforming tuned baselines and AutoGluon while enabling faster inference and faster SHAP computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a need for scalable, fast, and accurate prediction on tabular data, including large-scale datasets and diverse data modalities (time-series, relational, tabular-text). Existing models struggle with training/inference efficiency and performance at scale; synthetic-data pretraining offers a controlled, scalable way to improve tabular foundation models.

Method: Develop TabPFN-3 (and TabPFN-3-Plus) trained exclusively on synthetic data from the prior TabPFN. Scale to 1M training rows with reduced KV-cache and row-chunking for fast inference. Introduce specialized checkpoints (TabPFN-TS-3) for time-series and SHAP acceleration. Evaluate on TabArena and RelBenchV1/TabSTAR, comparing to tuned/ensemble baselines and AutoGluon, and quantify Elo improvements.

Result: TabPFN-3 forward pass outperforms all other models on TabArena, with Pareto-dominant speed/performance. It ranks first on datasets with many classes and beats 8-hour-tuned GBMs on datasets up to 1M rows and 200 features. TabPFN-3-Plus beats non-TabPFN models by 200+ Elo on TabArena (420 Elo on large subsets), is 10x faster than AutoGluon 1.5 extreme, and SHAP computation is up to 120x faster. Training speed improves up to 20x over TabPFN-2.5. Scaling techniques enable 1M-row inference on a single H100 with reduced KV cache and row-chunking.

Conclusion: TabPFN-3 extends tabular foundation-model capabilities to relational data (RelBenchV1) and tabular-text data (TabSTAR), delivering SOTA performance across diverse tabular modalities. It enhances integrations with specialized checkpoints and accelerates SHAP, marking a significant advance in scalable, fast tabular AI without external data or LLMs.

Abstract: Tabular data underpins most high-value prediction problems in science and industry, and TabPFN has driven the foundation model revolution for this modality. Designed with feedback from our users, TabPFN-3 builds on this foundation to scale state-of-the-art performance to datasets with 1M training rows and substantially reduce training and inference time. Pretrained exclusively on synthetic data from our prior, TabPFN-3 dramatically pushes the frontier of tabular prediction and brings substantial gains on time series, relational, and tabular-text data. On the standard tabular benchmark TabArena, a forward pass of TabPFN-3 outperforms all other models, including tuned and ensembled baselines, by a significant margin, and pareto-dominates the speed/performance frontier. On more diverse datasets, TabPFN-3 ranks first on datasets with many classes, and beats 8-hour-tuned gradient-boosted-tree baselines on datasets up to 1M training rows and 200 features. TabPFN-3 introduces test-time compute scaling to tabular foundation models. Our API offering TabPFN-3-Plus (Thinking) exploits this to beat all non-TabPFN models by over 200 Elo on TabArena, rising to 420 Elo on the largest data subset, and outperforms AutoGluon 1.5 extreme while being 10x faster, without using LLMs, real data, internet search or any other model besides TabPFN. TabPFN-3 extends the capabilities of our models, enabling SOTA prediction on relational data (new SOTA foundation model on RelBenchV1) and tabular-text data (SOTA on TabSTAR via TabPFN-3-Plus); and improves existing integrations: a specialized checkpoint, TabPFN-TS-3, ranks 2nd on the time-series benchmark fev-bench, and SHAP-value computation is up to 120x faster. TabPFN-3 achieves this performance while being up to 20x faster than TabPFN-2.5. In addition, a reduced KV cache and row-chunking scale to 1M rows on one H100 with fast inference speed.

</details>


### [258] [Neural Fields for NV-Center Inverse Sensing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13988)
*Zhixuan Zhao,Tao Zhong,Yixun Hu,Nathalie P. de Leon,Christine Allen-Blanchette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces NeTMY, a neural inverse method using a corrected tensor dipolar forward operator for NV-center noise spectroscopy, mitigating center-collapse in density optimization and achieving superior localization in synthetic benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve inverse sensing of sparse spin sources when forward models are nonlinear and spectrally coupled; traditional scalar/coherent approximations underperform, necessitating physics-faithful, neural approaches.

Method: Replace the forward operator with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator; propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses.

Result: NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics on synthetic reconstructions using the corrected operator; mechanism experiments show the method smooths and redistributes density updates, mitigating center-collapse; NV sensing demonstrated as a fertile testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

Conclusion: Physics-faithful forward models combined with tailored neural inverse architectures can substantially improve nonlinear inverse problems; NV quantum sensing serves as a valuable benchmark for developing such methods and may generalize to broader sensing tasks.

Abstract: Inverse problems in scientific sensing are often solved with either hand-designed regularizers or supervised networks trained on simulated labels, yet both can fail when the forward model is nonlinear, spectrally coupled, and physically delicate. We study this issue for noise sensing based on nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond, where a quantum sensor measures magnetic-noise spectra generated by sparse spin sources. We show that replacing a common scalar/coherent forward approximation with a tensor power-summed dipolar operator changes the inverse landscape and exposes a center-collapse failure mode in free-density optimization. We propose NeTMY, an amortization-free coordinate neural field coupled to the differentiable NV forward model, with annealed positional encoding, multiscale optimization, sparsity/gating, and spectrum-fidelity losses. Across sparse synthetic reconstructions generated by the corrected operator, NeTMY achieves the best localization and distributional metrics in the tested benchmark. Mechanism experiments show that NeTMY does not directly execute the raw density-space gradient; its parameterization smooths and redistributes updates, mitigating the center-collapse pathology. These results position NV quantum sensing as a useful testbed for physics-faithful neural inverse problems.

</details>


### [259] [HodgeCover: Higher-Order Topological Coverage Drives Compression of Sparse Mixture-of-Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13997)
*Tao Zhong,Dongzhe Zheng,Christine Allen-Blanchette*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies a fundamental obstruction to learning-free compression of Sparse MoE layers: triple-wise mergeability can form an irreducible cycle, captured by the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex. Proposes HodgeCover, a topological compressor, which together with a hybrid pruning strategy improves aggressive compression while maintaining balanced topological mass.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable cost-effective inference for Sparse MoEs by compressing experts without retraining, by diagnosing why pairwise scoring fails and leveraging topological signals to guide compression.

Method: Model the MoE as a 2-complex: experts as vertices, KL merge barriers on edges, triplet barriers on faces. Compute the harmonic component of the edge-barrier signal via Hodge decomposition of the simplicial Laplacian. Develop HodgeCover to greedily cover harmonic-critical edges and triangles; create a hybrid variant that pairs HodgeCover with standard weight pruning on survivors.

Result: On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines for expert reduction, leads on aggressive compression in the hybrid axis, and achieves balanced retention across all four Hodge components.

Conclusion: Uncovering the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure shifts which compressor dominates in the regime that matters, demonstrating that topological diagnostics can inform robust, learning-free compression strategies for MoEs.

Abstract: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) layers route tokens through a handful of experts, and learning-free compression of these layers reduces inference cost without retraining. A subtle obstruction blocks every existing compressor in this family: three experts can each be pairwise compatible yet form an irreducible cycle when merged together, so any score that ranks experts on pairwise signals is structurally blind to which triples are jointly mergeable. We show the obstruction is a precise mathematical object, the harmonic kernel of the simplicial Laplacian on a 2-complex whose vertices are experts, whose edges carry KL merge barriers, and whose faces carry triplet barriers; Hodge-decomposing the edge-barrier signal isolates the kernel exactly. We turn the diagnostic into a selection objective: HodgeCover greedily covers the harmonic-critical edges and triplet-critical triangles, and a hybrid variant of HodgeCover pairs it with off-the-shelf weight pruning on survivors. On three open-weight Sparse MoE backbones under aggressive expert reduction, HodgeCover matches state-of-the-art learning-free baselines on the expert-reduction axis, leads on the aggressive-compression frontier of the hybrid axis, and uniquely balances retained mass across all four Hodge components. These results show that exposing the harmonic kernel of a learned MoE structure changes which compressor wins at the regime that matters most.

</details>


### [260] [Support Before Frequency in Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13999)
*Adrian Müller,Antoine Gonon,Zebang Shen,Ya-Ping Hsieh,Niao He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Discrete diffusion models for language learning organize learning hierarchically: coarse data-support (validity) information is learned before fine-grained frequencies. In uniform diffusion, reverse edits have a three-part effect (validity-improving, preserving, worsening); in absorbing diffusion, mass concentrates on validity-improving moves. Theory and experiments on masked diffusion and synthetic tasks confirm early support localization before frequency ranking, with diffusion mechanism predicting rate differences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how denoising objectives in discrete diffusion models structure learning for language data, beyond the objective targeting the full data distribution.

Method: Theoretically analyze the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps to show that each single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale (toward data support vs not) and a finer coefficient (within the same scale). Compare uniform vs absorbing diffusion (trichotomy vs mass on validity-improving moves). Validate predictions with experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks.

Result: A hierarchy is revealed: recovering validity structure (data support) requires only the order of magnitude of reverse probabilities, while recovering data frequencies requires estimating coefficients. Support localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking. Uniform diffusion shows a three-way edit classification; absorbing diffusion emphasizes validity-improving moves. Experiments corroborate the rate separation and the order of learning.

Conclusion: Discrete diffusion models appear to learn and encode data support earlier than data frequencies, with the diffusion mechanism shaping the pace and nature of this separation. This has implications for training dynamics and model design in discrete diffusion-based language modeling.

Abstract: Discrete diffusion models are increasingly competitive for language modeling, yet it remains unclear how their denoising objectives organize learning. Although these objectives target the full data distribution, we show that the exact reverse process induces a hierarchy between coarse support information and finer frequency information. For uniform and absorbing (a.k.a. masking) diffusion, we prove that, in the small-noise regime of the final denoising steps, each single-token reverse edit decomposes into a leading scale, determined by whether it moves toward the data support (e.g., grammatically valid sentences), and a finer coefficient, determining relative probabilities within the same scale. Thus, recovering validity structure only requires learning the correct order of magnitude of reverse probabilities, whereas recovering data frequencies requires coefficient-level estimation. The separation is mechanism-dependent: uniform diffusion exhibits a trichotomy into validity-improving, validity-preserving, and validity-worsening edits, while absorbing diffusion places its leading-order mass on validity-improving moves. Experiments on a masked language diffusion model and synthetic regular-language tasks support these predictions: support-localization emerges earlier than within-support frequency ranking, and the contrast between uniform and absorbing diffusion matches the predicted rate separation. Together, our results suggest that discrete diffusion models learn data support before data frequencies.

</details>


### [261] [Dywave: Event-Aligned Dynamic Tokenization for Heterogeneous IoT Sensing Signal](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14014)
*Tomoyoshi Kimura,Denizhan Kara,Jinyang Li,Hongjue Zhao,Yigong Hu,Yizhuo Chen,Xiaomin Ouyang,Shengzhong Liu,Tarek Abdelzaher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dywave proposes a dynamic tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals using wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition to create compact, semantically meaningful tokens aligned with temporal events, improving accuracy and efficiency while being robust to domain shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT sensing data are non-stationary and multi-scale; standard tokenization struggles to capture temporal semantics, leading to inefficiencies and degraded model performance.

Method: Use wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition to reveal temporal boundaries corresponding to semantic events; adaptively compress redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence.

Result: On five real-world IoT datasets (activity recognition, stress assessment, nearby object detection), Dywave improves accuracy by up to 12% and reduces input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models; shows robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

Conclusion: Dywave delivers compact, temporally coherent token representations that enhance performance and efficiency in IoT sensing tasks and facilitate robust deployment across diverse sequences.

Abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) systems continuously collect heterogeneous sensing signals from ubiquitous sensors to support intelligent applications such as human activity analysis, emotion monitoring, and environmental perception. These signals are inherently non-stationary and multi-scale, posing unique challenges for standard tokenization techniques. This paper proposes Dywave, a dynamic tokenization framework for IoT sensing signals that constructs compact input representations aligned with intrinsic temporal structures and underlying physical events. Dywave leverages wavelet-based hierarchical decomposition, identifies meaningful temporal boundaries corresponding to underlying semantic events, and adaptively compresses redundant intervals while preserving temporal coherence. Extensive evaluations on five real-world IoT sensing datasets across activity recognition, stress assessment, and nearby object detection demonstrate that Dywave outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to 12% in accuracy, while improving computational efficiency by reducing input token lengths by up to 75% across mainstream sequence models. Moreover, Dywave exhibits improved robustness to domain shifts and varying sequence lengths.

</details>


### [262] [R2R2: Robust Representation for Intensive Experience Reuse via Redundancy Reduction in Self-Predictive Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14026)
*Sanghyeob Song,Donghyeok Lee,Jinsik Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: R2R2 introduces a non-centered redundancy-reduction regularizer for Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) to combat overfitting in data-reuse RL. It resolves conflicts with SPL's spectral properties, improves SPL-based TD7 by ~22% at high Update-to-Data (UTD=20), and yields further gains when combined with SimbaV2-SPL, achieving a new state-of-the-art across 11 continuous-control tasks. Code released at GitHub.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In data-scarce RL domains, aggressive data reuse leads to overfitting. Existing work focuses on critic bias, while representation-level instability in SPL under high UTD remains underexplored. A robust, SPL-compatible representation regularizer could mitigate overfitting and enhance sample efficiency.

Method: Propose R2R2, a redundancy-reduction regularizer embedded within SPL. The authors show that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral structure and craft a non-centered objective. They validate R2R2 on SPL-native algorithms such as TD7 and extend SimbaV2 with a tailored SPL module (SimbaV2-SPL) to demonstrate orthogonality to prior work.

Result: Empirical evaluation on 11 continuous control tasks shows R2R2 reduces overfitting under high data reuse. At UTD=20, TD7 improves by ~22% with R2R2, and SimbaV2-SPL+R2R2 achieves further gains, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

Conclusion: R2R2 effectively stabilizes representations under high data reuse in SPL by adopting a non-centered redundancy-reduction objective, addressing an overlooked source of instability. The approach is compatible with existing SPL-based and SPL-augmented methods and yields notable performance gains.

Abstract: For reinforcement learning in data-scarce domains like real-world robotics, intensive data reuse enhances efficiency but induces overfitting. While prior works focus on critic bias, representation-level instability in Self-Predictive Learning (SPL) under high Update-to-Data (UTD) regimes remains underexplored. To bridge this gap, we propose Robust Representation via Redundancy Reduction (R2R2), a regularization method within SPL. We theoretically identify that standard zero-centering conflicts with SPL's spectral properties and design a non-centered objective accordingly. We verify R2R2 on SPL-native algorithms like TD7. Furthermore, to demonstrate its orthogonality to prior advancements, we extend the state-of-the-art SimbaV2, which originally lacks SPL, by integrating a tailored SPL module, termed SimbaV2-SPL. Experiments across 11 continuous control tasks confirm that R2R2 effectively mitigates overfitting; specifically, at a UTD ratio of 20, it improves TD7 by ~22% and provides additional gains on top of SimbaV2-SPL, which itself establishes a new state-of-the-art. The code can be found at: https://github.com/songsang7/R2R2

</details>


### [263] [Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention: Learning When to Write by Predicting Future Utility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14037)
*Gergely Szilvasy,Manuel Faysse,Maria Lomeli,Matthijs Douze,Pierre-Emmanuel Mazaré,Loïc Cabannes,Wen-tau Yih,Hervé Jégou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV) is a dynamic, end-to-end trainable mechanism that predicts future KV utility to prune the long-term KV cache in transformers, achieving 3–10x cache and speed improvements with minimal performance loss and revealing exploitable sparsity patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long sequences in modern transformers are bottlenecked by KV cache size and bandwidth. Reducing KV cache footprints can enable longer contexts, faster decoding, and lower memory usage without sacrificing accuracy.

Method: A lightweight per-KV utility predictor scores each key-value pair. Recent KVs remain in a local window; older ones are stored in the KV cache and are used in global attention only if their predicted utility exceeds a threshold. The LLM and predictor are trained jointly end-to-end from pretrained checkpoints using only next-token prediction loss, enabling dynamic, input-adaptive sparsification.

Result: The method typically reduces KV cache size by 3–10×, with longer sequences more compressible. This yields substantial memory and decoding speed gains while preserving validation loss and downstream task performance. It also uncovers structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns that can guide hybrid local-global attention design.

Conclusion: SP-KV demonstrates effective, dynamic KV-cache reduction with minimal performance cost and provides insights into sparsity structure that can inform future attention architectures.

Abstract: Under modern test-time compute and agentic paradigms, language models process ever-longer sequences. Efficient text generation with transformer architectures is increasingly constrained by the Key-Value cache memory footprint and bandwidth. To address this limitation, we introduce Self-Pruned Key-Value Attention (SP-KV), a mechanism designed to predict future KV utility in order to reduce the size of the long-term KV cache. This strategy operates at a fine granularity: a lightweight utility predictor scores each key-value pair, and while recent KVs are always available via a local window, older pairs are written in the cache and used in global attention only if their predicted utility surpasses a given threshold. The LLM and the utility predictor are trained jointly end-to-end exclusively through next-token prediction loss, and are adapted from pretrained LLM checkpoints.
  Rather than enforcing a fixed compression ratio, SP-KV performs dynamic sparsification: the mechanism adapts to the input and typically reduces the KV cache size by a factor of $3$ to $10\times$, longer sequences often being more compressible. This leads to vast improvements in memory usage and decoding speed, with little to no degradation of validation loss nor performance on a broad set of downstream tasks. Beyond serving as an effective KV-cache reduction mechanism, our method reveals structured layer- and head-specific sparsity patterns that we can use to guide the design of hybrid local-global attention architectures.

</details>


### [264] [Reliability-Gated Source Anchoring for Continual Test-Time Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14063)
*Vikash Singh,Debargha Ganguly,Weicong Chen,Sabyasachi Sahoo,Sreehari Sankar,Biyao Zhang,Mohsen Harir,Shouren Wang,Osama Zafar,Christian Gagné,Vipin Chaudhary*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RMemSafe is a reliability-gated CTTA extension to ROID that gates source-dependent losses with the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy, enabling graceful degradation when the source becomes unreliable and yielding robust gains across CTTA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address blind anchoring in continual test-time adaptation (CTTA): when the frozen source deteriorates, maintaining fixed anchor strength harms performance. A reliability-aware gate is needed to attenuate source-coupled objectives.

Method: Introduce RMemSafe as a reliability-gated extension of ROID. Compute the frozen source’s normalized predictive entropy and use it to attenuate explicit source-coupled terms in the CTTA objective. When entropy is high (source uncertainty), the gate closes and the objective reduces to a source-agnostic fallback (base ROID losses plus calibration). Integrate with ASR and evaluate on matched CTTA cells under degradation.

Result: RMemSafe achieves the lowest error on 8 of 9 matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all 9. It improves ROID+ASR by 1.05 percentage points on ResNet-50 and 0.48 pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled source-degradation sweep shows a 1.13× shallower harm slope than ROID+ASR, indicating graceful decay. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse rather than confidently wrong low-entropy sources; this scope is evaluated and discussed.

Conclusion: Reliability gating through predictive-entropy-based attenuation of source-coupled terms effectively mitigates blind anchoring in CTTA, enabling graceful degradation and improved performance across architectures and datasets. The entropy gate’s focus on source collapse (not mislabelled confident errors) is validated and discussed.

Abstract: Continual test-time adaptation (CTTA) updates a pretrained model online on an unlabeled, non-stationary stream while anchoring it to a frozen source checkpoint. This anchor is useful only when the source remains reliable. On CCC-Hard, however, a ResNet-50 source falls to approximately $1.3\%$ top-$1$ accuracy, while existing source-anchored CTTA methods continue applying the same anchor strength. We call this failure mode blind anchoring and propose RMemSafe, a reliability-gated extension of ROID that uses the frozen source's normalized predictive entropy to attenuate all explicit source-coupled uses in the objective. When the source posterior approaches uniformity, the gate closes: the source anchor and agreement filter vanish, and the objective reduces to a source-agnostic fallback comprising ROID's base losses plus marginal calibration. Combined with ASR, RMemSafe achieves the lowest error on $8$ of $9$ matched-split continual-corruption cells and is the best reset-based method on all $9$, improving ROID+ASR by $1.05$~pp on ResNet-50 and $0.48$~pp on ViT-B/16. A controlled source-degradation sweep shows a $1.13{\times}$ shallower harm slope than ROID+ASR, consistent with the graceful-decay prediction. The entropy gate detects high-entropy source collapse, not confidently wrong low-entropy sources; this scope is explicitly evaluated and discussed.

</details>


### [265] [SurF: A Generative Model for Multivariate Irregular Time Series Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14069)
*Mohammad R. Rezaei,Tejas Balaji,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SurF uses Time Rescaling Theorem to map irregular multivariate event streams to i.i.d. unit-rate exponential noise, enabling cross-dataset training with scalable intensity parameterizations and a Transformer encoder; achieves state-of-the-art results on six benchmarks, and shows foundation-model-like potential for asynchronous events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Irregularly sampled multivariate event streams challenge generative modeling; tokenization-based methods falter with long inter-event intervals; neural temporal point processes rely on windowed numerical quadrature and are often dataset-specific. A unified, scalable approach across heterogeneous datasets is needed.

Method: Leverages TRT as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d. unit-rate exponential noise; provides three scalable parameterizations of the cumulative intensity for long sequences; uses a Transformer encoder for multi-dataset pretraining.

Result: Empirically, SurF achieves best time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, Taobao and is within trial-level noise of the top specialist on the other three; under leave-one-out, beats all baselines on 5/6 datasets and on Amazon and Earthquake beats all baselines.

Conclusion: Demonstrates foundation-model-like potential for asynchronous event streams; scalable, cross-dataset pretraining; sets new state-of-the-art on multiple benchmarks; indicates viability of a unified generative model for irregular multivariate event streams.

Abstract: Irregularly sampled multivariate event streams remain a stubbornly difficult modality for generative modeling: tokenization-based approaches break down when inter-event intervals vary by orders of magnitude, and neural temporal point processes are bottlenecked by window-level numerical quadrature. We (i) propose SurF, a generative model that uses the Time Rescaling Theorem (TRT) as a learnable bijection between event sequences and i.i.d.\ unit-rate exponential noise, enabling a single model to be trained across heterogeneous event-stream datasets; (ii) three efficient parameterizations of the cumulative intensity that scale to long sequences; and (iii) a Transformer-based encoder for multi-dataset pretraining. On six real-world benchmarks, SurF achieves the best reported time RMSE on Earthquake, Retweet, and Taobao, and is within trial-level noise of the strongest specialist on the remaining three. Under a strict leave-one-out protocol, the held-out checkpoint beats every classical and neural-autoregressive baseline on 5/6 datasets and beats every baseline on Amazon and Earthquake, an initial step toward foundation models over asynchronous event streams.

</details>


### [266] [AttnGen: Attention-Guided Saliency Learning for Interpretable Genomic Sequence Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14073)
*Rayhaneh Shabani Nia,Ali Karkehabadi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AttnGen integrates interpretability into training via an attention-guided masking process, improving genomic sequence classification and interpretability by focusing on a compact set of informative regions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between high predictive performance and biologically meaningful sequence patterns by embedding interpretability directly into optimization.

Method: An attention mechanism computes nucleotide-level importance; during training, low-contribution positions are progressively masked to force the model to rely on informative regions; evaluated on a 200-nucleotide demo_human_or_worm benchmark; compared with a CNN baseline; perturbation analysis by removing high-saliency nucleotides.

Result: With moderate masking, AttnGen achieves 96.73% validation accuracy vs. 95.83% for the CNN baseline, with faster convergence and improved stability. Perturbation (removing high-saliency nucleotides) drops accuracy from 96.9% to near chance on 3,000 sequences. Masking 10–20% of positions yields best trade-off between performance and interpretability.

Conclusion: Attention-guided masking enhances both predictive performance and interpretability in short genomic sequences and may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling tasks.

Abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved strong performance in genomic sequence classification; however, relating their predictions to biologically meaningful sequence patterns remains challenging. In this work, we present AttnGen, an attention-guided training framework that embeds interpretability directly into the optimization process. AttnGen computes nucleotide-level importance scores using an attention mechanism and progressively suppresses low-contribution positions during training. This encourages the model to focus its predictions on a compact set of informative regions while reducing reliance on noisy sequence elements. We evaluate AttnGen on the standardized demo_human_or_worm benchmark, a binary classification task over 200-nucleotide sequences. With moderate masking, AttnGen achieves a validation accuracy of 96.73%, outperforming a conventional CNN baseline with 95.83% accuracy, while also exhibiting faster convergence and improved training stability. To assess whether the learned importance scores reflect functionally relevant signal, we conduct perturbation-based analysis by removing high-saliency nucleotides. This causes accuracy to drop from 96.9% to near chance level on a 3,000-sequence evaluation set, indicating that the model relies on a relatively small subset of informative positions. Our analysis shows that masking 10--20% of positions provides the most favorable trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability. These results suggest that attention-guided masking not only improves classification performance but also reshapes how models distribute importance across sequence positions. Although this study focuses on short genomic sequences, the proposed approach may extend to more complex interpretable sequence modeling settings.

</details>


### [267] [Fair and Calibrated Toxicity Detection with Robust Training and Abstention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14074)
*Mokshit Surana*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-axis fairness evaluation reveals complex tradeoffs: ERM can be globally well-calibrated but miscalibrated for identity groups; reweighting improves ranking yet worsens calibration gaps; Group DRO removes identity calibration disparity at the cost of global miscalibration; post-hoc methods inherit training biases; abstention introduces group-level fairness issues. A holistic, multi-axis framework is needed to assess harm beyond aggregate ranking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fairness in toxicity classification cannot be captured by a single metric like AUC or aggregate calibration. Training-time interventions (ERM, reweighting, Group DRO) interact with post-hoc safety mechanisms (temperature scaling, abstention, per-identity thresholds). Evaluating them in isolation misses critical failure modes that affect real-world harm across identity subgroups and content types.

Method: Compare three training objectives (ERM, instance-level reweighting, Group DRO) combined with calibration/post-hoc strategies: temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation metrics include subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap CIs (n=1000).

Result: (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation: ERM yields near-perfect aggregate calibration (0.013) but large subgroup miscalibration (+0.029 to +0.134). (2) Training interventions reshape disparity: Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC +0.06 to +0.12) but widens calibration gaps; Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity yet becomes globally miscalibrated (ECE ~0.118). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failures: Temperature scaling fails due to non-uniform miscalibration; confidence-based abstention works under ERM but fails under DRO, where risk-coverage curves deferral increases risk. (4) Abstention is itself unfair: confidence-based deferral benefits non-identity content more than identity-mentioning content. Conclusion: SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework since ranking improvements can accompany divergent real-world harms across calibration, abstention, and subgroups; therefore, evaluation must go beyond aggregate metrics.

Conclusion: A single-axis focus (e.g., ranking or aggregate calibration) is insufficient for safety and fairness in toxicity classification. Models and intervention choices produce distinct failure modes across identity groups and content types; designing fair systems demands explicit multi-axis evaluation, accounting for calibration across subgroups and the fairness implications of abstention.

Abstract: Fairness in toxicity classification involves three integrated axes: ranking, calibration, and abstention. Training-time interventions and post-hoc safety mechanisms cannot be evaluated independently because the former determines the efficacy of the latter. We compare Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM), instance-level reweighting, and Group DRO across these axes, combined with temperature scaling, confidence-based abstention, and per-identity threshold optimization. Evaluation uses subgroup AUC, BPSN/BNSP AUC, error gaps, and per-subgroup Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with bootstrap CIs ($n = 1000$).
  We report four findings. (1) Calibration disparity is a hidden fairness violation. ERM has near-perfect aggregate calibration ($0.013$) but is significantly miscalibrated across all identity subgroups ($+0.029$ to $+0.134$). (2) Training interventions reshape rather than eliminate disparity. Reweighted ERM improves ranking (BPSN AUC $+0.06$ to $+0.12$) but worsens the calibration-fairness gap by up to $+0.232$. Group DRO eliminates calibration disparity but only by becoming uniformly miscalibrated globally (ECE $0.118$). (3) Post-hoc methods inherit training failure modes. Temperature scaling fails because miscalibration is non-uniform. Confidence-based abstention works under ERM but breaks under DRO, where the risk-coverage curve rises with deferral. (4) Abstention itself is unfair. Confidence-based deferral helps background content far more than identity-mentioning content. We argue that SRAI fairness requires a multi-axis framework: methods that differ only in aggregate ranking can differ sharply in failure modes that determine real-world harm.

</details>


### [268] [Rethinking Layer Relevance in Large Language Models Beyond Cosine Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14075)
*Cristian Hinostroza,Rodrigo Toro Icarte,Christ Devia,Andres Carvallo De Ferari,Eugenio Herrera-Berg,Denis Parra,Jorge F Silva*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cosine similarity is a poor proxy for layer importance in LLMs; measuring the actual accuracy drop after removing a layer provides a more reliable relevance signal, albeit with higher computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Questioning the routine use of cosine similarity in mechanistic interpretability and identifying a robust, task- and model-agnostic metric for layer relevance to guide pruning and efficiency.

Method: Theoretical analysis showing possible discordance between cosine similarity and layer importance; empirical evaluation across multiple LLMs comparing cosine similarity with actual performance degradation; measuring the actual accuracy drop upon individual layer removal; advocating this as the primary metric for layer relevance.

Result: Cosine similarity often weakly to moderately correlates with true performance loss; some crucial layers have low cosine similarity; using actual accuracy drop provides a more faithful assessment of layer importance and can inform pruning strategies and lightweight model design.

Conclusion: Recommend moving beyond cosine similarity toward a direct accuracy-based measure of layer relevance; despite higher computational cost, this approach yields more accurate interpretability and can improve pruning and efficiency of LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing. Understanding their internal mechanisms is crucial for developing more interpretable and optimized architectures. Mechanistic interpretability has led to the development of various methods for assessing layer relevance, with cosine similarity being a widely used tool in the field. On this work, we demonstrate that cosine similarity is a poor proxy for the actual performance degradation caused by layer removal. Our theoretical analysis shows that a layer can exhibit an arbitrarily low cosine similarity score while still being crucial to the model's performance. On the other hand, empirical evidence from a range of LLMs confirms that the correlation between cosine similarity and actual performance degradation is often weak or moderate, leading to misleading interpretations of a transformer's internal mechanisms. We propose a more robust metric for assessing layer relevance: the actual drop in model accuracy resulting from the removal of a layer. Even though it is a computationally costly metric, this approach offers a more accurate picture of layer importance, allowing for more informed pruning strategies and lightweight models. Our findings have significant implications for the development of interpretable LLMs and highlight the need to move beyond cosine similarity in assessing layer relevance.

</details>


### [269] [Mini-JEPA Foundation Model Fleet Enables Agentic Hydrologic Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14120)
*Mashrekur Rahman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fleet of five small, sensor-specialized JEPA models (22M params each) with a shared ViT backbone and 64-d outputs are trained per sensor (Sentinel-2, Sentinel-1, MODIS, etc.). A routing LLM selects the appropriate sensor model for a query. This approach improves hydrologic signals and physics-mounded predictions compared to a single global model, while enabling modest compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of a single planetary-scale geospatial model (e.g., AlphaEarth) that may underperform on specialized hydrologic signals and is expensive to train/deploy; provide accessible, sensor-specific embeddings and a routing mechanism to handle diverse queries.

Method: Train five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs with identical Vision Transformer backbone and 64-d output space. Each model reconstructs the variable matched to its sensor from multispectral data (Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, and a topography-soil stack). Evaluate with cross-validated R^2 (0.97 for elevation; 0.97 for temperature; 0.81 for precipitation). Analyze manifold geometry with participation ratios (8.9–20.2) and local intrinsic dimensionalities (2.3–9.0). Combine topography-soil and phenology models to assess added predictive value for soil moisture, aridity, and precipitation (ΔR^2 up to 0.031). Employ a router LLM to read per-modality references and select sensors; in LLM-as-Judge setups, compare dual retrieval over AlphaEarth vs routed fleet on physics-matched questions (Cohen’s d = 1.10, p = 0.031). Note: locally-trained Mini-JEPAs are feasible with modest compute.

Result: Cross-validated R^2: elevation 0.97; temperature 0.97; precipitation 0.81. Manifolds: global participation ratios 8.9–20.2; local intrinsic dimensionalities 2.3–9.0. Added predictive value for soil moisture, aridity, precipitation (ΔR^2 up to 0.031) with joint topography-soil and phenology models. Routing achieves perfect hit rate in sensor selection. LLM-Judge evaluation shows outperformance of AlphaEarth when using the routed fleet (Cohen’s d = 1.10, p = 0.031). Local models support modest-compute hydrologic intelligence.

Conclusion: Sensor-specialized Mini-JEPAs offer an accessible, compute-efficient alternative to a single global geospatial model, with effective routing and measurable gains in hydrologic and climate-related predictions.

Abstract: Geospatial foundation models compress multispectral observations into dense embeddings increasingly used in natural-language environmental reasoning systems. A single planetary-scale model, e.g. Google AlphaEarth, handles broad characterization well but may compromise on specialized hydrologic signals. Such generalist models are also often inaccessible, expensive, and require large-scale compute. We propose Mini-JEPAs: a fleet of small sensor-specialized Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) foundation models consulted by a routing agent for specialized questions. We pretrained five 22M-parameter Mini-JEPAs sharing an identical Vision Transformer backbone, JEPA recipe, and 64-d output space, using Sentinel-2 optical, Sentinel-1 SAR, MODIS thermal, multi-temporal Sentinel-2 phenology, and a topography-soil stack. Each Mini-JEPA reconstructs the variable matched to its sensor, with cross-validated $R^2$ reaching 0.97 for elevation, 0.97 for temperature, and 0.81 for precipitation. The five manifolds differ in geometric structure, with global participation ratios from 8.9 to 20.2 and local intrinsic dimensionalities from 2.3 to 9.0. Joint topography-soil and phenology models add predictive value beyond AlphaEarth alone for soil moisture, aridity, and precipitation ($ΔR^2$ up to 0.031). A router LLM reads per-modality references and selects appropriate sensors with a perfect hit rate over a curated question set. In paired LLM-as-Judge evaluation, dual retrieval over AlphaEarth and the routed fleet outperforms AlphaEarth alone on physics-matched questions (Cohen's $d = 1.10$, $p = 0.031$). Locally-trained Mini-JEPAs can be operationalized for hydrologic intelligence with modest compute.

</details>


### [270] [Reinforcement Learning for Tool-Calling Agents in Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14126)
*Marius S. Knorr,Robert Müller,Jan P. Bremer,Nils Schweingruber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RL post-training of a CodeAct agent improves multi-turn reasoning over FHIR graphs; achieves 77% accuracy on FHIR-AgentBench with a small model, outperforming prompt-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address incorrect resource selection and violation of traversal constraints in LLM-based FHIR QA; enforce data-integrity and realistic reasoning over structured clinical graphs.

Method: Develop a multi-turn CodeAct agent trained with reinforcement learning using a custom environment and tools; use an LLM Judge to provide execution-grounded rewards; build an end-to-end post-training pipeline including environment, harness, training, and evaluation.

Result: Accuracy on FHIR-AgentBench improves from 50% to 77% using a smaller 8B-class model; outperforms prompt-based baselines; demonstrates reliable improvements in multi-turn reasoning over clinical graphs.

Conclusion: RL post-training with an execution-grounded judge improves resource traversal and reasoning over FHIR graphs, enabling effective QA over real hospital data with compact models.

Abstract: Fast Healthcare Interoperability Resources (FHIR) is the dominant standard for interoperable exchange of healthcare data. In FHIR, electronic health records form a directed graph of resources. Answering clinically meaningful questions over FHIR requires agents to perform multi-step reasoning, filtering, and aggregation across multiple resource types. Prior work shows that even tool-augmented LLM agents (retrieval, code execution, multi-turn planning) often select the wrong resources or violate traversal constraints. We study this problem in the context of FHIR-AgentBench, a benchmark for realistic question answering over real-world hospital data, and frame reasoning on FHIR as a sequential decision-making problem over a queryable structured graph. We implement a multi-turn CodeAct agent and post-train it with reinforcement learning using a custom harness and tools. A LLM Judge provides execution-grounded rewards. Compared to prompt-based, closed-model baselines, RL post-training improves performance while enforcing data-integrity constraints. Empirically, our approach improves answer correctness from 50% (o4-mini) to 77% on FHIR-AgentBench using a smaller and cheaper Qwen3-8B model. We present an end-to-end post-training pipeline (environment building, harness construction, model training and custom evaluation) that reliably improves multi-turn reasoning over structured clinical graphs.

</details>


### [271] [bde: A Python Package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles via MILE](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14146)
*Vyron Arvanitis,Angelos Aslanidis,Emanuel Sommer,David Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: bde introduces a user-friendly Python package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles on tabular data, built on a JAX implementation of Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE), offering scikit-learn compatible estimators, efficient MCMC sampling, and uncertainty quantification for regression and classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide accessible, scalable Bayesian deep ensembles for tabular data with reliable uncertainty quantification, leveraging efficient MCMC via MILE and modern auto-diff (JAX) to enable practical use.

Method: Develops bde: a Python package implementing Bayesian deep ensembles using Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE) with a JAX implementation; exposes scikit-learn compatible estimators for fast training; enables efficient MCMC sampling for uncertainty quantification in regression and classification.

Result: Claims of fast training, efficient MCMC sampling, and uncertainty quantification; API compatibility with scikit-learn; applicability to both regression and classification; no explicit empirical results reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: bde provides a practical, user-friendly tool for Bayesian deep ensembles on tabular data, combining MILE-based inference with a familiar scikit-learn API to facilitate fast training and robust uncertainty quantification.

Abstract: bde is a user-friendly Python package for Bayesian Deep Ensembles with a particular focus on tabular data. Built on an efficient JAX implementation of the sampling-based inference method Microcanonical Langevin Ensembles (MILE), it provides scikit-learn compatible estimators for fast training, efficient Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling, and uncertainty quantification in both regression and classification tasks.

</details>


### [272] [Uncovering Trajectory and Topological Signatures in Multimodal Pediatric Sleep Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14156)
*Scott Ye,Harlin Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Multimodal pediatric PSG embeddings (via a masked autoencoder) are augmented with geometry (PHATE), topology (persistent homology), movement descriptors, and EHR. Simple linear/MLP models show complementary gains; late-fusion outperforms early fusion in predicting desaturation, EEG arousal, hypopnea, and apnea, with improved calibration and robustness in imbalance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and leverage the latent geometric/topological structure of multimodal embeddings for pediatric sleep analysis, and to determine whether additional signals from geometry, topology, movement, and EHR can yield task-relevant information beyond the embeddings themselves.

Method: Embeddings of 30-second pediatric PSG epochs produced by a multimodal masked autoencoder. Augmentations include PHATE-derived per-epoch coordinates, whole-night movement descriptors, and persistent homology summaries of the embedding cloud, plus EHR features. Evaluated using simple linear and MLP models (chosen for interpretability) with early vs late fusion. Tasks: binary predictions for desaturation, EEG arousal, hypopnea, apnea. Metrics: AUPRC, Brier score, Expected Calibration Error (ECE).

Result: Late-fusion models incorporating geometry, topology, movement, and EHR achieve AUPRC gains across all four tasks (e.g., desaturation 0.26→0.34, EEG arousal 0.31→0.48, hypopnea 0.09→0.22, apnea 0.05→0.14). The full fusion model yields the best calibration across tasks (lower Brier, ECE). Geometry/topology and EHR provide complementary, interpretable signals that improve calibration and robustness under extreme class imbalance.

Conclusion: Latent geometric/topological features and EHR complement multimodal embeddings, enabling more calibrated and robust predictive signals for pediatric sleep disorders. Simple, interpretable models suffice to harvest these signals, suggesting practical avenues for clinically robust analysis and potential improvements in sleep-disorder screening.

Abstract: While generative models have shown promise in pediatric sleep analysis, the latent structure of their multimodal embeddings remains poorly understood. This work investigates session-wide diagnostic information contained in the sequences of 30-second pediatric PSG epochs embedded by a multimodal masked autoencoder. We test whether augmenting embeddings with PHATE-derived per-epoch coordinates and whole-night movement descriptors, persistent homology summaries of the embedding cloud, and EHR yields task-relevant signals. Simple linear and MLP models, chosen for interpretability rather than state-of-the-art performance, show that geometric, topological, and clinical features each provide complementary gains. For binary predictions, feature importance is task-dependent, and more expressive late-fusion models generally perform better, with AUPRC improving from 0.26 to 0.34 for desaturation, 0.31 to 0.48 for EEG arousal, 0.09 to 0.22 for hypopnea, and 0.05 to 0.14 for apnea. We also report Brier score and Expected Calibration Error, where the full fusion model yields the best calibration across all four binary tasks. Our study reveals that latent geometry/topology and EHR offer complementary, interpretable signals beyond embeddings, improving calibration and robustness under extreme imbalance.

</details>


### [273] [Finite Sample Bounds for Learning with Score Matching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14168)
*Devin Smedira,Abhijith Jayakumar,Sidhant Misra,Marc Vuffray,Andrey Y. Lokhov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Non-asymptotic sample complexity bounds for learning polynomial-exponential-family structures via score matching; polynomial dependence on dimension.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge the gap between practical score-matching learning and theory by providing finite-sample guarantees for continuous exponential families with unbounded support; address lack of non-asymptotic results.

Method: Derives finite-sample (non-asymptotic) bounds for structure learning of exponential families of polynomials using score matching; analyzes how sample size scales with dimension; shows polynomial dependence.

Result: First non-asymptotic sample complexity bounds for this problem; bounds indicate polynomial dependence on model dimension; contrasts with known asymptotic results.

Conclusion: Score matching yields tractable finite-sample guarantees for high-dimensional polynomial-exponential families; results enable reliable structure learning in practical settings.

Abstract: Learning of continuous exponential family distributions with unbounded support remains an important area of research for both theory and applications in high-dimensional statistics. In recent years, score matching has become a widely used method for learning exponential families with continuous variables due to its computational ease when compared against maximum likelihood estimation. However, theoretical understanding of the statistical properties of score matching is still lacking. In this work, we provide a non-asymptotic sample complexity analysis for learning the structure of exponential families of polynomials with score matching. The derived sample bounds show a polynomial dependence on the model dimension. These bounds are the first of its kind, as all prior work has shown only asymptotic bounds on the sample complexity.

</details>


### [274] [CSI-JEPA: Towards Foundation Representations for Ubiquitous Sensing with Minimal Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14171)
*Xuanhao Luo,Zhizhen Li,Yuchen Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CSI-JEPA is a self-supervised predictive representation learning framework for label-efficient, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing that learns reusable temporal-spectral representations by predicting masked channel regions; achieves strong accuracy gains and substantial label savings over supervised baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CSI sensing models are largely supervised and task-specific, requiring large labeled datasets for each task, device, user, or environment; unlabeled CSI is plentiful but underutilized, limiting scalability.

Method: Pretrain an encoder through self-supervised predictive representation learning by tokenizing channel-response amplitude windows along time and subcarrier dimensions, and applying a channel variation–aware masking strategy that predicts latent features of masked regions from visible context. After pretraining, freeze the encoder and attach lightweight task-specific adapters for downstream tasks.

Result: Evaluated on seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks; CSI-JEPA yields improved downstream performance over competitive baselines, including up to 10.64 percentage points mean accuracy gain over state-of-the-art supervised Transformer, and achieves up to 98.0% matched-budget label savings.

Conclusion: CSI-JEPA demonstrates scalable, label-efficient multi-task Wi-Fi sensing with transferable representations learned via self-supervised pretraining and adapter-based fine-tuning, performing robustly across diverse deployment settings.

Abstract: Channel state information (CSI) provides a widely available sensing modality for human and environment perception, but existing CSI sensing models usually rely on task-specific supervised training and require substantial labeled data for each task, device, user, or environment. This limits their scalability in practical deployments where unlabeled CSI is abundant but labeled data is costly to collect. In this paper, we present CSI-JEPA, a self-supervised predictive representation learning framework for label-efficient, multi-task Wi-Fi sensing. CSI-JEPA learns reusable temporal-spectral representations from unlabeled CSI samples by predicting latent features of masked channel regions from visible context. To better match the physical structure of CSI, CSI-JEPA tokenizes channel-response amplitude windows along the time and subcarrier dimensions. It then introduces a channel variation-aware masking strategy that samples predictive targets from regions with stronger local temporal and subcarrier-domain variations. After pretraining, the encoder is frozen and used as a backbone, with lightweight task-specific adapters added for downstream sensing tasks. We evaluate CSI-JEPA on seven real-world Wi-Fi sensing tasks spanning diverse objectives and deployment settings. The results show that CSI-JEPA improves downstream sensing performance over competitive baselines, achieving up to 10.64 percentage points mean accuracy gain over state-of-the-art supervised Transformer and matched-budget label savings of up to 98.0%.

</details>


### [275] [LLMs Know When They Know, but Do Not Act on It: A Metacognitive Harness for Test-time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14186)
*Qi Cao,Yufan Wang,Peijia Qin,Shuhao Zhang,Pengtao Xie*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A metacognitive harness converts LLM self-monitoring signals (FOK before solving and JOL after attempts) into active control of inference, improving accuracy by guiding trust, retries, and aggregation without parameter updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond passive confidence estimates by leveraging latent metacognitive signals to actively govern reasoning, inspired by Nelson–Narens theory.

Method: For each problem, the model reports a pre-solve FOK signal and a post-solve JOL signal after each attempt. The harness uses these signals to decide whether to trust the current solution, retry with compact metacognitive feedback, or pass to a final aggregator. Evaluated on a fixed Claude Sonnet-4.6 base model across text, code, and multimodal benchmarks without parameter updates or fine-tuning.

Result: Pooled accuracy on public benchmarks increased from 48.3 to 56.9. The approach surpasses the strongest listed leaderboard entries on HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V. Performance gains observed across text, code, and multimodal tasks.

Conclusion: Strong LLMs may possess latent metacognitive ability that can be harnessed via an explicit control interface to improve reasoning; this enables better decision-making about trust, retries, and aggregation without changing model parameters.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often expose useful signals of self-monitoring: before solving a problem, they can estimate whether they are likely to succeed, and after solving it, they can judge whether their answer is likely to be correct. However, these signals are typically measured or elicited in isolation, rather than used to control inference. In this work, we ask whether LLMs possess latent metacognitive ability that can be turned into effective test-time control. Inspired by the Nelson--Narens theory from cognitive psychology, we propose a metacognitive harness that separates monitoring from reasoning. For each problem, the model first reports a pre-solve feeling-of-knowing (FOK) signal; after each solve attempt, it reports a post-solve judgment-of-learning (JOL) signal. Rather than treating these signals as passive confidence estimates, the harness turns them into an explicit control interface for reasoning: it decides when to trust the current solution, when to retry with compact metacognitive feedback, and when to pass multiple attempts to a final aggregator. Across text, code, and multimodal reasoning benchmarks, our harness substantially improves a fixed Claude Sonnet-4.6 base model without parameter updates or benchmark-specific fine-tuning. On the evaluated public benchmark snapshots, it raises pooled accuracy from 48.3 to 56.9 and exceeds the strongest listed leaderboard entries on the three primary evaluation settings: HLE-Verified, LiveCodeBench v6, and R-Bench-V. These results suggest that strong LLMs may already possess useful metacognitive ability, but require an explicit control harness to act on it during reasoning.

</details>


### [276] [How to Scale Mixture-of-Experts: From muP to the Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14200)
*Leena Chennuru Vankadara,Moritz Haas,Luke Hayward,Sebastian Bordt,Alessandro Breccia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Develops DMFT-based scaling laws for MoE networks across three regimes, introduces Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) that overcomes μP's instability, and provides a complete scaling prescription for width, depth, expert width, and number of experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of principled hyperparameter scaling guidelines for Mixture-of-Experts models as network width, expert width, number of experts, sparsity, and depth grow.

Method: Three scaling regimes are analyzed via Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) to model limiting training dynamics. Derives a unique SGD/Adam parameterization under maximal-update desiderata (μ). Identifies scale-dependent aggregation issues, motivates maximal scale stability, and derives MSSP for SGD and Adam. Provides a separate DMFT characterization of MSSP dynamics and validates with experiments.

Result: μP does not guarantee monotonic improvement with scale or robust learning-rate transfer due to scale-dependent aggregation dynamics. MSSP restores monotonic scaling benefits and rate transfer across regimes; DMFT reveals distinct dynamics under MSSP. Together with existing depth-scaling theory, MSSP yields a complete scaling prescription for MoEs in terms of width, depth, N_e, M, and K.

Conclusion: MSSP provides a robust, scale-stable parameterization for MoEs across three scaling regimes, enabling reliable learning-rate transfer and monotonic performance gains with scale and offering a complete, principled scaling framework.

Abstract: Recent frontier large language models predominantly rely on Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Despite empirical progress, there is still no principled understanding of how hyperparameters should scale with network width $N$, expert width $N_e$, number of experts $M$, sparsity $K$, and depth $L$ to ensure both stability and optimal performance at scale. We take a principled step toward resolving this gap by analyzing three different scaling regimes: (I) co-scaling $N\asymp N_e$, (II) co-scaling $N\asymp M\asymp K$, and (III) full proportional scaling of $N, N_e, M$, and $K$. For each regime, we develop a novel Dynamical Mean Field Theory (DMFT) description of the limiting training dynamics of MoEs that provides a formal foundation for our analysis. Within this framework, we derive the unique parameterization for SGD and Adam satisfying all maximal-update ($μ$) desiderata. We then show that the resulting $μ$P prescription does not reliably induce monotonic improvement with scale or robust learning-rate transfer. We trace these pathologies to scale-dependent observables in the aggregation dynamics, which motivates a refined set of desiderata that we term maximal scale stability. Guided by this principle, we derive a Maximally Scale-Stable Parameterization (MSSP) for both SGD and Adam in all three scaling regimes, and characterize the corresponding limiting dynamics - qualitatively distinct from the $μ$P limit - through a separate DMFT analysis. Experiments verify that MSSP robustly recovers learning rate transfer and monotonic improvement with scale across regimes. Combined with existing depth-scaling theory, these results provide a complete scaling prescription for MoE architectures as a function of width, depth, expert width, and number of experts.

</details>


### [277] [Slot-MPC: Goal-Conditioned Model Predictive Control with Object-Centric Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14937)
*Jonathan Spieler,Angel Villar-Corrales,Sven Behnke*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Slot-MPC is an object-centric world model that enables planning via gradient-based MPC, improving generalization and planning efficiency in unseen robotic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reactive policies fixed at inference time limit generalization; object-centric representations can model scene dynamics and support planning to adapt to novel situations; gradient-based MPC offers computational efficiency over sampling-based methods.

Method: Use vision encoders to learn slot-based object representations; train an action-conditioned, object-centric dynamics model; at inference, perform Model Predictive Control using the differentiable dynamics for gradient-based action optimization.

Result: In simulated robotic manipulation tasks, Slot-MPC improves task performance and planning efficiency over non-object-centric baselines; in offline settings with limited state-action coverage, gradient-based MPC outperforms gradient-free MPC.

Conclusion: Explicitly structured, object-centric representations provide a strong inductive bias for controllable and generalizable decision-making; code and results are available at the project URL.

Abstract: Predictive world models enable agents to model scene dynamics and reason about the consequences of their actions. Inspired by human perception, object-centric world models capture scene dynamics using object-level representations, which can be used for downstream applications such as action planning. However, most object-centric world models and reinforcement learning (RL) approaches learn reactive policies that are fixed at inference time, limiting generalization to novel situations. We propose Slot-MPC, an object-centric world modeling framework that enables planning through Model Predictive Control (MPC). Slot-MPC leverages vision encoders to learn slot-based representations, which encode individual objects in the scene, and uses these structured representations to learn an action-conditioned object-centric dynamics model. At inference time, the learned dynamics model enables action planning via MPC, allowing agents to adapt to previously unseen situations. Since the learned world model is differentiable, we can use gradient-based MPC to directly optimize actions, which is computationally more efficient than relying on gradient-free, sampling-based MPC methods. Experiments on simulated robotic manipulation tasks show that Slot-MPC improves both task performance and planning efficiency compared to non-object-centric world model baselines.
