In the considered offline setting with limited state-action coverage, we find that gradient-based MPC performs better than gradient-free, sampling-based MPC. Our results demonstrate that explicitly structured, object-centric representations provide a strong inductive bias for controllable and generalizable decision-making. Code and additional results are available at https://slot-mpc.github.io.

</details>


### [278] [Towards Fine-Grained and Verifiable Concept Bottleneck Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14210)
*Yingying Fang,Haijie Xu,Shuang Wu,Mariathasan Anish,Guang Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A fine-grained, groundable Concept Bottleneck Model (CBM) that localizes and verifies concept evidence, improving transparency and reliability while maintaining competitive predictive performance on medical imaging benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the verifiability gap in standard CBMs by ensuring each predicted concept is grounded in localized visual evidence and is evidence-correct, not just correlated, thereby increasing trust and clinical actionability.

Method: Propose a fine-grained CBM framework that grounds each concept in localized visual evidence. The concept space is designed to be information-complete, enabling direct inspection of what concepts are encoded and where. System validates both presence and correctness of concept representations, not just post-hoc attributions. Evaluate on medical imaging benchmarks.

Result: Predictive performance is comparable to standard CBMs; the learned concept space is information-complete; substantial transparency gains through direct grounding and verifiability of concepts; framework bridges interpretability with verifiability.

Conclusion: This approach enhances trustworthiness and enables principled human-model interaction at the concept level, advancing reliable and clinically actionable concept-based learning systems, and offering a principled alternative to post-hoc attribution methods.

Abstract: Concept Bottleneck Models (CBMs) offer interpretable alternatives to black-box predictors by introducing human-relatable concepts before the final output. However, existing CBMs struggle to verify whether predicted concepts correspond to the correct visual evidence, limiting their reliability. We propose a fine-grained CBM framework that grounds each concept in localized visual evidence, enabling direct inspection of where and how concepts are encoded. This design allows users to interpret predictions and verify that the model learns intended concepts rather than spurious correlations. Experiments on medical imaging benchmarks show that our learned concept space is information-complete and achieves predictive performance comparable to standard CBMs, while substantially improving transparency. Unlike post-hoc attribution methods, our framework validates both the presence and correctness of concept representations, bridging interpretability with verifiability. Our approach enhances the trustworthiness of CBMs and establishes a principled mechanism for human-model interaction at the concept level, paving the way toward more reliable and clinically actionable concept-based learning systems.

</details>


### [279] [PreFT: Prefill-only finetuning for efficient inference](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14217)
*Andrew Lanpouthakoun,Aryaman Arora,Zhengxuan Wu,Dhruv Pai,Ben Keigwin,Dan Jurafsky,Christopher Potts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Prefill-only finetuning (PreFT) applies adapters only during the prefill phase, boosting multi-adapter throughput with minimal performance loss; implemented for LoRA and ReFT on vLLM; achieves up to ~1.9x higher throughput with 512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B; RL tasks near parity with standard PEFTs, SFT shows higher loss but offset by larger rank.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address a throughput bottleneck in serving many personalized adapters: a mismatch between prefill (bulk token processing) and decode (autoregressive token-by-token generation) makes multi-adapter serving inefficient. Shift focus from parameter-count efficiency to throughput efficiency.

Method: Introduce PreFT (prefill-only finetuning) where adapters are applied only to prefill tokens and discarded during decoding. Implement two prefill-only PEFTs, LoRA and ReFT, on the vLLM inference engine. Evaluate throughput for multi-user serving (512 adapters on Llama 3.1 70B) and compare prefill-only vs all-token adapters across supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning tasks, across varying model scales.

Result: PreFTs yield significantly higher throughput than traditional PEFTs (1.9x with 512 adapters on the specified setup). Performance impact is task-dependent: supervised fine-tuning sees higher eval loss with PreFT but can be mitigated by increasing adapter rank with negligible throughput penalty; reinforcement learning results show PreFTs approaching parity with standard PEFTs.

Conclusion: Prefill-only adaptation offers a superior accuracy-throughput tradeoff for multi-adapter personalized serving, enabling scalable deployment of many adapters with limited performance degradation; practical and effective across tasks, though task-model tradeoffs exist and rank tuning is important.

Abstract: Large language models can now be personalised efficiently at scale using parameter efficient finetuning methods (PEFTs), but serving user-specific PEFTs harms throughput, even with specialised kernels and memory management techniques. This is because, theoretically and empirically, a mismatch exists between prefill (processing a large number of tokens at once) and decode (generating a single token autoregressively): the latter has far lower throughput when serving multiple adapters. Rather than optimising performance relative to parameter count, for efficient multi-adapter serving, we instead ought to optimise performance relative to serving throughput. We therefore propose PreFT (Prefill-only Finetuning), wherein we only apply the adapter to prefill tokens and discard it afterwards. PreFT significantly increases throughput with minimal effect on performance. We develop and release an efficient implementation of two prefill-only PEFTs, LoRA and ReFT, on the vLLM inference engine. We first show that serving multi-user PreFTs is more efficient than traditional PEFTs ($1.9\times$ the throughput when serving $512$ adapters on Llama 3.1 70B). Then, we compare the performance of prefill-only vs. all-token adapters on a variety of supervised finetuning and reinforcement learning tasks with LMs at varying scales. On SFT, we observe that the evaluation loss of PreFTs is higher than PEFTs, but can be compensated by increasing rank with nearly no reduction in throughput. On RL, we consistently find that PreFTs approach parity with standard PEFTs. Together, this work validates prefill-only adaptation of LLMs as a more favourable accuracy-throughput tradeoff than existing PEFTs for personalised serving.

</details>


### [280] [Diagnosing Training Inference Mismatch in LLM Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14220)
*Tianle Zhong,Neiwen Ling,Yifan Pi,Zijun Wei,Tianshu Yu,Geoffrey Fox,Peng Wu,Xiao Yu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM) arises from token-probability discrepancies between rollout generation and policy optimization in LLM RL, which can cause training collapse; VeXact isolates TIM; TIM is a systemic perturbation, not mere numerical noise, with remedies proposed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM RL pipelines assume identical token probabilities across rollout and optimization, but practical implementation differences create TIM. Understanding TIM is essential for diagnosing stability and informing mitigation strategies.

Method: Introduce VeXact, a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting, to isolate TIM; demonstrate that small token-level numerical disagreements independently trigger training collapse; show TIM alters the effective optimization problem; propose a set of remedies to mitigate TIM.

Result: TIM can induce training collapse even from tiny numerical disagreements; TIM changes the effective optimization problem; a set of remedies is identified to mitigate TIM; TIM should be treated as a first-order, systems-level perturbation affecting LLM RL stability.

Conclusion: TIM is a non-benign, systemic perturbation that must be considered a first-order factor in LLM RL stability analyses, rather than mere numerical noise; addressing TIM is necessary for robust RL-based LLM systems.

Abstract: Modern LLM RL systems separate rollout generation from policy optimization. These two stages are expected to produce token probabilities that match exactly. However, implementation differences can make them assign different values to the same sequence under the same model weights, inducing Training-Inference Mismatch (TIM). TIM is difficult to inspect because it is entangled with off-policy drift and common stabilization mechanisms. In this work, we isolate TIM in a zero-mismatch diagnostic setting (VeXact), and show that small token-level numerical disagreements can independently cause training collapse. We further show that TIM changes the effective optimization problem, and identify a set of remedies that could mitigate TIM. Our results suggest that TIM is not benign numerical noise, but a systems-level perturbation that should be treated as a first-order factor in analyzing LLM RL stability.

</details>


### [281] [DT-Transformer: A Foundation Model for Disease Trajectory Prediction on a Real-world Health System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14227)
*Yunying Zhu,Andrew R Weckstein,Kueiyu Joshua Lin,Jie Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DT-Transformer: a foundation model for disease trajectory prediction trained on ~57M EHR entries from Mass General Brigham, achieving strong multi-disease next-event prediction (median AUC 0.871).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridges gap between research cohorts/single-hospital data and real-world deployment; leverages large multi-hospital EHRs to capture complex patient trajectories and improve generalization.

Method: Train a DT-Transformer on 57.1M structured EHR entries from 1.7M patients across 11 hospitals; evaluate next-event prediction across 896 disease categories using held-out and prospective validation; report age- and sex-stratified AUC.

Result: Median AUC across 896 diseases: 0.871; all categories > 0.5; demonstrates strong discrimination and real-world applicability; supports health system-scale foundation models.

Conclusion: Health system-scale training is a viable path toward foundation models suited for real-world clinical forecasting.

Abstract: Accurate disease trajectory prediction is critical for early intervention, resource allocation, and improving long-term outcomes. While electronic health records (EHRs) provide a rich longitudinal view of patient health in clinical environments, models trained on curated research cohorts may not reflect routine deployment settings, and those trained on single-hospital datasets capture only fragments of each patient's trajectory. This highlights the importance of leveraging large, multi-hospital health systems for training and validation to better reflect real-world clinical complexity. In this work, we develop DT-Transformer, a foundation model trained on 57.1M structured EHR entries over 1.7M patients from Mass General Brigham (MGB), spanning 11 hospitals and a broad network of outpatient clinics. DT-Transformer achieves strong discrimination in both held-out and prospective validation settings. Next-event prediction achieves a median age- and sex-stratified AUC of 0.871 across 896 disease categories, with all categories exceeding AUC 0.5. These results support health system-scale training as a path toward foundation models suited to real-world clinical forecasting.

</details>


### [282] [AudioMosaic: Contrastive Masked Audio Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14231)
*Hanxun Huang,Qizhou Wang,Xingjun Ma,Cihang Xie,Christopher Leckie,Sarah Erfani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A contrastive self-supervised audio encoder, AudioMosaic, uses structured time-frequency masking on spectrogram patches to form positive pairs, enabling memory-efficient large-batch training and superior utterance-level representations with strong transferability; achieves state-of-the-art results on standard benchmarks and benefits audio-language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address limitations of generative SSL in audio and the high memory/batch requirements of contrastive methods by designing an augmentation that yields effective positive pairs while reducing memory usage, enabling scalable contrastive pre-training for general audio understanding.

Method: AudioMosaic performs contrastive pre-training by applying structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches to create positive pairs. This masking reduces memory usage and supports efficient large-batch training. The approach emphasizes utterance-level discriminative representations and demonstrates transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions; integration into audio-language models further boosts performance.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple standard audio benchmarks under both linear probing and fine-tuning. Demonstrates strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. The pretrained encoder improves performance when integrated into audio-language models. Code is publicly available.

Conclusion: Structured masking-based contrastive SSL yields discriminative utterance-level representations with efficient training, offering strong generalization for audio understanding and beneficial cross-modal transfer when integrated with audio-language systems.

Abstract: Audio self-supervised learning (SSL) aims to learn general-purpose representations from large-scale unlabeled audio data. While recent advances have been driven mainly by generative reconstruction objectives, contrastive approaches remain less explored, partly due to the difficulty of designing effective audio augmentations and the large batch sizes required for contrastive pre-training. We introduce \textbf{AudioMosaic}, a contrastive learning-based audio encoder for general audio understanding. During pre-training, AudioMosaic constructs positive pairs by applying structured time-frequency masking to spectrogram patches, which reduces memory usage and enables efficient large-batch training. Compared with generative approaches, the AudioMosaic encoder learns more discriminative utterance-level representations that demonstrate strong transferability across datasets, domains, and acoustic conditions. Extensive experiments show that AudioMosaic achieves state-of-the-art performance on several standard audio benchmarks under both linear probing and fine-tuning. We further show that integrating the pretrained AudioMosaic encoder into audio-language models improves performance on audio-language tasks. The code is publicly available in our \href{https://github.com/HanxunH/AudioMosaic}{GitHub repository}.

</details>


### [283] [Quantum Advantage in Multi Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14235)
*Simranjeet Singh Dahia,Claudia Szabo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entangled quantum policies in MARL can yield quantum advantage: entangled VQC actors outperform classical baselines in CHSH and CoopNav, while unentangled quantum circuits do not; entanglement structure critically affects performance; hybrid quantum-classical setups can outperform both fully classical and fully quantum approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide rigorous baselines for quantum MARL and isolate genuine quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidences; to understand the role of entanglement in multi-agent coordination and how entanglement structure impacts performance across tasks.

Method: Decentralized QMARL with variational quantum circuit actors sharing entangled states. Evaluations include the CHSH game (classical win-rate ceiling 0.75, Tsirelson limit 0.854) and CoopNav. Comparisons across: entangled QMARL, unentangled quantum circuits, classical baselines, and a hybrid quantum actor with classical centralized critic.

Result: Entangled QMARL agents approach Tsirelson limit in CHSH, demonstrating quantum advantage; unentangled circuits align with classical baselines; entanglement structure matters with some Bell states enhancing and others hindering performance; in CoopNav, entanglement-free QMARL yields ~2× improvement over classical MAA2C (0.85 vs 0.40), and a hybrid quantum-classical configuration outperforms both fully classical and fully quantum solutions.

Conclusion: Entanglement is the active coordination mechanism in these QMARL settings. The specific entanglement structure critically influences performance, suggesting careful design of entanglement resources. Future work should examine scalability, robustness, and broader task domains, as well as systematic entanglement engineering for MARL.

Abstract: We present an empirical evaluation of quantum entanglement in agent coordination within quantum multi agent reinforcement learning (QMARL). While QMARL has attracted growing interest recently, most prior work evaluates quantum policies without provable baselines, making it impossible to rigorously distinguish quantum advantage from algorithmic coincidence. We address this directly by evaluating a decentralized QMARL framework with variational quantum circuit (VQC) actors with shared entangled states. In the CHSH game, which has a mathematically proven classical performance ceiling of 0.75 win rate, we show that entangled QMARL agents approach the Tsirelson limit of 0.854, providing clear evidence of their quantum advantage. We show that unentangled quantum circuits match the classical baseline, confirming that entanglement and not the quantum circuit itself is the active coordination mechanism. We also explore the effect of specific entanglement structures, as some Bell states enable coordination gains while others actively harm performance. On cooperative navigation (CoopNav), QMARL without entanglement achieves $\sim2\times$ improvement in success rate over classical MAA2C ($\sim$0.85 versus $\sim$0.40), with the hybrid configuration, quantum actor paired with a classical centralised critic, outperforming both fully classical and fully quantum solutions. We present our experimental analysis and discuss future work.

</details>


### [284] [Active Learners as Efficient PRP Rerankers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14236)
*Jeremías Figueiredo Paschmann,Juan Kaplan,Francisco Nattero Santiago Mauricio Barron Bucolo,Juan Wisznia,Luciano del Corro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Active learning for ranking from noisy pairwise judgments improves top-K ranking under budget; introduces a one-shot randomized-direction oracle to convert position bias into zero-mean noise, enabling unbiased aggregation without bidirectional LLM calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pairwise prompting (PRP) yields noisy, order-sensitive, sometimes intransitive judgments. Sorting a full permutation is unreliable when only a top-K is needed under call budgets.

Method: Reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons. Propose active rankers as drop-in replacements for sorting. Introduce a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair to mitigate position bias.

Result: Active rankers improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime and enable unbiased aggregate ranking without needing bidirectional calls.

Conclusion: A noise-robust, budget-aware ranking framework: active rankers with a randomized-direction oracle yield better top-K rankings with fewer LLM calls, reducing cost while maintaining accuracy.

Abstract: Pairwise Ranking Prompting (PRP) elicits pairwise preference judgments from an LLM, which are then aggregated into a ranking, usually via classical sorting algorithms. However, judgments are noisy, order-sensitive, and sometimes intransitive, so sorting assumptions do not match the setting. Because sorting aims to recover a full permutation, truncating it to meet a call budget does not produce a dependable top-K. We thus reframe PRP reranking as active learning from noisy pairwise comparisons and show that active rankers are drop-in replacements that improve NDCG@10 per call in the call-constrained regime. Our noise-robust framework also introduces a randomized-direction oracle that uses a single LLM call per pair. This approach converts systematic position bias into zero-mean noise, enabling unbiased aggregate ranking without the cost of bidirectional calls.

</details>


### [285] [Paraphrasing Attack Resilience of Various AI-Generated Text Detection Methods](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14240)
*Andrii Shportko,Inessa Verbitsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Paraphrase-robustness of AI-generated text detectors is studied across three approaches (fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis) and their Random Forest ensembles; Binoculars ensembles perform best but are most vulnerable to paraphrasing attacks, highlighting a trade-off between detection performance and resilience.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs proliferate, detecting machine-generated text becomes harder due to paraphrase and detector-bypassing tools; understanding the resilience of detectors under adversarial paraphrasing informs robust defense design.

Method: Evaluate three detector methods—fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis—along with ensembles using Random Forests. Assess performance and robustness against paraphrase attacks; compare individual methods versus ensembles.

Result: Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest detection performance but incur the largest losses under paraphrase attacks; overall, there is a clear trade-off between detection accuracy and resilience.

Conclusion: There is a performance–resilience dichotomy in AI text detection, complicating assessment of detector reliability under adversarial conditions, and suggesting that robust detector design should balance accuracy with resistance to paraphrase-based evasion.

Abstract: The recent large-scale emergence of LLMs has left an open space for dealing with their consequences, such as plagiarism or the spread of false information on the Internet. Coupling this with the rise of AI detector bypassing tools, reliable machine-generated text detection is in increasingly high demand. We investigate the paraphrasing attack resilience of various machine-generated text detection methods, evaluating three approaches: fine-tuned RoBERTa, Binoculars, and text feature analysis, along with their ensembles using Random Forest classifiers. We discovered that Binoculars-inclusive ensembles yield the strongest results, but they also suffer the most significant losses during attacks. In this paper, we present the dichotomy of performance versus resilience in the world of AI text detection, which complicates the current perception of reliability among state-of-the-art techniques.

</details>


### [286] [Latency-Quality Routing for Functionally Equivalent Tools in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14241)
*Kexin Chu,Dawei Xiang,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes LQM-ContextRoute, a capacity-aware contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers, ranking providers by expected answer quality per service cycle to improve downstream metrics under heterogeneous latency and quality, outperforming SW-UCB.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address provider-routing under runtime load where tools with the same function (web-search, retrievers, LLM backends) differ in latency, reliability, and answer quality, and where deployment-time gold labels are unavailable; traditional additive-reward bandits misweight latency against quality.

Method: Introduce LQM-ContextRoute, a contextual bandit router that uses latency-quality matching to rank providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines a capacity-aware score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback to adapt online to load changes and provider-quality differences across providers.

Result: On main web-search load: F1 +2.18 percentage points over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In high-heterogeneity StrategyQA: accuracy up to +18 pp over SW-UCB (avoiding additive-reward collapse). For heterogeneous retriever pools: NDCG +2.91 to +3.22 pp over SW-UCB.

Conclusion: Treating latency as service capacity yields better same-function tool routing, especially under runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity."

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents increasingly access the same tool type through multiple functionally equivalent providers, such as web-search APIs, retrievers, or LLM backends exposed behind a shared interface. This creates a provider-routing problem under runtime load: the router must choose among providers that differ in latency, reliability, and answer quality, often without gold labels at deployment time. We introduce LQM-ContextRoute, a contextual bandit router for same-function tool providers. Its key design is latency-quality matching: instead of letting low latency offset poor answers in an additive reward, the router ranks providers by expected answer quality per service cycle. It combines this capacity-aware score with query-specific quality estimation and LLM-as-judge feedback, allowing it to adapt online to both load changes and provider-quality differences. On the main web-search load benchmark, LQM-ContextRoute improves F1 by +2.18 pp over SW-UCB while staying on the latency-quality frontier. In a high-heterogeneity StrategyQA setting, LQM-ContextRoute avoids additive-reward collapse and improves accuracy by up to +18 pp over SW-UCB; on heterogeneous retriever pools, it improves NDCG by +2.91--+3.22 pp over SW-UCB. These results show that same-function tool routing benefits from treating latency as service capacity, especially when runtime pressure and provider-quality heterogeneity coexist.

</details>


### [287] [Artificial Intelligence-Assistant Cardiotocography: Unified Model for Signal Reconstruction, Fetal Heart Rate Analysis, and Variability Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14242)
*Xiaohua Wang,Kai Yu,XuXiao Liang,Liang Wang,Chao Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AI-based fetal heart rate monitoring model (FHrCTG) with noise-mitigating signal reconstruction, pretrained on a large unlabeled dataset and refined with expert-labeled data; introduces Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) to frame analysis as categorical judgments; achieves high diagnostic metrics for decelerations/accelerations and substantial AUCs for periodicity/amplitude variation, indicating strong clinical potential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional FHR monitoring faces equipment limitations, data transmission issues, and subjective interpretation, leading to variable assessments and potential fetal risk; there is a need for robust, automated, objective, and scalable FHR analysis.

Method: Developed a tailored FHrCTG AI model with noise suppression and precise signal reconstruction; pre-trained on 558,412 unlabeled data points and fine-tuned on 7,266 expert-reviewed entries; introduced Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) to convert rate analysis into categorical judgments; evaluated performance on decelerations, accelerations, periodicity, and amplitude variation.

Result: Decelerations: sensitivity 89.13%, specificity 87.78%. Accelerations: sensitivity 62.5%, specificity 92.04%. AUC for periodicity 0.7214; AUC for amplitude variation 0.9643, per Fischer's criteria.

Conclusion: The FHrCTG framework demonstrates robust noise-resilient signal reconstruction and high diagnostic performance, with a clinically meaningful categorization approach (IOL) and strong validation metrics, suggesting promising clinical applicability.

Abstract: The monitoring of fetal heart rate (FHR) and the assessment of its variability are crucial for preventing fetal compromise and adverse outcomes. However, traditional methods encounter limitations arising from equipment performance, data transmission, and subjective assessments by doctors. We have developed a tailored AI-based FHrCTG model specifically for FHR monitoring, which effectively mitigates noise interference and precisely reconstructs signals. Our model was pre-trained on a massive dataset consisting of 558,412 unlabeled data points and further refined using 7,266 expert-reviewed entries. To validate FHR, we introduced the Intersection Overlapping Labels (IOL) approach, which transforms rate analysis into categorical judgments. Testing revealed that our model demonstrates high sensitivity and specificity in detecting critical FHR decelerations (89.13% and 87.78%, respectively) and accelerations (62.5% and 92.04%, respectively). Furthermore, based on Fischer's criteria for clinical application, our model achieved impressive AUC scores of 0.7214 and 0.9643 for verifying FHR periodicity and amplitude variation, respectively.

</details>


### [288] [Action-Conditioned Risk Gating for Safety-Critical Control under Partial Observability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14246)
*Yushen Liu,Yin-Jen Chen,Ziyi Chen,Tao Wang,Heng Huang,Xugui Zhou,Yanfu Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight risk-gated RL method for risk-sensitive control under partial observability that uses a finite-history proxy state and an action-conditioned near-term risk predictor, applied as a penalty during learning and as a decision-time risk gate to interpolate ensemble value estimates, with improved performance and runtime on glucose regulation and Safety-Gym navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Belief-space planning for risk-sensitive POMDPs is often computationally expensive and sensitive to model misspecification. A lightweight approximation that leverages near-term risk can provide practical, robust decisions.

Method: Constructs a compact finite-history proxy state; trains an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violations. Uses predicted risk as (i) a penalty in value learning and (ii) a gate to interpolate between optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates at decision time, biasing evaluation toward low-risk actions while preserving reward-seeking behavior for low-risk actions.

Result: Empirically improves glycemic tradeoffs and reduces runtime relative to belief-space planning in automated glucose regulation; achieves better reward-cost balance on Safety-Gym benchmarks than unconstrained RL and standard safe-RL baselines across adult and adolescent cohorts.

Conclusion: Action-conditioned near-term risk signals can provide effective local guidance for approximate risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical.

Abstract: Many safety-critical control problems are modeled as risk-sensitive partially observable Markov decision processes, where the controller must make decisions from incomplete observations while balancing task performance against safety risk. Although belief-space planning provides a principled solution, maintaining and planning over beliefs can be computationally costly and sensitive to model specification in practical domains. We propose a lightweight risk-gated reinforcement learning approximation for risk-sensitive control under partial observability. The method constructs a compact finite-history proxy state and learns an action-conditioned predictor of near-term safety violation. This predicted candidate-action risk is used in two complementary ways: as a risk penalty during value learning, and as a decision-time gate that interpolates between optimistic and conservative ensemble value estimates. As a result, low-risk actions are evaluated closer to reward-seeking estimates, while high-risk actions are evaluated more conservatively. We evaluate the approach in two safety-critical partially observable domains: automated glucose regulation and safety-constrained navigation. Across adult and adolescent glucose-control cohorts, the method improves overall glycemic tradeoffs and substantially reduces runtime relative to a belief-space planning baseline. On Safety-Gym navigation benchmarks, it achieves a more favorable reward-cost balance than unconstrained RL and several standard safe-RL baselines. These results suggest that action-conditioned near-term risk can provide an effective local signal for approximate risk-sensitive POMDP control when full belief-space planning is impractical.

</details>


### [289] [Not All Timesteps Matter Equally: Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation for Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14252)
*Kai Sun,Peibo Duan,Yongsheng Huang,Guowei Zhang,Benjamin Smith,Nanxu Gong,Levin Kuhlmann*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeAl-KD introduces selective alignment for SNN knowledge distillation, targeting erroneous timesteps and weighting temporal alignment by confidence and inter-timestep similarity, yielding consistent gains over standard KD on static and event-based datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To close the performance gap between SNNs and ANNs by addressing the assumption that all timesteps should be equally supervised in KD; SNN outputs vary over time and not all intermediate steps need to be correct; thus selective supervision can improve distillation.

Method: Compute selective supervision signals: align class-level logits at erroneous timesteps; reweight temporal alignment by a confidence estimate and similarity across adjacent timesteps; perform KD using these selective targets to guide temporal dynamics while preserving useful temporal structure.

Result: Empirical evaluation on static image and neuromorphic datasets shows consistent improvements over existing KD methods; code provided at the linked repository.

Conclusion: Selective alignment KD effectively leverages useful temporal dynamics and avoids forcing uniform per-timestep targets, enhancing SNN performance with KD.

Abstract: Spiking neural networks (SNNs), which are brain-inspired and spike-driven, achieve high energy efficiency. However, a performance gap between SNNs and artificial neural networks (ANNs) still remains. Knowledge distillation (KD) is commonly adopted to improve SNN performance, but existing methods typically enforce uniform alignment across all timesteps, either from a teacher network or through inter-temporal self-distillation, implicitly assuming that per-timestep predictions should be treated equally. In practice, SNN predictions vary and evolve over time, and intermediate timesteps need not all be individually correct even when the final aggregated output is correct. Under such conditions, effective distillation should not force every timestep toward the same supervision target, but instead provide corrective guidance to erroneous timesteps while preserving useful temporal dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Selective Alignment Knowledge Distillation (SeAl-KD), which selectively aligns class-level and temporal knowledge by equalizing competing logits at erroneous timesteps and reweighting temporal alignment based on confidence and inter-timestep similarity. Extensive experiments on static image and neuromorphic event-based datasets demonstrate consistent improvements over existing distillation methods. The code is available at https://github.com/KaiSUN1/SeAl

</details>


### [290] [Architecture-Aware Explanation Auditing for Industrial Visual Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14255)
*Sibo Jia,Zihang Zhao,Kunrong Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Architecture-aware explanation audit protocol shows that faithfulness is bounded by a model's native readout structure rather than architecture family. Perturbation-based evaluation (three-seed zero-fill) on WM-811K reveals native explanations lag behind model-agnostic RISE; results and rankings depend on perturbation and dataset; co-design explanations with readout structure and report quantitative faithfulness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Heatmap explanations in industrial visual inspection can look plausible but fail to reflect actual decision regions. There is a need for a formal, architecture-aware audit that ties explanation faithfulness to the model's native decision mechanism.

Method: Propose a perturbation-based audit anchored in the native-readout hypothesis. Use a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol to measure Deletion AUC of various explainers across multiple architectures (ViT-Tiny with Attention Rollout; Swin-Tiny; ResNet18+CBAM; DenseNet121+Grad-CAM) on WM-811K (9 classes, 172k images). Compare with model-agnostic RISE. Assess impact of readout structure vs architecture family, perturbation baselines, and dataset/task (MVTec AD for boundary conditions). Include a blur-fill sensitivity analysis.

Result: ViT-Tiny + Attention Rollout achieves Deletion AUC 0.211, while Swin-Tiny, ResNet18+CBAM, DenseNet121+Grad-CAM reach 0.432–0.525 (abs(Cohen's d) > 1.1) despite lower classification accuracy. Swin-Tiny’s hierarchy makes it Grad-CAM compatible, indicating readout structure, not architecture family, governs fidelity. RISe compresses all methods to ~0.1 Deletion AUC, outperforming native methods, implying the explainer pathway, not optimality, drives fidelity. Perturbation baselines invert family ordering (blur-fill). Boundary-condition study on MVTec AD shows dataset/task dependence and need for qualification. Policy: co-design explanation pathways with model readout structure and report quantitative faithfulness metrics.

Conclusion: Explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure; heatmaps should be reported with quantitative faithfulness metrics; audit results are dataset- and task-dependent, requiring qualification and guidance for deployment.

Abstract: Industrial visual inspection systems increasingly rely on deep classifiers whose heatmap explanations may appear visually plausible while failing to identify the image regions that actually drive model decisions. This paper operationalizes an architecture-aware explanation audit protocol grounded in the native-readout hypothesis: the perturbation-based faithfulness of an explanation method is bounded by its structural distance from the model's native decision mechanism. On WM-811K wafer maps (9 classes, 172k images) under a three-seed zero-fill perturbation protocol, ViT-Tiny + Attention Rollout attains Deletion AUC 0.211 against 0.432-0.525 for Swin-Tiny / ResNet18+CBAM / DenseNet121 + Grad-CAM (abs(Cohen's d) > 1.1), despite lower classification accuracy. Swin-Tiny disentangles architecture family from readout structure: despite being a Transformer, its spatial feature-map hierarchy makes it Grad-CAM compatible, showing that the operative factor is readout structure rather than architecture family. A model-agnostic control (RISE) compresses all families to Deletion AUC about 0.1, indicating the gap arises from the explainer pathway; notably, RISE outperforms all native methods, so native readout is a compatibility principle rather than an optimality guarantee. A blur-fill sensitivity analysis shows that the family ordering reverses under a different perturbation baseline, reinforcing that faithfulness rankings are joint properties of (model, explainer, perturbation operator) triples. An exploratory boundary-condition study on MVTec AD (pretrained models) indicates that audit results are dataset/task dependent and identifies conditions requiring qualification. The protocol yields actionable guidance: explanation pathways should be co-designed with model architectures based on readout structure, and deployed heatmaps should be accompanied by quantitative faithfulness metrics.

</details>


### [291] [Dynamics of the Transformer Residual Stream: Coupling Spectral Geometry to Network Topology](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14258)
*Jesseba Fernando,Grigori Guitchounts*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Full Jacobian eigendecomposition across production-scale LLMs reveals a learned spectral geometry: a monotonic depth gradient from rotation-dominated, non-normal early layers to near-symmetric late layers, plus a cumulative low-rank bottleneck funneling perturbations into few dimensions; the gradient and collapse are learned, depend on non-normality, and relate to functional topology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how computation and perturbation propagate through depth in LLMs by analyzing the complete Jacobian spectrum, not just scalar proxies, and relate spectral geometry to network topology and dynamics.

Method: Compute full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production-scale LLMs; analyze depth-wise spectral properties (normality, symmetry, eigenvalue distribution); identify low-rank bottlenecks; compare trained vs initial states; assess effects of removing structured non-normality; examine graph-community positioning and coupling signs with respect to local operator types.

Result: A depth-wise spectral gradient is observed: early layers are non-normal and rotation-dominated; later layers become near-symmetric. A cumulative low-rank bottleneck concentrates perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream’s effective dimensions. The gradient and dimensional collapse are learned during training and largely disappear when structured non-normality is removed. The topological positions of graph communities predict whether the Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them, with the coupling sign determined by the local operator type; such relationships are absent at initialization.

Conclusion: The study maps a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links how perturbations propagate and compress across depth to the network’s functional topology; training, not architecture, shapes this spectral structure, offering a deeper understanding of perturbation propagation and dimensionality reduction in deep language models.

Abstract: Large language models are remarkably capable, yet how computation propagates through their layers remains poorly understood. A growing line of work treats depth as discrete time and the residual stream as a dynamical system, where each layer's nonlinear update has a local linear description. However, previous analyses have relied on scalar summaries or approximate linearizations, leaving the full spectral geometry of trained LLMs unknown. We perform full Jacobian eigendecomposition across three production--scale LLMs and show that training installs a monotonic spectral gradient through depth -- from non-normal, rotation-dominated early layers to near--symmetric late layers -- together with a cumulative low-rank bottleneck that funnels perturbations into a small fraction of the residual stream's effective dimensions. Our experiments reveal that this gradient and the dimensional collapse are learned rather than architectural, and is largely dissolved when structured non-normality is removed. We further show that the topological positioning of graph communities predicts whether the Jacobian amplifies or suppresses them, with the sign of the coupling determined by the local operator type, a relationship absent at initialization. These results map a learned spectral geometry in LLMs that links perturbation propagation and compression to the network's functional topology.

</details>


### [292] [TILT: Target-induced loss tilting under covariate shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14280)
*Kakei Yamamoto,Martin J. Wainwright*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TILT introduces Target-Induced Loss Tilting for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift by decomposing the predictor into f + b, training on source labels while penalizing b on target data, yielding a target predictor f and a theory with an oracle inequality and uniform bounds; empirically improves over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address covariate shift in unsupervised domain adaptation with a predictor decomposition and target-side regularization to bias the model toward components that generalize to the target distribution.

Method: Decompose predictor as f + b; optimize on labeled source data with a penalty on b applied to unlabeled target inputs; final predictor is f; provide population-level interpretation and finite-sample oracle inequality; apply to sparse ReLU nets; experiments on regression and CIFAR-100 distillation.

Result: Population-level analysis shows a self-localized estimand b*_f and uniform boundedness; finite-sample oracle inequality; end-to-end guarantees for sparse ReLU networks; empirical gains over source-only, exact importance weighting, and density-ratio baselines with stable regularization dependence.

Conclusion: TILT provides a principled unsupervised domain adaptation approach with target-side penalty yielding robust target performance, backed by theory and experiments.

Abstract: We introduce and analyze Target-Induced Loss Tilting (TILT) for unsupervised domain adaptation under covariate shift. It is based on a novel objective function that decomposes the source predictor as $f+b$, fits $f+b$ on labeled source data while simultaneously penalizing the auxiliary component $b$ on unlabeled target inputs. The resulting fit $f$ is deployed as the final target predictor. At the population level, we show that this target-side penalty implicitly induces relative importance weighting at the population level, but in terms of an estimand $b^*_f$ that is self-localized to the current error, and remains uniformly bounded for any source-target pair (even those with disjoint supports). We prove a general finite-sample oracle inequality on the excess risk, and use it to give an end-to-end guarantee for training with sparse ReLU networks. Experiments on controlled regression problems and shifted CIFAR-100 distillation show that TILT improves target-domain performance over source-only training, exact importance weighting, and relative density-ratio baselines, with a stable dependence on the regularization parameter.

</details>


### [293] [Smooth Multi-Policy Causal Effect Estimation in Longitudinal Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14284)
*Wenxin Chen,Weishen Pan,Kyra Gan,Fei Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PEQ-Net provides a policy-aware, joint estimation framework for evaluating multiple dynamic treatment policies, reducing second-order bias and finite-sample variance compared to ICE-based methods by sharing representations via a policy encoder trained with kernel mean embeddings and corrected by LTMLE; showing RMSE gains on semi-synthetic data, especially for similar policies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional longitudinal causal inference estimates each policy in isolation, causing a structurally uncontrolled second-order bias and inflated finite-sample variance. A joint, policy-aware representation can stabilize estimation across related counterfactuals.

Method: Introduce Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net) with a shared policy encoder. Reparameterize ICE-based Q-functions to share representations across policies. Train the encoder with kernel mean embeddings to reflect population-level policy dissimilarities. Apply an LTMLE correction step and prove the second-order remainder is structurally constrained, reducing variance.

Result: Empirical evaluation on semi-synthetic datasets shows PEQ-Net consistently outperforms ICE-based methods, achieving substantial RMSE reductions, particularly when policies are closely related.

Conclusion: Policy-aware joint estimation via PEQ-Net stabilizes finite-sample variance and improves accuracy for evaluating multiple dynamic policies, leveraging shared encodings and LTMLE corrections to outperform existing ICE-based approaches.

Abstract: Comparative evaluation of multiple dynamic treatment policies is essential for healthcare and policy decisions, yet conventional longitudinal causal inference methods estimate each in isolation, preventing information sharing across counterfactuals. We demonstrate that this separate estimation paradigm induces a structurally uncontrolled second-order bias, inflating finite-sample variance even after standard debiasing with longitudinal targeted maximum likelihood estimation(LTMLE). To address this, we propose a policy-aware reparameterization of Iterative Conditional Expectation (ICE) Q-functions that enables joint estimation through shared representations. We implement this approach in the Policy-Encoded Q Network (PEQ-Net), an architecture centered on a shared policy encoder. The encoder is trained using kernel mean embeddings, ensuring that the learned representation space reflects population-level policy dissimilarities. After applying an LTMLE correction step, we prove this design imposes a structural constraint on the second-order remainder, thereby stabilizing finite-sample variance. Experiments on semi-synthetic datasets demonstrate that PEQ-Net consistently outperforms existing ICE-based methods, achieving substantial reductions in root-mean-square error, particularly when evaluating closely related policies.

</details>


### [294] [MetaMoE: Diversity-Aware Proxy Selection for Privacy-Preserving Mixture-of-Experts Unification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14289)
*Weisen Jiang,Shuhao Chen,Sinno Jialin Pan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MetaMoE introduces privacy-preserving unification of domain-specialized Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) using public proxy data and diversity-aware proxy selection to approximate inaccessible private data distributions. It improves router learning and expert coordination at unification time, with strong empirical gains on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable unified training of domain-specialized MoE models when private data is distributed and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making centralized MoE training infeasible.

Method: Use public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. Employ diversity-aware proxy selection to pick client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data to approximate private distributions and supervise router learning. Use these proxies to align expert training and improve coordination at unification time; incorporate a context-aware router to handle heterogeneous inputs.

Result: MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks; code is available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: Diversity-aware, public-proxy-based unification enables effective privacy-preserving MoE training across heterogeneous domains, improving expert coordination and router performance; the approach generalizes across vision and language tasks.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models scale capacity by combining specialized experts, but most existing approaches assume centralized access to training data. In practice, data are distributed across clients and cannot be shared due to privacy constraints, making unified MoE training challenging. We propose MetaMoE, a privacy-preserving framework that unifies independently trained, domain-specialized experts into a single MoE using public proxy data as surrogates for inaccessible private data. Central to MetaMoE is diversity-aware proxy selection, which selects client-domain-relevant and diverse samples from public data to effectively approximate private data distributions and supervise router learning. These proxies are further used to align expert training, improving expert coordination at unification time, while a context-aware router enhances expert selection across heterogeneous inputs. Experiments on computer vision and natural language processing benchmarks demonstrate that MetaMoE consistently outperforms recent privacy-preserving MoE unification methods. Code is available at https://github.com/ws-jiang/MetaMoE.

</details>


### [295] [Minimal-Intervention KV Retention: A Design-Space Study and a Diversity-Penalty Survivor](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14292)
*Libo Sun,Po-wei Harn,Peixiong He,Xiao Qin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Minimal, scoring-focused KV-cache optimization (α) beats multiple heavyweight cache redesigns under small budget regimes, validated on MATH-500 with two distilled models, via a pre-registered protocol and held-out confirmation; asymmetry across model/budget settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Explore efficient KV-cache compression strategies in the constrained budget regime; identify whether lightweight scoring modifications can outperform structural changes, emphasizing reproducible evaluation.

Method: Compared seven mechanisms across families under matched memory; used Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants; budgets 64 and 128; proposed α: a one-function modification to TriAttention retention scorer replacing argmax-top-k with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under V-space redundancy penalty with λ. Pre-registered tuning on frozen dev and held-out evaluation; Branch A triggered.

Result: λ=0.5 yields Bonferroni-significant improvement in two of four cells (Qwen-128, Llama-64); no cells significantly negative; overall evidence of asymmetry: small modification outperforms heavier changes under this regime.

Conclusion: Small, principled scoring adjustments combined with careful evaluation can outperform larger architectural redesigns in constrained cache budgets; the pre-registered, memory-matched, held-out protocol strengthens the evidence; suggests focus on scoring mechanics and replication in KV-cache research.

Abstract: KV-cache compression at small budgets is a crowded design space spanning cache representation, head-wise routing, compression cadence, decoding behavior, and within-budget scoring. We study seven mechanisms across these five families under matched mean cache on long-form mathematical reasoning (MATH-500~\cite{hendrycks2021math}) with two distilled-reasoning models (Qwen-7B and Llama-8B variants of DeepSeek-R1-Distill~\cite{deepseek2025r1}) at budgets $b \in \{64, 128\}$. All seven were rejected. We then propose $α$, a one-function modification to the TriAttention~\cite{mao2026triattention} retention scorer that replaces argmax-top-$k$ with greedy facility-location-inspired selection under a V-space redundancy penalty controlled by a single weight $λ$. A pre-registered protocol tunes $λ$ on a frozen development split and confirms on a disjoint held-out split; with $λ= 0.5$, $α$ clears Bonferroni on two of the four (model, budget) cells (Qwen $b{=}128$ and Llama $b{=}64$), no cell is significantly negative, and the pre-registered Branch~A triggers. The finding is asymmetric: a minimal scoring modification beat heavier structural redesigns in this regime, and the combined matched-memory, sympy-graded, held-out confirmation protocol is the evidence standard that made the asymmetry visible.

</details>


### [296] [Policy Optimization in Hybrid Discrete-Continuous Action Spaces via Mixed Gradients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14297)
*Matias Alvo,Daniel Russo,Yash Kanoria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO) combines differentiable simulation with a mixed pathwise+score-function gradient to handle hybrid discrete–continuous action spaces, outperforming PPO and enabling approximate decentralized updates near optima.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL with hybrid actions suffers credit assignment and bias from non-smooth dynamics; differentiable simulators struggle with discrete decisions; a principled, unbiased method that leverages both gradient types is needed.

Method: Propose HPO: backpropagate through the simulator where smooth, use a mixed gradient estimator that fuses pathwise and score-function components; reformulate action-discontinuity problems in hybrid form; analyze the gradient structure (cross terms) and enable near-decoupled updates; provide public code.

Result: HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched LQR, with larger gains as the continuous action dimension grows; the mixed-gradient cross-term vanishes near a discrete best response, enabling approximate decentralized updates.

Conclusion: The mixed-gradient framework offers an unbiased, broadly applicable approach to hybrid RL, yielding strong empirical gains and a clearer understanding of when continuous and discrete updates can be treated separately.

Abstract: We study reinforcement learning in hybrid discrete-continuous action spaces, such as settings where the discrete component selects a regime (or index) and the continuous component optimizes within it -- a structure common in robotics, control, and operations problems. Standard model-free policy gradient methods rely on score-function (SF) estimators and suffer from severe credit-assignment issues in high-dimensional settings, leading to poor gradient quality. On the other hand, differentiable simulation largely sidesteps these issues by backpropagating through a simulator, but the presence of discrete actions or non-smooth dynamics yields biased or uninformative gradients. To address this, we propose Hybrid Policy Optimization (HPO), which backpropagates through the simulator wherever smoothness permits, using a mixed gradient estimator that combines pathwise and SF gradients while maintaining unbiasedness. We also show how problems with action discontinuities can be reformulated in hybrid form, further broadening its applicability. Empirically, HPO substantially outperforms PPO on inventory control and switched linear-quadratic regulator problems, with performance gaps increasing as the continuous action dimension grows. Finally, we characterize the structure of the mixed gradient, showing that its cross term -- which captures how continuous actions influence future discrete decisions -- becomes negligible near a discrete best response, thereby enabling approximate decentralized updates of the continuous and discrete components and reducing variance near optimality. All resources are available at github.com/MatiasAlvo/hybrid-rl.

</details>


### [297] [Language-Induced Priors for Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14301)
*Qiyuan Chen,Jiayu Zhou,Raed Al Kontar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A probabilistic framework, Language-Induced Prior (LIP), leverages expert textual descriptions of a target domain to guide source selection in domain adaptation under cold-start. It translates semantic descriptions into a prior over source relevance via a pretrained LLM and embeds this in an EM algorithm. It is model-agnostic (any likelihood-based method) and yields an oracle-like MSE under a correct prior, with asymptotic consistency regardless of LIP quality. Empirical validation spans descriptive, predictive, and prescriptive tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the cold-start domain adaptation paradox: when target data is scarce, distinguishing relevant from irrelevant source domains is difficult, causing negative transfer. Use readily available expert textual descriptions of the target domain to inform source selection and mitigate negative transfer.

Method: Introduce a probabilistic framework that converts semantic target-domain descriptions into a Language-Induced Prior (LIP) via a pretrained Large Language Model. Integrate the LIP into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance, guiding source selection when target signals are weak and refining choices as data accumulates. The framework is compatible with any parametric model with a likelihood.

Result: The approach yields an estimator that, with a correct prior, matches the performance of an oracle cold-start MSE, and remains asymptotically consistent regardless of LIP quality. Empirical validation across three tasks—Gaussian estimation (descriptive), C-MAPSS (predictive), and MuJoCo Hopper (prescriptive)—demonstrates practical efficacy.

Conclusion: Using semantic knowledge about the target domain, encoded as a Language-Induced Prior and integrated into an EM framework, can effectively disambiguate source relevance in cold-start settings. The method is theoretically sound (oracle-like performance with correct prior; asymptotic consistency) and empirically versatile across tasks.

Abstract: Domain adaptation faces a fundamental paradox in the cold-start regime. When target data is scarce, statistical methods fail to distinguish relevant source domains from irrelevant ones, which often leads to negative transfer. In this paper, we address this challenge by leveraging expert textual descriptions of the target domain, a resource that is often available but overlooked. We propose a probabilistic framework that translates these semantic descriptions into a choice model, namely a Language-Induced Prior (LIP), that learns the preferences from a pretrained Large Language Model (LLM). The LIP is then integrated into an Expectation-Maximization algorithm to identify source relevance. Methodologically, this framework is compatible with any parametric model where a likelihood is available. It allows the LIP to guide the selection of sources when target signals are weak, while gradually refining these choices as samples accumulate. Theoretically, we prove that the estimator roughly matches an oracle cold-start MSE under a correct prior, while remaining asymptotically consistent regardless of the quality of the LIP. Empirically, we validated the framework on a descriptive (Gaussian estimation), a predictive (C-MAPSS dataset), and a prescriptive task (MuJoCo hopper).

</details>


### [298] [Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning for Reusing Local Transition Geometry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14304)
*Zuyuan Zhang,Carlee Joe-Wong,Tian Lan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MSRL introduces matrix-space RL: trajectory segments are represented by positive semidefinite matrices that encode first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. This geometric descriptor enables additive composition, gauge-invariance, and first-order value initialization, with obstruction filtering for plausibility and plug-in compatibility with standard RL methods. Empirically, it achieves state-of-the-art finite-budget AUC (0.73).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve compositional generalization in sequential decision-making by leveraging rich local transition geometry and dynamics often ignored by skill- or model-based reuses, enabling principled transfer via an algebraic matrix-descriptor space.

Method: Define trajectory-segment descriptors as PSD matrices aggregating statistics of lifted one-step transitions; prove gauge-invariance, completeness for low-order additive signals, additivity under valid composition, and minimal sufficiency among admissible additive descriptors. Condition value functions on the matrix to obtain a first-order smooth value approximation and learn matrix-to-value mappings that bootstrap new tasks. Provide obstruction filtering and ensure plug-in compatibility with model-free/model-based methods.

Result: The descriptor is well defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. Conditioning value functions on the segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation enabling transfer. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).

Conclusion: MSRL offers a principled, algebraic framework for compositional transfer in RL that is compatible with existing RL methods and improves finite-budget performance, by exploiting geometry of trajectory segments through PSD matrices and a first-order value mapping.

Abstract: Compositional generalization in sequential decision-making requires identifying which parts of prior rollouts remain useful for new tasks. Existing methods reuse skills or predictive models, but often overlook rich local transition geometry and dynamics. We propose Matrix-Space Reinforcement Learning (MSRL), a geometric abstraction that represents trajectory segments through positive semidefinite matrix descriptors aggregating first- and second-order statistics of lifted one-step transitions. These descriptors expose shared hidden structure, support algebraic composition in an abstract matrix space, and reveal opportunities for transfer. We prove that the descriptor is well defined up to coordinate gauge, complete for the induced low-order additive signal class, additive under valid segment composition, and minimally sufficient among admissible additive descriptors. We further show that conditioning value functions on the trajectory-segment matrix yields a first-order smooth approximation of action values, enabling source-learned matrix-to-value mappings to bootstrap learning in new tasks. MSRL is plug-in compatible with standard model-free and model-based methods, while obstruction filtering rejects implausible compositions. Empirically, MSRL achieves the best average finite-budget target AUC of 0.73, outperforming MSRL from scratch (0.65), TD-MPC-PT+FT (0.63), and TD-MPC (0.57).

</details>


### [299] [Beyond Binary: Reframing GUI Critique as Continuous Semantic Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14311)
*Yuchen Sun,Pei Fu,Shaojie Zhang,Anan Du,Xiuwen Xi,Ruoceng Zhang,Zhenbo Luo,Jian Luan,Chongyang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: BBCritic introduces a Beyond-Binary Critic for GUI agents, using two-stage contrastive learning to recover a hierarchical Affordance Space, addressing Affordance Collapse and Noise Sensitivity of binary critics; pairs with BBBench benchmark; demonstrates superior zero-shot performance versus binary SOTA models without extra annotations, framing GUI critique as metric learning rather than classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Binary GUI critics compress rich hierarchical affordances into 0/1 labels, causing entanglement between valid actions and distractors (Affordance Collapse) and overfitting to noisy decision boundaries (Noise Sensitivity). There is a need for fine-grained, transferable ranking signals to guide test-time sampling (TTS).

Method: Two-stage contrastive learning that aligns instructions and actions within a shared Affordance Space, leveraging a hierarchical four-level taxonomy in BBBench. The approach recovers hierarchical structure lost by binary supervision and enables dense action representations for ranking without extra annotation (BBCritic-3B).

Result: BBCritic-3B outperforms 7B-parameter SOTA binary models on the proposed GUI critic benchmark and shows strong zero-shot transfer across platforms and tasks.

Conclusion: GUI critique should be treated as metric learning rather than pure classification; the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis and two-stage contrastive learning can recover a meaningful, hierarchical affordance representation, enabling fine-grained action ranking and better generalization.

Abstract: Test-Time Scaling (TTS), which samples multiple candidate actions and ranks them via a Critic Model, has emerged as a promising paradigm for generalist GUI agents. Its efficacy thus hinges on the critic's fine-grained ranking ability. However, existing GUI critic models uniformly adopt binary classification. Our motivational analysis of these models exposes a severe entanglement: scores for valid actions and plausible-but-invalid distractors become indistinguishable. We attribute this failure to two structural defects: Affordance Collapse--the hierarchical affordance space is compressed into 0/1 labels; and Noise Sensitivity--binary objectives overfit to noisy decision boundaries. To resolve this, we introduce BBCritic (Beyond-Binary Critic), a paradigm shift grounded in the Functional Equivalence Hypothesis. Through two-stage contrastive learning, BBCritic aligns instructions and actions in a shared Affordance Space, recovering the hierarchical structure that binary supervision flattens. We also present BBBench (Beyond-Binary Bench), the first GUI critic benchmark that pairs a dense action space with a hierarchical four-level taxonomy, enabling fine-grained ranking evaluation. Experimental results show that BBCritic-3B, trained without any extra annotation, outperforms 7B-parameter SOTA binary models. It demonstrates strong zero-shot transferability across platforms and tasks, supporting our methodological view: GUI critique is fundamentally a metric-learning problem, not a classification one.

</details>


### [300] [Guided Diffusion Sampling for Precipitation Forecast Interventions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14317)
*Ayumu Ueyama,Kazuhiko Kawamoto,Hiroshi Kera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Gradient-guided diffusion sampling to reduce precipitation in data-driven forecasts; improves physical plausibility of interventions and shows transferability across models compared to adversarial perturbations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme precipitation causes significant damage; perturbation-based weather-control methods on data-driven forecasts are underexplored and may lack physical plausibility.

Method: Steer the diffusion sampling trajectory using gradient information in a diffusion-based weather forecasting model, perturbing the sampling process rather than atmospheric states to reduce precipitation while staying consistent with the atmospheric distribution. Evaluate physical plausibility via: (i) vertical/variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, (iii) cross-model transferability. Tested on extreme-precipitation events from WeatherBench2.

Result: The approach achieves effective precipitation reduction and yields more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations; demonstrates cross-model transferability.

Conclusion: Diffusion-guided interventions offer a physically-consistent mechanism for weather-control in data-driven forecasts, potentially outperforming adversarial perturbations in plausibility and robustness.

Abstract: Extreme precipitation causes severe societal and economic damage, and weather control has long been discussed as a potential mitigation strategy. However, to the best of our knowledge, perturbation-based interventions for weather control using data-driven weather forecasting models have not yet been explored. While adversarial attacks also generate perturbations that alter forecasts, they aim to exploit model artifacts and do not account for physical plausibility. In this paper, we propose a gradient-based guidance framework for precipitation-reduction interventions through diffusion sampling in diffusion-based weather forecasting models. Instead of directly perturbing atmospheric states, our method steers the diffusion sampling trajectory, enabling precipitation reduction while maintaining consistency with the atmospheric distribution. To assess physical plausibility, we evaluate from three perspectives: (i) vertical and variable-wise perturbation profiles, (ii) latent-space trajectory deviation, and (iii) cross-model transferability. Experiments on extreme precipitation events from WeatherBench2 demonstrate that our method achieves effective precipitation reduction while yielding more physically plausible interventions than adversarial perturbations.

</details>


### [301] [Dynamic Latent Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14323)
*Fangyuan Yu,Xin Su,Amir Abdullah*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes General Dijkstra Search (GDS) to achieve globally optimal goal-reaching in time-varying MDPs via temporal concatenation of intermediate sub-policies; introduces Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR) as a language-model post-training method to jointly learn discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search. It reports data-efficient gains in low-data fine-tuning and shows that DLR learns structured routing with distinct causal roles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable globally optimal planning in time-varying reward settings by decomposing policies into temporally concatenated sub-policies, leveraging a search-based routing principle.

Method: Theoretically: GDS proves that global optima can be recovered by composing intermediate optimal sub-policies in a time-extended graph. Practically: DLR trains an LM in a single stage to learn discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters via dynamic search during fine-tuning.

Result: DLR matches or surpasses supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models in low-data regimes, with an average gain of 6.6 percentage points; discrete-latent baselines underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and code ablations reveal structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.

Conclusion: Temporal composition via GDS enables effective, interpretable routing in LM-based fine-tuning; DLR offers a data-efficient, routing-aware fine-tuning paradigm, though scalability and robustness across broader tasks warrant further study.

Abstract: We investigate the temporal concatenation of sub-policies in Markov Decision Processes (MDP) with time-varying reward functions. We introduce General Dijkstra Search (GDS), and prove that globally optimal goal-reaching policies can be recovered through temporal composition of intermediate optimal sub-policies. Motivated by the "search, select, update" principle underlying GDS, we propose Dynamic Latent Routing (DLR), a language-model post-training method that jointly learns discrete latent codes, routing policies, and model parameters through dynamic search in a single training stage. In low-data fine-tuning settings, DLR matches or outperforms supervised fine-tuning across four datasets and six models, achieving a mean gain of +6.6 percentage points, while prior discrete-latent baselines consistently underperform SFT. Mechanistic analyses and targeted code ablations show that DLR learns structured routing behaviors with distinct causal roles.

</details>


### [302] [AIM-DDI: A Model-Agnostic Multimodal Integration Module for Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14327)
*Yerin Park,Sangseon Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A reusable, architecture-independent multimodal integration module (AIM-DDI) for DDI prediction that tokenizes heterogeneous modalities into a shared latent space with a unified fusion module, improving unseen-drug DDI performance across models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unseen-drug generalization in DDI prediction is challenging; existing multimodal fusion is tied to specific architectures, hindering reuse across models.

Method: Introduce AIM-DDI: represent structural, chemical, and semantic drug information as tokens in a shared latent space and model dependencies via a unified fusion module, enabling architecture-agnostic multimodal integration.

Result: Empirical evaluation across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings shows consistent performance gains, with the largest improvements in the most challenging about both-unseen setting (neither drug in test pair observed during training).

Conclusion: Treating multimodal integration as a reusable module rather than model-specific fusion improves robustness for unseen-drug DDI prediction and supports architecture-agnostic deployment.

Abstract: Drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction is a critical task in computational biomedicine, as adverse interactions between co-administered drugs can cause severe side effects and clinical risks. A key challenge is unseen-drug generalization, where interactions must be predicted for drugs not observed during training. Although multimodal DDI models exploit diverse drug-related information, their fusion mechanisms are often tied to specific prediction architectures, limiting their reuse across models. To address this, we propose AIM-DDI, an architecture-independent multimodal integration module that represents heterogeneous modality information as tokens in a shared latent space. By modeling dependencies across modality tokens through a unified fusion module, AIM-DDI enables model-agnostic integration of structural, chemical, and semantic drug signals across different DDI prediction architectures. Extensive evaluations across diverse DDI models and DrugBank-based settings show that AIM-DDI consistently improves prediction performance, with the strongest gains under the most challenging both-unseen setting where neither drug in a test pair is observed during training. These results suggest that treating multimodal integration as a reusable module, rather than a model-specific fusion component, is an effective strategy for robust unseen-drug DDI prediction.

</details>


### [303] [Nearest-Neighbor Radii under Dependent Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14343)
*Yuanyuan Gao,Yilong Hou,Zhexiao Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling with strong mixing converge almost surely in a distribution-free way under polynomial mixing, and admit sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing; bounds depend on local intrinsic dimension rather than ambient dimension, enabling high-dimensional manifold data—supported by synthetic and real-world time-series experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how dependence among samples affects NN geometry, which is typically analyzed under i.i.d.; extend NN theory to time-series and dependent data.

Method: Theoretical analysis of NN radii under strong mixing. Proves distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing; derives sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing; bounds scale with local intrinsic dimension.

Result: Established a.s. convergence of NN radii under polynomial mixing; obtained non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing; moment bounds depend on local intrinsic dimension rather than ambient dimension.

Conclusion: NN geometry remains informative under dependent sampling; results extend applicability to high-dimensional data near low-dimensional manifolds; empirical results on synthetic data and real-world time-series support the theory.

Abstract: Nearest-neighbor methods are fundamental to classical and modern machine learning, yet their geometric properties are typically analyzed under independent sampling. In this paper, we study the nearest-neighbor radii under dependent sampling. We consider strong mixing dependent observations and ask whether dependence changes the scale of nearest-neighbor neighborhoods. We establish distribution-free almost sure convergence under polynomial mixing and sharp non-asymptotic moment bounds under geometric mixing. The moment bounds depend on the local intrinsic dimension rather than the ambient dimension, making the results applicable to high-dimensional data concentrated near lower-dimensional manifolds. Synthetic experiments and real-world time-series benchmarks support the theory, showing that nearest-neighbor geometry remains informative under dependence sampling.

</details>
