### [304] [Exemplar Partitioning for Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14347)
*Jessica Rumbelow*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Exemplar Partitioning (EP) builds interpretable activation dictionaries via exemplar-driven Voronoi partitions, enabling cross-layer/model comparability, causal interventions, and efficient use of tokens (~1e3x less compute) vs SAEs; demonstrated on Gemma variants and AxBench with cross-checkpoint insights and OOD signaling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide an interpretable, comparable surface over LLM activations that remains stable across models and training stages, while enabling causal interventions and reducing data/compute compared to learned sparse autoencoders.

Method: Leader-clustering within a distance threshold to form Voronoi regions; each region anchored by an observed exemplar; no prespecified dictionary size; dictionaries are defined by activation geometry and threshold; cross-layer/model/checkpoint comparability; evaluation includes interpretability demonstrations, cross-checkpoint matching, one-hot probes, and AxBench AUROC analysis.

Result: EP regions are interpretable and support causal interventions (e.g., refusal behavior localized to a region whose exemplar ablation affects withheld responses); cross-checkpoint analysis separates preservation from change due to finetuning; EP regions align with SAE features only partially (≈20% match at F1>0.5); EP one-hot probes retain ≈97% of raw-activation probe accuracy at l0=1; nearest-exemplar distance provides an OOD signal; on AxBench (Gemma-2-2B-it L20), EP achieves AUROC 0.881, +0.126 over GemmaScope SAE baseline, within 0.030 of SAE-A, at ~1000x less build compute.

Conclusion: EP offers an interpretable, geometry-driven dictionary construction that yields causal insights, cross-model comparability, and competitive recognition with substantial compute savings. It complements SAE-based methods by providing a stable, exemplar-centered view of activation space and a practical OOD signal.

Abstract: We introduce Exemplar Partitioning (EP), an unsupervised method for constructing interpretable feature dictionaries from large language model activations with $\sim 10^{3}\times$ fewer tokens than comparable sparse autoencoders (SAEs). An EP dictionary is a Voronoi partition of activation space, built by leader-clustering streamed activations within a distance threshold. Each region is anchored by an observed exemplar that serves as both its membership criterion and intervention direction; dictionary size is not prespecified, but determined by the activation geometry at that threshold. Because exemplars are observed rather than learned, dictionaries built from the same data stream are directly comparable across layers, models, and training checkpoints.
  We characterise EP as an interpretability object via targeted demonstrations of properties newly accessible through this construction, plus one head-to-head benchmark. In Gemma-2-2B, EP dictionary regions are interpretable and support causal interventions: refusal in instruction-tuned Gemma concentrates in a region whose exemplar ablation can collapse held-out refusal. Cross-checkpoint matching between base and instruction-tuned dictionaries separates the directions preserved through finetuning from those introduced by it. EP regions and Gemma Scope SAE features decompose activation space differently but agree on a shared core: $\sim 20\%$ of EP regions match an SAE feature at $F_{1} > 0.5$, and EP one-hot probes retain $\sim 97\%$ of raw-activation probe accuracy at $\ell_{0} = 1$. Nearest-exemplar distance provides a free out-of-distribution signal at inference. On AxBench latent concept detection at Gemma-2-2B-it L20, EP at $p_{1}$ reaches mean AUROC $0.881$, $+0.126$ over the canonical GemmaScope SAE leaderboard entry and within $0.030$ of SAE-A's $0.911$, at $\sim 10^{3}\times$ less build compute.

</details>


### [305] [Distributionally Robust Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning via Adaptive Task Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14350)
*Nicholas E. Corrado,Wenyuan Huang,Josiah P. Hanna*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DRATS is a distributionally robust adaptive task sampling method for multi-task reinforcement learning that prioritizes sampling tasks farthest from being solved, using a minimax objective to minimize the worst-case return gap, improving data efficiency and worst-task performance on MetaWorld MT10/MT50.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced data allocation in MTRL leads to overfitting easy tasks and under-sampling hard tasks; prior work focuses on gradient manipulation or specialized architectures but data allocation remains under-explored.

Method: Formulates MTRL as a feasibility problem and derives a minimax objective to minimize the worst-case gap between a target return and observed return; DRATS adaptively reweights/redirects environment interactions toward tasks with largest remaining gap.

Result: DRATS yields improved data efficiency and higher worst-task performance relative to existing task sampling baselines in MT10 and MT50 benchmarks.

Conclusion: Adaptive, distributionally robust task sampling can mitigate data imbalance in MTRL, yielding more balanced learning and stronger worst-case task performance; the approach demonstrates practical gains on standard benchmarks and suggests broader applicability.

Abstract: Multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) aims to train a single agent to efficiently optimize performance across multiple tasks simultaneously. However, jointly optimizing all tasks often yields imbalanced learning: agents quickly solve easy tasks but learn slowly on harder ones. While prior work primarily attributes this imbalance to conflicting task gradients and proposes gradient manipulation or specialized architectures to address it, we instead focus on a distinct and under-explored challenge: imbalanced data allocation. Standard MTRL allocates an equal number of environment interactions to each task, which over-allocates data to easy tasks that require relatively few interactions to solve and under-allocates data to hard tasks that require substantially more experience to solve. To address this challenge, we introduce Distributionally Robust Adaptive Task Sampling (DRATS), an algorithm that adaptively prioritizes sampling tasks furthest from being solved. We derive DRATS by formalizing MTRL as a feasibility problem from which we derive a minimax objective for minimizing the worst-case return gap, the difference between a desired target return and the agent's return on a task. In benchmarks like MetaWorld-MT10 and MT50, DRATS improves data efficiency and increases worst-task performance compared to existing task sampling algorithms.

</details>


### [306] [RQ-MoE: Residual Quantization via Mixture of Experts for Efficient Input-Dependent Vector Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14359)
*Zhengjia Zhong,Shuyan Ke,Zaizhou Lin,Jiaqi Song,Hongyi Lan,Hui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces RQ-MoE: Residual Quantization via a two-level Mixture of Experts and dual-stream quantization for input-dependent codebooks; achieves faster decoding and supports parallelism; standard RQ and QINCo are recoverable as special cases; code released.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static codebooks limit expressiveness for high-dimensional embeddings with heterogeneous geometry; dynamic quantizers like QINCo improve expressiveness but impose sequential decoding bottlenecks; need input-adaptive, parallelizable vector quantization.

Method: Two-level Mixture-of-Experts with dual-stream quantization enabling input-dependent codebook construction; decouples instruction from quantization to allow parallel decoding; provides theoretical reductions showing standard Residual Quantization and QINCo as constrained special cases; includes guidance for setting expert dimensionality.

Result: Extensive experiments report state-of-the-art or on-par performance for reconstruction and retrieval; decoding is 6x-14x faster than prior vector quantization methods; implementation available at GitHub.

Conclusion: RQ-MoE enables dynamic, input-adaptive codebooks with decoupled instruction and parallel decoding; unifies prior approaches as constrained cases and offers practical performance and design guidelines.

Abstract: Vector quantization is a fundamental tool for compressing high-dimensional embeddings, yet existing multi-codebook methods rely on static codebooks that limit expressiveness under heterogeneous data geometry. While recent dynamic quantizers like QINCo adapt codebooks to individual inputs and improve expressiveness, their strict sequential dependencies create decoding bottlenecks. We propose Residual Quantization via Mixture of Experts (RQ-MoE), a framework combining a two-level MoE with dual-stream quantization to enable input-dependent codebook adaptation for efficient vector quantization. RQ-MoE enables dynamic codebook construction and decouples instruction from quantization, facilitating parallel decoding. Theoretically, we show that standard Residual Quantization and QINCo can be recovered as constrained special cases of RQ-MoE, and derive a guideline for setting expert dimensionality in RQ-MoE. Extensive experiments show that RQ-MoE achieves state-of-the-art or on-par performance in reconstruction and retrieval, while providing 6x-14x faster decoding than prior vector quantization methods. The implementation is available at https://github.com/KDEGroup/RQ-MoE.

</details>


### [307] [MoRe: Modular Representations for Principled Continual Representation Learning on Squantial Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14364)
*Jiaqi Sun,Boyang Sun,Mohamad Rasmy,Xiangchen Song,Kun Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MoRe identifies modular representations within a model's activations (not just architecture), forming a hierarchy of fundamental and specific modules with identifiability guarantees to enable principled continual adaptation and module reuse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning faces interference when integrating new information. Representational modularity that mirrors intrinsic data organization—revealed by time-delayed dependencies—offers a robust path to selective updates and knowledge preservation.

Method: MoRe decomposes knowledge into a hierarchy of modules (fundamental and specific) within the representation, with identifiability guarantees. It supports principled module reuse, alignment, and expansion during adaptation, aiming to preserve old modules by construction rather than by explicit regularization.

Result: Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real-world LLM activations show interpretable hierarchical structure and improved plasticity-stability trade-offs.

Conclusion: Treats modular representation learning as a principled foundation for continual adaptation, enabling scalable, reusable, and expandable modules that preserve prior knowledge while adapting to new information.

Abstract: Continual learning requires models to adapt to new data while preserving previously acquired knowledge. At its core, this challenge can be viewed as principled one-step adaptation: incorporating new information with minimal interference to existing representations. Most existing approaches address this challenge by modifying model parameters or architectures in a supervised, task-specific manner. However, the underlying issue is representational: tasks require distinct yet structured representations that can be selectively updated without disrupting representations, while structure should reflect intrinsic organization in the data rather than task boundaries. In sequential data, time-delayed dependencies provide a natural signal for uncovering this organization, revealing how fundamental representations give rise to more specific ones. Inspired by the modular organization of the human brain, we propose MoRe, a framework that identifies modularity in the representation itself rather than allocating it at the architectural level. MoRe decomposes knowledge into a hierarchy of fundamental and specific modules with identifiability guarantees, enabling principled module reuse, alignment, and expansion during adaptation while preserving old modules by construction. Experiments on synthetic benchmarks and real-world LLM activations demonstrate interpretable hierarchical structure, improved plasticity-stability trade-offs, suggesting MoRe as a principled foundation for continual adaptation

</details>


### [308] [LoMETab: Beyond Rank-1 Ensembles for Tabular Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14365)
*Changryeol Choi,Hyewon Park,Yujin Kwon,Gowun Jeong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoMETab generalizes multiplicative implicit ensembles to rank-r, enabling controllable diversity and performance in neural tabular models; the adapter rank r and initialization scale σ_init tune hypothesis capacity and diversity, with dataset-dependent gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand why simple neural tabular models remain competitive and develop explicit mechanisms to control predictive diversity beyond rank-1 modulation.

Method: Introduce W_k = W ⊙ (1 + A_k B_k^T) with a shared base weight W and member-specific low-rank factors (A_k, B_k). Systematically vary adapter rank r and initialization scale σ_init; prove that for r ≥ 2 the hypothesis class strictly enlarges BatchEnsemble; evaluate on representative classification tasks, measuring pairwise KL, argmax disagreement, and regression ambiguity; compare to additive low-rank ablations and across a grid of (r, σ_init).

Result: For r ≥ 2, LoMETab strictly enlarges the hypothesis class than Rank-1; empirical results show increased predictive diversity as reflected by higher pairwise KL, and by task-level measures such as argmax disagreement (classification) and ambiguity (regression). The diversity and performance are modulated by (r, σ_init), with several orders of magnitude variation in KL across configurations and clear dataset-dependent performance.

Conclusion: LoMETab constitutes a controllable family of implicit ensembles for tabular learning, where adapter rank r and initialization scale σ_init serve as practical levers to induce and tune diversity; it generalizes beyond fixed rank-1 modulation and yields dataset-dependent trade-offs between diversity and accuracy.

Abstract: Recent tabular learning benchmarks increasingly show a tight performance cluster rather than a clear hierarchy among leading methods, spanning gradient boosted decision trees, attention-based architectures, and implicit ensembles such as TabM. As benchmark gains plateau, a complementary goal is to understand and control the mechanisms that make simple neural tabular models competitive. We propose LoMETab, a rank-$r$ generalization of multiplicative implicit ensembles. LoMETab lifts the rank-1 BatchEnsemble/TabM modulation to a rank-$r$ identity-residual Hadamard family by parameterizing each member weight as $W_k = W \odot (1 + A_kB_k^\top)$, where $W$ is shared and $(A_k, B_k)$ are member-specific low-rank factors. This exposes two practical diversity-control axes: the adapter rank $r$ and the initialization scale $σ_{\mathrm{init}}$, and we prove that for $r \ge 2$ this generalization strictly enlarges BatchEnsemble's hypothesis class. Empirically, we show that this added capacity manifests as measurable predictive diversity after training: on representative classification datasets, LoMETab sustains higher pairwise KL than an additive low-rank ablation, and $(r, σ_{\mathrm{init}})$ provides broad control over pairwise KL, varying by up to several orders of magnitude across configurations. The induced diversity is reflected in task-appropriate output-level measures: argmax disagreement for classification and ambiguity for regression, indicating that the control extends beyond pairwise KL to decision- and output-level member variation. Finally, experiments sweeping over adapter rank $r$ and initialization scale $σ_{\mathrm{init}}$ reveal that predictive performance is dataset-dependent over the $(r, σ_{\mathrm{init}})$ grid, supporting LoMETab as a controllable family of implicit ensembles rather than a fixed rank-1 construction.

</details>


### [309] [Turning Stale Gradients into Stable Gradients: Coherent Coordinate Descent with Implicit Landscape Smoothing for Lightweight Zeroth-Order Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14373)
*Chen Liang,Xiatao Sun,Qian Wang,Daniel Rakita*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoCD is a deterministic, sample-efficient zeroth-order optimizer that leverages gradient coherence to convert stale gradients into a computational asset, achieving constant per-step query complexity and improved stability over randomized ZO methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the inefficiency of zeroth-order methods: standard finite differences are sample-inefficient, while randomized subspace methods suffer high variance. The goal is a deterministic, budget-aware optimizer suitable for memory-constrained, black-box settings.

Method: Introduce Coherent Coordinate Descent (CoCD), a deterministic ZO optimizer. Formalize gradient coherence and show CoCD is equivalent to Block Cyclic Coordinate Descent with warm starts, turning stale gradients into an asset. Achieve O(1) query per step with global descent directions. Derive error bounds indicating larger finite-difference steps can implicitly smooth the landscape by reducing the effective smoothness constant.

Result: Theoretical: gradient coherence formalization; CoCD ≡ BCCD with warm starts; per-step queries O(1); global descent directions. Practical: explicit error bounds showing smoothing effect from larger step sizes; experiments on MLP, CNN, ResNet (up to 270k parameters) show CoCD outperforms BCCD in sample efficiency and convergence loss/accuracy and provides greater stability than randomized ZO baselines.

Conclusion: Deterministic, structure-aware coordinate updates (CoCD) offer a superior alternative to randomization for lightweight zeroth-order optimization, balancing sample efficiency, convergence stability, and budget-awareness.

Abstract: Zeroth-Order (ZO) optimization is pivotal for scenarios where backpropagation is unavailable, such as memory-constrained on-device learning and black-box optimization. However, existing methods face a stark trade-off: they are either sample-inefficient (e.g., standard finite differences) or suffer from high variance due to randomized estimation (e.g., random subspace methods). In this work, we propose Coherent Coordinate Descent (CoCD), a deterministic, sample-efficient, and budget-aware ZO optimizer. Theoretically, we formalize the notion of gradient coherence and demonstrate that CoCD is equivalent to Block Cyclic Coordinate Descent (BCCD) with ``warm starts,'' effectively converting historical (stale) gradients from a liability into a computational asset. This mechanism enables $O(1)$ query complexity per step while maintaining global descent directions. Furthermore, we derive error bounds revealing a counter-intuitive insight: larger finite-difference step sizes can induce an implicit smoothing effect on the optimization landscape by reducing the effective smoothness constant, thereby improving convergence stability. Experiments on MLP, CNN, and ResNet architectures (up to 270k parameters) demonstrate that CoCD significantly outperforms BCCD in terms of sample efficiency and convergence loss/accuracy, and exhibits superior stability over randomized ZO methods. Our results suggest that deterministic, structure-aware updates offer a superior alternative to randomization for lightweight ZO optimization.

</details>


### [310] [Optimal Pattern Detection Tree for Symbolic Rule-Based Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14374)
*Young-Chae Hong,Yangho Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPDT is a mixed-integer programming-based rule learner that finds a single optimal decision rule (pattern) for binary classification, with an accompanying Branching Structure Constraints framework to inject domain knowledge and compliance constraints, maximizing coverage while minimizing false positives; experiments show optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Provide interpretable, transparent alternatives to black-box models; enable incorporation of prior knowledge and regulatory/compliance constraints; detect hidden patterns when present with optimality guarantees.

Method: Formulates a single-rule discovery as a mixed-integer program (OPDT). Introduces Branching Structure Constraints (BSC) to encode domain knowledge and constraints directly into the optimization problem; objective to maximize data coverage while minimizing misclassification (FP rate). Computational experiments validate optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets.

Result: OPDT yields an optimal-pattern with optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets within reasonable runtime; BSC enables user-driven constraints; demonstrates capability to recover underlying patterns when they exist.

Conclusion: The approach delivers an interpretable, rule-based classifier with provable optimality under the given model, suitable when a single interpretable rule suffices and domain constraints are important; scalable to moderate datasets.

Abstract: Pattern discovery in data plays a crucial role across diverse domains, including healthcare, risk assessment, and machinery maintenance. In contrast to black-box deep learning models, symbolic rule discovery emerges as a key data mining task, generating human-interpretable rules that offer both transparency and intuitive explainability. This paper introduces the Optimal Pattern Detection Tree (OPDT), a rule-based machine learning model based on novel mixed-integer programming to discover a single optimal pattern in data through binary classification. To incorporate prior knowledge and compliance requirements, we further introduce the Branching Structure Constraints (BSC) framework, which enables decision makers to encode domain knowledge and constraints directly into the model. This optimization-based approach discovers a hidden underlying pattern in datasets, when it exists, by identifying an optimal rule that maximizes coverage while minimizing the false positive rate due to misclassification. Our computational experiments show that OPDT discovers a pattern with optimality guarantees on moderately sized datasets within reasonable runtime.

</details>


### [311] [Data-Augmented Game Starts for Accelerating Self-Play Exploration in Imperfect Information Games](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14379)
*JB Lanier,Nathan Monette,Pierre Baldi,Roy Fox*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS) to speed up exploration in regularized policy-gradient methods for two-player zero-sum imperfect-information games by initializing RL data collection at intermediate states drawn from offline skilled-human demonstrations. Demonstrates reduced exploitability under fixed compute on synthetic and long-horizon game variants; discusses bias from augmented starting states and mitigates with multi-task observation flags; releases stronger OpenSpiel benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imperfect-information, large-scale games have sparse rewards and long horizons, making online exploration and equilibrium computation infeasible. Offline demos can cover high-level strategic regimes, motivating data-augmented starting states to explore relevant subgames.

Method: Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS): initialize RL data collection at starting states sampled from offline data; integrate with regularized policy-gradient for 2p0s games; test on long-horizon Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a crafted counterexample game; analyze bias via augmented start distributions and mitigate with multi-task observation flags; provide new benchmark environments with higher exploration challenges.

Result: Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS yields lower exploitability with challenging exploration. Demonstrates that biased equilibria can arise from augmented starts and shows a straightforward mitigation via multi-task observation flags. Introduces new OpenSpiel benchmarks with greater exploration difficulty while keeping exploitability tractable.

Conclusion: Data-Augmented Game Starts can substantially accelerate exploration and reduce exploitability in challenging imperfect-information games within regularized policy-gradient frameworks. However, starting-state augmentation can bias equilibria, necessitating simple mitigations (multi-task flags); the work also provides stronger benchmarks for exploration in OpenSpiel.

Abstract: Finding approximate equilibria for large-scale imperfect-information competitive games such as StarCraft, Dota, and CounterStrike remains computationally infeasible due to sparse rewards and challenging exploration over long horizons. In this paper, we propose a multi-agent starting-state sampling strategy designed to substantially accelerate online exploration in regularized policy-gradient game methods for two-player zero-sum (2p0s) games. Motivated by an assumption that offline demonstrations from skilled humans can provide good coverage of high-level strategies relevant to equilibrium play, we propose the initialization of reinforcement learning data collection at intermediate states sampled from offline data to facilitate exploration of strategically relevant subgames. Referring to this method as Data-Augmented Game Starts (DAGS), we perform experiments using synthetic datasets and analytically tractable, long-horizon control variants of two-player Kuhn Poker, Goofspiel, and a counterexample game designed to penalize biased beliefs over hidden information. Under fixed computational budgets, DAGS enables regularized policy gradient methods to achieve lower exploitability in games with significantly more challenging exploration. We show that augmenting starting state distributions when solving imperfect information games can lead to biased equilibria, and we provide a straightforward mitigation to this in the form of multi-task observation flags. Finally, we release a new set of benchmark environments that drastically increase exploration challenges and state counts in existing OpenSpiel games while keeping exploitability measurements analytically tractable.

</details>


### [312] [NodeSynth: Socially Aligned Synthetic Data for AI Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14381)
*Qazi Mamunur Rashid,Xuan Yang,Zhengzhe Yang,Yanzhou Pan,Erin van Liemt,Darlene Neal,Kshitij Pancholi,Jamila Smith-Loud*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NodeSynth is an evidence-grounded method for generating socially relevant synthetic queries to evaluate AI safety. It uses a TaG taxonomy generator anchored in real-world evidence, producing granular taxonomic expansions. Evaluated against multiple LLMs, it reports higher failure rates than human benchmarks and highlights weaknesses in guard models. The work is open-sourced for scalable evaluation and safety interventions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the lack of sociotechnical nuance in synthetic datasets used for evaluating high-stakes AI systems by generating targeted, evidence-grounded queries.

Method: Fine-tune a taxonomy generator (TaG) anchored in real-world evidence to produce granular, taxonomy-based synthetic queries. Evaluate these queries across multiple LLMs (e.g., Claude 4.5 Haiku) and perform ablation studies to quantify the impact of taxonomic expansion. Conduct independent validation on guard models (e.g., Llama-Guard-3). Open-source the end-to-end prototype and datasets.

Result: NodeSynth elicited failure rates up to five times higher than human-authored benchmarks across four mainstream LLMs. Ablation studies show that granular taxonomic expansion drives these failure rates. Independent validation reveals critical deficiencies in prominent guard models.

Conclusion: Open-source the end-to-end research prototype and datasets to enable scalable, high-stakes model evaluation and targeted safety interventions.

Abstract: Recent advancements in generative AI facilitate large-scale synthetic data generation for model evaluation. However, without targeted approaches, these datasets often lack the sociotechnical nuance required for sensitive domains. We introduce NodeSynth, an evidence-grounded methodology that generates socially relevant synthetic queries by leveraging a fine-tuned taxonomy generator (TaG) anchored in real-world evidence. Evaluated against four mainstream LLMs (e.g., Claude 4.5 Haiku), NodeSynth elicited failure rates up to five times higher than human-authored benchmarks. Ablation studies confirm that our granular taxonomic expansion significantly drives these failure rates, while independent validation reveals critical deficiencies in prominent guard models (e.g., Llama-Guard-3). We open-source our end-to-end research prototype and datasets to enable scalable, high-stakes model evaluation and targeted safety interventions (https://github.com/google-research/nodesynth).

</details>


### [313] [Watch your neighbors: Training statistically accurate chaotic systems with local phase space information](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14405)
*Joon-Hyuk Ko,Andrus Giraldo,Deok-Sun Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework for chaotic dynamics surrogates that jointly optimizes local Jacobian accuracy and long-term statistics by locally covering the attractor and minimizing pushforward distribution discrepancy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chaotic systems hinder long-term prediction; prior work either matches local Jacobians (local expansion/contraction) or mimics global statistics. There is a need for surrogates that honor both local and global dynamical properties.

Method: Construct a local covering of a chaotic attractor in phase space and analyze expansion/contraction of these coverings under the dynamics. Train a surrogate model by minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy (MMD) between the pushforward distributions of the coverings under the surrogate and the ground-truth dynamics.

Result: The approach yields improved Jacobian accuracy and remains competitive with state-of-the-art statistically accurate dynamics learning methods.

Conclusion: Bridges the local Jacobian fidelity and long-term statistical properties in a unified surrogate modeling framework for chaotic dynamics.

Abstract: Chaotic systems pose fundamental challenges for data-driven dynamics discovery, as small modeling errors lead to exponentially growing trajectory discrepancies. Since exact long-term prediction is unattainable, it is natural to ask what a good surrogate model for chaotic dynamics is. Prior work has largely focused either on reproducing the Jacobian of the underlying dynamics, which governs local expansion and contraction rates, or on training surrogate models that reproduce the ground-truth dynamics' long-term statistical behavior. In this work, we propose a new framework that aims to bridge these two paradigms by training surrogate dynamics models with accurate Jacobians and long-term statistical properties. Our method constructs a local covering of a chaotic attractor in phase space and analyzes the expansion and contraction of these coverings under the dynamics. The surrogate model is trained by minimizing the maximum mean discrepancy between the pushforward distributions of the coverings under the surrogate and ground-truth dynamics. Experiments show that our method significantly improves Jacobian accuracy while remaining competitive with state-of-the-art statistically accurate dynamics learning methods. Our code is fully available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/neighborwatch.

</details>


### [314] [GeoViSTA: Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer for Multimodal Environment Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14406)
*Yuhao Liu,Sadeer Al-Kindi,Ashok Veeraraghavan,Guha Balakrishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GeoViSTA is a vision-tabular transformer that learns unified geospatial embeddings by jointly modeling co-registered imagery and tabular census data, improving downstream predictive tasks and enabling holistic geospatial reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a modality gap: existing geospatial foundation models mainly handle imagery and neglect structured socioeconomic covariates in tabular form, limiting holistic inference about environmental, social, and health outcomes.

Method: GeoViSTA uses a vision–tabular transformer with bilateral cross-attention and a geography-aware mechanism to align continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. It is trained with a self-supervised joint masked autoencoding objective that reconstructs missing image patches and tabular rows using local spatial context and cross-modal cues on co-registered data.

Result: Unified embeddings yield better linear probing performance on downstream tasks, outperforming baselines in predicting disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency across held-out regions.

Conclusion: Joint modeling of the physical environment and structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

Abstract: Large-scale pretraining on Earth observation imagery has yielded powerful representations of the natural and built environment. However, most existing geospatial foundation models do not directly model the structured socioeconomic covariates typically stored in tabular form. This modality gap limits their ability to capture the complete total environment, which is critical for reasoning about complex environmental, social, and health-related outcomes. In this work, we propose GeoViSTA (Geospatial Vision-Tabular Transformer), a vision-tabular architecture that learns unified geospatial embeddings from co-registered gridded imagery and tabular data. GeoViSTA utilizes bilateral cross-attention to exchange spatial and semantic information across modalities, guided by a geography-aware attention mechanism that aligns continuous image patches with irregular census-tract tokens. We train GeoViSTA with a self-supervised joint masked-autoencoding objective, forcing it to recover missing image patches and tabular rows using local spatial context and cross-modal cues. Empirically, GeoViSTA's unified embeddings improve linear probing performance on high-impact downstream tasks, outperforming baselines in predicting disease-specific mortality and fire hazard frequency across held-out regions. These results demonstrate that jointly modeling the physical environment alongside structured socioeconomic context yields highly transferable representations for holistic geospatial inference.

</details>


### [315] [MahaVar: OOD Detection via Class-wise Mahalanobis Distance Variance under Neural Collapse](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14413)
*Donghwan Kim,Hyunsoo Yoon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-hoc OOD detector MahaVar adds a class-wise distance variance term to Mahalanobis distances, grounded in Neural Collapse, achieving state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet under OpenOOD v1.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OOD detection is critical for safe deployment of DNNs. The authors observe that in-distribution (ID) samples exhibit a sharp minimum structure with high variance across class-wise Mahalanobis distances, while OOD samples show flatter, lower-variance patterns. This motivates a variance-based augmentation to improve discrimination.

Method: Compute per-class Mahalanobis distances for an input, then compute the variance of these distances across classes. Combine this class-wise variance term with the standard Mahalanobis distance to form the MahaVar score in a post-hoc detector. The approach is theoretically underpinned by relaxed Neural Collapse assumptions relating within-class compactness and inter-class separation.

Result: MahaVar yields state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet benchmarks under the OpenOOD v1.5 protocol, with consistent improvements in AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods across all benchmarks.

Conclusion: Incorporating a class-wise distance variance term into the Mahalanobis-based OOD detector provides a simple, effective post-hoc method with theoretical grounding in Neural Collapse, delivering strong empirical gains on standard OOD benchmarks.

Abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection is a critical component for ensuring the reliability of deep neural networks in safety-critical applications. In this work, we present a key empirical observation: for in-distribution (ID) samples, class-wise Mahalanobis distances exhibit a pronounced sharp minimum structure, where the distance to the nearest class is small while distances to all other classes remain large, resulting in high variance across classes. In contrast, OOD samples tend to exhibit a less pronounced sharp minimum structure, producing comparatively lower variance across classes. We further provide a theoretical analysis grounding this observation in Neural Collapse geometry: under relaxed Neural Collapse assumptions on within-class compactness and inter-class separation, ID samples are shown to structurally exhibit high class-wise distance variance, offering a theoretical basis for its use as an OOD score. Motivated by this observation and its theoretical backing, we propose MahaVar, a simple and effective post-hoc OOD detector that augments the Mahalanobis distance with a class-wise distance variance term. Following the OpenOOD v1.5 benchmark protocol, MahaVar achieves state-of-the-art performance on CIFAR-100 and ImageNet, with consistent improvements in both AUROC and FPR@95 over existing Mahalanobis-based methods across all benchmarks.

</details>


### [316] [What if Tomorrow is the World Cup Final? Counterfactual Time Series Forecasting with Textual Conditions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14422)
*Shuqi Gu,Yongxiang Zhao,Baoyu Jing,Kan Ren*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, proposes an evaluation framework for factual and counterfactual settings, and a text-attribution mechanism to separate mutable vs immutable factors to improve forecasting under complex textual conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Forecasting future sequences is influenced by both historical data and forthcoming events; existing methods rely on historical or factual futures and fail to handle counterfactual scenarios or rich textual conditions.

Method: Defines the task of counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, proposes an evaluation framework covering factual and counterfactual cases (even without ground-truth series), and introduces a text-attribution mechanism to distinguish mutable from immutable factors to improve forecast accuracy under stochastic textual conditions; mentions a project page for implementation.

Result: Conceptual framework and proposed methodology; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.

Conclusion: Counterfactual forecasting with textual conditions, supported by an evaluation framework and text-attribution, offers better generalization to real-world, complex scenarios; the approach aims to enable more flexible, condition-aware forecasting.

Abstract: Time series forecasting has become increasingly critical in real-world scenarios, where future sequences are influenced not only by historical patterns but also by forthcoming events. In this context, forecasting must dynamically adapt to complex and stochastic future conditions, which introduces fundamental challenges in both forecasting and evaluation. Traditional methods typically rely on historical data or factual future conditions, while overlooking counterfactual scenarios. Furthermore, many existing approaches are restricted to simple structured conditions, limiting their ability to generalize to the real-world complexities. To address these gaps, we introduce the task of counterfactual time series forecasting with textual conditions, enabling more flexible and condition-aware forecasting. We propose a comprehensive evaluation framework that encompasses both factual and counterfactual settings, even in the absence of ground truth time series. Additionally, we present a novel text-attribution mechanism that distinguishes mutable from immutable factors, thereby improving forecast accuracy under sophisticated and stochastic textual conditions. The project page is at https://seqml.github.io/TADiff/

</details>


### [317] [Collaborative Yet Personalized Policy Training: Single-Timescale Federated Actor-Critic](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14423)
*Leo Muxing Wang,Pengkun Yang,Lili Su*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Federated actor-critic with a shared linear subspace and personalized local policies achieving finite-time convergence under Markovian sampling, with linear speedup in the number of agents; validated through PPO experiments on Hopper-v5 with action-map heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address environmental heterogeneity and personalization in multi-agent RL by learning a common subspace that is shared across agents while keeping personalized local policy components.

Method: Agents maintain a common linear subspace representation and own local critic heads and actors. They iteratively update the shared subspace, local critics, and local policies under single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling. The analysis introduces a joint linear-approximation framework, a perturbation analysis for projected subspace updates and QR decomposition, conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise, and fine-grained comparisons between Markovian sampling and temporally frozen policies.

Result: Convergence rates: critic error ~ O~(1/((1-γ)^4 sqrt(TK))) and policy gradient norm ~ O~(1/((1-γ)^6 sqrt(TK))). Demonstrates linear speedup in K despite heterogeneous trajectories. Empirical results show gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO, with transfer from the learned shared trunk in federated Hopper-v5 experiments.

Conclusion: The proposed federated actor-critic framework successfully combines shared representation with personalized policies, provides finite-time convergence guarantees, and yields practical performance gains in heterogeneous multi-agent RL settings.

Abstract: Despite the popularity of the actor-critic method and the practical needs of collaborative policy training, existing works typically either overlook environmental heterogeneity or give up personalization altogether by training a single shared policy across all agents. We consider a federated actor-critic framework in which agents share a common linear subspace representation while maintaining personalized local policy components, and agents iteratively estimate the common subspace, local critic heads, and local policies (i.e., actors). Under canonical single-timescale updates with Markovian sampling, we establish finite-time convergence via a novel joint linear approximation framework. Specifically, we show that the critic error converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^4\sqrt{TK}))$, and the policy gradient norm converges to zero at the rate of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(1/((1-γ)^6\sqrt{TK}))$, where $T$ is the number of rounds, $K$ is the number of agents, and $γ\in (0,1)$ is the discount factor. These results demonstrate linear speedup with respect to the number of agents $K$, despite heterogeneous Markovian trajectories under distinct transition kernels and coupled learning dynamics. To address these challenges, we develop a new perturbation analysis for the projected subspace updates and QR decomposition steps, together with conditional mixing arguments for heterogeneous Markovian noise. Furthermore, to handle the additional complications induced by policy updates and temporal dependence, we establish fine-grained characterizations of the discrepancies between function evaluations under Markovian sampling and under temporally frozen policies. Experiments instantiate the framework within PPO on federated \texttt{Hopper-v5} action-map heterogeneity, showing gains over Single PPO and FedAvg PPO and downstream transfer from the learned shared trunk.

</details>


### [318] [FrontierSmith: Synthesizing Open-Ended Coding Problems at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14445)
*Runyuan He,Qiuyang Mang,Shang Zhou,Kaiyuan Liu,Hanchen Li,Huanzhi Mao,Qizheng Zhang,Zerui Li,Bo Peng,Lufeng Cheng,Tianfu Fu,Yichuan Wang,Wenhao Chai,Jingbo Shang,Alex Dimakis,Joseph E. Gonzalez,Alvin Cheung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FrontierSmith automates the generation of open-ended coding problems from existing closed-ended seeds, selects diverse problem variants via an idea-divergence metric, and uses agents to create tests/verifiers. Training on synthesized data yields notable gains on two open-ended benchmarks, suggesting scalable bootstrapping of long-horizon coding data from seeds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Open-ended coding tasks are scarce and expensive to construct, hindering LLM progress on long-horizon coding. The paper proposes an automated pipeline to synthesize large-scale open-ended problems starting from closed-ended seeds to train stronger coders.

Method: Iteratively evolve open-ended variants from competitive programming tasks by changing goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs; quantify diversity with an idea-divergence metric; select high-divergence problems; have agents generate test cases and verifiers; train models on synthesized data; evaluate on FrontierCS and ALE-bench.

Result: On Qwen3.5-9B: +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 Elo on ALE-bench; on Qwen3.5-27B: +12.12 score and +309.12 Elo. Synthesized problems induce longer interactions and more token usage, similar to human-curated data.

Conclusion: Closed-ended seeds can practically bootstrap long-horizon, open-ended coding data; FrontierSmith demonstrates scalable generation of diverse, verifiable open-ended problems and yields substantial model gains.

Abstract: Many real-world coding challenges are open-ended and admit no known optimal solution. Yet, recent progress in LLM coding has focused on well-defined tasks such as feature implementation, bug fixing, and competitive programming. Open-ended coding remains a weak spot for LLMs, largely because open-ended training problems are scarce and expensive to construct. Our goal is to synthesize open-ended coding problems at scale to train stronger LLM coders. We introduce FrontierSmith, an automated system for iteratively evolving open-ended problems from existing closed-ended coding tasks. Starting from competitive programming problems, FrontierSmith generates candidate open-ended variants by changing the problems'goals, restricting outputs, and generalizing inputs. It then uses a quantitative idea divergence metric to select problems that elicit genuinely diverse approaches from different solvers. Agents then generate test cases and verifiers for the surviving candidates. On two open-ended coding benchmarks, training on our synthesized data yields substantial gains over the base models: Qwen3.5-9B improves by +8.82 score on FrontierCS and +306.36 (Elo-rating-based performance) on ALE-bench; Qwen3.5-27B improves by +12.12 and +309.12, respectively. The synthesized problems also make agents take more turns and use more tokens, similar to human-curated ones, suggesting that closed-ended seeds can be a practical starting point for long-horizon coding data.

</details>


### [319] [When Answers Stray from Questions: Hallucination Detection via Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14449)
*Siyang Yao,Erhu Feng,Yubin Xia*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: QAOD is a single-pass probing framework that isolates question-orthogonal signals from answer representations to improve LLM hallucination detection, combining joint in-domain probing with an orthogonal-only, robust OOD transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hallucination detection in LLMs must balance accuracy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Black-box, iterative probes are accurate but costly; white-box single-pass probes struggle under domain shift by treating answer representations in isolation.

Method: QAOD performs an orthogonal decomposition: it projects the question-aligned direction from the answer representation to obtain a question-orthogonal component that suppresses domain-conditioned variation. It identifies informative layers via diversity-penalized Fisher scoring and discriminative neurons via Fisher importance. It uses two probing strategies: (1) joint probe combining the orthogonal component with question context for high in-domain discriminability, and (2) orthogonal component alone for domain-agnostic, robust transfer.

Result: The joint probe achieves the best in-domain AUROC across evaluated model-dataset pairs. The orthogonal-only probe yields the strongest out-of-domain transfer, surpassing the best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ, at under 25% of generation cost.

Conclusion: QAOD offers a single-pass, efficient probing framework that reduces domain-conditioned variation and provides competitive in-domain and robust cross-domain performance, with a trade-off between joint disambiguation and domain-agnostic factuality signals.

Abstract: Hallucination detection in large language models (LLMs) requires balancing accu racy, efficiency, and robustness to distribution shift. Black-box consistency methods are effective but demand repeated inference; single-pass white-box probes are effi cient yet treat answer representations in isolation, often degrading sharply under domain shift. We propose QAOD (Question-Answer Orthogonal Decomposition), a single-pass framework that projects away the question-aligned direction from the answer representation to obtain a question-orthogonal component that suppresses domain-conditioned variation. To identify informative signals, QAOD further selects layers via diversity-penalized Fisher scoring and discriminative neurons via Fisher importance. To address both in-domain detection and cross-domain generalization, we design two complementary probing strategies: pairing the or thogonal component with question context yields a joint probe that maximizes in-domain discriminability, while using the orthogonal component alone preserves domain-agnostic factuality signals for robust transfer. QAOD's joint probe achieves the best in-domain AUROC across all evaluated model-dataset pairs, while the orthogonal-only probe delivers the strongest OOD transfer, surpassing the best white-box baseline by up to 21% on BioASQ at under 25% of generation cost.

</details>


### [320] [LiSA: Lifelong Safety Adaptation via Conservative Policy Induction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14454)
*Minbeom Kim,Lesly Miculicich,Bhavana Dalvi Mishra,Mihir Parmar,Phillip Wallis,Bharath Chandrasekhar,Kyomin Jung,Tomas Pfister,Long T. Le*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LiSA is a lifelong safety adaptation framework that enhances fixed guardrails via memory-driven policy induction, enabling reuse of failures as abstractions with conflict-aware rules and evidence-based gating under sparse/noisy feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guardrails in AI agents must adapt to local privacy norms, organizational policies, and user expectations in real-world settings. Deployment feedback is sparse and noisy, and repeated fine-tuning is impractical. There is a need for an approach that incrementally learns from limited signals while preventing harmful overgeneralization.

Method: Structured memory-based policy induction: convert occasional failures into reusable policy abstractions; add conflict-aware local rules to mitigate overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts; apply evidence-aware confidence gating via a posterior lower bound so memory reuse scales with accumulated evidence; evaluate across PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, and AgentHarm, comparing to memory-based baselines; robustness tested under noisy feedback (up to 20% label flips) and latency/performance improvements with backbone scaling.

Result: LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback; remains robust under noisy feedback up to 20% label flips; pushes the latency–performance frontier beyond backbone model scaling across three deployments.

Conclusion: LiSA offers a practical, scalable path to securing AI agents against unpredictable long-tail edge risks in real-world deployments.

Abstract: As AI agents move from chat interfaces to systems that read private data, call tools, and execute multi-step workflows, guardrails become a last line of defense against concrete deployment harms. In these settings, guardrail failures are no longer merely answer-quality errors: they can leak secrets, authorize unsafe actions, or block legitimate work. The hardest failures are often contextual: whether an action is acceptable depends on local privacy norms, organizational policies, and user expectations that resist pre-deployment specification. This creates a practical gap: guardrails must adapt to their own operating environments, yet deployment feedback is typically limited to sparse, noisy user-reported failures, and repeated fine-tuning is often impractical. To address this gap, we propose LiSA (Lifelong Safety Adaptation), a conservative policy induction framework that improves a fixed base guardrail through structured memory. LiSA converts occasional failures into reusable policy abstractions so that sparse reports can generalize beyond individual cases, adds conflict-aware local rules to prevent overgeneralization in mixed-label contexts, and applies evidence-aware confidence gating via a posterior lower bound, so that memory reuse scales with accumulated evidence rather than empirical accuracy alone. Across PrivacyLens+, ConFaide+, and AgentHarm, LiSA consistently outperforms strong memory-based baselines under sparse feedback, remains robust under noisy user feedback even at 20% label-flip rates, and pushes the latency--performance frontier beyond backbone model scaling. Ultimately, LiSA offers a practical path to secure AI agents against the unpredictable long tail of real-world edge risks.

</details>


### [321] [Focused PU learning from imbalanced data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14467)
*Elias Zavitsanos,Georgios Paliouras*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces a focused empirical risk estimator for PU learning in highly imbalanced data, achieving state-of-the-art results under SCAR and SAR, with a real-world validation in financial misstatement detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PU learning is well-studied but struggles in highly imbalanced settings and when positives are hard to distinguish from negatives; many real-world problems have mostly unlabeled data dominated by negatives, with only a subset of positives.

Method: Proposes a focused empirical risk estimator that leverages both positive and unlabeled data to train binary classifiers under PU learning settings.

Result: Empirical evaluations show state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced datasets under two labeling mechanisms (SCAR and SAR); validated by a real-world application in financial misstatement detection.

Conclusion: The proposed PU learning approach effectively handles imbalanced and hard-to-detect positives, offering strong performance in both controlled benchmarks and real-world tasks.

Abstract: We propose a new method of learning from positive and unlabeled (PU) examples in highly imbalanced datasets. Many real-world problems, such as disease gene identification, targeted marketing, fraud detection, and recommender systems, are hard to address with machine learning methods, due to limited labeled data. Often, training data comprises positive and unlabeled instances, the latter typically being dominated by negative, but including also several positive instances. While PU learning is well-studied, few methods address imbalanced settings or hard-to-detect positive examples that resemble negative ones. Our approach uses a focused empirical risk estimator, incorporating both positive and unlabeled examples to train binary classifiers. Empirical evaluations demonstrate state-of-the-art performance on imbalanced datasets under two labeling mechanisms - selecting positives completely at random (SCAR) and selecting at random (SAR). Beyond these controlled experiments, we demonstrate the value of the proposed method in the real-world application of financial misstatement detection.

</details>


### [322] [Test-Time Learning with an Evolving Library](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14477)
*Weijia Xu,Alessandro Sordoni,Chandan Singh,Zelalem Gero,Michel Galley,Xingdi Yuan,Jianfeng Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EvoLib is a test-time learning framework that builds a shared library of knowledge abstractions from a model’s inference trajectories, enabling LLMs to accumulate, reuse, and evolve knowledge without parameter updates or external supervision. It uses a principled weighting/consolidation mechanism to optimize for immediate usefulness and long-term value, allowing instance-specific ideas to become general reusable abstractions. It outperforms top test-time scaling/learning methods on challenging math, code, and multi-turn agentic tasks without ground-truth feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable continual improvement of LLM capabilities without retraining or external supervision by creating reusable knowledge abstractions that evolve over time, addressing limitations of existing test-time adaptation methods.

Method: Maintain a shared library of modular skills and reflective insights automatically extracted from the model’s inference trajectories. Employ a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that jointly optimizes immediate utility and long-term value, allowing simple instance-specific abstractions to evolve into more general reusable ones. No parameter updates or external supervision are required.

Result: EvoLib achieves substantial improvements over the top test-time scaling and learning methods without ground-truth feedback on benchmarks spanning mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments.

Conclusion: EvoLib demonstrates a practical, self-evolving test-time knowledge framework that enables LLMs to accumulate and generalize knowledge autonomously, with broad applicability across domains and tasks.

Abstract: We introduce EvoLib, a test-time learning framework that enables large language models to accumulate, reuse, and evolve knowledge across problem instances without parameter updates or external supervision. Instead of adapting model parameters, our approach maintains a shared library of knowledge abstractions, including modular skills and reflective insights, automatically extracted from the model's own inference trajectories. To support continual improvement, we introduce a principled weighting and consolidation mechanism that jointly optimizes for immediate utility and long-term value. This allows simple, instance-specific abstractions to evolve into more general and reusable ones over time. Across challenging benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, code generation, and multi-turn agentic environments, EvoLib improves substantially over the top test-time scaling and learning methods without ground-truth feedback.

</details>


### [323] [A Novel Schur-Decomposition-Based Weight Projection Method for Stable State-Space Neural-Network Architectures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14489)
*Sergio Vanegas,Lasse Lensu,Fredy Ruiz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Schur-based projection enforces discrete-time stability in linear state-space layers via projecting the Schur form factor onto a stable region, yielding a backprop-friendly, low-parameter method with competitive accuracy to stable-system identification on synthetic data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Guarantee asymptotic stability when learning dynamical systems from data, while keeping models lightweight and trainable via backpropagation.

Method: Compute the real Schur decomposition of the state matrix, project its quasi-triangular factor onto the nearest stable peer (eigenvalues within the unit circle) during training; maintain (or reuse) a factorized formulation to reduce parameter count; ensures stable dynamics with minimal overparameterization; backpropagation-compatible.

Result: On synthetic linear systems, the approach achieves accuracy and convergence comparable to state-of-the-art stable-system identification methods, with only a modest increase in computational complexity; lower parameter count aids convergence in stacked networks with static nonlinearities, without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: Schur-based projection provides a numerically robust framework for identifying complex discrete-time dynamics that satisfies strict asymptotic-stability requirements and performs on par with current State-of-the-Art methods, with favorable training properties.

Abstract: Building black-box models for dynamical systems from data is a challenging problem in machine learning, especially when asymptotic stability guarantees are required. In this paper, we introduce a novel stability-ensuring and backpropagation-compatible projection scheme based on the Schur decomposition for the state matrix of linear discrete-time state-space layers, as well as an alternative pre-factorized formulation of the methodology. The proposed methods dynamically project the quasi-triangular factor of the state matrix's real Schur decomposition onto its nearest stable peer, ensuring stable dynamics with minimal overparameterization. Experiments on synthetic linear systems demonstrate that the method achieves accuracy and convergence rates comparable to those of state-of-the-art stable-system identification techniques, despite a marginal increase in computational complexity. Furthermore, the lower weight count facilitates convergence during training without sacrificing accuracy in stacked neural-network architectures with static nonlinearities targeting real-world datasets. These results suggest that the Schur-based projection provides a numerically robust framework for identifying complex dynamics on par with the State of the Art while satisfying strict asymptotic-stability requirements.

</details>


### [324] [ROAD: Adaptive Data Mixing for Offline-to-Online Reinforcement Learning via Bi-Level Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14497)
*Letian Yang,Xu Liu,Yiqiang Lu,Jian Liu,Weiqiang Wang,Shuai Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ROAD dynamically adapts data replay in offline-to-online RL via a bi-level optimization with a bandit-based surrogate, improving stability and asymptotic performance without manual tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-stationary distribution shift between offline data and online policy; static replay strategies fail to adapt to varying environments and training dynamics.

Method: Formulate data selection as bi-level optimization: outer level meta-decision on data mixing for policy performance; inner level standard Q-learning updates; implement via a multi-armed bandit using a surrogate objective approximating the bi-level gradient; preserves offline priors and mitigates value overestimation; plug-and-play.

Result: Empirical results show ROAD consistently outperforms existing data replay methods across diverse datasets, reducing need for context-specific tuning, achieving better stability and superior final performance.

Conclusion: ROAD provides an automatic, adaptable data replay mechanism for offline-to-online RL, enabling stable fine-tuning and improved asymptotic performance across environments.

Abstract: Offline-to-online reinforcement learning harnesses the stability of offline pretraining and the flexibility of online fine-tuning. A key challenge lies in the non-stationary distribution shift between offline datasets and the evolving online policy. Common approaches often rely on static mixing ratios or heuristic-based replay strategies, which lack adaptability to different environments and varying training dynamics, resulting in suboptimal tradeoff between stability and asymptotic performance. In this work, we propose Reinforcement Learning with Optimized Adaptive Data-mixing (ROAD), a dynamic plug-and-play framework that automates the data replay process. We identify a fundamental objective misalignment in existing approaches. To tackle this, we formulate the data selection problem as a bi-level optimization process, interpreting the data mixing strategy as a meta-decision governing the policy performance (outer-level) during online fine-tuning, while the conventional Q-learning updates operate at the inner level. To make it tractable, we propose a practical algorithm using a multi-armed bandit mechanism. This is guided by a surrogate objective approximating the bi-level gradient, which simultaneously maintains offline priors and prevents value overestimation. Our empirical results demonstrate that this approach consistently outperforms existing data replay methods across various datasets, eliminating the need for manual, context-specific adjustments while achieving superior stability and asymptotic performance.

</details>


### [325] [Enjoy Your Layer Normalization with the Computational Efficiency of RMSNorm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14521)
*Yuxin Guo,Yihao Yue,Yunhao Ni,Yizhou Ruan,Jie Luo,Wenjun Wu,Lei Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A framework to identify and replace layer normalization (LN) with RMSNorm without changing model function by folding LN centering into preceding linear layers using CCC and CBWC, enabling exact or near-exact inference-time acceleration in many DNNs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LN adds per-sample centering/scale overhead at inference. RMSNorm is more efficient but may discard centering benefits. A principled method to determine when LN can be replaced without altering the function enables practical speedups with minimal accuracy impact.

Method: Introduce CCC (column-centered constraint) and CBWC (column-based weight centering) to force zero-mean outputs and centered weights, allowing LN centering to be folded into upstream linear layers. Define foldable LN for arbitrary DNNs, and develop a graph-based detection algorithm to identify foldable LN instances across architectures.

Result: Many LNs in widely used architectures are foldable, enabling exact inference-time conversion to RMSNorm and end-to-end acceleration of 2%–12% with no change in predictions. Experiments across multiple tasks show that even when exact equivalence is imperfect in practice, the approach remains competitive with vanilla LN while improving efficiency.

Conclusion: The framework generalizes to arbitrary DNNs, enabling exact or near-exact LN-to-RMSNorm replacements with practical speedups and minimal accuracy loss, thereby enhancing normalization efficiency across diverse models.

Abstract: Layer normalization (LN) is a fundamental component in modern deep learning, but its per-sample centering and scaling introduce non-negligible inference overhead. RMSNorm improves efficiency by removing the centering operation, yet this may discard benefits associated with centering. This paper propose a framework to determine whether an LN in an arbitrary DNN can be replaced by RMSNorm without changing the model function. The key idea is to fold LN's centering operation into upstream general linear layers by enforcing zero-mean outputs through the column-centered constraint (CCC) and column-based weight centering (CBWC). We extend the analysis to arbitrary DNNs, define such LNs as foldable LNs, and develop a graph-based detection algorithm. Our analysis shows that many LNs in widely used architectures are foldable, enabling exact inference-time conversion and end-to-end acceleration of 2% to 12% without changing model predictions. Experiments across multiple task families further show that, when exact equivalence is partially broken in practical training settings, our method remains competitive with vanilla LN while improving efficiency.

</details>


### [326] [Lang2MLIP: End-to-End Language-to-Machine Learning Interatomic Potential Development with Autonomous Agentic Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14527)
*Wenwen Li,Yuki Orimo,Nontawat Charoenphakdee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Lang2MLIP proposes an LLM-powered, multi-agent framework that treats MLIP development as a sequential decision-making process, removing fixed pipelines and enabling self-correction, demonstrated on a solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) system; it shows promise for automating MLIP development and widening accessibility for non-experts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Developing MLIPs for complex materials demands cross-disciplinary expertise and iterative active learning. Fixed, expert-designed pipelines limit adaptability to heterogeneous materials and unknown curricula. A flexible, automated approach could lower barriers and tailor workflows to diverse materials systems.

Method: A multi-agent system uses large language models to interpret natural-language input and act in a sequential decision-making loop. At each step, agents observe the current dataset, model, evaluation results, and execution log, then autonomously select actions to improve the MLIP. The pipeline is not fixed; agents can revisit earlier subsystems to correct failures. Evaluation is conducted on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components/interfaces.

Result: The approach is evaluated on an SEI system, and results indicate that LLM-based multi-agent automation can effectively develop MLIPs and make the process more accessible to non-experts, suggesting viability of the framework.

Conclusion: Lang2MLIP demonstrates that LLM-driven, adaptive, end-to-end MLIP development is feasible and can bypass rigid pipelines, potentially accelerating MLIP creation for heterogeneous materials while lowering the required expertise; further validation across diverse materials and robustness analyses are warranted.

Abstract: Developing machine learning interatomic potentials (MLIPs) for complex materials systems remains challenging because it requires expertise in atomistic simulations, machine learning, and workflow design, as well as iterative active learning procedures. Existing automated pipelines typically assume a fixed sequence of stages or depend on domain experts, which limits their adaptability to heterogeneous materials systems where the optimal curriculum is not known in advance. To lower the barrier to developing MLIPs for non-experts, we propose Lang2MLIP, a multi-agent framework that takes natural-language input and formulates end-to-end MLIP development as a sequential decision-making problem solved by large language models (LLMs). At each step, a decision-making agent observes the current dataset, model, evaluation results, and execution log, and then automatically selects an appropriate action to improve the model. This removes the need for a predefined pipeline and enables the agent to self-correct by revisiting earlier subsystems when new failures arise. We evaluate this approach on a solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) system with multiple components and interfaces. These results suggest that LLM-based multi-agent systems are a promising direction for automating MLIP development and making it more accessible to non-experts.

</details>


### [327] [Exploring Geographic Relative Space in Large Language Models through Activation Patching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14535)
*Stef De Sabbata,Rahul Baiju,Stefano Mizzaro,Kevin Roitero*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This abstract proposes studying how LLMs represent relative geographic space using activation patching to support mechanistic interpretability and safety in geographic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addresses safety concerns and the interpretability gap when deploying LLMs for geographic reasoning, where inner workings are poorly understood.

Method: Apply activation patching, a mechanistic interpretability technique, to analyze LLM activations related to relative geographic space.

Result: The abstract outlines a methodological approach and aims to yield insights into spatial representations; no empirical results are provided in the abstract.

Conclusion: If successful, activation patching could illuminate how geographic reasoning is encoded in LLMs and inform safety and governance in spatial AI applications.

Abstract: The increased use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in geography raises substantial questions about the safety of integrating these tools across a wide range of processes and analyses, given our very limited understanding of their inner workings. In this extended abstract, we examine how LLMs process relative geographic space using activation patching, an emerging tool for mechanistic interpretability.

</details>


### [328] [RxEval: A Prescription-Level Benchmark for Evaluating LLM Medication Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14543)
*Shuhao Chen,Weisen Jiang,Changmiao Wang,Xiaoqing Wu,Xuanren Shi,Yu Zhang,James T. Kwok*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RxEval is a novel, prescription-level benchmark for evaluating LLMs on time-sensitive, patient-specific medication decision-making, addressing gaps in prior admission-level benchmarks that use coarse drug codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To capture the per-timepoint, information-rich nature of real prescribing and assess LLMs’ ability to select exact medication-dose-route triples from real prescriptions with patient-specific distractors.

Method: Construct a multiple-choice benchmark (1,547 questions, 584 patients, 18 diagnostic categories, 969 medications) where each question presents a detailed patient profile and time-ordered trajectory; distractors generated via reasoning-chain perturbation; evaluation across 16 LLMs.

Result: RxEval is challenging and discriminative: F1 45.18–77.10 across models; best Exact Match 46.10%; error analysis shows models can miss stated patient information and fail to derive clinical conclusions.

Conclusion: RxEval provides a robust testbed for evaluation of LLM prescribing capabilities, highlighting gaps even in frontier models and signaling need for improved reasoning, information integration, and clinical inference in generated prescriptions.

Abstract: Inpatient medication recommendation requires clinicians to repeatedly select specific medications, doses, and routes as a patient's condition evolves. Existing benchmarks formulate this task as admission-level prediction over coarse drug codes with multi-hot diagnostic and procedure code inputs, failing to capture the per-timepoint, information-rich nature of real prescribing. We propose RxEval, a prescription-level benchmark that evaluates LLM prescribing capability by multiple-choice questions: each question presents a detailed patient profile and time-ordered clinical trajectory, requiring selection of specific medication-dose-route triples from real prescriptions and patient-specific distractors generated via reasoning-chain perturbation. RxEval comprises 1,547 questions spanning 584 patients, 18 diagnostic categories, and 969 unique medications. Evaluation of 16 LLMs shows that RxEval is both challenging and discriminative: F1 ranges from 45.18 to 77.10 across models, and the best Exact Match is only 46.10%. Error analysis reveals that even frontier models may overlook stated patient information and fail to derive clinical conclusions.

</details>


### [329] [Discovering Physical Directions in Weight Space: Composing Neural PDE Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14546)
*Pengkai Wang,Pengwei Liu,Yuanyi Wang,Guanyu Chen,Xingyu Ren,Xiaolong Li,Zhongkai Hao,Yuting Kong,Qixin Zhang,Dong Ni*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Calibration-Conditioned Merge (CCM) enables post-hoc assembly of neural PDE experts along a learned physical direction, enabling training-free transfer across PDE regimes with a single merged checkpoint. Endpoints reveal a calibratable weight-space direction tied to physical parameters, not mere drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Understand how fine-tuning a shared neural PDE operator across multiple physical regimes reorganizes weight updates and whether a reusable physical structure emerges; develop a practical method to compose experts along that structure for extrapolation.

Method: Begin with a shared family anchor; fine-tune low- and high-regime endpoint experts and observe that updates separate into a family-shared adaptation and a parameter-aligned direction. Interpret endpoints as finite-difference probes of a local physical direction in weight space. Propose CCM, a post-hoc coordinate readout that uses physical metadata, a calibrated coordinate mapping, or a short rollout prefix to infer a target composition coordinate and deploy a single merged checkpoint for the remainder of rollout.

Result: CCM achieves strongest gains in extrapolative regimes across reaction–diffusion, viscosity-parameterized 2D Navier–Stokes, and radial dam-break dynamics, reducing out-of-distribution rollout error relative to the family anchor by 54.2%, 42.8%, and 13.8%, respectively. Ablations and additional experiments indicate endpoint fine-tuning reflects a calibratable physical direction rather than arbitrary checkpoint drift, enabling training-free transfer across PDE regimes.

Conclusion: Endpoint fine-tuning discloses a calibratable physical direction in weight space; CCM provides a simple, effective mechanism to compose neural PDE experts along that direction, enabling robust cross-regime deployment with a single merged model.

Abstract: Recent advances in neural operators have made partial differential equation (PDE) surrogate modeling increasingly scalable and transferable through large-scale pretraining and in-context adaptation. However, after a shared operator is fine-tuned to multiple regimes within a continuous physical family, it remains unclear whether the resulting weight-space updates merely form isolated regime experts or reveal reusable physical structure. Starting from a shared family anchor, we fine-tune low- and high-regime endpoint experts and show that their updates can be separated into a family-shared adaptation and a direction aligned with the underlying physical parameter. This separation reinterprets endpoint experts as finite-difference probes of a local physical direction in weight space, explaining why static averaging can interpolate between regimes but attenuates endpoint-specific physics. Building on this perspective, we propose Calibration-Conditioned Merge (CCM), a post-hoc coordinate readout method for composing neural PDE experts along this physical direction. Given physical metadata, a calibrated coordinate mapping, or a short observed rollout prefix, CCM infers the target composition coordinate and deploys a single merged checkpoint for the remaining rollout.
