We evaluate CCM on the reaction--diffusion system, viscosity-parameterized two-dimensional Navier--Stokes equations, and radial dam-break dynamics. Across these benchmarks, CCM achieves its strongest gains in extrapolative regimes, reducing out-of-distribution rollout error relative to the family anchor by 54.2%, 42.8%, and 13.8%, respectively. Further experiments across FNO scales, a DPOT-style backbone, and ablations confirm that endpoint fine-tuning is not arbitrary checkpoint drift, but reveals a calibratable physical direction for training-free transfer across PDE regimes.

</details>


### [330] [Multi-Dimensional Model Integrity and Responsibility Assessment Index and Scoring Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14550)
*Phuc Truong Loc Nguyen,Thanh Hung Do,Truong Thanh Hung Nguyen,Hung Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MIRAI is a unified, multi-dimension evaluation index for tabular models that aggregates five dimensions—explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, sustainability—along with predictive performance into a single score, showing that higher accuracy does not always imply better overall integrity; simpler models can outperform deep tabular models in cross-dimensional balance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current practice evaluates the five dimensions in isolation, limiting direct model comparison and practical selection in regulated settings; a unified, comparable framework is needed.

Method: Define MIRAI as normalized, direction-aligned scores for five dimensions, aggregated into a single score; apply the framework to healthcare, financial, and socioeconomic tabular datasets to compare models with different architectures and compute budgets.

Result: Better predictive performance does not necessarily equate to higher overall integrity; in several cases, simpler models achieve stronger cross-dimensional balance than more complex deep tabular models.

Conclusion: MIRAI provides a compact, practical basis for responsible model selection in regulated environments.

Abstract: Artificial intelligence in high-stakes tabular domains cannot be evaluated by predictive performance alone, yet current practice still assesses explainability, fairness, robustness, privacy, and sustainability mostly in isolation. We propose the Model Integrity and Responsibility Assessment Index (MIRAI), a unified evaluation framework that measures tabular models across these five dimensions under a controlled comparison setting and aggregates them into a single score. MIRAI combines established metrics through normalized and direction-aligned dimension scores, which enables direct comparison across models with different architectural and computational profiles. Experiments on healthcare, financial, and socioeconomic datasets show that higher predictive performance does not necessarily imply better overall integrity and responsibility. In several cases, simpler models achieve a stronger cross-dimensional balance than more complex deep tabular architectures. MIRAI provides a compact and practical basis for responsible model selection in regulated settings.

</details>


### [331] [SeesawNet: Towards Non-stationary Time Series Forecasting with Balanced Modeling of Common and Specific Dependencies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14551)
*Hao Li,Lu Zhang,Liu Chong,Yankai Chen,Pengyang Wang,Yingjie Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SeesawNet introduces Adaptive Stationary-Nonstationary Attention (ASNA) to balance common and instance-specific dependencies in non-stationary multivariate time series. It alternates temporal and channel modeling to capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies, achieving state-of-the-art performance on real benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instance normalization reduces distribution shifts but can erase instance-specific temporal and cross-channel structure essential for non-stationary data. There is a need to adaptively model both shared (common) and instance-specific dependencies tailored to each sample.

Method: ASNA captures common dependencies from normalized sequences and instance-specific dependencies from raw sequences, then adaptively fuses them based on instance non-stationarity. SeesawNet alternates dedicated temporal and channel relationship modeling on top of ASNA to jointly capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies.

Result: Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks show that SeesawNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: SeesawNet provides a unified architecture that dynamically balances common and instance-specific dependency modeling in both temporal and channel dimensions, addressing non-stationarity more effectively than previous approaches.

Abstract: Instance normalization (IN) is widely used in non-stationary multivariate time series forecasting to reduce distribution shifts and highlight common patterns across samples. However, IN can over-smooth instance-specific structural information that is essential for modeling temporal and cross-channel heterogeneity. While prior methods further suppress distribution discrepancies or attempt to recover temporal specific dependencies, they often ignore a central tension: how to adaptively model common and instance-specific dependency based on each instance's non-stationary structures. To address this dilemma, we propose SeesawNet, a unified architecture that dynamically balances common and instance-specific dependency modeling in both temporal and channel dimensions. At its core is Adaptive Stationary-Nonstationary Attention (ASNA), which captures common dependencies from normalized sequences and specific dependencies from raw sequences, and adaptively fuses them according to instance-level non-stationarity. Built upon ASNA, SeesawNet alternates dedicated temporal and channel relationship modeling to jointly capture long-range and cross-variable dependencies. Extensive experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that SeesawNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [332] [Efficient Multi-objective Prompt Optimization via Pure-exploration Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14553)
*Donghao Li,Chengshuai Shi,Weijuan Ou,Cong Shen,Jing Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a multi-objective, bandit-based framework for prompt selection, addressing Pareto set recovery and best-feasible prompt identification; provides algorithms with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation showing improvements over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prompt performance is inherently multi-faceted and cannot be captured by a single metric; a principled, efficient method to identify optimal prompts across multiple objectives is needed.

Method: Cast the problem as pure-exploration multi-objective bandits. Adapt provably efficient algorithms from multi-objective bandits and introduce a novel best-feasible-arm identification design for structured (linear) bandits, with theoretical guarantees on identification error.

Result: Developed bandit-based approaches that outperform baselines on diverse LLMs, with theoretical guarantees in the linear regime and practical effectiveness demonstrated empirically.

Conclusion: The work establishes a principled and efficient framework for multi-objective prompt optimization, enabling robust Pareto-based selection and reliable best feasible prompts in LLM prompt engineering.

Abstract: Prompt engineering has become central to eliciting the capabilities of large language models (LLMs). At its core lies prompt selection -- efficiently identifying the most effective prompts. However, most prior investigations overlook a key challenge: the inherently multi-faceted nature of prompt performance, which cannot be captured by a single metric. To fill this gap, we study the multi-objective prompt selection problem under two practical settings: Pareto prompt set recovery and best feasible prompt identification. Casting the problem into the pure-exploration bandits framework, we adapt provably efficient algorithms from multi-objective bandits and further introduce a novel design for best feasible arm identification in structured bandits, with theoretical guarantees on the identification error in the linear case. Extensive experiments across multiple LLMs show that the bandit-based approaches yield significant improvements over baselines, establishing a principled and efficient framework for multi-objective prompt optimization.

</details>


### [333] [Resolving Action Bottleneck: Agentic Reinforcement Learning Informed by Token-Level Energy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14558)
*Langzhou He,Junyou Zhu,Yue Zhou,Zhengyao Gu,Junhua Liu,Wei-Chieh Huang,Henry Peng Zou,David Wipf,Philip S. Yu,Qitian Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Token-level credit assignment in agentic RL is biased toward action tokens; ActFocus reweights gradients to downweight reasoning tokens and boosts uncertain action tokens, yielding large gains with no extra cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the misallocation of credit in token-level training signals where action tokens disproportionately drive rewards; use energy-based modeling to quantify token-reward correlations.

Method: Introduce ActFocus: (1) downweight gradients on reasoning tokens; (2) energy-based redistribution to emphasize high-uncertainty action tokens; evaluate across four environments and multiple model sizes with no additional runtime/memory cost.

Result: Consistent outperformance of PPO and GRPO; final-step gains up to 65.2 and 63.7 percentage points respectively across settings.

Conclusion: A simple token-reweighting approach can mitigate the Action Bottleneck, improving policy-gradient-based RL for LLMs by correcting token-level credit assignment without added computational burden.

Abstract: Agentic reinforcement learning trains large language models using multi-turn trajectories that interleave long reasoning traces with short environment-facing actions. Common policy-gradient methods, such as PPO and GRPO, treat each token in a trajectory equally, leading to uniform credit assignment. In this paper, we critically demonstrate that such uniform credit assignment largely misallocates token-level training signals. From an energy-based modeling perspective, we show that token-level training signals, quantified by their correlations with reward variance of different rollouts sampled from a given prompt, concentrate sharply on action tokens rather than reasoning tokens, even though action tokens account for only a small fraction of the trajectory. We refer to this phenomenon as the Action Bottleneck. Motivated by this observation, we propose an embarrassingly simple token reweighting approach, ActFocus, that downweights gradients on reasoning tokens, along with an additional energy-based redistribution mechanism that further increases the weights on action tokens with higher uncertainty. Across four environments and different model sizes, ActFocus consistently outperforms PPO and GRPO, yielding final-step gains of up to 65.2 and 63.7 percentage points, respectively, without any additional runtime or memory cost.

</details>


### [334] [Woodelf++: A Fast and Unified Partial Dependence Plot Algorithm for Decision Tree Ensembles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14578)
*Ron Wettenstein,Alexander Nadel,Udi Boker*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Woodelf++ provides a unified, efficient framework for computing PDPs, Joint-PDPs, and Any-Order-PDIVs (and Full PDPs) on decision tree ensembles by leveraging a pseudo-Boolean metric derived from Woodelf. It achieves large speedups over the state of the art and scikit-learn, including exponential improvements for high-order interactions, and supports GPU acceleration in pure Python.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interpretability of complex tree ensembles requires scalable computation of feature effects and interactions (PDPs, Joint-PDPs, PDIVs). Existing methods are computationally expensive, especially for Any-Order-PDIVs; there is a need for a unified, efficient approach that leverages the structure of tree ensembles.

Method: Extend Woodelf (efficient SHAP computation) by deriving metrics over pseudo-Boolean functions to compute PDPs (exact and approximate), Joint-PDPs, Any-Order-PDIVs, and Full PDPs within a single framework. Implemented in pure Python with GPU support; uses model split thresholds to capture behavior across feature ranges.

Result: Significant performance gains: PDP/Joint-PDP up to 6x faster than the state of the art and up to 5 orders of magnitude faster than scikit-learn on 400k-row data; Any-Order-PDIVs computed in ~5 minutes vs an estimated >1e6 years for the prior art. Demonstrates scalability and efficiency of the unified approach.

Conclusion: Woodelf++ delivers substantial complexity reductions for a broad set of explainability tools on tree ensembles, enabling practical, scalable interpretation via a single unified framework and extending the utility of PDPs and interaction measures.

Abstract: Partial Dependence Plots (PDPs) visualize how changes in a single feature affect the average model prediction. They are widely used in practice to interpret decision tree ensembles and other machine learning models. Joint-PDPs extend this idea to pairs of features, revealing their combined effect. Partial Dependence Interaction Values (PDIVs) measure feature interactions. The Any-Order-PDIVs task computes these interactions for every feature subset across all rows of the dataset.
  We introduce Woodelf++, a unified and efficient approach for computing all these useful explainability tools on decision tree ensembles, building on Woodelf, an algorithm for efficient SHAP computation. By deriving suitable metrics over pseudo-Boolean functions, Woodelf++ can compute PDPs (exact and approximate), Joint-PDPs, and Any-Order-PDIVs in a unified framework. Our method delivers substantial complexity improvements over the state of the art, including an exponential gain for Any-Order-PDIVs. Additionally, we introduce and efficiently compute Full PDPs, which leverage the model's split thresholds to faithfully capture its behavior across all possible feature values.
  Woodelf++ is implemented in pure Python and supports GPU acceleration. On a dataset with 400,000 rows, Woodelf++ computes PDP and Joint-PDP up to 6x faster than the state of the art and up to five orders of magnitude faster than scikit-learn. For Any-Order-PDIVs, the gap is even larger: Woodelf++ computes all interaction values in 5 minutes, while the state of the art is estimated to require over 1,000,000 years.

</details>


### [335] [Angel or Demon: Investigating the Plasticity Interventions' Impact on Backdoor Threats in Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14587)
*Oubo Ma,Ruixiao Lin,Yang Dai,Jiahao Chen,Chunyi Zhou,Linkang Du,Shouling Ji*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Empirically study how DRL plasticity interventions affect backdoor vulnerabilities across 14,664 cases; find that most interventions mitigate backdoors except SAM, which amplifies backdoor threat via gradient amplification; identify mechanisms and propose SCC as a framework and abnormal loss landscape sharpness as a detection cue.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the neglected interaction between plasticity/interventions in DRL and backdoor vulnerabilities, extending beyond vanilla DRL backdoor studies to practical, intervention-aware settings.

Method: Large-scale empirical analysis of DRL with representative plasticity interventions under various backdoor attack scenarios (14,664 cases); combine pathological analysis of gradients and representations; derive mechanistic explanations; propose a conceptual framework (SCC) and a diagnostic indicator (loss landscape sharpness).

Result: Most interventions mitigate backdoor risks; only SAM exacerbates threat. Backdoor gradient amplification identified as the reason for SAM’s deterioration, while intervention success attributed to disruption of activation pathways and compression of representation space. Introduces SCC framework for robust backdoor injection/deconstruction and highlights abnormal loss landscape sharpness as a potential detection signal.

Conclusion: The study suggests that, with careful intervention design, DRL backdoor vulnerabilities can be mitigated in practice, though SAM requires caution. It also provides a theoretical lens (SCC) and a practical detector (loss landscape sharpness) to guide secure DRL deployments.

Abstract: Extensive research has highlighted the severe threats posed by backdoor attacks to deep reinforcement learning (DRL). However, prior studies primarily focus on vanilla scenarios, while plasticity interventions have emerged as indispensable built-in components of modern DRL agents. Despite their effectiveness in mitigating plasticity loss, the impact of these interventions on DRL backdoor vulnerabilities remains underexplored, and this lack of systematic investigation poses risks in practical DRL deployments. To bridge this gap, we empirically study 14,664 cases integrating representative interventions and attack scenarios. We find that only one intervention (i.e., SAM) exacerbates backdoor threats, while other interventions mitigate them. Pathological analysis identifies that the exacerbation is attributed to backdoor gradient amplification, while the mitigation stems from activation pathway disruption and representation space compression. From these findings, we derive two novel insights: (1) a conceptual framework SCC for robust backdoor injection that deconstructs the mechanistic interplay between interventions and backdoors in DRL, and (2) abnormal loss landscape sharpness as a key indicator for DRL backdoor detection.

</details>


### [336] [Silent Collapse in Recursive Learning Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14588)
*Zhipeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Silent collapse is a gradual degradation of internal model distributions during recursive learning, detectable by trajectory precursors before standard metrics worsen; a meta-learning loop (MTR) can monitor and regulate learning to prevent it without real data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current metrics miss early internal degradation in recursive training; early detection is needed to prevent irreversible degradation when training data is reused or unavailable.

Method: Identify three trajectory-level precursors of silent collapse (anchor entropy contraction, representation drift freezing, tail coverage erosion). Propose MTR (Monitor–Trust–Regulator), a lightweight metacognitive loop that tracks trajectory statistics, estimates a slow-timescale trust variable, and modulates learning intensity.

Result: Demonstrates that silent collapse onset is preceded by the three precursors across multiple generations, enabling early warning; MTR can provide early detection and actively prevent silent collapse without access to pristine data.

Conclusion: Internal distributions degrade gradually under recursion; monitoring trajectory statistics with MTR offers early warning and prevention, extending safe recursive learning when data is scarce or private.

Abstract: Recursive learning -- where models are trained on data generated by previous versions of themselves -- is increasingly common in large language models, autonomous agents, and self-supervised systems. However, standard performance metrics (loss, perplexity, accuracy) often fail to detect internal degradation before it becomes irreversible. Here we identify a phenomenon we call silent collapse: under broad recursive conditions, model internal distributions -- predictive entropy, representational diversity, and tail coverage -- progressively contract even as conventional metrics appear stable or improving.
  We discover that silent collapse is not abrupt. Its onset is reliably preceded by three trajectory-level precursors: (1) contraction of anchor entropy, (2) freezing of representation drift, and (3) erosion of tail coverage. These signals manifest multiple generations before any degradation in standard validation metrics, enabling early warning.
  Based on these precursors, we propose the MTR (Monitor--Trust--Regulator) framework, a lightweight metacognitive loop that monitors trajectory statistics, estimates a slow-timescale trust variable, and adaptively modulates the effective learning intensity. MTR provides early warning and actively prevents silent collapse without requiring access to pristine real data -- a critical advantage when original data is unavailable, contaminated, or private.

</details>


### [337] [Fast Rates for Inverse Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14599)
*Andreas Schlaginhaufen,Maryam Kamgarpour*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Entropy-regularized Min-Max IRL with linear rewards in finite-horizon MDPs on Borel spaces achieves population-level equivalence between MLE and Min-Max-IRL, empirical equivalence under deterministic dynamics, and O(n^{-1}) rates for KL divergence and Hessian-norm parameter error; robust to misspecification; extends identifiability to general Borel spaces and analyzes derivatives of the soft-optimal value with respect to reward parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve identifiability and statistical efficiency of inverse reinforcement learning in broad state/action spaces, without requiring exploration or perfect model specification.

Method: Entropy-regularized Min-Max-IRL with linear reward class; derive population-level equivalence with MLE; establish empirical equivalence under deterministic dynamics; leverage pseudo-self-concordance of the Min-Max-IRL loss to prove fast, O(n^{-1}), convergence rates for trajectory KL divergence and Hessian-norm parameter error; extend identifiability to general Borel spaces; derive derivatives of the soft-optimal value with respect to reward parameters.

Result: Structural: MLE and Min-Max-IRL coincide at the population level; empirical coincidence under deterministic dynamics. Statistical: trajectory KL divergence and squared Hessian-norm parameter error decay at O(n^{-1}); guarantees hold under misspecification; no exploration assumptions. Extensions: reward identifiability extended to general Borel spaces; derivatives of soft-optimal value with respect to reward parameters derived.

Conclusion: The work advances IRL theory by unifying estimators under a single structural framework, delivering efficient, misspecification-tolerant statistical guarantees, and broadening identifiability and sensitivity analyses to general spaces—serving as a solid foundation for robust IRL in realistic, high-dimensional settings.

Abstract: We establish novel structural and statistical results for entropy-regularized min-max inverse reinforcement learning (Min-Max-IRL) with linear reward classes in finite-horizon MDPs with Borel state and action spaces. On the structural side, we show that maximum likelihood estimation (MLE) and Min-Max-IRL are equivalent at the population level, and at the empirical level under deterministic dynamics. On the statistical side, exploiting pseudo-self-concordance of the Min-Max-IRL loss, we prove that both the trajectory-level KL divergence and the squared parameter error in the Hessian norm decay at the fast rate $\mathcal{O}(n^{-1})$, where $n$ is the number of expert trajectories. Our guarantees apply under misspecification and require no exploration assumptions. We further extend reward-identifiability results to general Borel spaces and derive novel results on the derivatives of the soft-optimal value function with respect to reward parameters.

</details>


### [338] [An Amortized Efficiency Threshold for Comparing Neural and Heuristic Solvers in Combinatorial Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14624)
*Sohaib Afifi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces an Amortized Efficiency Threshold (AET) to compare neural solvers with heuristics under deployment volume, showing energy superiority becomes possible once deployment volume passes threshold. Demonstrates with MTVRP: crossover around 1.58e5 deployments; per-instance energy ratio ~0.41. Contributions: framework, open instrumentation, measurement protocol; convergence of ratio at larger sizes left for future work.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Resolve the common claim that training costs render neural combinatorial-optimization solvers energy-inefficient by focusing on deployment-volume trade-offs rather than training costs alone.

Method: Define AET as deployment volume where neural solvers break even with a heuristic under a fixed quality constraint; analyze cumulative energy ratio, showing it converges to a constant below 1 when the network wins per instance; include embodied-carbon amortization; instantiate framework on MTVRP (n=20, 5 seeds, 19 variants) with HGS via PyVRP as baseline, measure energy and carbon across deployments.

Result: Crossover deployment estimated at ~1.58×10^5; reported per-instance energy ratio is 0.41 (neural solver uses 41% of the energy per instance of the heuristic); the tech shows energy-cost symmetry in amortization and a deployment-driven energy advantage when satisfied per the AET framework.

Conclusion: The paper provides a framework, instrumentation, and protocol for deployment-volume-aware energy comparisons of neural solvers; notes that convergence of the energy ratio with larger instances is left for future empirical work.

Abstract: A common critique of neural combinatorial-optimization solvers is that they are less energy-efficient than CPU metaheuristics, given the operational energy cost of training them on GPUs. This paper examines the inferential step from "training is expensive" to "neural solvers are net-inefficient", which is where the critique actually goes wrong. Training the network costs a large fixed amount of GPU energy; running the metaheuristic costs a small amount of CPU energy on every instance, repeated as long as the solver is deployed. The two are not commensurable until a deployment volume is fixed. We define the Amortized Efficiency Threshold (AET) as the deployment volume above which a neural solver breaks even with a heuristic baseline in total energy or carbon, under an explicit constraint on solution quality. We show that the cumulative-energy ratio between the two solvers tends to a constant strictly below one whenever the network wins per-instance, and that this limit does not depend on how the training cost was measured. An embodied-carbon term amortizes hardware fabrication symmetrically on both sides. We instantiate the framework on the Multi-Task VRP (MTVRP) environment at n=20 customers across 19 problem variants and five training seeds, with HGS via PyVRP as the heuristic baseline. The measured crossover sits near $1.58 \times 10^5$ deployed instances; the per-instance ratio is 0.41, reflecting the moderate size of the instances tested. The contribution is the framework, the open instrumentation, and the measurement protocol; structural convergence of the ratio at larger problem sizes is left to future empirical work.

</details>


### [339] [Action-Inspired Generative Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14631)
*Eshwar R. A.,Debnath Pal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Action-Inspired Generative Models (AGMs): a dual-network framework that uses a lightweight learned scalar potential Vφ to score bridge samples and modulate the drift via importance weights, improving generation quality with negligible overhead; Vφ is discarded at inference, maintaining standard Euler-Maruyama drift for EMA, and the approach is plug-and-play for bridge-matching methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bridge-matching methods currently apply uniform regression weights to all stochastic transitions, ignoring whether samples lie on coherent vs. degenerate trajectories. This can lead to inefficient learning and suboptimal generation quality.

Method: A dual-network setup where a lightweight potential Vφ scores bridge samples online. The drift objective is modulated by importance weights derived through a stop-gradient barrier to prevent backpropagation between networks while preserving Vφ's guidance. Vφ has ~1.4% of the drift network's parameters, adds no inference overhead, and requires no iterative half-bridge fitting or extra SDE solvers. At inference, Vφ is discarded and standard Euler-Maruyama integration with EMA drift is used. The approach is presented as a plug-and-play enhancement to existing bridge-matching training loops.

Result: Selective penalization of uninformative transport paths via the learned potential yields consistent improvements in generation quality across fidelity and coverage metrics.

Conclusion: AGMs provide a lightweight, plug-and-play enhancement to bridge-matching training by introducing a small, online-learned potential to guide sample selection; the guiding signal is discarded at inference, preserving standard sampling.

Abstract: We introduce Action-Inspired Generative Models (AGMs), a dual-network generative framework motivated by the observation that existing bridge-matching methods assign uniform regression weight to every stochastic transition in the transport landscape, regardless of whether a given bridge sample lies along a structurally coherent trajectory or a degenerate one. We address this by introducing a lightweight learned scalar potential $V_φ$ that scores bridge samples online and modulates the drift objective via importance weights derived through a stop-gradient barrier -- preventing adversarial feedback between the two networks whilst preserving $V_φ$'s guiding signal. Crucially, $V_φ$ comprises only $\sim$1.4% of the primary drift network's parameter count, adds no overhead to the inference graph, and requires no iterative half-bridge fitting or auxiliary stochastic differential equation (SDE) solvers: it is a plug-and-play enhancement to any bridge-matching training loop. At inference, $V_φ$ is discarded entirely, leaving standard Euler-Maruyama integration of the exponential moving average (EMA) drift. We demonstrate that selectively penalising uninformative transport paths through the learned potential yields consistent improvements in generation quality across fidelity and coverage metrics.

</details>


### [340] [DRL-STAF: A Deep Reinforcement Learning Framework for State-Aware Forecasting of Complex Multivariate Hidden Markov Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14632)
*Manrui Jiang,Jingru Huang,Yong Chen,Chen Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A DRL-STAF framework combines deep nonlinear emission modeling with RL-based state estimation to forecast multivariate hidden Markov processes, addressing nonlinearity, nonstationarity, and state-space explosion while providing interpretable hidden states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Overcome limitations of DL (poor explicit state modeling) and HMMs (limited nonlinear emissions and scalability) in multivariate, sequential forecasting.

Method: Deep neural networks model complex nonlinear emissions; reinforcement learning estimates discrete hidden states, enabling joint next-step prediction and state estimation without heavily predefined transition structures, mitigating state-space blow-up and adapting to diverse dynamics.

Result: DRL-STAF outperforms HMM variants, standalone DL models, and existing DL-HMM hybrids in most experiments and yields reliable hidden-state estimates.

Conclusion: The framework offers improved predictive performance with interpretable latent states and scalable training for complex multivariate HMM-like processes.

Abstract: Forecasting multivariate hidden Markov processes is challenging due to nonlinear and nonstationary observations, latent state transitions, and cross-sequence dependencies. While deep learning methods achieve strong predictive accuracy, they typically lack explicit state modeling, whereas Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) provide interpretable latent states but struggle with complex nonlinear emissions and scalability. To address these limitations, we propose DRL-STAF, a Deep Reinforcement Learning based STate-Aware Forecasting framework that jointly predicts next-step observations and estimates the corresponding hidden states for complex multivariate hidden Markov processes. Specifically, DRL-STAF models complex nonlinear emissions using deep neural networks and estimates discrete hidden states using reinforcement learning, reducing the reliance on predefined transition structures and enabling flexible adaptation to diverse temporal dynamics. In particular, DRL-STAF mitigates the state-space explosion encountered by typical multivariate HMM-based methods. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DRL-STAF outperforms HMM variants, standalone deep learning models, and existing DL-HMM hybrids in most cases, while also providing reliable hidden-state estimates.

</details>


### [341] [Unbiased and Second-Order-Free Training for High-Dimensional PDEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14643)
*Jaemin Seo,Surin Lee,Jae Yong Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EM discretization biases BSDE training losses; the work introduces an unbiased, second-order-free framework that preserves BSDEs' computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: BSDE-based DL methods offer a scalable alternative for solving high-dimensional PDEs, but Euler-Maruyama (EM) induced bias degrades training; high-order schemes remove bias but reintroduce second-order derivatives and extra cost.

Method: Perform a principled analysis of EM-induced loss bias and design an unbiased training framework that avoids second-order derivatives, retaining the computational advantages of BSDE methods.

Result: Demonstrates an unbiased, second-order-free BSDE training framework (Un-EM-BSDE); code available at the provided GitHub link.

Conclusion: An EM-bias-free BSDE training paradigm preserves the efficiency of BSDE-based approaches for high-dimensional PDEs, offering a practical alternative to high-order discretizations.

Abstract: Deep learning methods based on backward stochastic differential equations (BSDEs) have emerged as competitive alternatives to physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving high-dimensional partial differential equations (PDEs). By leveraging probabilistic representations, BSDE approaches can avoid the curse of dimensionality and often admit second-order-free training objectives that do not require explicit Hessian evaluations. It has recently been established that the commonly used Euler-Maruyama (EM) time discretization induces an intrinsic bias in BSDE training losses. While high-order schemes such as Heun can fully eliminate this bias, such schemes re-introduce second-order spatial derivatives and incur substantial computational overhead. In this work, we provide a principled analysis of EM-induced loss bias and propose an unbiased, second-order-free training framework that preserves the computational advantages of BSDE methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/seojaemin22/Un-EM-BSDE.

</details>


### [342] [Slower Generalization, Faster Memorization: A Sweet Spot in Algorithmic Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14659)
*Shin So,Kyelim Lee,Albert No*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dataset size that minimizes update-based convergence can differ from the size that yields fastest generalization in structured-output grokking; small Transformers show a sweet spot where validation accuracy peaks fastest, and larger datasets can slow convergence despite enabling generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Disentangle when grokking occurs and how update dynamics interact with rule-learning versus memorization in structured-output tasks, to understand the separation between generalization onset and update efficiency.

Method: Empirical study using Transformer models on Needleman–Wunsch matrix generation tasks. Systematically vary dataset size, measure validation exact-match accuracy and the number of gradient updates to reach predefined thresholds, and compare with a multiplication baseline.

Result: There exists a dataset-size sweet spot where validation exact-match converges fastest; beyond this point, generalization remains possible but requires more gradient updates. In the regime where partial validation competence first appears, larger datasets can require fewer updates to reach high training accuracy. The multiplication baseline does not show the same post-threshold slowdown.

Conclusion: Critical data size for onset of generalization can be distinct from the dataset size that optimizes update-based convergence. Structured-output tasks can exhibit divergence between learning the rule and completing exact-fitting, revealing nuanced dynamics of grokking in rule-based generation tasks.

Abstract: Critical-data-size accounts of grokking suggest a natural post-threshold intuition: once training data is sufficient to identify the underlying rule, additional data should accelerate validation convergence. We show that this intuition can fail in a controlled structured-output task. In Needleman--Wunsch (NW) matrix generation, small Transformers reach high validation exact-match accuracy fastest at an intermediate dataset size, not at the largest one. Past this dataset-size sweet spot, generalization remains achievable but requires more gradient updates. Conversely, in the regime where partial validation competence first appears, larger datasets can require fewer updates to reach high training accuracy, suggesting that emerging rule structure can accelerate fitting beyond example-wise memorization. A multiplication baseline does not show the same post-threshold slowdown. These results separate the critical data size for the onset of generalization from the dataset size that optimizes update-based convergence, and identify structured-output tasks where learning the rule and completing exact-fitting can diverge.

</details>


### [343] [AQKA: Active Quantum Kernel Acquisition Under a Shot Budget](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14672)
*Jian Xu,Chao Li,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AQKA introduces a regime-aware shot allocation for quantum kernel learning, yielding substantial gains over uniform strategies in budget-limited settings, plus a closed-form pairwise acquisition theory for allocating shots with provable bounds, demonstrated on both synthetic benchmarks and live IBM hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Estimating quantum kernels requires O(N^2) measurements; existing budget-saving subsampling methods still distribute shots uniformly within the selected subset, ignoring each entry's impact on downstream classifier performance. This work targets efficient shot allocation to improve kernel methods under budget constraints.

Method: 1) Regime decomposition with AQKA: a principled allocator that selects the best strategy per budget regime, achieving dominance in the budget-limited regime (B ≲ 16 n_pairs) for sparse-sensitivity KRR; 2) Closed-form pair-level acquisition theory: s_ij* ∝ |g_ij| sqrt(K_ij(1−K_ij)) with explicit gradient for KRR and SVM via the envelope theorem; corrected ρ ≤ 2m/N sparsity-aware bound; plug-in regret bound (Theorem 2); tighter SVM ceiling ρ^SVM ≤ m_sv^2/N^2; 3) Empirical validation: multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation on IBM hardware showing significant gains at N=20 and N=30 across datasets/hardware.

Result: AQKA dominates budget-limited regime on sparse-sensitivity KRR with gains growing with N (≈+8 to +25 points; 225→1000) and reaching +26–32 points on ibm_pittsburgh 156-qubit kernel; Nyström-QKE dominates only at saturating budgets on planted-sparse with low-rank reconstruction; ShoFaR is competitive only at extreme low budgets. First multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation yields +17.0±4.8 points at N=20 on ibm_aachen and +14.0±8.5 points at N=30 on ibm_berlin with 5 seeds.

Conclusion: AQKA provides a principled, regime-aware approach to shot budgeting in quantum kernel learning, backed by a closed-form acquisition theory with provable bounds and validated through hardware experiments; it substantially improves downstream classifier performance under realistic shot budgets and offers a practical adaptive-shot allocation framework for near-term quantum devices.

Abstract: Estimating an $N \times N$ quantum kernel from circuit fidelities requires $Θ(N^2 S)$ measurement shots, the dominant bottleneck for deployment on near-term hardware. Existing budget-saving methods (Nyström-QKE, ShoFaR, kernel-target alignment) sub-sample \emph{which} entries to measure but allocate shots \emph{uniformly} within their chosen subset, ignoring how much each entry drives the downstream classifier. We close this gap with two contributions. \textbf{First, a complete regime decomposition} for shot-budgeted quantum kernel learning: a principled menu of when each allocator wins. Our method, \emph{AQKA}, dominates the budget-limited regime ($B \lesssim 16 n_{\mathrm{pairs}}$) on sparse-sensitivity KRR, with the gap \emph{growing} from $+8$ to $+25$ pts over uniform as $N$ scales $225{\to}1000$ and reaching $+26$--$32$ pts on an \texttt{ibm\_pittsburgh} (156-qubit Heron) hardware kernel; Nyström-QKE wins at saturating budgets on planted-sparse via low-rank reconstruction; ShoFaR is competitive only at extreme low budgets. \textbf{Second, a closed-form pair-level acquisition theory}: $s_{ij}^{\star} \propto |g_{ij}|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$ with explicit gradient $g_{ij}$ for KRR (Lemma~1, $|β_iα_j+β_jα_i|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$) and SVM via the envelope theorem ($|η_i^*η_j^*|\sqrt{K_{ij}(1-K_{ij})}$); a \emph{corrected} sparsity-aware Cauchy--Schwarz rate $ρ\le 2m/N$ matching empirics (vs.\ the naive $m^2/N^2$); an explicit-constant plug-in regret bound (Theorem~2); and a tighter SVM ceiling $ρ^{\mathrm{SVM}} \le m_{\mathrm{sv}}^2/N^2$. We close with the first multi-seed live online adaptive shot allocation on quantum hardware: $+17.0 \pm 4.8$ pts at $N{=}20$ on \texttt{ibm\_aachen} ($3.5σ$, 5 seeds), with the advantage holding at $N{=}30$ at higher budget on \texttt{ibm\_berlin} ($+14.0 \pm 8.5$ pts, 5 seeds).

</details>


### [344] [Spontaneous symmetry breaking and Goldstone modes for deep information propagation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14685)
*Nabil Iqbal,T. Anderson Keller,Yue Song,Takeru Miyato,Max Welling*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Goldstone-like degrees of freedom in equivariant neural networks enable coherent information propagation across depth and time, improving trainability and long-sequence memory without stabilizers; validated analytically and empirically in feedforward and recurrent models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage continuous symmetry to sustain information propagation across deep and recurrent architectures, inspired by Goldstone modes in physics; aims to reduce reliance on architectural stabilizers like residual connections or normalization.

Method: Impose layer-wise equivariance under a continuous symmetry to invoke Goldstone-like excitations; provide analytic insights and empirical demonstrations; evaluate both feedforward and recurrent architectures on tasks requiring long-range information flow and memory.

Result: Demonstrates coherent propagation of information across depth and recurrent iterations; stable information flow without residuals/normalization; improved trainability and representational diversity in feedforward nets; enhanced long-term memory and performance of RNNs/GRUs on long-sequence tasks.

Conclusion: Continuous-symmetry-based design can sustain information flow in deep and recurrent networks via Goldstone-like modes, offering an alternative to traditional stabilizers and guiding architecture design for long-range computation.

Abstract: In physical systems, whenever a continuous symmetry is spontaneously broken, the system possesses excitations called Goldstone modes, which allow coherent information propagation over long distances and times. In this work, we study deep neural networks whose internal layers are equivariant under a continuous symmetry and may therefore support analogous Goldstone-like degrees of freedom. We demonstrate, both analytically and empirically, that these degrees of freedom enable coherent signal propagation across depth and recurrent iterations, providing a mechanism for stable information flow without relying on architectural stabilizers such as residual connections or normalization. In feedforward networks, this results in improved trainability and representational diversity across layers. In recurrent settings, we demonstrate the same mechanism is valuable for long-term memory by propagating information over recurrent iterations, thereby improving performance of RNNs and GRUs on long-sequence modeling tasks.

</details>


### [345] [ReMIA: a Powerful and Efficient Alternative to Membership Inference Attacks against Synthetic Data Generators](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14686)
*Davide Scassola,Andrea Coser,Sebastiano Saccani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReMIA is a practical relative membership inference attack for tabular synthetic data that requires only two SDG trainings and data no larger than the original training set. It assesses privacy risk by testing whether a classifier can tell which of two source datasets a record came from, achieving sensitivity comparable to state-of-the-art MIAs while being far more efficient.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MIAs are powerful but impractical (shadow models with many SDG trainings and large auxiliary data). Proxy metrics like distance to closest record (DCR) are fast but insensitive. There is a need for a practical, sensitive, low-cost metric to evaluate privacy risk of SDGs.

Method: From two source datasets, generate two synthetic datasets using a given SDG. Train a classifier to predict the source of a record. If the classifier can reliably distinguish sources, privacy risk is higher. ReMIA requires only two SDG training runs and auxiliary data no larger than the original training set.

Result: Across multiple tabular datasets and SDGs, ReMIA's sensitivity to privacy risk is comparable to state-of-the-art MIAs, while requiring substantially less computation and data. SDGs exhibit privacy-utility trade-offs not matched by traditional noise-based anonymization methods.

Conclusion: ReMIA provides a practical, effective privacy risk metric for SDGs that balances attack sophistication with operational feasibility, enabling better evaluation of privacy guarantees in synthetic data sharing. Code is available at the provided URL.

Abstract: Tabular data sharing under privacy constraints is increasingly important for research and collaboration. Synthetic data generators (SDGs) are a promising solution, but synthetic data remains vulnerable to attacks, such as membership inference attacks (MIAs), which aim to determine whether a specific record was part of the training data. State-of-the-art MIAs are powerful but impractical: they rely on shadow modeling, requiring hundreds of SDG training runs, and need auxiliary data several times larger than the original training set. Fast proxy metrics like distance to closest record (DCR) are efficient but have limited sensitivity to MIA risk. We introduce ReMIA (Relative Membership Inference Attack), a practical privacy metric that requires only two SDG training runs and additional data no larger than the original training set. Rather than predicting whether a record was in the training set, ReMIA generates two synthetic datasets from two source datasets and measures whether a classifier can identify which source a record came from. Experiments across multiple tabular datasets and SDGs show that ReMIA has a sensitivity comparable to state-of-the-art MIAs while being substantially more practical. We further observe that SDGs can achieve privacy-utility trade-offs that traditional noise-based anonymization methods do not match. Code is available at https://github.com/aindo-com/remia.

</details>


### [346] [The Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity Tradeoff in SAEs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14694)
*Tommaso Mencattini,Francesco Montagna,Francesco Locatello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper defines a Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity tradeoff in Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs), showing that enforcing monosemanticity raises both rate and distortion, and that polysemanticity is shaped by the data distribution (co-occurrence of features). It also proposes principled conditions for measuring polysemanticity and validates these ideas empirically on data connected to Large Language Models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why SAEs struggle to learn interpretable, monosemantic representations without sacrificing reconstruction quality, and to identify how data statistics influence polysemanticity and its measurement.

Method: Theoretical analysis under toy-models; derivation of a rate-distortion-polysemanticity relationship; assumption of an underlying generative model; analysis of how feature co-occurrence probabilities govern polysemanticity; proposal of necessary conditions for polysemanticity metrics when the data-generating process is unknown; empirical benchmarking of proxy metrics on SAEs trained on data related to Large Language Models.

Result: Monosemantic restriction increases rate and distortion; optimal SAEs' polysemanticity is dictated by the training data distribution, particularly feature co-occurrence; proposed necessary conditions for polysemanticity metrics; benchmarking on LLM-related data supports a data-driven view of polysemanticity.

Conclusion: Polysemanticity in SAEs is fundamentally data-driven; addressing it requires accounting for the data-generating process in model design and optimization, alongside principled, well-conditioned metrics for polysemanticity.

Abstract: Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) that can accurately reconstruct their input (minimizing distortion) by making efficient use of few features (minimizing the rate) often fail to learn monosemantic representations (highly interpretable), limiting their usefulness for mechanistic interpretability. In this paper, we characterise this tension in learning faithful, efficient, and interpretable explanations, introducing the Rate-Distortion-Polysemanticity tradeoff in SAEs. Under toy-modeling assumptions, we theoretically and empirically show that restricting the SAE to be monosemantic necessarily comes with an increase in rate and distortion. Assuming a generative model behind the input observations, we further demonstrate that the degree of polysemanticity of optimal SAEs is determined by the training data distribution, especially by the probability of features to co-occur. Finally, we extend the analysis to real-world settings by deriving necessary conditions that a polysemanticity measure should satisfy when the data-generating process is unknown, and we benchmark existing proxy metrics on SAEs trained on Large Language Models. Taken together, our findings show that polysemanticity is a data problem that should be accounted for when addressing it at the architectural and optimization level.

</details>


### [347] [NeuroAtlas: Benchmarking Foundation Models for Clinical EEG and Brain-Computer Interfaces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14698)
*Konstantinos Kontras,Trui Osselaer,Stylianos G. Mouslech,Angeliki-Ilektra Karaiskou,Guido Gagliardi,Thomas Strypsteen,Mohammad Hossein Badiei,Anku Rani,Maarten Vanmarcke,Miguel Bhagubai,Chanakya Ekbote,Jaedong Hwang,Christos Chatzichristos,Paul Pu Liang,Maarten De Vos*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large EEG benchmark (NeuroAtlas) shows that pretrained EEG foundation models offer limited consistent advantage over generic time-series models, and standard metrics miss clinical utility; model rankings vary by domain; currently no out-of-the-box unified EEG FM, but the benchmark enables next-gen development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve inconsistent evaluations of EEG foundation models by creating a large, diverse, clinically oriented benchmark and runtime metrics that reflect practical clinical utility across epilepsy, sleep, brain age, and brain-computer interfaces.

Method: Compilation of NeuroAtlas: 42 datasets (260k hours), multiple datasets per task, bespoke clinical evaluation metrics; compare EEG-specialized FMs with generic time-series FMs (no EEG-specific pretraining for the latter); evaluate using clinically relevant metrics such as event-level decision quality, hypnogram-derived features, and brain-age gap across tasks.

Result: EEG-specific FMs do not consistently outperform generic time-series FMs. Standard ML metrics fail to capture clinical utility; event-level and domain-specific metrics reveal different performance aspects; model rankings vary substantially within domains; pretrained models offer limited advantages and there is no universally superior EEG FM.

Conclusion: Current pretrained models do not deliver a unified out-of-the-box EEG representation. NeuroAtlas provides datasets and evaluation protocols to drive future development of a truly unified EEG foundation model.

Abstract: Foundation models (FMs) promise to extract unified representations that generalize across downstream tasks. They have emerged across fields, including electroencephalography (EEG), but it is less clear how effective they are in this particular field. Published evaluations differ in datasets, in the EEG-specific preprocessing that might influence reported results, and in the reported metrics, frequently obscuring the clinical relevance in EEG. We introduce NeuroAtlas, the largest EEG benchmark to date: 42 datasets and 260k hours covering clinical EEG (epilepsy, sleep medicine, brain age estimation) and brain-computer interfaces, and include multiple datasets per task along with bespoke clinical evaluation metrics. Besides evaluating EEG-FMs with respect to supervised baselines, we present results from generic time-series FMs. We report three findings. First, EEG-specific FMs do not consistently outperform time-series FMs, which have neither EEG-focused architectures nor been pretrained on EEG. Second, standard machine learning metrics are insufficient to assess clinical utility: thus, we thoroughly evaluate more appropriate measures such as the quality of event-level decision-making, hypnogram-derived features, and the brain-age gap in the domains of epilepsy, sleep, and brain age, respectively. Third, model rankings and performance can vary substantially within domains. We conclude that pretrained models perform largely on par, with only narrow advantages for a few, and that current models do not yet deliver on the promise of an out-of-the-box unified EEG model. NeuroAtlas exposes this gap and provides the datasets and metrics for the next generation of unified EEG FMs.

</details>


### [348] [TAPIOCA: Why Task- Aware Pruning Improves OOD model Capability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14738)
*Krish Sharma,Omar Naim,Soumadeep Saha,Nicholas Asher*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Task-aware layer pruning yields robust improvements on out-of-distribution data but provides no gains on in-distribution data; pruning removes layers that distort OOD representations, realigning them with the task-adapted geometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain when and why task-aware pruning helps, linking empirical gains to a geometry-based account of task-adapted representations and distortion under distribution shifts.

Method: Controlled experiments on polynomial regression tasks and large language models; measure layerwise norms and pairwise distances for ID and OOD inputs; characterize 'task-adapted geometry' from ID profiles; test causal interventions (residual scaling) and distribution shifts; replicate across model scales.

Result: Pruning yields no ID performance benefit but consistent OOD accuracy gains; OOD inputs distort layerwise norms and pairwise distances relative to ID; pruning removes distortion layers, shifting OOD representations toward the task-adapted geometry; findings robust across scales and with causal interventions.

Conclusion: Task-aware pruning improves OOD robustness by aligning OOD representations with the model's task-adapted geometry, rather than by enhancing ID performance. The mechanism is distortion removal in the geometry induced by distribution shifts, supported by causal evidence and scale-consistency.

Abstract: Recent work has promoted task-aware layer pruning as a way to improve model performance on particular tasks, as shown by TALE. In this paper, we investigate when such improvements occur and why. We show first that, across controlled polynomial regression tasks and large language models, such pruning yields no benefit on in-distribution (ID) data but consistently improves out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy. We further show empirically that OOD inputs induce layerwise norm and pairwise-distance profiles that deviate from the corresponding ID profiles. This leads to a geometric explanation of task-aware pruning: each task induces a task-adapted geometry, characterized empirically by the representation profiles observed on ID inputs. OOD inputs can introduce a distorted version of the task-adapted geometry. Task-aware pruning identifies layers that create or amplify this distortion; by removing them, it shifts OOD representational norms and pairwise distances toward those observed on the adapted distribution. This realigns OOD inputs with the model's task-adapted geometry and improves performance. We provide causal evidence through controlled distribution shifts and residual-scaling interventions, and demonstrate consistent behavior across model scales.

</details>


### [349] [Selective Safety Steering via Value-Filtered Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14746)
*Bat-Sheva Einbinder,Hen Davidov,Yee Whye Teh,Yarin Gal,Yaniv Romano*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A value-filtered decoding method reduces unnecessary safety interventions while improving safety, via a value-based token filter with a single threshold that bounds false interventions; it trades off interventions for safety.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current decoding-time safety approaches often over-intervene, altering safe outputs and harming base-model properties (helpfulness, fluency, style, coherence). There is a need to minimize unnecessary interventions while preserving or improving safety.

Method: Introduce a value-based safety criterion to filter tokens during decoding. Provide an explicit bound on the probability of a false intervention, controlled by a single hyperparameter (threshold). This enables practitioners to trade off higher rates of unnecessary intervention for better safety.

Result: Empirical evaluation across multiple datasets/experiments shows the value-filtered decoding method achieves better trade-offs between safety, helpfulness, and similarity to the base model, outperforming existing baselines.

Conclusion: A tunable, value-based decoding filter reduces unnecessary interventions while enhancing safety, preserving alignment with base-model qualities; the threshold enables practical calibration for desired safety-helpfulness trade-offs.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) are trained to align with human values, their generations may still violate safety constraints. A growing line of work addresses this problem by modifying the model's sampling policy at decoding time using a safety reward. However, existing decoding-time steering methods often intervene unnecessarily, modifying generations that would have been safe under the base model. Such unnecessary interventions are undesirable, as they can distort key properties of the base model such as helpfulness, fluency, style, and coherence. We propose a new test-time steering method designed to reduce such unnecessary interventions while improving the safety of unsafe responses. Our approach filters tokens using a value-based safety criterion and provides an explicit bound on the probability of false interventions. A single threshold hyperparameter controls this bound, allowing practitioners to trade off higher rates of unnecessary intervention for better output safety. Across multiple datasets and experiments, we show that our value-filtered decoding method outperforms existing baselines, achieving better trade-offs between safety, helpfulness, and similarity to the base model.

</details>


### [350] [Cognitive-Uncertainty Guided Knowledge Distillation for Accurate Classification of Student Misconceptions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14752)
*Qirui Liu,Hao Chen,Weijie Shi,Jiajie Xu,Jia Zhu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-stage knowledge distillation with a cognitive-uncertainty guided sample selection to mine high-value data from limited cues, enabling small models to outperform larger baselines on misconception identification tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Identify student misconceptions efficiently under data scarcity, label noise, and edge deployment constraints, by leveraging high-value samples rather than large-scale data synthesis.

Method: Stage 1: standard distillation to transfer task capabilities from teacher to student. Stage 2: a dual-layer marginal selection using cognitive uncertainty to identify four critical sample types based on teacher uncertainty and confidence differences. Data subsets use a difficulty-adaptive mechanism to balance hard/soft label contributions. Students inherit inter-class relationships from teacher soft labels while distinguishing ambiguous error types.

Result: With only 10.30% of filtered samples for augmentation, MAP@3 on MAP-Charting dataset reaches 0.9585 (+17.8%). A 4B parameter model achieves 84.38% accuracy on cross-topic middle-school algebra misconception benchmarks, outperforming SOTA LLM (67.73%) and a 72B fine-tuned model (81.25%). Code available.

Conclusion: The approach efficiently leverages high-value samples to enable small models to excel in misconception detection under data scarcity and noise, though success hinges on teacher quality and selection mechanism; feasible for edge deployment due to small model size, but computational cost of the two-stage process and generalization to other domains should be examined.

Abstract: Accurately identifying student misconceptions is crucial for personalized education but faces three challenges: (1) data scarcity with long-tail distribution, where authentic student reasoning is difficult to synthesize; (2) fuzzy boundaries between error categories with high annotation noise; (3) deployment parado-large models overlook unconventional approaches due to pretraining bias and cannot be deployed on edge, while small models overfit to noise. Unlike traditional methods that increase diversity through large-scale data synthesis, we propose a two-stage knowledge distillation framework that mines high-value samples from existing data. The first stage performs standard distillation to transfer task capabilities. The second stage introduces a dual-layer marginal selection mechanism based on cognitive uncertainty, identifying four types of critical samples based on teacher model uncertainty and confidence differences. For different data subsets, we design difficulty-adaptive mechanism to balance hard/soft label contributions, enabling student models to inherit inter-class relationships from teacher soft labels while distinguishing ambiguous error types. Experiments show that with augmented training on only 10.30% of filtered samples, we achieve MAP@3 of 0.9585 (+17.8%) on the MAP-Charting dataset, and using only a 4B parameter model, we attain 84.38% accuracy on cross-topic tests of middle school algebra misconception benchmarks, significantly outperforming sota LLM (67.73%) and standard fine-tuned 72B models (81.25%). Our code is available at https://github.com/RoschildRui/acl2026_map.

</details>


### [351] [Crys-JEPA: Accelerating Crystal Discovery via Embedding Screening and Generative Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14759)
*Nian Liu,Nikita Kazeev,Stephen Gregory Dale,Artem Maevskiy,Yuwei Zeng,Ryoji Kubo,Pengru Huang,Thomas Laurent,Yann LeCun,Kostya S. Novoselov,Xavier Bresson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Identifies a stability–novelty trade-off in de novo crystal generation and introduces Crys-JEPA, an energy-aware latent embedding, plus a screening/refinement pipeline, achieving large gains on V.S.U.N metric.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current crystal generators trained to maximize likelihood bias samples toward known materials, harming discovery of truly novel, stable crystals. A method is needed that preserves formation-energy differences and enables efficient stability assessment without expensive evaluations.

Method: Propose Crys-JEPA, a joint embedding predictive architecture that learns an energy-aware latent space preserving formation-energy differences. Stability is assessed via embedding-based comparison to training crystals rather than direct energy evaluation. Build a screening-and-refinement pipeline that identifies generated candidates and re-trains the model with refinements.

Result: Empirical results show substantial improvements over baselines on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20: up to 81.4% and 82.6% gains on the V.S.U.N metric.

Conclusion: Energy-aware latent embeddings coupled with a screening/refinement loop can mitigate the stability–novelty trade-off in crystal generation, enabling more stable yet novel materials discovery and improving discovery efficiency as measured by V.S.U.N.

Abstract: De novo crystal generation seeks to discover materials that are not merely realistic, but also stable and novel. However, most existing generative models are trained to maximize the likelihood of observed crystals, which encourages samples to stay close to known materials yet not necessarily align with the criteria that matter in discovery. Through an empirical investigation, we show that current crystal generative models are caught in a pronounced stability--novelty trade-off: moving toward the observed distribution preserves stability but limits novelty, whereas moving away from it quickly destroys stability. This suggests that the useful region for discovering crystals that are both stable and novel is extremely narrow. To escape the trade-off, we introduce Crys-JEPA, a joint embedding predictive architecture for crystals that learns an energy-aware latent space preserving formation-energy differences. In this space, stability assessment can be reformulated as an embedding-based comparison against accessible training crystals, reducing the reliance on expensive energy evaluation and task-specific external references. Building on Crys-JEPA, we further develop a screening-and-refinement pipeline that identifies promising generated crystals and reintroduces them to refine the generative model. On MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 datasets, we achieve improvements over baselines up to 81.4% and 82.6% on V.S.U.N metric, respectively.

</details>


### [352] [Compositional Sparsity as an Inductive Bias for Neural Architecture Design](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14764)
*Hongyu Lin,Antonio Briola,Yuanrong Wang,Tomaso Aste*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes Homological Neural Networks (HNNs) combined with Information Filtering Networks (IFNs) to exploit compositional sparsity for high-dimensional learning; they yield sparse, interpretable graphs that match or exceed dense baselines with far fewer parameters and robustness.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the curse of dimensionality by identifying structural priors—compositional sparsity—in target functions; test whether mapping inferred sparse dependencies to fixed-wiring graphs via topology-aware networks improves scalability and performance.

Method: Use IFNs to extract sparse dependency structures via constrained information maximisation; map inferred topology into fixed-wiring sparse neural graphs using HNNs; formalize design principles and present an interpretable hierarchical pipeline.

Result: HNNs are orders of magnitude sparser than standard DNNs; recover compositional structure on synthetic tasks; remain stable as dimensionality grows; on real-world data they match or outperform dense baselines with fewer parameters, lower variance, reduced hyperparameter sensitivity.

Conclusion: HNNs enable abstraction through hierarchical composition, providing scalable, robust, data-efficient high-dimensional learning by exploiting structural priors; the approach yields interpretable sparse graphs and improved efficiency.

Abstract: Identifying the structural priors that enable Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) to overcome the curse of dimensionality is a fundamental challenge in machine learning theory. Existing literature suggests that effective high-dimensional learning is driven by compositional sparsity, where target functions decompose into constituents supported on low-dimensional variable subsets. To investigate this hypothesis, we combine Information Filtering Networks (IFNs), which extract sparse dependency structures via constrained information maximisation, with Homological Neural Networks (HNNs), which map the inferred topology into fixed-wiring sparse neural graphs. We formalise the design principles underlying this construction and present an interpretable pipeline in which abstraction emerges through hierarchical composition. HNNs are orders of magnitude sparser than standard DNNs and require only minimal hyperparameter tuning. On synthetic tasks with known sparse hierarchies, HNNs recover the underlying compositional structure and remain stable in regimes where dense alternatives degrade as dimensionality increases. Across a broad suite of real-world datasets, HNNs consistently match or outperform dense baselines while using far fewer parameters, exhibiting lower variance and showing reduced sensitivity to hyperparameters.

</details>


### [353] [Composable Crystals: Controllable Materials Discovery via Concept Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14769)
*Nian Liu,Yuwei Zeng,Ryoji Kubo,Nikita Kazeev,Stephen Gregory Dale,Artem Maevskiy,Pengru Huang,Thomas Laurent,Kostya S. Novoselov,Xavier Bresson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A concept-based, controllable crystal generator using a vector-quantized VAE to learn reusable crystal concepts for guided, compositional generation; an iterative composition generator refines concepts; achieves significant gains in the V.S.U.N metric on MP-20 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the limitations of black-box stochastic sampling in crystal generation by introducing interpretable, reusable building blocks (concepts) that generalize across distributions and enable controllable exploration beyond the training data.

Method: Train a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to discover a shared set of discrete crystal concepts; recombine these concepts to condition generation; introduce a composition generator and iteratively refine it with high-quality samples produced by the model.

Result: Experiments on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 show large gains in the V.S.U.N metric (53.2% and 51.7% respectively) with noticeable improvements in novelty; concepts show interpretability in local atomic environments and global symmetry patterns; the approach generalizes across crystal distributions.

Conclusion: A concept-based compositional framework can provide controllable, interpretable crystal generation, enabling exploration beyond training data and improving generation efficiency and novelty.

Abstract: De novo crystal generation, a central task in materials discovery, aims to generate crystals that are simultaneously valid, stable, unique, and novel. Existing methods mainly rely on black-box stochastic sampling, providing limited control over how generated structures move beyond the observed distribution. In this paper, we introduce a concept-based compositional framework for crystal generation. We train a vector-quantized variational autoencoder to automatically discover a shared set of reusable crystal concepts, which serve as building blocks for guided generation. These learned concepts naturally exhibit interpretability from both local atomic environments and global symmetry patterns, and generalize to crystals from different distributions. By recombining such concepts, our framework enables controllable exploration of novel crystals beyond the training distribution, rather than relying solely on unconstrained random sampling. To further improve composition efficiency, we introduce a composition generator and iteratively refine it using high-quality samples generated by the model itself. The resulting concept compositions are then used to condition downstream crystal generation. Numerical experiments on MP-20 and Alex-MP-20 show that compositing concepts separately increase base model up to 53.2% and 51.7% on V.S.U.N metric, with particular gains in novelty.

</details>


### [354] [Beyond What to Select: A Plug-and-play Oscillatory Data-Volume Scheduling for Efficient Model Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14773)
*Suorong Yang,Hanqi Zhu,Hai Gan,Fangjian Su,Guang Li,Furao Shen,Soujanya Poria*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PODS introduces oscillatory data-volume scheduling to improve efficiency and generalization in data-selected training, without changing sample scoring methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the static data-volume limitation of existing data selection methods and harness regularization from varying data volumes to enhance training efficiency and generalization.

Method: A plug-and-play module that, under a target selection ratio, alternates between low-ratio regularization phases and high-ratio recovery phases during training; compatible with static and dynamic selection methods; ratio-level, lightweight scheduling.

Result: Empirically yields consistent gains across datasets, architectures, and tasks; reduces ImageNet-1k training cost by ~50% with improved accuracy; accelerates LLM instruction tuning by >2x without performance loss.

Conclusion: PODS offers a versatile, task-agnostic scheduling mechanism that leverages selection-induced regularization to improve the efficiency-generalization trade-off in data-selected training, with broad applicability.

Abstract: Data selection accelerates training by identifying representative training data while preserving model performance. However, existing methods mainly focus on designing sample-importance criteria, i.e., deciding what to select, while typically fixing the selected data volume as the target ratio throughout training. Thus, they are often dynamic in sample identity but static in data volume. In this work, we revisit data selection from an optimization perspective and show that selected-data training induces an implicit regularization effect modulated by the instantaneous selection ratio. This reveals a key trade-off: lower ratios amplify selection-induced regularization, whereas higher ratios preserve data coverage and optimization fidelity. Motivated by this insight, we propose PODS, a Plug-and-play Oscillatory Data-volume Scheduling framework. Rather than introducing another sample-scoring metric, PODS serves as a lightweight module that dynamically schedules how much data to select over training. Under the target selection ratio, PODS alternates between low-ratio regularization phases and high-ratio recovery phases to exploit selection-induced regularization without sacrificing optimization stability. With its lightweight, ratio-level, and task-agnostic design, PODS is compatible with existing static and dynamic selection methods and broadly applicable across training paradigms. Experiments across various datasets, architectures, and tasks show that PODS consistently improves the efficiency-generalization trade-off, e.g., reducing ImageNet-1k training cost by 50% with improved accuracy and accelerating LLM instruction tuning by over 2x without performance degradation.

</details>


### [355] [Peng's Q($λ$) for Conservative Value Estimation in Offline Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14779)
*Byeongchan Kim,Min-hwan Oh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conservative Peng's Q(λ) (CPQL) is a model-free offline multi-step RL method that uses a conservative Q(λ) operator to stabilize learning, achieving near-behavior performance with theoretical guarantees and strong D4RL results, while also enhancing offline-to-online transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL suffers from distribution shift and overestimation/pessimism when using single-step Bellman backups. A multi-step conservative operator aims to leverage offline trajectories more effectively while regularizing values to align with the behavior policy.

Method: Introduce CPQL by adapting Peng's Q(λ) operator for conservative value estimation in a fully offline setting. Analyze the fixed point to show it lies near the behavior policy’s value, providing implicit behavior regularization. Demonstrate offline-to-online benefits by pretraining the Q-function with CPQL before online fine-tuning.

Result: CPQL consistently outperforms existing offline single-step baselines on the D4RL benchmark and offers near-optimal performance guarantees not achieved by prior conservative methods. In offline-to-online learning, CPQL-pretrained Q-functions reduce initial performance drops during fine-tuning and improve robustness.

Conclusion: Multi-step conservative value estimation via the CPQL operator is effective for offline RL, yielding strong empirical performance, theoretical guarantees, and beneficial offline-to-online transfer, with publicly available code.

Abstract: We propose a model-free offline multi-step reinforcement learning (RL) algorithm, Conservative Peng's Q($λ$) (CPQL). Our algorithm adapts the Peng's Q($λ$) (PQL) operator for conservative value estimation as an alternative to the Bellman operator. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work in offline RL to theoretically and empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of conservative value estimation with a \textit{multi-step} operator by fully leveraging offline trajectories. The fixed point of the PQL operator in offline RL lies closer to the value function of the behavior policy, thereby naturally inducing implicit behavior regularization. CPQL simultaneously mitigates over-pessimistic value estimation, achieves performance greater than (or equal to) that of the behavior policy, and provides near-optimal performance guarantees -- a milestone that previous conservative approaches could not achieve. Extensive numerical experiments on the D4RL benchmark demonstrate that CPQL consistently and significantly outperforms existing offline single-step baselines. In addition to the contributions of CPQL in offline RL, our proposed method also contributes to the offline-to-online learning framework. Using the Q-function pre-trained by CPQL in offline settings enables the online PQL agent to avoid the performance drop typically observed at the start of fine-tuning and to attain robust performance improvements. Our code is available at https://github.com/oh-lab/CPQL.

</details>
