### [356] [Understanding Imbalanced Forgetting in Rehearsal-Based Class-Incremental Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14785)
*Alberto Tamajo,Srinandan Dasmahapatra,Rahman Attar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Imbalanced forgetting in rehearsal-based class-incremental learning is systematic; authors derive three last-layer gradient-interference coefficients that predict forgetting rankings; self-induced interference is strongest and modulated by new-class interference; suggests mitigation by targeting these channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Catastrophic forgetting in CIL; clipping/ rehearsal helps but forgetting is uneven across past classes; need mechanistic understanding to guide mitigation.

Method: From principled analysis, derive three last-layer coefficients capturing gradient-level interference for each past class during an incremental step; evaluate their predictive power for forgetting rankings; conduct controlled experiments to relate coefficients and assess causality.

Result: The coefficients jointly predict the forgetting ranking of past classes; self-induced interference is the strongest predictor; evidence that new-class interference affects the self-induced coefficient; results point toward mitigating imbalanced forgetting by reducing disparities in interference sources.

Conclusion: Provides a mechanistic account linking last-layer gradient interactions to class-wise forgetting; offers directions for mitigating imbalanced forgetting by targeting identified interference channels; further work needed to translate into practical algorithms.

Abstract: Neural networks suffer from catastrophic forgetting in class-incremental learning (CIL) settings. Rehearsal$\unicode{x2013}$replaying a subset of past samples$\unicode{x2013}$is a well-established mitigation strategy. However, recent results suggest that, despite balanced rehearsal allocation, some classes are forgotten substantially more than others. Despite its relevance, this imbalanced forgetting phenomenon remains underexplored. This work shows that imbalanced forgetting arises systematically and severely in rehearsal-based CIL and investigates it extensively. Specifically, we construct, from a principled analysis, three last-layer coefficients that capture different gradient-level sources of interference affecting each past class during an incremental step. We then demonstrate that, together, they reliably predict how past classes will rank in terms of forgetting at the end of that step. While predictive performance alone does not establish causality, these results support the interpretation of the coefficients as a plausible mechanistic account linking last-layer gradient-level interactions during training to class-level forgetting outcomes. Notably, one coefficient$\unicode{x2013}$capturing self-induced interference$\unicode{x2013}$emerges as the strongest predictor, with controlled experiments providing evidence consistent with this coefficient being influenced by the new-class interference coefficient. Overall, our findings provide valuable insights and suggest promising directions for mitigating imbalanced forgetting by reducing class-wise disparities in the identified sources of interference.

</details>


### [357] [GFMate: Empowering Graph Foundation Models with Test-time Prompt Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14809)
*Yan Jiang,Ruihong Qiu,Zi Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GFMate enables pre-training-agnostic test-time graph prompt tuning by introducing centroid and layer prompts and a complementary learning objective, yielding significant gains across domains/datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current graph prompt methods entangle prompts with source-domain pre-training, limiting cross-domain/generalization; they underutilize unlabelled target data and rely on few-shot fine-tuning.

Method: Apply centroid and layer prompts post pre-training on target domains; use test-time complementary learning objective to leverage labeled and unlabeled target data for prompt tuning.

Result: Extensive experiments on 12 benchmarks show superior performance and efficiency, with improvements up to 30.63%.

Conclusion: GFMate offers a pre-training-agnostic, test-time tuning framework for GFMs that generalizes across domains and models, boosting performance while remaining efficient; code released.

Abstract: Graph prompt tuning has shown great potential in graph learning by introducing trainable prompts to enhance the model performance in conventional single-domain scenarios. Recent research has extended graph prompts to improve Graph Foundation Models (GFMs) by few-shot tuning auxiliary prompts. Despite their progress, most existing methods embed source-domain information into prompts, which serve either as input to GFMs or encoded during model pre-training. Such prompt entanglement with specific source domains and GFM pre-training strategy restricts their generalisability to other domains and different GFMs. Furthermore, existing GFM prompts merely rely on few-shot tuning for adaptation, neglecting the rich information in unlabelled target domain test data. Motivated by these insights, this paper aims to empower GFMs with pre-training-agnostic test-time graph prompt tuning, named GFMate. GFMate introduces centroid and layer prompts applied after pre-training on target domains, avoiding entanglement with specific source domains and model pre-training. In addition, a test-time complementary learning objective is devised to exploit both labelled and unlabelled target domain data for effective test-time prompt tuning. Extensive experiments on 12 benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance and efficiency of GFMate, achieving improvements of up to 30.63%. Code is available at https://github.com/YanJiangJerry/GFMate.

</details>


### [358] [ToMAToMP: Robust and Multi-Parameter Topological Clustering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14824)
*Ludo Andrianirina,Mathieu Carrière*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces ToMAToMP, a multi-function topological clustering method that extends ToMATo using MMA decomposition from multi-parameter persistent homology to provide robust, graph-tuning-free clustering with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ToMATo struggles with single-function input, graph hyper-parameter tuning, and sensitivity to outliers; real-world data often require integrating multiple feature functions. A multi-function, theory-backed clustering method is needed.

Method: Leverages MMA decomposition to combine multiple scalar functions into a multi-parameter persistence framework. Develops ToMAToMP with theoretical robustness guarantees; derives corollaries including independence from graph tuning and outlier robustness; provides algorithmic steps for clustering using persistent components across functions.

Result: Proves robustness guarantees; demonstrates improved clustering quality and efficiency over baselines in multiple datasets through numerical experiments.

Conclusion: ToMAToMP successfully generalizes ToMATo to multi-function data with provable guarantees and practical benefits, enabling more reliable and user-friendly topological clustering.

Abstract: Topological clustering, and its main algorithm ToMATo, is a clustering method from Topological Data Analysis (TDA) which has been applied successfully in several applications during the last few years. This is due to its high versatility, as clusters are detected from the persistent components in the sublevel sets of any user-defined function (gene expression, pixel values, etc), and efficiency, as topological clustering enjoys robustness guarantees. However, ToMATo is also limited in several ways. First, a graph on the data points needs to be provided as a hyper-parameter of the method (whose fine-tuning is left to the user). Second, ToMATo is known to be very sensitive to outlier values in the function range. Finally, and most importantly, ToMATo can only handle one function at a time, whereas it is critical to use several functions in various applications. In this article, we introduce ToMAToMP: the first topological clustering method able to handle several functions at the same time with theoretical guarantees. More specifically, we leverage a recent tool from multi-parameter persistent homology, called MMA decomposition, to design our clustering algorithm, and prove that it enjoys robustness properties. As corollaries, we show that it can be used to make ToMATo independent of graph tuning, and robust to outliers. Finally, we provide a set of numerical experiments showcasing the efficiency and quality of the clusterings produced by ToMAToMP, by showing strong improvement over non-topological and topological baselines for various datasets.

</details>


### [359] [GenAI for Energy-Efficient and Interference-Aware Compressed Sensing of GNSS Signals on a Google Edge TPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14839)
*Thorben Wegner,Lucas Heublein,Tobias Feigl,Felix Ott,Christopher Mutschler,Alexander Rügamer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A hardware-friendly GenAI approach using VAEs on Google Edge TPUs for real-time GNSS jamming compression and classification, achieving >42x data reduction with high fidelity and ~72 interference-type classification (F2 ~0.91) close to original signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time GNSS interference mitigation in power-constrained environments without relying on cloud processing or large data transmissions.

Method: Evaluate multiple VAE architectures for compressing GNSS signals (IQ, FFT, and handcrafted features) and classifying jamming/spoofing on-edge. 8-bit quantization enables deployment on Google Edge TPUs. Compare conditional and FactorVAE variants for latent disentanglement. Assess performance on reconstruction quality (F2) and interference-type classification (F2) over ~72 classes.

Result: Achieves >42x compression; reconstructed signals F2 ~0.923, classification on reconstructed data F2 ~0.915 across ~72 interference types; on-device inference with 8-bit quantized VAEs; reduces jammer transmission costs; ablations show benefit of latent disentanglement via conditional/FactorVAE models.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of on-device GenAI-based compression and classification for GNSS interference, enabling real-time, power-efficient mitigation. Latent disentanglement improves interpretability and trust in ML-based interference solutions; substantial data reduction without significant loss in classification accuracy.

Abstract: Traditional methods for classifying global navigation satellite system (GNSS) jamming signals typically involve post-processing raw or spectral data streams, requiring complex and costly data transmission to cloud-based interference classification systems. In contrast, our proposed approach efficiently compresses GNSS data streams directly at the hardware receiver while simultaneously classifying jamming and spoofing attacks in real time. Given the growing prevalence of GNSS jamming, there is a critical need for real-time solutions suitable for power-constrained environments. This paper introduces a novel method for compressing and classifying GNSS jamming threats using generative artificial intelligence (GenAI), specifically variational autoencoders (VAEs), deployed on Google Edge tensor processing units (TPUs). The study evaluates various autoencoder (AE) architectures to compress and reconstruct GNSS signals, focusing on preserving interference characteristics while minimizing data size near the receiver hardware. The pipeline adapts large-scale AE models for Google Edge TPUs through 8-bit quantization to ensure energy-efficient deployment. Tests on raw in-phase and quadrature-phase (IQ) data, Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) data, and handcrafted features show the system achieves significant compression (>42x) and accurate classification of approximately 72 interference types on reconstructed signals (F2-score 0.915), closely matching the original signals (F2-score 0.923). The hardware-centric GenAI approach also substantially reduces jammer signal transmission costs, offering a practical solution for interference mitigation. Ablation studies on conditional and factorized VAEs (i.e., FactorVAE) explore latent feature disentanglement for data generation, enhancing model interpretability and fostering trust in machine learning (ML) solutions for sensitive interference applications.

</details>


### [360] [In-Context Learning for Data-Driven Censored Inventory Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14840)
*Sohom Mukherjee,Anh-Duy Pham,Richard Pibernik,Yunbei Xu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ICGPS for censored demand in newsvendor with decision-dependent censoring, combining offline generative priors and online in-context sampling; theoretical Bayesian regret bound; practical ChronosFlow implementation with strong empirical performance and robustness to prior mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address brittleness of parametric TS under prior mismatch and limitations of offline imputation for online learning in decision-dependent censored feedback (R-NV). Leverage predictive decision-making view and learned latent completions.

Method: Propose ICGPS: use oracle actions on completions of latent demand produced by a meta-trained generative model, deployed online via in-context autoregressive generation. The ChronosFlow architecture combines a frozen time-series transformer backbone with a trainable conditional normalizing-flow head to generate censoring-consistent samples. Theoretically bound Bayesian regret relative to a TS benchmark with ideal completion, plus deployment penalty due to completion mismatch. For R-NV, reduce censored feedback to bandit convex optimization feedback; establish transfer of offline predictive mismatch to online performance under conditions of coverage and stability.

Result: Theoretical: Bayesian regret of ICGPS with learned completion kernel is bounded by TS regret with ideal kernel plus deployment penalty scaling as sqrt(T) * sqrt(completion mismatch). Sublinear Bayesian regret for R-NV. Empirical: ChronosFlow-ICGPS matches well-specified TS, outperforms myopic and UCB baselines, robust to prior mismatch and distribution shift; strong performance on SuperStore under heavy censoring.

Conclusion: ICGPS provides a plug-in template for problems with known TS regret bounds, enabling robust online learning under decision-dependent censoring. ChronosFlow offers a practical instantiation achieving strong empirical performance and robustness, suggesting broader applicability to censored feedback in online optimization.

Abstract: We study inventory control with decision-dependent censoring, focusing on the censored or repeated newsvendor (R-NV), where each order quantity determines whether demand is fully observed or censored by sales. Existing approaches based on parametric Thompson sampling (TS) can be brittle under prior mismatch, while offline imputation methods need not transfer to online learning. Motivated by the predictive view of decision making, we combine these ideas by taking oracle actions on learned completions of latent demand. We propose in-context generative posterior sampling (ICGPS), which uses modern generative models that are meta-trained offline and deployed online by in-context autoregressive generation. Theoretically, we show that the Bayesian regret of ICGPS with a learned completion kernel is bounded by the Bayesian regret of a TS benchmark with the ideal completion kernel plus a deployment penalty scaling as $\sqrt{T}$ times the square root of the completion mismatch. This yields a plug-in template for operational problems with known TS regret bounds. For R-NV, we derive sublinear Bayesian regret by reducing censored feedback to bandit convex optimization feedback. We also show that, under reasonable coverage and stability assumptions, the online completion mismatch is controlled by the offline censored predictive mismatch, so offline predictive quality transfers to online performance. Practically, we instantiate ICGPS with ChronosFlow, which combines a frozen time-series transformer backbone with a trainable conditional normalizing-flow head for fast censoring-consistent sampling. In benchmark experiments, ChronosFlow-ICGPS matches correctly specified TS, outperforms myopic and UCB-style baselines, and is robust to prior mismatch and distribution shift. ChronosFlow-ICGPS also performs well for the real-world SuperStore dataset, especially under heavy censoring.

</details>


### [361] [GPart: End-to-End Isometric Fine-Tuning via Global Parameter Partitioning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14841)
*Paolo Mandica,Michał Brzozowski,Zuzanna Dubanowska,Neo Christopher Chung*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GPart is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses a single isometric partition matrix to map a low-dimensional trainable vector directly into full model weights, removing the LoRA low-rank bottleneck and preserving isometry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA’s bilinear, low-rank mapping distorts the optimization landscape; existing PEFT methods (e.g., Uni-LoRA) do not preserve end-to-end isometry; there is a need for a highly parameter-efficient, distance-preserving fine-tuning approach.

Method: Employ a single isometric partition matrix to map a d-dimensional trainable vector directly into the full weight space, achieving end-to-end isometry with only d+1 storage (the trainable vector plus a random seed); no low-rank factorization is used; random projection drives the mapping.

Result: Empirically shows superior or comparable performance to existing PEFT methods across NLP, computer vision, and mathematical reasoning tasks, with state-of-the-art efficiency by removing structural constraints.

Conclusion: Removing structural constraints and the low-rank bottleneck yields a simple, elegant, and highly efficient PEFT approach; random low-dimensional subspaces of the full weight space can suffice for effective fine-tuning.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) has become the dominant paradigm for parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) of large language models (LLMs). However, its bilinear structure introduces a critical limitation: the mapping from trainable parameters to weight updates is not distance-preserving, distorting the optimization landscape. Methods that project a low-dimensional vector into LoRA's parameter space, such as Uni-LoRA, improve parameter efficiency, but the subsequent bilinear LoRA map breaks end-to-end isometry, leaving the core distance-preservation problem unresolved. We propose GPart (Global Partition fine-tuning), a highly parameter-efficient fine-tuning method which removes the low-rank bottleneck entirely. Our method uses a single isometric partition matrix to map a $d$-dimensional trainable vector directly into the full weight space of the model. The result is an extremely minimal fine-tuning pipeline: one random projection, end-to-end isometric, with a single clean hyperparameter ($d$) and storage cost of $d+1$ values (the trainable vector plus a random seed). GPart builds on the theoretical premise that effective fine-tuning can emerge from random low-dimensional subspaces of the full weight space, without imposing low-rank matrix structure. We empirically demonstrate the superior or comparable performance of GPart to existing PEFT methods on natural language understanding, computer vision tasks, and mathematical reasoning. Overall, GPart achieves state-of-the-art efficiency and performance by removing structural constraints, offering a straightforward and elegant path to PEFT.

</details>


### [362] [Exploitation of Hidden Context in Dynamic Movement Forecasting: A Neural Network Journey from Recurrent to Graph Neural Networks and General Purpose Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14855)
*Lukas Schelenz,Shobha Rajanna,Denis Gosalci,Lucas Heublein,Jonas Pirkl,Jonathan Ott,Felix Ott,Christopher Mutschler,Tobias Feigl*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ML-based trajectory forecasting with context-aware hybrid LSTM yields best final displacement error (~1.51 m) for NBA-like movements, but no single model dominates all metrics; linear models lag behind across horizons up to 2 s.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mitigate prediction delays in fast-changing, interactive sports by improving trajectory forecasting; classical filters struggle with non-linear, context-dependent dynamics; evaluate a spectrum of models to identify strengths and trade-offs.

Method: Compare classical models (SARIMA(X), KF, PF) with ML approaches (LSTM, GNNs, Transformers) and a context-augmented hybrid LSTM; test across input history lengths, generalizability, and contextual information; measure final displacement error (FDE) up to 2 s horizon.

Result: ML methods outperform linear models across horizons up to 2 s; hybrid LSTM with contextual information achieves FDE 1.51 m, beating TCNN, GAT, and Transformers; smaller data and training time requirements than GAT/Transformers; no single architecture excels across all metrics.

Conclusion: Emphasize task-specific model selection and integration of contextual interactions; prioritize hybrid/context-aware designs for fast-paced trajectory prediction in sports; further work to balance trade-offs and data requirements.

Abstract: Forecasting within signal processing pipelines is crucial for mitigating delays, particularly in predicting the dynamic movements of objects such as NBA players. This task poses significant challenges due to the inherently interactive and unpredictable nature of sports, where abrupt changes in velocity and direction are prevalent. Traditional approaches, including (S)ARIMA(X), Kalman filters (KF), and Particle filters (PF), often struggle to model the non-linear dynamics present in such scenarios. Machine learning (ML) methods, such as long short-term memory (LSTM) networks, graph neural networks (GNNs), and Transformers, offer greater flexibility and accuracy but frequently fail to explicitly capture the interplay between temporal dependencies and contextual interactions, which are critical in chaotic sports environments. In this paper, we evaluate these models and assess their strengths and weaknesses. Experimental results reveal key performance trade-offs across input history length, generalizability, and the ability to incorporate contextual information. ML-based methods demonstrated substantial improvements over linear models across forecast horizons of up to 2s. Among the tested architectures, our hybrid LSTM augmented with contextual information achieved the lowest final displacement error (FDE) of 1.51m, outperforming temporal convolutional neural network (TCNN), graph attention network (GAT), and Transformers, while also requiring less data and training time compared to GAT and Transformers. Our findings indicate that no single architecture excels across all metrics, emphasizing the need for task-specific considerations in trajectory prediction for fast-paced, dynamic environments such as NBA gameplay.

</details>


### [363] [REALM: Retrospective Encoder Alignment for LFP Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14867)
*Peicheng Wu,Zhenyu Bu,Runze Ma,Lin Du*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: REALM is a retrospective distillation framework enabling causal LFP decoding, achieving competitive performance with 2× fewer parameters and 10× faster training, bridging the offline-online gap for wireless, spike-free BCIs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spikes offer high resolution but are power- and bandwidth-intensive for high-channel-count, wireless BCIs. LFPs are more power-efficient and stable but typically yield lower accuracy and rely on non-causal models ill-suited for real-time deployment. There is a need for causal, high-performance LFP-based decoding.

Method: Pretrain a bidirectional Mamba-2 teacher on multi-session LFP data using a masked autoencoding objective. Distill to a causal student via a combined loss for representation alignment and task supervision, i.e., offline-to-online retrospective distillation.

Result: REALM consistently outperforms both causal and non-causal SOTA LFP-based decoding. It achieves comparable or better decoding with a 2× reduction in parameter count and a 10× reduction in training time, demonstrating that LFP-only models can achieve competitive decoding without spikes.

Conclusion: Retrospective distillation effectively bridges the offline-online gap in neural decoding, making LFP-based, spike-free models practical for next-generation wireless implantable BCIs.

Abstract: Spike activity has been the dominant neural signal for behavior decoding due to its high spatial and temporal resolution. However, as brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) move toward high channel counts and wireless operation, the high sampling frequency of spike signals becomes a bottleneck due to high power and bandwidth requirements. Local field potentials (LFPs) represent a different spatial-temporal scale of brain activity compared to spikes, offering key advantages including improved long-term stability, reduced energy consumption, and lower bandwidth requirement. Despite these benefits, LFP-based decoding models typically show reduced accuracy and often rely on non-causal architectures that are unsuitable for real-time deployment. To address these challenges, we propose REALM: a retrospective distillation framework that enables causal LFP decoding. Inspired by offline-to-online distillation strategies in speech recognition, REALM transfers representational knowledge from a pretrained multi-session bidirectional LFP model to a causal version for real-time deployment. We first pretrain a bidirectional Mamba-2 teacher model using a masked autoencoding objective. We then distill this teacher model into a compact student model via a combined objective of representation alignment and task supervision. REALM consistently outperforms both causal and non-causal LFP-based SOTA methods for behavior decoding. Notably, our REALM improves decoding performance while achieving a $2\times$ reduction in parameter count and a $10\times$ reduction in training time. These results demonstrate that retrospective distillation effectively bridges the gap between offline and real-time neural decoding. REALM shows that LFP-only models can achieve competitive decoding performance without reliance on spike signals, offering a practical and scalable alternative for next-generation wireless implantable BCIs.

</details>


### [364] [AIMing for Standardised Explainability Evaluation in GNNs: A Framework and Case Study on Graph Kernel Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14884)
*Magdalena Proszewska,N. Siddharth*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AIM is a flexible, multi-maceted framework for evaluating explainability in GNNs, enabling cross-model comparison by focusing on accuracy, instance-level explanations, and model-level explanations, and it guides improvements like xGKN.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation mostly relies on post-hoc explanations or model-specific signals, making cross-model comparison difficult and inhibiting insights across a suite of interpretability measures. AIM aims to provide a broad, constraint-light framework to assess and compare explainability in inherently interpretable GNNs.

Method: Define AIM with three axes—Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, and Model-level explanations. Apply AIM in a pipeline to extract and evaluate explanations from inherently interpretable GNNs (e.g., graph kernel networks and prototype networks), analyze limitations, and derive design insights. Use insights to develop xGKN that maintains accuracy while improving explainability.

Result: Identification of limitations of GKNs and PN-based explanations when evaluated with AIM, actionable insights into their interpretability, and the demonstration that xGKN can achieve high accuracy with enhanced explainability compared to the baseline models.

Conclusion: AIM offers a robust, generalizable framework for evaluating and guiding explainability in GNNs, enabling systematic improvements to interpretable models and advancing XAI for graph learning.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have advanced significantly in handling graph-structured data, but a comprehensive framework for evaluating explainability remains lacking. Existing evaluation frameworks primarily involve post-hoc explanations, and operate in the setting where multiple methods generate a suite of explanations for a single model. This makes comparison of explanations across models difficult. Evaluation of inherently interpretable models often targets a specific aspect of interpretability relevant to the model, but remains underdeveloped in terms of generating insight across a suite of measures. We introduce AIM, a comprehensive framework that addresses these limitations by measuring Accuracy, Instance-level explanations, and Model-level explanations. AIM is formulated with minimal constraints to enhance flexibility and facilitate broad applicability. Here, we use AIM in a pipeline, extracting explanations from inherently interpretable GNNs such as graph kernel networks (GKNs) and prototype networks (PNs), evaluating these explanations with AIM, identifying their limitations and obtaining insights to their characteristics. Taking GKNs as a case study, we show how the insights obtained from AIM can be used to develop an updated model, xGKN, that maintains high accuracy while demonstrating improved explainability. Our approach aims to advance the field of Explainable AI (XAI) for GNNs, providing more robust and practical solutions for understanding and improving complex models.

</details>


### [365] [Critic-Driven Voronoi-Quantization for Distilling Deep RL Policies to Explainable Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14897)
*Senne Deproost,Denis Steckelmacher,Ann Nowé*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces Critic-Driven Voronoi State Partitioning to distill a deep RL policy into interpretable linear subpolicies using Voronoi partitioning guided by the critic; achieves near-original policy performance with a compact set of linear functions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address the performance-interpretability trade-off in RL policy distillation by incorporating RL-specific components (the critic) into the surrogate, rather than minimizing only behavior mismatch.

Method: Partition the state space via a Voronoi quantizer into regions assigned to simple linear subpolicies. Use the critic/value network to identify regions with insufficient value and iteratively add subpolicies; employ gradient descent for optimization; assign subpolicies using nearest-neighbor Voronoi cells in the state space.

Result: Empirical validation on standard benchmarks shows the distillation closely approximates the original policy using a reasonably small set of linear functions.

Conclusion: Critic-guided Voronoi partitioning provides a model-agnostic, interpretable surrogate for deep RL policies that preserves performance with a compact, region-based linear representation.

Abstract: Despite many successful attempts at explaining Deep Reinforcement Learning policies using distillation, it remains difficult to balance the performance-interpretability trade-off and select a fitting surrogate model. In addition to this, traditional distillation only minimizes the distance between the behavior of the original and the surrogate policy while other RL-specific components such as action value are disregarded. To solve this, we introduce a new model-agnostic method called Critic-Driven Voronoi State Partitioning, which partitions a black box control policy into regions where a simple class of model can be optimized using gradient descent. By exploiting the critic value network of the original policy, we iteratively introduce new subpolicies in regions with insufficient value, standing in for a measure of policy complexity. The partitioning, a Voronoi quantizer, uses nearest neighbor lookups to assign a linear function to each point in the state space resulting in a cell-like diagram. We validate our approach on several well known benchmarks and proof that this distillation approaches the original policy using a reasonable sized set of linear functions.

</details>


### [366] [TILBench: A Systematic Benchmark for Tabular Imbalanced Learning Across Data Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14915)
*Ruizhe Liu,Jiaqi Luo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A comprehensive large-scale benchmark (TILBench) evaluates 40+ imbalanced-learning algorithms on 57 tabular datasets with 200k experiments, showing no universal winner and providing practical method recommendations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imbalanced learning in tabular data lacks systematic empirical understanding across diverse data characteristics; previous work has not clarified how method families perform under varying data traits and resource constraints.

Method: Introduce TILBench: a large-scale empirical benchmark evaluating representative algorithms across many datasets and controlled data characteristics; assess predictive performance, robustness, and scalability; provide analysis and recommendations.

Result: No single method consistently dominates across all settings; performance strongly depends on dataset characteristics and computational constraints; the benchmark yields dataset-aware guidance.

Conclusion: TILBench enables informed method selection for real-world imbalanced learning on tabular data and highlights the importance of dataset-aware benchmarking for algorithm choice.

Abstract: Imbalanced learning remains a fundamental challenge in tabular data applications. Despite decades of research and numerous proposed algorithms, a systematic empirical understanding of how different imbalanced learning methods behave across diverse data characteristics is still lacking. In particular, it remains unclear how different method families compare in predictive performance, robustness under varying data characteristics, and computational scalability. In this work, we present Tabular Imbalanced Learning Benchmark (TILBench), a large-scale empirical benchmark for tabular imbalanced learning. TILBench evaluates more than 40 representative algorithms across 57 diverse tabular datasets, resulting in over 200000 controlled experiments across a wide range of data characteristics. Our findings show that no single method consistently dominates across all settings; instead, the effectiveness of imbalanced learning methods depends strongly on dataset characteristics and computational constraints. Based on these findings, we provide practical recommendations for selecting appropriate methods in real-world applications.

</details>


### [367] [A Mutual Information Lower Bound for Multimodal Regression Active Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14917)
*Leonardo Ferreira Guilhoto,Akshat Kaushal,Paris Perdikaris*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MI-LB acquisition for continuous regression with multimodal predictions using a Two-Index uncertainty decomposition; derives a closed-form MI lower bound for MDN ensembles; empirically strong on multimodal benchmarks, outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address epistemic uncertainty in continuous regression with multimodal predictive distributions, where variance or BALD are inadequate for capturing modal disagreement.

Method: Introduce a Two-Index framework separating epistemic (model hypothesis) and aleatoric (within-hypothesis) randomness; perform entropy decomposition to identify mutual information between outputs and the epistemic index; derive a tractable MI-LB acquisition function for Mixture Density Network ensembles.

Result: MI-LB matches or outperforms baselines on multimodal benchmarks; baselines relying on geometric/Fisher criteria only succeed when multimodality is encoded in inputs and fail otherwise.

Conclusion: The MI-LB acquisition is a principled, data-vanishing objective capturing the uncertainty that data can resolve; provides a practical, closed-form surrogate for MI in continuous, multimodal regression.

Abstract: Active learning for continuous regression has lacked an acquisition function that targets epistemic uncertainty when the predictive distribution is multimodal: variance misses modal disagreement, and information-theoretic targets like BALD are designed for discrete outputs. We introduce a Two-Index framework that makes this separation explicit: one stochastic index selects among competing model hypotheses (epistemic source), while a second governs within-hypothesis randomness (aleatoric source). An entropy decomposition within the framework identifies the mutual information between the output and the epistemic index as a principled acquisition objective, and we prove this quantity vanishes as the model is trained on growing datasets, confirming that it captures exactly the uncertainty data can resolve. Because this mutual information is intractable for continuous outputs, we derive the Mutual Information Lower Bound (MI-LB) acquisition function, a closed-form approximation for Mixture Density Network ensembles. On benchmarks featuring multimodal systems, MI-LB matches or beats every baseline evaluated and is the only method to do so consistently -- geometric and Fisher-based baselines compete only when the input space already encodes the multimodality, and collapse otherwise.

</details>


### [368] [Learning with Shallow Neural Networks on Cluster-Structured Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14927)
*Elisabetta Cornacchia,Laurent Massoulié*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A tractable data model with latent Boolean variables and clustered, correlated inputs shows that layerwise gradient descent on shallow nets can achieve sample complexity independent of input dimension (up to log factors) when SNR is high; results support theory with experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how intra-input correlations (beyond simple low-dimensional projection) influence the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent, in contrast to standard models that assume low-dimensional structure in the target function; real data (images, text, genomes) exhibit spatial correlations.

Method: Propose a tractable model where the target depends on a small number of latent Boolean variables; input features are grouped into clusters and correlated with latent variables; assume identifiability; analyze a layerwise gradient-descent training of a shallow neural network; derive how sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables and input dimension under sufficient signal-to-noise ratio.

Result: Under identifiability, the sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables; when the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, it becomes independent of input dimension up to logarithmic terms; empirical validation on synthetic and real data supports the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Input-space correlations can dramatically reduce the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent in shallow networks under the proposed structured model; the results offer explanation for practical data where high-dimensional inputs yield efficient learning despite rich spatial correlations.

Abstract: The success of deep learning in high-dimensional settings is often attributed to the presence of low-dimensional structure in real-world data. While standard theoretical models typically assume that this structure lies in the target function, projecting unstructured inputs onto a low-dimensional subspace, data such as images, text or genomic sequences exhibit strong spatial correlations within the input space itself. In this paper, we propose a tractable model to study how these correlations affect the sample complexity of learning with gradient descent on shallow neural networks. Specifically, we consider targets that depend on a small number of latent Boolean variables, and input features grouped into clusters and correlated with the latent variables. Under an identifiability assumption, we show that for a layerwise gradient-descent variant, the sample complexity scales with the number of hidden variables and, when the signal-to-noise ratio is sufficiently high, is independent of the input dimension, up to logarithmic terms. We empirically test our theoretical findings on both synthetic and real data.

</details>


### [369] [A Hardware-Aware, Per-Layer Methodology for Post-Training Quantization of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14929)
*Earl Killian*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Post-training quantization (SOP) for LLM weights using per-layer codebooks and LUT-based decoding; achieves 6.5 bpw FP6 with 1.5 bpw storage savings vs FP8 across six model families; introduces HIF LUT format; full 4.5–6 bpw evaluation in a companion paper.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce weight storage with near-lossless fidelity for large language models while enabling hardware-efficient decoding via per-layer LUTs and block-scale techniques.

Method: Per-layer search over fixed and dynamic codebooks selected by per-block bits; signed per-block scales; activation-weighted cosine-based selection; multiple-choice knapsack promotion for sensitive layers; outlier and sparse-residual correction; fixed codebooks (NF4, BOF4, Split87, SH4), optimized DD4 codebooks in LUT SRAM; hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF).

Result: FP6 operating point E2M3sUE4M4 at 6.5 bpw achieves lower weight reconstruction error than FP8 baseline E4M3 at 8.0 bpw, while reducing storage by 1.5 bpw; validated across six open-model families; full 4.5–6 bpw evaluation and techniques such as layer promotion and sparse residual correction are covered in a companion paper.

Conclusion: Block-scaled, small-atom quantization with carefully chosen scale precision can replace FP8 in large models; the approach demonstrates hardware-friendly, near-lossless weight quantization with reduced storage, with additional results in a companion paper.

Abstract: Scaled Outer Product (SOP) is a post-training quantization methodology for large language model weights, designed to deliver near-lossless fidelity at 4.5--6 bits per weight on hardware with per-layer LUT decode. The methodology combines per-layer search of fixed and dynamic codebook pairs selected by a per-block selection bit, signed per-block scales, activation-weighted cosine selection, and multiple-choice knapsack promotion of sensitive layers with outlier and sparse-residual correction. Fixed codebooks include NF4, BOF4, Split87, and SH4; per-layer optimized codebooks (DD4) are hosted in LUT SRAM. A new hardware-efficient LUT output format (HIF) is proposed to improve performance, energy, and cost. Across six open model families, the recommended FP6 operating point (E2M3sUE4M4, 6.5 bpw) achieves lower weight reconstruction error than the conventional per-layer-POT FP8 baseline (E4M3, 8.0 bpw) at 1.5 bpw lower storage cost, demonstrating that block-scaled small atoms with carefully chosen scale precision can replace conventionally-deployed FP8. Full evaluation across the 4.5--6 bpw range, including layer promotion and sparse residual correction, is reported in a companion paper.

</details>


### [370] [Octopus: History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization for Continual Learning in Multimodal Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14938)
*Yuehao Liu,Shanyan Guan,Weijia Zhang,Xuanming Shang,Yanhao Ge,Wei Li,Chao Ma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Octopus uses History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO) in a two-stage finetuning framework to enable continual learning in multimodal large language models without data replay, achieving state-of-the-art performance on UCIT and beating prior SOTA by 2.14% in Avg and 6.82% in Last.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address core limitations of existing continual learning strategies: architecture-based methods incur overhead and poor generalization; rehearsal-based methods raise privacy/storage concerns due to data retention; regularization alone fails to fully prevent parameter interference. The aim is to enforce gradient-level orthogonality without historical task data to balance plasticity and stability.

Method: Propose Octopus with History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO) and a two-stage finetuning strategy that decouples task adaptation from regularization, enabling gradient orthogonality without access to old task data.

Result: Experimentation on UCIT shows Octopus achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% (Avg) and 6.82% (Last).

Conclusion: Octopus provides an effective, data-replay-free continual learning solution for multimodal LLMs, delivering strong plasticity–stability balance and leading performance on UCIT.

Abstract: Continual learning in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) aims to sequentially acquire knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, yet existing methods face inherent limitations: architecture-based approaches incur additional computational overhead and often generalize poorly to new tasks, rehearsal-based methods rely on storing historical data, raising privacy and storage concerns, and conventional regularization-based strategies alone are insufficient to fully prevent parameter interference. We propose Octopus, a two-stage continual learning framework based on History-Free Gradient Orthogonalization (HiFGO), which enforces gradient-level orthogonality without historical task data. Our proposed two-stage finetuning strategy decouples task adaptation from regularization, achieving a principled balance between plasticity and stability. Experiments on UCIT show that Octopus establishes state-of-the-art performance, surpassing prior SOTA by 2.14% and 6.82% in terms of Avg and Last.

</details>


### [371] [Not All Symbols Are Equal: Importance-Aware Constellation Design for Semantic Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14940)
*Albert Shaju,Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas,Mayukh Roy Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A joint semantic-physical layer design for goal-oriented transmission, using VQ-VAE to extract discrete concepts, a semantic criticality indicator to rank them, and RL to adaptively select transmission; a learned semantic-aware M-QAM constellation rearranges symbol positions by semantic importance and co-occurrence. Introduces semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) and semantic protection probability (SPP); proves Gray-coded constellations are strictly suboptimal under SCI-Weighted SSV when semantic importance/co-occurrence are non-uniform. Reports near-100% SPP and high semantic quality with 21:1 compression across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, FSDD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Protect task-relevant information in semantic communications beyond source compression by jointly considering semantic content and physical-layer mapping; traditional approaches separate encoding and modulation, making critical symbols equally vulnerable to channel errors.

Method: A pipeline combining a vector quantized-variational autoencoder (VQ-VAE) to extract discrete latent concepts, a semantic criticality indicator (SCI) to score concept relevance to the task, and a deep reinforcement learning (DRL) agent that selects which concepts to transmit based on current channel conditions. At the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M-QAM constellation positions symbols according to joint co-occurrence statistics and SCI; introduces SSV and SPP metrics; provides theoretical proof of suboptimality of Gray coding under SCI weighting.

Result: Empirical results show the learned constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders from 4-QAM to 1024-QAM (vs ~50% for standard constellations) at high spectral efficiency. Demonstrates a 21:1 compression ratio maintaining semantic quality above 0.9 and generalizes across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.

Conclusion: Joint semantic-physical layer design with SCI-driven concept selection and semantic-aware constellations significantly improves protection of task-relevant information. Gray-coded constellations are strictly suboptimal under SCI-Weighted SSV in the presence of non-uniform semantic importance and co-occurrence.

Abstract: Semantic communication systems for goal-oriented transmission must protect task-relevant information not only through source compression but also via physical layer mapping. Existing approaches decouple constellation design and semantic encoding, exposing critical symbols to channel errors at the same rate as irrelevant ones. Contrary to this, in this paper, a joint semantic-physical layer framework is proposed, which is composed of a vector quantized-variational autoencoder that extracts discrete latent concepts, a semantic criticality indicator (SCI) that scores each concept by task relevance, and a deep reinforcement learning agent that dynamically selects the transmission subset based on instantaneous channel conditions. At the physical layer, a learned semantic-aware M -QAM constellation assigns symbol positions according to joint co-occurrence statistics and SCI scores, departing from the uniform spacing and Gray coding of standard M -QAM which minimizes average BER without regard for semantic content. We introduce a novel semantic symbol vulnerability (SSV) metric and a semantic protection probability (SPP) to quantify the exposure of task-critical symbols to decoding errors, and prove that any Gray-coded constellation is strictly suboptimal in SCI-Weighted SSV whenever the source exhibits non-uniform semantic importance and co-occurrence statistics. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed constellation achieves near 100% SPP across modulation orders from 4-QAM to 1024-QAM versus 50% for standard constellations at high spectral efficiency, a 21:1 compression ratio with semantic quality above 0.9, generalizing across MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and FSDD without modification.

</details>


### [372] [Efficient Online Conformal Selection with Limited Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14953)
*Sreenivas Gollapudi,Kostas Kollias,Kamesh Munagala,Ali Sinop*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) with bandit or semi-bandit feedback yields adversarial validity and sublinear efficiency regret; leverages a simple update on a control dual variable to unify limited-feedback conformal selection across adversarial and stochastic settings via Lyapunov-function analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable conformal selection that meets a target success probability with minimal resource usage under extremely limited feedback, and to maintain distribution-free validity under possible distribution shifts.

Method: Apply the Adaptive Conformal Inference update to a control parameter/dual variable within a unifying algorithmic framework; analyze under canonical bandit and semi-bandit feedback models using Lyapunov-based techniques to prove adversarial validity and sublinear efficiency regret.

Result: Adversarial validity: the target success probability φ is met on average for any input sequence. Efficiency: sublinear efficiency regret in i.i.d. settings against a stochastic benchmark. The framework covers bandit and semi-bandit feedback with reduced feedback relative to prior work.

Conclusion: The proposed Lyapunov-based framework extends efficient online learning with very limited feedback into the realm of distribution-free uncertainty quantification, providing a theoretical bridge between conformal prediction and online learning under limited feedback.

Abstract: We address the problem of conformal selection, where an agent must select a minimal subset of options to ensure that at least one ``success'' is identified with a pre-specified target probability $φ$. While traditional online conformal prediction focuses on maintaining validity for the observed sequence, minimizing the resource cost (efficiency) of such selections, especially under limited feedback, remains a significant challenge. In this work, we consider settings with the most limited ``bandit'' feedback, and demonstrate that the simple Adaptive Conformal Inference (ACI) update rule, when applied to the appropriate control parameter or dual variable, is both adversarially valid, ensuring the success target is met on average for any input sequence (and hence under distribution shifts), and stochastically efficient, achieving sublinear efficiency regret for $i.i.d.$ inputs against an appropriate stochastic benchmark. We show such guarantees under canonical models capturing bandit and semi-bandit feedback to the agent via a unifying algorithmic technique, and analytic framework involving Lyapunov functions. Our approach handles more complex settings than prior work, while requiring significantly less feedback, and our results provide a new theoretical bridge between efficient online learning with limited feedback and distribution-free uncertainty quantification.

</details>


### [373] [InfoSFT: Learn More and Forget Less with Information-Aware Token Weighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14967)
*Mahdi Sabbaghi,George Pappas,Adel Javanmard,Hamed Hassani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: InfoSFT introduces a simple token-wise weighting that emphasizes informative, medium-confidence tokens during SFT, yielding better generalization and preservation of base capabilities with a one-line loss modification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vanilla SFT overfits to high- or low-likelihood samples, potentially altering policies and eroding existing capabilities; existing mitigations often suppress novel behaviors.

Method: A principled weighting scheme that shifts emphasis to tokens with medium confidence; implemented as a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss, targeting maximally informative signals.

Result: Empirically improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks, and across diverse model families; better preserves pre-existing capabilities.

Conclusion: A minimal change to SFT that concentrates learning on informative prompts, balancing learning new behaviors with retaining prior abilities.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) provides the standard approach for teaching LLMs new behaviors from offline expert demonstrations. However, standard SFT uniformly fits all samples -- including those with low likelihood under the base model -- which can disproportionately drive training updates toward overfitting specific samples rather than learning the target behavior. Moreover, adapting to these unlikely samples induces substantial policy shifts that degrade prior capabilities. Existing methods mitigate this by filtering, regenerating, or down-weighting low-likelihood data. In doing so, they often suppress precisely the novel behaviors the base model has yet to learn.
  We propose InfoSFT, a principled weighting scheme for the SFT objective that concentrates learning signals on maximally informative, medium-confidence tokens -- those neither overly familiar to the base model nor too unlikely to cause instability. Requiring only a one-line modification to the standard token-wise loss, InfoSFT demonstrably improves generalization over vanilla SFT and likelihood-weighted baselines across math, code, and chain-of-thought tasks with diverse model families, while better preserving pre-existing capabilities.

</details>


### [374] [Distance-Matrix Wasserstein Statistics for Scalable Gromov--Wasserstein Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14981)
*Ao Xu,Tieru Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DMW (Distance-Matrix Wasserstein) is proposed as a scalable, interpretable proxy for Gromov–Wasserstein (GW) distances. It compares laws of random finite distance matrices by sampling points, outlining a hierarchy of Wasserstein statistics. The authors prove DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, with a reverse bound: GW–DMW gap is controlled by the sampling approximation error. Population DMW converges to GW as samples become dense; finite-sample bounds with intrinsic-dimensional rates are established. They introduce sliced and multi-scale DMW, with p=1 yielding positive-definite kernels, and provide experiments on synthetic spaces, scalability, graph classification, and two-sample testing, validating theory and offering a scalable GW-style proxy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GW distances are powerful for aligning unaligned structures (graphs, shapes, point clouds) but are nonconvex and hard to scale. A scalable, theoretically grounded alternative is needed that preserves invariances and allows meaningful comparisons.

Method: Sample n points from each space, compute their pairwise distances to obtain distance matrices, treat these as random objects, and transport their laws (i.e., compare distributions of distance matrices). Develop a theory showing DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, plus a reverse inequality. Propose sliced and multi-scale variants for scalability; for p=1, obtain positive-definite kernels. Provide computational methods and experiments.

Result: DMW provides a scalable proxy for GW with theoretical guarantees: asymptotic convergence to GW as sampling becomes dense; finite-sample and intrinsic-dimensional bounds; construction of positive-definite kernels in the sliced multi-scale setting; empirical validation on synthetic, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing.

Conclusion: DMW offers an interpretable, scalable GW-style metric by comparing laws of random distance matrices, supported by rigorous bounds and practical variants (sliced/multi-scale, p=1 kernels). It serves as a practical surrogate for structural comparison across domains.

Abstract: Gromov--Wasserstein (GW) distances compare graphs, shapes, and point clouds through internal distances, without requiring a common coordinate system. This invariance is powerful, but discrete GW is a nonconvex quadratic optimal transport problem and is difficult to estimate at scale. We propose \emph{Distance-Matrix Wasserstein} (DMW), a hierarchy of Wasserstein statistics comparing laws of random finite distance matrices. Rather than optimizing a global point-level alignment, DMW samples $n$ points from each space, records their pairwise distances, and transports the resulting matrix laws. We prove that DMW is a relaxation and lower bound of GW, and establish a reverse approximation inequality: the GW--DMW gap is controlled by the Wasserstein error of approximating each original measure with $n$ samples. Hence population DMW converges to GW as sampled subspaces become dense. We further give finite-sample bounds, including intrinsic-dimensional rates that depend on the data manifold rather than the ambient matrix dimension $\binom n2$. For scalable computation, we introduce sliced and multi-scale DMW; for $p=1$, the sliced multi-scale dissimilarity yields positive-definite exponential kernels. Experiments on synthetic metric spaces, scalability benchmarks, graph classification, and two-sample testing validate the theory and demonstrate an interpretable GW-style proxy for structural comparison.

</details>


### [375] [Second-Order Actor-Critic Methods for Discounted MDPs via Policy Hessian Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14982)
*Sanjeev Manivannan,Shuban V*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Second-order actor-critic method for discounted RL using Hessian-vector products, justified by two-timescale quasi-stationarity, offering curvature-aware updates with efficient computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To accelerate convergence and improve value estimation in policy-gradient RL by incorporating curvature information, overcoming limitations of first-order actor-critic methods and Hessian estimation costs.

Method: Develop theoretical justification for a locally-constant Q-function approximation under a two-timescale critic; derive a second-order actor update using Hessian-vector products; ensure computational efficiency and stability.

Result: Formulation of a computationally efficient and stable second-order actor-critic update for discounted rewards, based on HVPs and two-timescale quasi-stationarity (no empirical results stated in abstract).

Conclusion: Two-timescale dynamics justify curvature-aware updates in RL and enable practical second-order methods; potential improvements in convergence speed and stability for policy gradient methods, with feasible computation via HVP.

Abstract: We address the discounted reward setting in reinforcement learning (RL). To mitigate the value approximation challenges in policy gradient methods, actor-critic approaches have been developed and are known to converge to stationary points under suitable assumptions. However, these methods rely on first-order updates. In contrast, second-order optimization provides principled curvature-aware updates that are proven to accelerate convergence, but its application in RL is limited by the computational complexity of Hessian estimation. In this work, we analyze second-order approximations for the actor update that leverage the full curvature information of the objective as much as possible. A stable approximation requires treating the action-value function as locally constant with respect to policy parameters, which does not generally hold in policy gradient methods. We show that this approximation becomes well-justified under a two-timescale actor-critic framework, where the critic evolves on a faster timescale and can be treated as quasi-stationary during actor updates. Building on this insight, we formulate a second-order actor-critic method for the discounted reward setting that leverages Hessian-vector product (HVP) computations, resulting in a computationally efficient and stable second-order update.

</details>


### [376] [DeepTokenEEG Enhancing Mild Cognitive Impairment and Alzheimers Classification via Tokenized EEG Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15009)
*Thinh Nguyen-Quang,Minh Long Ngo,Ngoc-Son Nguyen,Nguyen Thanh Vinh,Huy-Dung Han,Bui Thanh Tung,Nguyen Quang Linh,Khuong Vo,Manoj Vishwanath,Hung Cao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight DeepTokenEEG model for AD detection from EEG using spatial and temporal tokenizers, with 0.29M parameters, achieving up to 100% accuracy on certain bands on a combined dataset of 274 subjects (180 AD, 94 controls), and claiming 1.41-15.35% improvement over SOTA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Improve non-invasive AD detection via EEG by reducing model size and inference time while maintaining or improving accuracy, enabling practical deployment and screening.

Method: Proposes DeepTokenEEG that uses spatial and temporal tokenizers to capture AD-related biomarkers in temporal and frequency domains. A lightweight architecture (0.29M parameters) trained on a combined dataset of 274 subjects (180 AD, 94 controls) for classification across subject groups (AD, other neurological conditions, healthy). Evaluation reportedly shows maximum accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands and improvements over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset.

Result: Reported maximum accuracy of 100% on particular frequency bands; claimed 1.41–15.35% improvement over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset.

Conclusion: The model shows potential for early detection and practical screening of AD due to compact size, with promising applicability for deployment; however, robustness, generalizability, and reproducibility require further validation.

Abstract: The detection of Alzheimers disease (AD) is considered crucial, as timely intervention can improve patient outcomes. Electroencephalogram (EEG)-based diagnosis has been recognized as a non-invasive, accessible, and cost-effective approach for AD detection; however, it faces challenges related to data availability, accuracy of modern deep learning methods, and the time-consuming nature of expert-based interpretation. In this study, a novel lightweight and high-performance model, DeepTokenEEG, was designed for the diagnosis of AD and the classification of EEG signals from AD patients, individuals with other neurological conditions, and healthy subjects. Unlike traditional heavy-weight models, DeepTokenEEG ultilizes spatial and temporal tokenizer that effectively captures AD-related biomarkers in both temporal and frequency domain with only 0.29 million paramaters. Trained in a combined dataset of 274 subjects, including 180 AD cases, and 94 healthy controls, the proposed method achieves a maximum recorded accuracy of 100% on specific frequency bands, representing an improvement of 1.41-15.35% over state-of-the-art methods on the same dataset. These results indicate the potential of DeepTokenEEG for early detection and screening of AD, with promising applicability for deployment due to its compact size.

</details>


### [377] [Boosting Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards via Randomly Selected Few-Shot Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15012)
*Kai Yan,Alexander G. Schwing,Yu-Xiong Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FEST is a few-shot demonstration-guided RLVR algorithm that achieves strong sample efficiency using only 128 demonstrations, outperforming baselines and matching full-data performance on several benchmarks by combining supervised signal, on-policy signal, and decaying weights to prevent overfitting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce label/data requirements in reinforcement-learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for difficult tasks where generating correct rollouts is hard, addressing the data-hungry nature of SFT-based RLVR.

Method: FEST selects 128 demonstrations from an existing SFT dataset and integrates three signals during training: (1) supervised loss from demonstrations, (2) on-policy RL signals, and (3) a decaying weight schedule on the SFT data to mitigate overfitting from multi-epoch fine-tuning.

Result: FEST achieves superior performance with far less SFT data and can match or exceed baselines that use the full SFT dataset across multiple benchmarks.

Conclusion: Few-shot demonstration-guided RLVR is viable; with the right combination of supervised and on-policy signals and regularization (decaying weights), high performance can be attained using substantially less labeled data.

Abstract: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has achieved great success in developing Large Language Models (LLMs) with chain-of-thought rollouts for many tasks such as math and coding. Nevertheless, RLVR struggles with sample efficiency on difficult problems where correct rollouts are hard to generate. Prior works propose to address this issue via demonstration-guided RLVR, i.e., to conduct Supervised FineTuning (SFT) when RL fails; however, SFT often requires a lot of data, which can be expensive to acquire. In this paper, we propose FEST, a FEw-ShoT demonstration-guided RLVR algorithm. It attains compelling results with only 128 demonstrations randomly selected from an SFT dataset. We find that three components are vital for the success: supervised signal, on-policy signal, and decaying weights on the few-shot SFT dataset to prevent overfitting from multiple-epoch training. On several benchmarks, FEST outperforms baselines with magnitudes less SFT data, even matching their performance with full dataset.

</details>


### [378] [Generalized Priority-Aware Shapley Value](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15018)
*Kiljae Lee,Ziqi Liu,Weijing Tang,Yuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces the Generalized Priority-Aware Shapley Value (GPASV), a random-order value on directed weighted priority graphs where edges penalize but do not forbid order violations; provides axiomatic foundations, computation methods, and a priority-sweeping diagnostic; demonstrates valuation differences in LLM ensemble settings on a cyclic preference graph.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing priority-aware Shapley methods require pairwise priority to be binary and acyclic, which is unmet in real data featuring cycles and weighted priorities. GPASV aims to model and compute valuations under arbitrary directed weighted priority structures, capturing partial or conflicting priorities.

Method: Define GPASV as a random-order value over arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs; implement penalties for order violations via edge weights; present an axiomatic characterization that generalizes classical Shapley and PASV-like models; develop computational algorithms; introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic extending PASV; show boundary cases that recover classical models.

Result: GPASV encompasses classical models as boundary cases; provides a general, axiomatized framework with computable methods; introduces a diagnostic tool to analyze how graph-priority vs individual-soft-priority balance affects valuations; applied to LLM ensemble valuation on a cyclic Chatbot Arena preference graph, revealing substantive valuation differences driven by the chosen priority balance.

Conclusion: GPASV broadens Shapley-based valuation to arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs, accommodating violations of acyclicity via penalties and offering a coherent axiomatic basis, practical computation, and diagnostic insight. It highlights that priority-aware valuation is sensitive to how priorities are modeled and balanced, demonstrated in LLM ensemble contexts.

Abstract: Shapley value and its priority-aware extensions are widely used for valuation in machine learning, but existing methods require pairwise priority to be binary and acyclic, a restriction spectacularly violated in real-data examples such as aggregated human preferences and multi-criterion comparisons. We introduce the generalized priority-aware Shapley value (GPASV), a random order value defined on arbitrary directed weighted priority graphs, in which pairwise edges penalize rather than forbid order violations. GPASV covers a range of classical models as boundary cases. We establish GPASV through an axiomatic characterization, develop the associated computational methods, and introduce a priority sweeping diagnostic extending PASV's. We apply GPASV to LLM ensemble valuation on the cyclic Chatbot Arena preference graph, illustrating that priority-aware valuation is not a one-button operation: different balances of pairwise graph priority versus individual soft priority produce substantively different valuations of the same data.

</details>


### [379] [TopoPrimer: The Missing Topological Context in Forecasting Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15035)
*Zara Zetlin,Kayhan Moharreri,Maria Safi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TopoPrimer injects global topological structure into forecasting models, delivering robust accuracy gains and cold-start/seasonality robustness via persistent-homology-based coordinates and spectral sheaf features, usable as per-token inputs or lightweight adapters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Leverage the global topological structure of a series population to improve forecasting accuracy, stabilize forecasts during seasonal spikes, and close cold-start gaps by encoding relational information that traditional per-series features may miss.

Method: Compute domain-specific topology features (persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates) once per domain, then inject them as per-token inputs for fully-trained models or as lightweight adapters for pre-trained backbones; the sheaf coordinates are identified as the primary accuracy driver.

Result: Across four public benchmarks on Chronos and TimesFM, TopoPrimer yields consistent accuracy gains, up to 7.3% MSE on ECL. Gains persist in zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones and are largest in difficult regimes; under peak seasonal demand, traditional models degrade by up to 50% while TopoPrimer remains within 10%. At cold start with no item history, MAE drops by 27% over a topology-free baseline.

Conclusion: Incorporating global topology via precomputed coordinates provides complementary signals to per-series training, delivering robust, regime-agnostic improvements; the topology-informed representation can be deployed as per-token features or lightweight adapters.

Abstract: We introduce TopoPrimer, a framework that makes the global topological structure of the series population an explicit input
  to any forecasting model. TopoPrimer improves accuracy across diverse domains, stabilizes forecasts under seasonal demand
  spikes, and closes the cold-start gap. Precomputed once per domain via persistent homology and spectral sheaf coordinates,
  TopoPrimer deploys per token for fully-trained models and as a lightweight adapter for pre-trained backbones. Of these two
  components, sheaf coordinates are the primary accuracy driver. Across four public benchmarks on Chronos and TimesFM,
  TopoPrimer consistently improves forecasting accuracy, with gains of up to 7.3% MSE on ECL. The topology advantage persists
  with near-identical magnitude across zero-shot and fine-tuned backbones, suggesting topology and per-series training
  capture complementary signals. The gains are most pronounced in difficult regimes. Under peak seasonal demand, classical
  and zero-shot models degrade by up to 50%, while TopoPrimer stays within 10%. At cold start with no item history,
  TopoPrimer reduces MAE by 27% over a topology-free baseline.

</details>


### [380] [Separating Intrinsic Ambiguity from Estimation Uncertainty in Deep Generative Models for Linear Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15050)
*Yuxin Guo,Dongrui Deng,Pulkit Grover*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Decompose posterior uncertainty in deep generative inverse problems to separate intrinsic ambiguity from forward-operator effects, enabling calibration-based diagnostics; validated on Gaussian/posterior structure, MRI, EEG source imaging.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Posterior uncertainty in inverse problems blends intrinsic ambiguity with forward-model-induced uncertainty, making interpretation difficult. A structured decomposition and cascade calibration can isolate intrinsic ambiguity and reveal failure modes not detectable by reconstruction quality alone.

Method: Introduce a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty with a cascade formulation that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. Use calibration analysis and simulation-based calibration tests. Validate on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then demonstrate on accelerated MRI and EEG source imaging.

Result: Demonstrates the ability to isolate intrinsic ambiguity; provides calibration diagnostics that reveal failure modes hidden by reconstruction-centric evaluation; shows applicability across MRI and EEG inverse problems.

Conclusion: The approach improves interpretability of posterior uncertainty in inverse problems and enables diagnostic tools to identify failure modes inaccessible through reconstruction quality alone, advancing reliability of deep generative inference in high-stakes applications.

Abstract: Recently, deep generative models have been used for posterior inference in inverse problems, including high-stakes applications in medical imaging and scientific discovery, where the uncertainty of a prediction can matter as much as the prediction itself. However, posterior uncertainty is difficult to interpret because it can mix ambiguity inherent to the forward operator with uncertainty propagated through inference. We introduce a structural decomposition of posterior uncertainty that isolates intrinsic ambiguity. A cascade formulation makes this ambiguity accessible for calibration analysis, enabling qualitative diagnostics and simulation-based calibration tests that reveal failure modes that remain hidden when models are selected by reconstruction quality alone. We first validate the approach on a Gaussian example with analytical posterior structure, then illustrate the decomposition on accelerated magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and finally apply the calibration diagnostics to electroencephalography (EEG) source imaging.

</details>


### [381] [An Interpretable Latency Model for Speculative Decoding in LLM Serving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15051)
*Linghao Kong,Megan Flynn,Michael Peng,Nir Shavit,Mark Kurtz,Alexandre Marques*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A latency model for speculative decoding (SD) in LLM serving is proposed, using Little's Law to infer effective batch size and decomposing per-request load into prefill, drafting, and verification costs; validated across model sizes and settings, it explains reduced speedups under higher load and guides deployment; the framework extends to mixture-of-experts (MoE) and offers practical configuration insights.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SD promises speedups for LLM inference but its behavior in production serving under varying load is poorly understood. A simple, interpretable latency model is needed to predict latency, understand bottlenecks, and guide deployment decisions.

Method: Propose a latency model that (i) infers effective batch size from request rate via Little’s Law, (ii) decomposes per-request demand into load-independent vs. load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification, (iii) validates the model with extensive measurements on vLLM across verifier/drafter sizes, prefill lengths, decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities, and (iv) extends the framework to mixture-of-experts where sparse activation shifts service costs across load regimes.

Result: The model accurately describes observed latency, clarifies why speedups from SD diminish as server load increases, and quantifies how draft length, acceptance rate, and the relative sizes of verifier/drafter affect latency across load conditions. It yields actionable guidance for configuring SD in production and demonstrates how the framework generalizes to MoE models.

Conclusion: A structured, interpretable framework for understanding speculative decoding in real LLM serving systems, enabling optimized deployment under varying load and informing future work on SD and mixed-expert architectures.

Abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a smaller draft model to propose multiple tokens that are verified by a larger target model in parallel. While prior work demonstrates substantial speedups in isolated or fixed-batch settings, the behavior of SD in production serving systems remains poorly understood: request load varies over time, and effective batch size emerges from the serving system rather than being directly controlled or observed. In this work, we develop a simple and interpretable latency model for SD in LLM serving. We infer effective batch size from request rate using Little's Law and decompose per-request demand into load-independent and load-dependent components for prefill, drafting, and verification. We validate our model using extensive measurements from vLLM across verifier and drafter model sizes, prefill and decode lengths, request rates, draft lengths, and acceptance probabilities.
