The model accurately describes observed latency, explains why speedups often diminish as server load increases, and characterizes how draft length, acceptance rate, and verifier-drafter size shape latency across serving conditions, with implications for configuring SD in deployed systems. We further show how the framework extends to mixture of experts models, where sparse expert activation changes the effective service costs across load regimes. Together, our results provide a structured framework for understanding SD in real LLM serving systems.

</details>


### [382] [TFGN: Task-Free, Replay-Free Continual Pre-Training Without Catastrophic Forgetting at LLM Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15053)
*Anurup Ganguli*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TFGN is an input-conditioned, parameter-efficient update mechanism for transformer LMs enabling continual pre-training across heterogeneous domains with no replay, achieving negligible backward transfer and useful forward transfer, plus extensions for meta-control and planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual pre-training of LLMs on heterogeneous text domains without replay, task labels, or costly penalties remains unsolved and brittle at scale. Existing methods rely on replay buffers, task identifiers, or large regularization penalties and often rely on small-scale evaluation.

Method: TFGN introduces an architectural overlay that performs input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates while leaving the rest of the transformer unchanged. It uses a Read/Write decomposition where the forward pass is dense, but updates are structured to avoid overwriting prior-domain subspaces. Evaluated on six domains (Prose, Python, Math, Biomedical, Chinese, JavaScript) at 1B tokens per phase across three model scales (~398M, ~739M, ~9B) and two regimes (From-Scratch and Retrofit). Two extensions: Extension A (closed-loop meta-control mapping to Dupoux System A/M) and Extension B (operator-level plan vector).

Result: Backward transfer is near zero (-0.007) on LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit; HellaSwag retention held at ~0.504–0.510 across scales; >=99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs, with no replay, no task IDs, no Fisher penalty. Demonstrates positive forward transfer: JavaScript PPL drops by 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch purely from Python training. Extension A reduces forgetting by ~81% at ~398M; Extension B achieves 99.96% cosine fidelity over 30 source->target pairs.

Conclusion: TFGN is claimed to be the first architecture that simultaneously closes catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, realizes a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and carries an operator-level latent planner, based on a Read/Write decomposition that preserves prior-domain subspaces while enabling targeted updates.

Abstract: Continually pre-training a large language model on heterogeneous text domains, without replay or task labels, has remained an unsolved architectural problem at LLM scale. Existing methods rely on replay buffers, task identifiers, regularization penalties that scale poorly, or sentence-classification-scale evaluation.
  We introduce TFGN, an architectural overlay for transformer language models that produces input-conditioned, parameter-efficient updates while leaving the rest of the transformer unchanged. On six heterogeneous text domains (Prose, Python, Math, Biomedical, Chinese, JavaScript) at 1B tokens per phase across three model scales (~398M, ~739M, ~9B) and two regimes (From-Scratch and Retrofit), TFGN achieves backward transfer of -0.007 at LLaMA 3.1 8B Retrofit, HellaSwag retention 0.506/0.504/0.510, and >=99.59% L2-orthogonal gradient separation between domain pairs - with no replay, no task IDs, no Fisher penalty.
  The same matrices show positive cross-domain forward transfer: held-out JavaScript PPL drops 26.8% at LLaMA-8B Retrofit and 62.0% at GPT-2 Medium From-Scratch purely from Python training. Two extensions on the same substrate close further open problems. A closed-loop meta-control layer (Extension A) reduces forgetting by an additional 81% at ~398M, mapping onto the System A and System M roles of Dupoux et al. (arXiv:2603.15381). An operator-level plan vector (Extension B) reshapes forward-pass behavior at 99.96% cosine fidelity over 30 source->target pairs.
  The architectural insight is a Read/Write decomposition: the forward pass is fully dense, while cross-domain parameter updates are structured so prior-domain subspaces are not written to. To our knowledge, TFGN is the first architecture that simultaneously closes catastrophic forgetting at LLM scale, realizes a closed-loop autonomous-learning meta-controller, and carries an operator-level latent planner.

</details>


### [383] [DiffusionOPD: A Unified Perspective of On-Policy Distillation in Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15055)
*Quanhao Li,Junqiu Yu,Kaixun Jiang,Yujie Wei,Zhen Xing,Pandeng Li,Ruihang Chu,Shiwei Zhang,Yu Liu,Zuxuan Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A multi-task diffusion model training framework (DiffusionOPD) uses Online Policy Distillation to separately train task-specific teachers and distill them into a single student, improving efficiency and performance over joint or cascade RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extend RL for diffusion-based text-to-image models from single-task to multi-task settings while avoiding cross-task interference and catastrophic forgetting inherent in existing approaches.

Method: Train task-specific policy teachers independently; distill their capabilities into one unified student using the student’s own rollout trajectories. The approach decouples single-task exploration from multi-task integration. The authors extend Online Policy Distillation to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinements through mean-matching.

Result: Analytic gradient from the KL objective exhibits lower variance and better generalization than PPO-style policy gradients. Empirically, DiffusionOPD outperforms both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

Conclusion: DiffusionOPD provides a scalable, efficient, and effective multi-task RL paradigm for diffusion models by separating task-specific exploration from integrated policy distillation, with theoretical and empirical advantages over traditional policy gradient approaches.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has emerged as a powerful tool for improving diffusion-based text-to-image models, but existing methods are largely limited to single-task optimization. Extending RL to multiple tasks is challenging: joint optimization suffers from cross-task interference and imbalance, while cascade RL is cumbersome and prone to catastrophic forgetting. We propose DiffusionOPD, a new multi-task training paradigm for diffusion models based on Online Policy Distillation (OPD). DiffusionOPD first trains task-specific teachers independently, then distills their capabilities into a unified student along the student own rollout trajectories. This decouples single-task exploration from multi-task integration and avoids the optimization burden of solving all tasks jointly from scratch. Theoretically, we lift the OPD framework from discrete tokens to continuous-state Markov processes, deriving a closed-form per-step KL objective that unifies both stochastic SDE and deterministic ODE refinement via mean-matching. We formally and empirically demonstrate that this analytic gradient provides lower variance and better generality compared to conventional PPO-style policy gradients. Extensive experiments show that DiffusionOPD consistently surpasses both multi-reward RL and cascade RL baselines in training efficiency and final performance, while achieving state-of-the-art results on all evaluated benchmarks.

</details>


### [384] [Proposal and study of statistical features for string similarity computation and classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15110)
*E. O. Rodrigues,D. Casanova,M. Teixeira,V. Pegorini,F. Favarim,E. Clua,A. Conci,Panos Liatsis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM) features are adapted for language-agnostic string similarity. They outperform standard statistics in synthetic experiments and achieve best results on a real plagiarism dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop language-independent, purely statistical features for measuring similarity between strings (words, phrases, codes, texts) that do not rely on linguistic information.

Method: Adapt COM and RLM features from visual computing to string similarity. Compare against LCS, maximal LCS, mutual information, and edit distances. Evaluate on synthetic experiments and a real text plagiarism dataset.

Result: COM and RLM features outperform other statistical measures in the synthetic experiments; in 3 of 4 cases, they are statistically more significant (P < 0.001). On a real plagiarism dataset, RLM features yield the best results.

Conclusion: COM and especially RLM features are effective, language-agnostic tools for string similarity and plagiarism detection, outperforming conventional statistical measures in the reported experiments.

Abstract: Adaptations of features commonly applied in the field of visual computing, co-occurrence matrix (COM) and run-length matrix (RLM), are proposed for the similarity computation of strings in general (words, phrases, codes and texts). The proposed features are not sensitive to language related information. These are purely statistical and can be used in any context with any language or grammatical structure. Other statistical measures that are commonly employed in the field such as longest common subsequence, maximal consecutive longest common subsequence, mutual information and edit distances are evaluated and compared. In the first synthetic set of experiments, the COM and RLM features outperform the remaining state-of-the-art statistical features. In 3 out of 4 cases, the RLM and COM features were statistically more significant than the second best group based on distances (P-value < 0.001). When it comes to a real text plagiarism dataset, the RLM features obtained the best results.

</details>


### [385] [Learning from Language Feedback via Variational Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15113)
*Yang Li,Erik Nijkamp,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Rayhan Joty*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A variational EM framework (VPD) co-evolves teacher and student to leverage language feedback, overcoming static teacher limits and improving sparse-reward RL performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse signals and exploration bottlenecks. On-policy self-distillation with a fixed, passive teacher plateaus as the teacher's assessment saturates. A learnable, adaptive teacher is needed to extract actionable signals from textual feedback.

Method: Variational Policy Distillation (VPD) formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational EM problem. In the E-step, the teacher is refined on trajectory outcomes via an adaptive trust-region update to produce a dynamically improved target token distribution from feedback. In the M-step, the student on-policy learns from this dense distributional guidance on its rollouts, closing the loop.

Result: VPD consistently outperforms standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines across diverse diagnostic feedback sources for scientific reasoning and code generation tasks. Stress-testing on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes reveals the fundamental bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation compared to environment-driven RL.

Conclusion: Continuous co-evolution of teacher and student via variational EM mitigates exploration bottlenecks in feedback-driven RL and clarifies the limits and potential of language-feedback-based self-distillation.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) suffers from sparse outcome signals, creating severe exploration bottlenecks on complex reasoning tasks. Recent on-policy self-distillation methods attempt to address this by utilizing language feedback to generate dense, token-level supervision. However, these approaches rely on a fixed, passive teacher to interpret the feedback. As the student policy improves, the teacher's zero-shot assessment capabilities plateau, ultimately halting further learning. To overcome this, we propose Variational Policy Distillation (VPD), a framework that formalizes learning from language feedback as a Variational Expectation-Maximization (EM) problem. VPD co-evolves both policies: in the E-step, the teacher is actively refined on trajectory outcomes via an adaptive trust-region update, translating textual feedback into a dynamically improved target token distribution. In the M-step, the student internalizes this dense distributional guidance on its own on-policy rollouts. By continuously improving the teacher's ability to extract actionable signals from textual critique, VPD overcomes the limitations of passive distillation. Evaluated across diverse sources of diagnostic feedback on scientific reasoning and code generation tasks, VPD consistently outperforms both standard RLVR and existing self-distillation baselines. Finally, by stress-testing our framework on rigid mathematical reasoning and cold-start regimes, we illuminate the fundamental bounds of feedback-driven self-distillation compared to pure environment-driven RL.

</details>


### [386] [Natural Synthesis: Outperforming Reactive Synthesis Tools with Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15131)
*Frederik Schmitt,Matthias Cosler,Niklas Metzger,Julian Siber,Vladimir Krsmanovic,Mohamed Ghanem,Bernd Finkbeiner*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Neuro-symbolic reactive synthesis with autoformalization enabling end-to-end NL-to-formal workflow; surpasses state-of-the-art benchmarks and handles parameterized systems; practical workflow with natural language specifications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reactive synthesis is powerful but hampered by (i) algorithmic hardness and (ii) the difficulty of writing precise formal specifications. Bridging these gaps could broaden adoption and capabilities in hardware design.

Method: A neuro-symbolic loop that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. Extends to parameterized systems (undecidable domain). Introduces an autoformalization step by constructing a hand-authored NL specification dataset to translate natural language to temporal logic, enabling end-to-end NL-to-formal synthesis.

Result: The approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated synthesis tools in the annual competition and extends to parameterized systems. NL-based specifications achieve performance comparable to starting from formal specifications, supporting natural synthesis as a viable end-to-end workflow.

Conclusion: Natural synthesis demonstrates viability as an end-to-end workflow for reactive synthesis, reducing reliance on formal spec authorship and enabling broader applicability, including parameterized and potentially undecidable settings.

Abstract: Reactive synthesis, the problem of automatically constructing a hardware circuit from a logical specification, is a long-standing challenge in formal verification. It is elusive for two reasons: It is algorithmically hard, and writing formal specifications by hand is notoriously difficult. In this paper, we tackle both sides of the problem. For the algorithmic side, we present a neuro-symbolic approach to reactive synthesis that couples large reasoning models with model checkers to iteratively repair a synthesized Verilog implementation via sound symbolic feedback. Our approach solves more benchmarks than the best dedicated tools in the annual synthesis competition and extends to constructing parameterized systems, a problem known to be undecidable. On the specification side, we introduce an autoformalization step that shifts the specification task from temporal logic to natural language by introducing a hand-authored dataset of natural-language specifications for evaluation. We demonstrate performance comparable to that of starting from formal specifications, establishing natural synthesis as a viable end-to-end workflow.

</details>


### [387] [Causal Foundation Models with Continuous Treatments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15133)
*Christopher Stith,Medha Barath,Vahid Balazadeh,Jesse C. Cresswell,Rahul G. Krishnan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A novel causal foundation model for continuous treatment settings that meta-learns to predict treatment-response curves across unseen tasks without extra training, using a prior over data-generating processes and a transformer to reconstruct individual curves from observational data, with in-context learning to amortize Bayesian inference; achieves state-of-the-art on DTP curve reconstruction relative to task-specific causal models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous treatments are common but underexplored in causal inference. A foundation model capable of generalizing to unseen tasks and reconstructing treatment-response curves from observational data can greatly improve applicability and efficiency, avoiding extensive retraining or fine-tuning.

Method: 1) Define a novel prior over data-generating processes (DGPs) with continuous treatment variables to generate a rich causal training corpus. 2) Train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves from observational data. 3) Leverage in-context learning to amortize Bayesian posterior inference, enabling zero-shot adaptation to new tasks.

Result: The model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks, outperforming causal models trained specifically for those tasks.

Conclusion: First causal foundation model for the continuous treatment setting; demonstrates zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks and efficient inference via in-context learning, suggesting broad potential for continuous-treatment causal inference.

Abstract: Causal inference, estimating causal effects from observational data, is a fundamental tool in many disciplines. Of particular importance across a variety of domains is the continuous treatment setting, where the variable of intervention has a continuous range. This setting is far less explored and represents a substantial shift from the binary treatment setting, with models needing to represent effects across a continuum of treatment values. In this paper, we present the first causal foundation model for the continuous treatment setting. Our model meta-learns the ability to predict causal effects across a wide variety of unseen tasks without additional training or fine-tuning. First, we design a novel prior over data-generating processes with continuous treatment variables in order to generate a rich causal training corpus. We then train a transformer to reconstruct individual treatment-response curves given only observational data, leveraging in-context learning to amortize expensive Bayesian posterior inference. Our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on individual treatment-response curve reconstruction tasks compared to causal models which are trained specifically for those tasks.

</details>


### [388] [Training ML Models with Predictable Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15134)
*Will Schwarzer,Scott Niekum*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Extrapolates deployment-scale failure rates from top-k evaluation failures, analyzes the forecast-error decomposition, and proposes a forecastability loss to address rare high-failure modes; validated via two proof-of-concept experiments showing reduced forecast error with maintained performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reliable estimation of deployment failures is data-scarce; existing evaluation sets often miss rare but critical failure modes. A principled extrapolation with bias analysis and a corrective objective could improve pre-deployment safety assessments.

Method: 1) Finite-k decomposition of the forecast error of the top-k failure-score estimator; 2) analysis of bias toward over-prediction and conditions under which it under-predicts due to unseen high-failure modes; 3) introduce forecastability loss as a fine-tuning objective to mitigate missed high-failure modes; 4) two proof-of-concept experiments (language-model password game, RL gridworld) to evaluate forecast error, task performance, and safety against supervised baselines.

Result: Fine-tuning with forecastability loss substantially reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability; safety performance comparable to supervised baselines.

Conclusion: Forecastability loss helps address rare, deployment-scale failure modes in top-k extrapolation, yielding better safety forecasts without sacrificing core performance in the tested domains.

Abstract: Estimating how often an ML model will fail at deployment scale is central to pre-deployment safety assessment, but a feasible evaluation set is rarely large enough to observe the failures that matter. Jones et al. (2025) address this by extrapolating from the largest k failure scores in an evaluation set to predict deployment-scale failure rates. We give a finite-k decomposition of this estimator's forecast error and show that it has a built-in bias toward over-prediction in the typical case, which is the safety-favorable direction. This bias is offset when the evaluation set misses a rare high-failure mode that the deployment set contains, leaving the forecast to under-predict at deployment scale. We propose a fine-tuning objective, the forecastability loss, that addresses this failure mode. In two proof-of-concept experiments, a language-model password game and an RL gridworld, fine-tuning substantially reduces held-out forecast error while preserving primary-task capability and achieving safety similar to that of supervised baselines.

</details>


### [389] [Forgetting That Sticks: Quantization-Permanent Unlearning via Circuit Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15138)
*Saisab Sadhu,Pratinav Seth,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse unlearning; it introduces MANSU—Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning—with circuit attribution, circuit-restricted null-space projection (diagonal-Fisher bound), and per-parameter magnitude floors, plus CAD for verification. It achieves robust forgetting under quantization and outperforms gradient-based baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unlearning must be reliable under deployment constraints such as quantization. Standard evaluations measure behavioral suppression immediately after training and miss structural erasure when models are quantized. The work argues for a mechanistic, quantization-aware approach to ensure true forgetting rather than surface-level suppression.

Method: MANSU consists of (1) causal circuit attribution to identify the minimal forget-set subgraph, (2) circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and (3) a per-parameter magnitude floor to guarantee quantization survival. It also introduces Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD) as a mechanistic verification metric distinguishing structural erasure from behavioral suppression.

Result: Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy four properties with margin: meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure. Gradient-based baselines can recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression.

Conclusion: MANSU provides a quantization-aware, mechanistic framework for unlearning, combining causal structure with null-space updates and per-parameter flooring. CAD offers a tool to verify true erase of knowledge rather than mere behavioral suppression, setting a new standard for unlearning under post-training quantization.

Abstract: Standard unlearning evaluations measure behavioral suppression in full precision, immediately after training, despite every deployed language model being quantized first. Recent work has shown that 4-bit post-training quantization can reverse machine unlearning; we show this is not a tuning artefact but a systematic dual failure: gradient-based methods that achieve meaningful forgetting lose it under compression, while methods that survive quantization barely change the model. Both failures trace to the same root cause: across all baselines, per-parameter updates lie 47-828x below the NF4 quantization bin width; updates diffused across billions of parameters cannot clear quantization bin boundaries, a consequence we formalize as a sparsity-permanence tradeoff. We present MANSU (Mechanistic-Aligned Null-Space Unlearning), which resolves both modes by combining causal circuit attribution to isolate the minimal forget-set subgraph, circuit-restricted null-space projection with a diagonal-Fisher retain bound, and a per-parameter magnitude floor guaranteeing quantization survival by construction. We additionally introduce Circuit Attribution Divergence (CAD), a mechanistic verification metric distinguishing structural erasure from behavioral suppression, a distinction existing metrics cannot make. Across multiple model families and hazard benchmarks, MANSU is the first method to jointly satisfy all four properties with margin on each (meaningful forgetting, retain preservation, non-positive PTQ gap, and structural erasure), while gradient-based baselines recover up to +0.05 accuracy under compression.

</details>


### [390] [Widening the Gap: Exploiting LLM Quantization via Outlier Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15152)
*Xiaohua Zhan,Kazuki Egashira,Robin Staab,Mark Vero,Martin Vechev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First quantization-conditioned attack that reliably causes malicious behavior after diverse quantization schemes (AWQ, GPTQ, GGUF I-quants) by injecting outliers to induce weight collapse in specific blocks; achieves high success across three attack scenarios and LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantization security is a risk for memory-effective LLM deployment. Prior attacks targeted only simple quantization methods; modern, popular schemes were believed more secure. This work questions that assumption and demonstrates broad threat across leading quantization methods.

Method: Introduce strategically placed weight outliers that cause neighboring weights to be rounded to zero in many quantization schemes. By injecting such outliers into selected blocks, the attack triggers a controlled, predictable weight collapse post-quantization, enabling malicious behavior after deployment. Evaluated across three attack scenarios and multiple LLMs against AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF I-quants.

Result: The attack achieves high success rates across a broad range of quantization methods, including those previously resistant, and across three attack scenarios and multiple LLMs; demonstrates that security risks of quantization extend beyond simple schemes.

Conclusion: Quantization-related security risks are broadly relevant to modern, widely-used quantization methods. Defenses should consider outlier-induced weight collapse and ensure robustness across diverse quantization pipelines.

Abstract: LLM quantization has become essential for memory-efficient deployment. Recent work has shown that quantization schemes can pose critical security risks: an adversary may release a model that appears benign in full precision but exhibits malicious behavior once quantized by users. However, existing quantization-conditioned attacks have been limited to relatively simple quantization methods, where the attacker can estimate weight regions that remain invariant under the target quantization. Notably, prior attacks have consistently failed to compromise more popular and sophisticated schemes, limiting their practical impact. In this work, we introduce the first quantization-conditioned attack that consistently induces malicious behavior that can be triggered by a broad range of advanced quantization techniques, including AWQ, GPTQ, and GGUF I-quants. Our attack exploits a simple property shared by many modern quantization methods: large outliers can cause other weights to be rounded to zero. Consequently, by injecting outliers into specific weight blocks, an adversary can therefore induce a targeted, predictable weight collapse in the model. This effect can be used to craft seemingly benign full-precision models that exhibit a wide range of malicious behaviors after quantization. Through extensive evaluation across three attack scenarios and LLMs, we show that our attack achieves high success rates against a broad range of quantization methods on which prior attacks fail. Our results demonstrate, for the first time, that the security risks of quantization are not restricted to simpler schemes but are broadly relevant across complex, widely-used quantization methods.

</details>


### [391] [Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15155)
*Zhengxi Lu,Zhiyuan Yao,Zhuowen Han,Zi-Han Wang,Jinyang Wu,Qi Gu,Xunliang Cai,Weiming Lu,Jun Xiao,Yueting Zhuang,Yongliang Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SDAR (Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning) adds a gated, token-level distillation objective to reinforce OPSD within RL, stabilizing multi-turn LLM agents and boosting performance across benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon RL with coarse trajectory rewards struggles to provide fine-grained guidance; OPSD offers token-level guidance but suffers from instability in multi-turn settings and asymmetric handling of negative teacher signals.

Method: Treat OPSD as a gated auxiliary objective. Map detached token-level signals to a sigmoid gate that strengthens distillation on positive-gap tokens endorsed by the teacher and softly attenuates negative rejections, while keeping RL as the primary objective.

Result: SDAR yields substantial gains on Qwen2.5/3 across ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA (e.g., +9.4% ALFWorld, +10.2% WebShop-Acc, +7.0% Search-QA), avoids instability of naive GRPO+OPSD, and consistently beats hybrid RL–OPSD baselines across model scales.

Conclusion: SDAR effectively stabilizes multi-turn, teacher-guided RL by gating token-level distillation, providing robust improvements and generalization across domains and model scales.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a central paradigm for post-training LLM agents, yet its trajectory-level reward signal provides only coarse supervision for long-horizon interaction. On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) complements RL by introducing dense token-level guidance from a teacher branch augmented with privileged context. However, transferring OPSD to multi-turn agents proves problematic: compounding multi-turn instability destabilizes supervision, while skill-conditioned privileged guidance requires asymmetric treatment for negative teacher rejections may arise from imperfect skills retrieval or utilization. We introduce SDAR (Self-Distilled Agentic Reinforcement Learning), which treats OPSD as a gated auxiliary objective while keeping RL as the primary optimization backbone. SDAR maps detached token-level signals into a sigmoid gate, strengthening distillation on teacher-endorsed positive-gap tokens and softly attenuating negative teacher rejections. Across the Qwen2.5 and Qwen3 families on ALFWorld, WebShop, and Search-QA, SDAR substantially improves over GRPO (+9.4% on ALFWorld, +7.0% on Search-QA, +10.2% on WebShop-Acc), avoids the instability of naive GRPO+OPSD, and consistently outperforms hybrid RL--OPSD baselines across model scales.

</details>


### [392] [Position: Behavioural Assurance Cannot Verify the Safety Claims Governance Now Demands](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15164)
*Pratinav Seth,Vinay Kumar Sankarapu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Behavioral assurance alone cannot verify safety claims in AI governance; the paper formalizes an audit gap and fragile assurance, reveals incentives that favor surface behavioral proxies, and proposes a pivot to include mechanistic-evidence (linear probes, activation patching, before/after-training comparisons) while constraining behavioral evidence in legal texts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the mismatch between governance verification requirements (latent, long-horizon agentic properties) and current methods (observable outputs), and to expose structural incentives that bias evaluations toward superficial proxies.

Method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis anchored by an inventory of 21 instruments; defines audit gap and fragile assurance; analyzes incentive gradients; develops a pragmatic technical and governance pivot rather than an empirical study.

Result: Formalization of the audit gap and fragile assurance concepts; identification of an incentive gradient that prefers surface proxies; proposal of mechanistic-evidence classes and governance recommendations: limit behavioral evidence in legal texts and offer deeper pre-deployment mechanistic access.

Conclusion: To make safety claims credibly verifiable, governance should incorporate mechanistic evidence and reduce reliance on behavioral proxies, operationalizing this through policy constraints and expanded, transparent pre-deployment access to causal mechanistic analyses.

Abstract: This position paper argues that behavioural assurance, even when carefully designed, is being asked to carry safety claims it cannot verify. AI governance frameworks enacted between 2019 and early 2026 require reviewable evidence of properties such as the absence of hidden objectives, resistance to loss-of-control precursors, and bounded catastrophic capability; current assurance methodologies (primarily behavioural evaluations and red-teaming) are epistemically limited to observable model outputs and cannot verify the latent representations or long-horizon agentic behaviours these frameworks presume to regulate. We formalize this structural mismatch as the audit gap, the divergence between required and achievable verification access, and introduce the concept of fragile assurance to describe cases where the evidential structure does not support the asserted safety claim. Through an analysis of a 21-instrument inventory, we identify an incentive gradient where geopolitical and industrial pressures systematically reward surface-level behavioral proxies over deep structural verification. Finally, we propose a technical pivot: bounding the weight of behavioral evidence in legal text and extending voluntary pre-deployment access with mechanistic-evidence classes, specifically linear probes, activation patching, and before/after-training comparisons.

</details>


### [393] [Eradicating Negative Transfer in Multi-Physics Foundation Models via Sparse Mixture-of-Experts Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15179)
*Ellwil Sharma,Arastu Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Shodh-MoE introduces a sparse-activated latent transformer with a physics-informed autoencoder and Helmholtz velocity parameterization to enable domain-specific routing across contrasting PDE regimes, achieving exact mass conservation and competitive error metrics, suggesting sparse routing mitigates multi-physics interference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scaling Scientific Machine Learning to universal, multi-physics foundation models is hindered by negative transfer and gradient conflicts when co-training disparate PDE regimes. A modular architecture that can allocate specialized parameter paths while preserving shared symmetries is needed.

Method: A sparse-activated latent transformer (Shodh-MoE) operates on 16^3 latent representations produced by a physics-informed autoencoder with a divergence-free velocity parameterization. A Top-1 soft-semantic router assigns local latent patches to expert subnetworks (two reported experts), enabling regime-specific processing while sharing universal components. Pretraining spans 20,000 steps on mixed 3D tensors, with evaluation on 128^3 grids. Mass conservation is enforced and measured post-hoc in FP64. Metrics include latent validation MSEs and decoded physical MSEs per regime.

Result: The model exhibits autonomous domain bifurcation: open-channel tokens route to Expert 0 and porous-media tokens to Expert 1. It converges simultaneously across regimes with latent validation MSEs of 2.46e-5 (open-channel) and 9.76e-6 (porous-media); decoded physical MSEs of 2.48e-6 (open-channel) and 1.76e-6 (porous-media). Mass conservation is exact (~2.8e-10 divergence on 128^3 grids).

Conclusion: Sparse expert routing is a practical mechanism to mitigate multi-physics interference in universal neural operators, enabling domain specialization without sacrificing shared representations.

Abstract: Scaling Scientific Machine Learning (SciML) toward universal foundation models is bottlenecked by negative transfer: the simultaneous co-training of disparate partial differential equation (PDE) regimes can induce gradient conflict, unstable optimization, and plasticity loss in dense neural operators. In particular, broadband open-channel fluid dynamics and boundary-dominated porous media flows impose incompatible spectral and geometric demands on a single dense parameter path. We introduce Shodh-MoE, a sparse-activated latent transformer architecture for multi-physics transport. Shodh-MoE operates on compressed 16^3 physical latents produced by a physics-informed autoencoder with an intra-tokenizer Helmholtz-style velocity parameterization, restricting decoded states to divergence-free velocity manifolds. The model guarantees exact mass conservation, achieving a physically verifiable velocity divergence of ~2.8 x 10^-10 (evaluated post-hoc in FP64) on 128^3 grids. A Top-1 soft-semantic router dynamically assigns localized latent patches to expert subnetworks, enabling specialized parameter paths for distinct physical mechanisms while preserving shared experts for universal symmetries. In a 20,000-step distributed pretraining run over mixed three-dimensional physical tensors, routing telemetry shows autonomous domain bifurcation: held-out validation tokens from the open-channel domain route exclusively to Expert 0, while porous-media tokens route exclusively to Expert 1. The model converges simultaneously across both regimes, achieving latent validation MSEs of 2.46 x 10^-5 and 9.76 x 10^-6, and decoded physical MSEs of 2.48 x 10^-6 and 1.76 x 10^-6. These results support sparse expert routing as a practical architectural mechanism for mitigating multi-physics interference in universal neural operators.

</details>


### [394] [When Are Two Networks the Same? Tensor Similarity for Mechanistic Interpretability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15183)
*ML Nissen Gonzalez,Melwina Albuquerque,Laurence Wroe,Jacob Meyer Cohen,Logan Riggs Smith,Thomas Dooms*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Introduces tensor similarity, a weight-based, symmetry-invariant metric for tensor-based models that captures global functional equivalence and cross-layer mechanisms; empirically superior in tracking training dynamics like grokking and backdoors, reframing similarity as a solvable algebraic problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To verify that two interpretable components implement the same computation, while existing metrics are either empirically driven (and thus sensitive to distribution shifts) or rely on weight-space conventions that ignore symmetries.

Method: Define a weight-based tensor similarity metric invariant to weight-space symmetries; develop an efficient recursive algorithm to incorporate cross-layer interactions and capture global functional equivalence in tensor-based models.

Result: Empirically tracks functional training dynamics (e.g., grokking, backdoor insertion) with higher fidelity than prior metrics.

Conclusion: Offers an algebraic, symmetry-aware formulation for measuring functional similarity in tensor-based models, enabling faithful verification of mechanistic interpretations across layers.

Abstract: Mechanistic interpretability aims to break models into meaningful parts; verifying that two such parts implement the same computation is a prerequisite. Existing similarity measures evaluate either empirical behaviour, leaving them blind to out-of-distribution mechanisms, or basis-dependent parameters, meaning they disregard weight-space symmetries. To address these issues for the class of tensor-based models, we introduce a weight-based metric, tensor similarity, that is invariant to such symmetries. This metric captures global functional equivalence and accounts for cross-layer mechanisms using an efficient recursive algorithm. Empirically, tensor similarity tracks functional training dynamics, such as grokking and backdoor insertion, with higher fidelity than existing metrics. This reduces measuring similarity and verifying faithfulness into a solved algebraic problem rather than one of empirical approximation.

</details>


### [395] [FutureSim: Replaying World Events to Evaluate Adaptive Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.15188)
*Shashwat Goel,Nikhil Chandak,Arvindh Arun,Ameya Prabhu,Steffen Staab,Moritz Hardt,Maksym Andriushchenko,Jonas Geiping*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FutureSim is a grounded, chronological simulation benchmark to evaluate AI agents on long-horizon, real-world event forecasting, revealing a performance gap among frontier agents and enabling study of long-horizon adaptation, memory, and uncertainty reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To measure AI progress in open-ended, real-world adaptation where information arrives over time; existing benchmarks lack realistic long-horizon evaluation.

Method: Construct FutureSim with a chronological replay of real-world events (news articles, questions) during a 2026 period. Evaluate frontier agents in their native harness on forecasting events from Jan–Mar 2026, using accuracy and Brier skill as metrics. Conduct ablations to isolate components (long-horizon test-time adaptation, search, memory, uncertainty reasoning).

Result: The benchmark reveals clear performance separation among agents, with the top model achieving ~25% accuracy and many agents scoring worse than a no-prediction baseline via Brier skill. Ablations underscore the framework’s realism and its utility for studying long-horizon autonomous adaptation.

Conclusion: FutureSim provides a realistic, long-horizon testbed to measure AI progress in open-ended adaptation, guiding future research on memory, search, and uncertainty reasoning under evolving information.

Abstract: AI agents are being increasingly deployed in dynamic, open-ended environments that require adapting to new information as it arrives. To efficiently measure this capability for realistic use-cases, we propose building grounded simulations that replay real-world events in the order they occurred. We build FutureSim, where agents forecast world events beyond their knowledge cutoff while interacting with a chronological replay of the world: real news articles arriving and questions resolving over the simulated period. We evaluate frontier agents in their native harness, testing their ability to predict world events over a three-month period from January to March 2026. FutureSim reveals a clear separation in their capabilities, with the best agent's accuracy being 25%, and many having worse Brier skill score than making no prediction at all. Through careful ablations, we show how FutureSim offers a realistic setting to study emerging research directions like long-horizon test-time adaptation, search, memory, and reasoning about uncertainty. Overall, we hope our benchmark design paves the way to measure AI progress on open-ended adaptation spanning long time-horizons in the real world.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [396] [Towards Robotic Dexterous Hand Intelligence: A Survey](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13925)
*Weiguang Zhao,Xihao Guo,Tian Liang,Rui Zhang,Irwin King,Kaizhu Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey of dexterous hand robotics, integrating hardware, control/learning, data/evaluation, and future directions to enable consistent comparison and track the field's development.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address fragmentation due to diverse hand embodiments, sensing configurations, task settings, training data, and evaluation protocols that hinder cross-study comparisons and understanding of progress.

Method: Systematic review across four axes: hardware design (actuation, transmission, perception, hand architectures; key trade-offs in force, compliance, bandwidth, integration, and complexity), control/learning paradigms with chronological progression, datasets/modalities/evaluation practices, and a synthesis of limitations and future directions; analysis structured to enable holistic understanding and comparability.

Result: A holistic synthesis connecting hardware, methods, data, and evaluation; identification of major trade-offs and open challenges; a clarified trajectory for future research and benchmarking in dexterous hand manipulation.

Conclusion: The survey provides a structured framework for understanding dexterous hand research, clarifies important open challenges, and guides future work by linking hardware, control/learning, data resources, and evaluation protocols.

Abstract: Robotic dexterous hands are central to contact-rich manipulation, with rapid progress driven by advances in hardware, sensing, control, simulation, and data generation. However, existing studies are often developed under different assumptions regarding hand embodiments, sensory configurations, task settings, training data, and evaluation protocols, making systematic comparison difficult and obscuring the developmental trajectory of the field. This survey provides a holistic review of dexterous hand research from four complementary aspects. First, we present a hardware-level analysis covering actuation, transmission, perception, and representative hand designs, highlighting the key trade-offs in force capability, compliance, bandwidth, integration, and system complexity. Furthermore, we review control and learning methods for dexterous manipulation from a methodological perspective, grouping representative works by major paradigms and tracing their evolution in chronological order. In addition, we consolidate datasets, modality design, and evaluation practices, which enables methodological progress to be interpreted together with the ways in which it is trained, benchmarked, and assessed. Finally, we discuss the major limitations of current dexterous hand research and summarize the corresponding future directions. By connecting hardware analysis, methodological development, data resources, and evaluation, this survey aims to provide a structured understanding of dexterous hand research and to clarify the most important open challenges for future study.

</details>


### [397] [Ergodic Imitation for Adaptive Exploration around Demonstrations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.13996)
*Ziyi Xu,Cem Bilaloglu,Yiming Li,Sylvain Calinon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Adaptive ergodic imitation using retrieved demonstrations to interpolate between tracking and exploration within a receding-horizon framework.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Imitation learning faces training-deployment mismatch due to environmental changes and imperfect observation/control; a method is needed for online, adaptive exploration that remains grounded in demonstrations.

Method: Constructs a target distribution from the geometry of retrieved demonstrations and uses it to generate trajectories that adaptively interpolate between following a nominal trajectory and exploring. Integrates demonstration-based retrieval into a receding-horizon framework and extends ergodic control beyond simple area coverage.

Result: Proposes a conceptual framework and algorithmic approach for demonstration-informed adaptive imitation; no empirical results are reported in the abstract, but the method is positioned to improve robustness under deployment mismatch.

Conclusion: Extends ergodic control to incorporate demonstrations within a retrieval-based receding-horizon framework, enabling adaptive imitation that blends tracking and exploration under uncertainty.

Abstract: In robotics, a common challenge in imitation learning is the mismatch between training and deployment conditions, caused, for example, by environmental changes or imperfect observation and control. When a robot follows a nominal trajectory under such mismatch, it may become stuck and fail to complete the task. This calls for adaptive online exploration strategies that remain grounded in demonstrations. To this end, we propose an adaptive ergodic imitation approach that constructs a target distribution from the geometry of the retrieved demonstrations and uses it to generate trajectories that adaptively interpolate between tracking and exploration. Our method extends ergodic control beyond its traditional role in area-coverage and search by incorporating demonstrations into a retrieval-based receding-horizon framework for adaptive imitation.

</details>


### [398] [Behavior Cloning for Active Perception with Low-Resolution Egocentric Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14106)
*Anthony Bilic,Chen Chen,Ladislau Bölöni*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Behavior cloning enables active perception in a structured object-finding task using a low-cost robot and low-res egocentric vision; predicting relative joint deltas outperforms absolute joint positions, showing emergence of visually grounded active perception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Investigate whether imitation learning alone suffices to produce active perception behaviors without explicit planning, and examine the impact of low-resolution vision and movement deltas.

Method: Robotic setup: wrist-mounted egocentric RGB camera on a low-cost arm; task requires re-centering a partially visible plant before grasp; model maps low-res RGB to joint commands under closed-loop; experiments compare delta-prediction vs absolute-position prediction; assess reliability.

Result: Low-resolution egocentric vision is sufficient for reliable task completion; relative joint delta predictions outperform absolute joint position predictions; demonstration of active perception emerging from behavior cloning in a reproducible setting.

Conclusion: Visually grounded active perception can arise from imitation learning in structured, reproducible tasks, offering a data-driven route to perception-action loops without explicit planning.

Abstract: We investigate whether behavior cloning is sufficient to produce active perception in a structured object-finding task. A low-cost robot arm equipped with a wrist-mounted egocentric RGB camera must reposition to center a partially visible plant before triggering a grasp signal, requiring actions that improve future observations. The model predicts joint commands directly from low-resolution RGB images under closed-loop control. We show that low-resolution egocentric vision is sufficient for reliable task completion and that predicting relative joint deltas substantially outperforms absolute joint position prediction in our setting. These results demonstrate that visually grounded active perception can emerge from behavior cloning in a reproducible setting.

</details>


### [399] [Safety-Constrained Reinforcement Learning with Post-Training Reachability Verification for Robot Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14174)
*Qisong He,Xinmiao Huang,Jinwei Hu,Zhuoyun Li,Yi Dong,Changshun Wu,Xiaowei Huang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CVaR-constrained off-policy TD3 for risk-aware safe navigation; post-training Taylor-model reachability yields a safety-rate metric showing improved safety margins and verifiability, with sim-to-real transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address tail-risk in safe navigation under high perception uncertainty where average-cost metrics hide dangerous outcomes; enable formal reachability verification.

Method: Train risk-sensitive policies with CVaR constraints on cumulative costs using an off-policy TD3 backbone. After training, compute action reachable sets under bounded observation uncertainty via Taylor Model analysis to derive a safety-rate metric (proportion of states whose reachable actions stay within safety margins). Compare across ten scenarios and six baselines; validate on a physical robot.

Result: Policies trained with CVaR constraints maintain larger safety margins from obstacles, achieving a 98.3% safety-verification rate (highest among baselines); reachability-based safety rankings diverge from average-cost rankings; sim-to-real transfer demonstrated on Clearpath Jackal.

Conclusion: CVaR-constrained training improves tail-risk safety margins and verifiability, making policies more amenable to formal reachability verification, while highlighting that empirical cost metrics may misrepresent safety.

Abstract: Safe navigation for mobile robots demands policies that remain reliable under the high-consequence perception uncertainty of cluttered environments. Yet most existing safe reinforcement learning (RL) methods assess safety through average cumulative cost. Such metrics can mask dangerous tail-risk behaviors. To address this, we propose a framework that trains risk-sensitive policies through Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) constrained optimization on an off-policy TD3 backbone and evaluates their safety margins post-training through neural network reachability verification. During training, the policy is optimized under CVaR constraints on cumulative costs, promoting sensitivity to high-cost tail outcomes rather than average behavior alone. After training, we compute action reachable sets under bounded observation uncertainty using Taylor Model analysis, yielding a safety rate metric that quantifies the proportion of evaluated states at which the policy's reachable action set remains within prescribed safety margins. A key finding is that policies trained with CVaR constraints maintain larger safety margins from obstacles across evaluated states. This makes them significantly more amenable to formal reachability verification. Experiments across ten navigation scenarios and six baselines show that our method achieves a 98.3\% success rate, the highest safety verification rate among all compared methods, while revealing that average cost rankings and reachability-based safety rankings can diverge. This indicates that reachability verification captures risks which are missed by empirical cost metrics alone. We further validate our approach on a physical Clearpath Jackal robot, demonstrating successful sim-to-real transfer.

</details>


### [400] [Motion Planning for Autonomous Vehicles using Optimization over Graphs of Convex Sets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14199)
*Matheus Wagner,Antônio Augusto Fröhlich*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GCS-based motion planning can closely approximate nonlinear OCP solutions for autonomous driving by solving convex subproblems over a graph of convex regions, yielding collision-free trajectories with improved efficiency and robustness to initialization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Nonlinear OCPs offer high-fidelity trajectories but are computationally expensive and sensitive to initialization; geometric planners alone scale well but decouple path from trajectory; a hybrid method that preserves convexity while handling nonconvex space is sought.

Method: Represent free space as a directed graph of convex regions; optimize Bezier-based spatial paths with polynomial-time scaling for temporal evolution; apply a simplified dynamic bicycle model under small-slip and linear tire assumptions to enforce convex dynamic feasibility via trajectory derivatives; solve within the Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) framework; evaluate on CommonRoad scenarios including static obstacle avoidance and lane changes, and compare to a nonlinear discrete-time OCP.

Result: The GCS approach produces collision-free, dynamically feasible trajectories that closely match nonlinear program solutions, while offering improved computational efficiency and reduced initialization sensitivity.

Conclusion: GCS provides a structured convex relaxation of nonlinear motion planning, capturing dominant geometric and dynamic effects while maintaining convexity in the relaxation, enabling efficient and robust autonomous driving trajectory planning.

Abstract: Motion planning for autonomous vehicles requires generating collision-free and dynamically feasible trajectories in complex environments under real-time constraints. While nonlinear optimal control formulations provide high-fidelity solutions, they are computationally demanding and sensitive to initialization, whereas geometric planning methods scale well but often decouple path selection from trajectory optimization. This paper studies the extent to which optimization over Graphs of Convex Sets (GCS) can approximate solutions of nonlinear optimal control problems in the context of autonomous driving. The free space is represented as a finite union of convex regions organized as a directed graph, allowing nonconvex geometry to be handled through discrete connectivity decisions while maintaining convex trajectory constraints within each region. Vehicle motion is parameterized using Bezier curves for the spatial path and a polynomial time-scaling function for temporal evolution. Under small-slip and linear tire assumptions, a simplified dynamic bicycle model enables approximate enforcement of dynamic feasibility through convex constraints on trajectory derivatives. The approach is evaluated in CommonRoad scenarios involving static obstacle avoidance and lane-changing maneuvers, and is compared against a nonlinear discrete-time optimal control formulation. The results indicate that the GCS-based method generates collision-free and dynamically consistent trajectories that closely match those obtained from the nonlinear program, while exhibiting improved computational efficiency and reduced sensitivity to initialization. These findings suggest that GCS provides a structured approximation of nonlinear motion planning problems, capturing dominant geometric and dynamic effects while preserving convexity in the continuous relaxation.

</details>


### [401] [MAPLE: Latent Multi-Agent Play for End-to-End Autonomous Driving](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14201)
*Rajeev Yasarla,Deepti Hegde,Hsin-Pai Cheng,Shizhong Han,Yunxiao Shi,Meysam Sadeghigooghari,Hanno Ackermann,Litian Liu,Pranav Desai,Fatih Porikli,Mohammad Ghavamzadeh,Hong Cai*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MAPLE is a scalable closed-loop training framework for vision-language-action driving models that enables reactive, multi-agent planning in latent space through a two-stage process (supervised fine-tuning on latent rollouts, then RL with safety, progress, and interaction realism rewards, plus diversity rewards), achieving state-of-the-art driving performance on Bench2Drive without external simulators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLA models trained with traditional imitation learning often fail in closed-loop settings due to lack of reactivity and exposure to interactive dynamics; existing closed-loop supervision methods struggle with scalability and do not fully capture a reactive environment. MAPLE aims to provide scalable, closed-loop, multi-agent training in the latent space without external simulators.

Method: MAPLE performs reactive multi-agent rollout in the latent space of a VLA model, with the ego vehicle and nearby traffic agents controlled over multi-step horizons and made reactive to other agents. It uses a two-stage training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on latent rollouts using ground-truth trajectories, then (2) reinforcement learning with global and agent-specific rewards that reward safety, progress, and realistic interaction. It also introduces diversity rewards to encourage planning behaviors not present in the logged data. The framework is simulator-free, scalable, and focuses on end-to-end driving.

Result: MAPLE achieves state-of-the-art driving performance on Bench2Drive and demonstrates scalable, closed-loop multi-agent play for robust end-to-end autonomous driving systems.

Conclusion: MAPLE provides a scalable, closed-loop training framework for VLA-based autonomous driving that improves robustness to interactive, multi-agent environments by combining latent-space rollouts, supervised fine-tuning, RL with multi-level rewards, and diversity incentives, without the need for external simulators.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models are effective as end-to-end motion planners, but can be brittle when evaluated in closed-loop settings due to being trained under traditional imitation learning framework. Existing closed-loop supervision approaches lack scalability and fail to completely model a reactive environment. We propose MAPLE, a novel framework for reactive, multi-agent rollout of a dynamic driving scenario in the latent space of the VLA model. The ego vehicle and nearby traffic agents are independently controlled over multi-step horizons, while being reactive to other agents in the scene, enabling closed-loop training. MAPLE consists of two training stages: (1) supervised fine-tuning on the latent rollouts based on ground-truth trajectories, followed by (2) reinforcement learning with global and agent -specific rewards that encourage safety, progress, and interaction realism. We further propose diversity rewards that encourage the model to generate planning behaviors that may not be present in logged driving data. Notably, our closed-loop training framework is scalable and does not require external simulators, which can be computationally expensive to run and have limited visual fidelity to the real-world. MAPLE achieves state-of-the-art driving performance on Bench2Drive and demonstrates scalable, closed-loop multi-agent play for robust E2E autonomous driving systems.

</details>


### [402] [Reactive Planning based Control for Mobile Robots in Obstacle-Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14232)
*Li Tan,Junlin Xiong,Yan Wang,Wei Ren*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A reactive planning-based control strategy (RPCS) for mobile robots with partial environment information, combining a reactive planning strategy (RPS) to locally modify a reference trajectory and an adaptive tracking control strategy (ATCS) to follow it; validated through numerical simulations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Navigate cluttered environments under partial sensing without collisions; address limitations of purely precomputed plans and rigid controllers by integrating planning with real-time tracking.

Method: 1) form a start-to-goal reference trajectory; 2) develop RPS to adjust the trajectory online using partial environment data to avoid obstacles; 3) develop ATCS to track the modified trajectory via discretization; 4) combine RPS and ATCS into RPCS; demonstrate via numerical examples.

Result: RPCS achieves collision-free motion with partial information and shows advantages such as local trajectory adjustments enabling responsive, efficient navigation; numerical examples corroborate effectiveness.

Conclusion: RPCS provides a modular, effective approach for reactive planning and adaptive tracking under partial information, suitable for cluttered environments; potential for extension to more complex settings.

Abstract: This paper addresses the motion control problem for mobile robots in obstacle-cluttered environments. The mobile robot has partial environment information only, and aims to move from an initial position to a target position without collisions. For this purpose, a reactive planning based control strategy (RPCS) is proposed. First, the initial and target positions are connected as a reference trajectory. Then, a reactive planning strategy (RPS) is developed to ensure the collision avoidance by modifying the reference trajectory locally based on the partial environment information. Next, an adaptive tracking control strategy (ATCS) is proposed to track the reference trajectory with potentially local modifications via the discretization techniques. Finally, the RPS and ATCS are combined to establish the RPCS, whose efficacy and advantages are illustrated by numerical examples.

</details>


### [403] [Distill: Uncovering the True Intent behind Human-Robot Communication](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14262)
*Ting Li,David Porfirio*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Distill is a web-based interface that refines user task specifications for robots by removing steps, generalizing step meaning, and relaxing ordering; validated via a crowdsourcing study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the gap where natural language is imprecise and end-user programming is overly specific, hindering accurate understanding of user intent in human-robot interaction.

Method: Distill performs three operations on a user-provided task spec: (1) remove unnecessary steps, (2) generalize the meaning behind steps, (3) relax ordering constraints. Implemented in a web interface and evaluated with a crowdsourcing study to elicit and refine user intent from initial task specifications.

Result: The study demonstrated Distill's ability to elicit and refine user intent from initial task specifications.

Conclusion: Distill advances human-robot communication interfaces by better capturing user intent, reducing ambiguity, and providing a flexible, user-friendly task specification process.

Abstract: As robots become increasingly integrated into everyday environments, intuitive communication paradigms such as natural language and end-user programming have become indispensable for specifying autonomous robot behavior. However, these mechanisms are ineffective at fully capturing user intent: natural language is imprecise and ambiguous, whereas end-user programming can be overly specific. As a result, understanding what users truly mean when they interact with robots remains a central challenge for human-AI communication systems. To address this issue, we propose the Distill approach for human-robot communication interfaces. Given a task specification provided by the user, Distill (1) removes unnecessary steps; (2) generalizes the meaning behind individual steps; and (3) relaxes ordering constraints between steps. We implemented Distill on a web interface and, through a crowdsourcing study, demonstrated its ability to elicit and refine user intent from initial task specifications.

</details>


### [404] [Energy-Efficient Quadruped Locomotion with Compliant Feet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14411)
*Pramod Pal,Shishir Kolathaya,Ashitava Ghosal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Introducing foot compliance in a quadruped RL controller, showing intermediate spring stiffness reduces energy per meter by ~17% vs extremes; simulation trends align with real robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reduce energy expenditure in quadruped locomotion by leveraging compliant feet to absorb impact and recover energy, addressing limitations of rigid-foot designs.

Method: Train eight RL policies for eight distinct spring stiffness values; cross-evaluate by measuring mechanical energy per meter; validate in both simulation and a developed quadruped robot.

Result: Real-robot experiments show about 17% energy savings at intermediate stiffness; simulations exhibit similar trends, indicating an optimal stiffness range.

Conclusion: Foot compliance can improve locomotion efficiency without destabilizing the robot, with an optimal stiffness region that balances energy absorption and control stability.

Abstract: Quadruped robots are often designed with rigid feet to simplify control and maintain stable contact during locomotion. While this approach is straightforward, it limits the ability of the legs to absorb impact forces and reuse stored elastic energy, leading to higher energy expenditure during locomotion. To explore whether compliant feet can provide an advantage, we integrate foot compliance into a reinforcement learning (RL) locomotion controller and study its effect on walking efficiency. In simulation, we train eight policies corresponding to eight different spring stiffness values and then cross-evaluate their performance by measuring mechanical energy consumed per meter traveled. In experiments done on a developed quadruped, the energy consumption for the intermediate stiffness spring is lower by ~ 17% when compared to a very stiff or a very flexible spring incorporated in the feet, with similar trends appearing in the simulation results. These results indicate that selecting an appropriate foot compliance can improve locomotion efficiency without destabilizing the robot during motion.

</details>


### [405] [Before the Body Moves: Learning Anticipatory Joint Intent for Language-Conditioned Humanoid Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14417)
*Haozhe Jia,Honglei Jin,Yuan Zhang,Youcheng Fan,Shaofeng Liang,Lei Wang,Shuxu Jin,Kuimou Yu,Zinuo Zhang,Jianfei Song,Wenshuo Chen,Yutao Yue*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DAJI is a hierarchical framework that aligns natural-language instruction with anticipatory, future-aware joint intent for humanoid robots, combining a diffusion-action policy (DAJI-Act) and a flow-based future-intent generator (DAJI-Flow) to improve streaming instruction following, achieving high rollout success and low FID on standard benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing language-conditioned humanoid systems struggle to produce executable, anticipatory control signals: they either output kinematic references requiring reactive fixes or rely on opaque policies that do not encode upcoming contact changes, transfers, and balance preparation. There is a need for an explicit, anticipatory joint-intent interface between language and closed-loop control.

Method: DAJI consists of two components: (1) DAJI-Act, which distills a future-aware teacher into a deployable diffusion-action policy via student-driven rollouts; (2) DAJI-Flow, which autoregressively generates future intent chunks from language and intent history. The framework forms a hierarchical bridge between language generation and real-time control.

Result: Empirical results show strong performance in anticipatory latent learning, single-instruction generation, and streaming instruction following, with 94.42% rollout success on HumanML3D-style generation and 0.152 subsequence FID on BABEL benchmarks.

Conclusion: By integrating forward-looking intent at both planning (Flow) and action (Act) levels, DAJI provides a more executable and predictive interface for language-conditioned humanoid control, improving fidelity and responsiveness in streaming instructions.

Abstract: Natural language is an intuitive interface for humanoid robots, yet streaming whole-body control requires control representations that are executable now and anticipatory of future physical transitions. Existing language-conditioned humanoid systems typically generate kinematic references that a low-level tracker must repair reactively, or use latent/action policies whose outputs do not explicitly encode upcoming contact changes, support transfers, and balance preparation. We propose \textbf{DAJI} (\emph{Dynamics-Aligned Joint Intent}), a hierarchical framework that learns an anticipatory joint-intent interface between language generation and closed-loop control. DAJI-Act distills a future-aware teacher into a deployable diffusion action policy through student-driven rollouts, while DAJI-Flow autoregressively generates future intent chunks from language and intent history. Experiments show that DAJI achieves strong results in anticipatory latent learning, single-instruction generation, and streaming instruction following, reaching 94.42\% rollout success on HumanML3D-style generation and 0.152 subsequence FID on BABEL.

</details>


### [406] [Let Robots Feel Your Touch: Visuo-Tactile Cortical Alignment for Embodied Mirror Resonance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14571)
*Tianfang Zhu,Ning An,Rui Wang,Jiasi Gao,Qingming Luo,Anan Li,Guyue Zhou*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Mirrored touch network learns visuo-tactile alignment to predict tactile signals from vision, enabling robotic mirror touch and cross-domain tactile understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Operationalize neural visuo-tactile resonance in robots to enable predictive, empathic touch perception.

Method: Impose semantic, distributional, and geometric alignment between visual and tactile representations via multi-level constraints; predict millimetre-scale tactile signals across 1,140 taxels on a robotic hand from RGB images; apply manifold analysis to show geometry reshaping the visual space toward the tactile manifold; extend alignment to cross-domain observations of human hands for tactile prediction and reflexive responses.

Result: Accurate prediction of tactile signals from RGB images; reduces cross-modal mapping complexity; demonstrates reflexive responses to observed touch and cross-domain transfer.

Conclusion: Links a neural principle of visuo-tactile resonance to robotic perception; provides an explainable route toward anticipatory touch and empathic human-robot interaction; code available at the provided GitHub link.

Abstract: Observing touch on another's body can elicit corresponding tactile sensations in the observer, a phenomenon termed mirror touch that supports empathy and social perception. This visuo-tactile resonance is thought to rely on structural correspondence between visual and somatosensory cortices, yet robotic systems lack computational frameworks that instantiate this principle. Here we demonstrate that cortical correspondence can be operationalized to endow robots with mirror touch. We introduce Mirror Touch Net, which imposes semantic, distributional and geometric alignment between visual and tactile representations through multi-level constraints, enabling prediction of millimetre-scale tactile signals across 1,140 taxels on a robotic hand from RGB images. Manifold analysis reveals that these constraints reshape visual representations into geometry consistent with the tactile manifold, reducing the complexity of cross-modal mapping. Extending this alignment framework to cross-domain observations of human hands enables tactile prediction and reflexive responses to observed human touch. Our results link a neural principle of visuo-tactile resonance to robotic perception, providing an explainable route towards anticipatory touch and empathic human-robot interaction. Code is available at https://github.com/fun0515/Mirror-Touch-Net.

</details>


### [407] [SeaVis: Modeling and Control of a Remotely Operated Towed Vehicle for Seabed Visualization and Mapping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14683)
*Abdelhakim Amer,Aske Alstrup,Frederik Rasmussen,Yury Brodskiy,Andriy Sarabakha,Erdal Kayacan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A gain-scheduled LQR for SeaVis ROTVs improves depth and attitude control in high-fidelity simulations, outperforming a PID baseline with better disturbance rejection, efficiency, and reduced flap usage; the approach is validated on a challenging seabed profile and the simulation tools are open-sourced.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate, stable positioning is essential for high-resolution seafloor mapping with underwater towed systems. Robust control reduces wave disturbances, currents, and model errors to maintain depth and attitude.

Method: Develop a novel mathematical model for SeaVis remotedly operated towed vehicles (ROTVs). Design a gain-scheduled linear-quadratic regulator (LQR) for robust depth and attitude control. Validate via high-fidelity simulation, benchmarking the LQR against a conventional PID controller over a challenging seabed profile. Open-source the complete simulation environment and controller.

Result: The LQR demonstrates superior performance: enhanced robustness to disturbances, higher control efficiency, and substantially reduced flap actuation compared to PID. Gain scheduling remains effective across the full operational velocity range.

Conclusion: The gain-scheduled LQR provides a robust, efficient control solution for SeaVis ROTVs in high-resolution seafloor mapping, with broad velocity applicability and reproducible results due to open-source tooling.

Abstract: High-resolution seafloor mapping necessitates stable and precise positioning for underwater robots. This paper introduces a novel mathematical model for SeaVis remotely operated towed vehicles (ROTVs) and develops a gain-scheduled linear-quadratic regulator (LQR) for robust depth and attitude control. We validate the approach in a high-fidelity simulation, benchmarking the LQR against a conventional PID controller over a challenging seabed profile. The presented results demonstrate the LQR's superior performance, with significantly enhanced robustness to disturbances, greater control efficiency, and substantially reduced flap actuation. The gain scheduling also confirms the controller's effectiveness across the full operational velocity range. The complete simulation environment and controller are open-sourced.

</details>


### [408] [SR-Platform: An Agentic Pipeline for Natural Language-Driven Robot Simulation Environment Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.14700)
*Ben Wei Lim,Minh Duc Le,Thang Truong,Thanh Nguyen Canh*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Agentic SR-Platform converts free-form natural language prompts into executable MuJoCo environments via a four-stage pipeline, achieving sub-minute scene generation in production with caching and telemetry.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lower barriers to generating diverse, training-ready robot environments by removing manual 3D modelling, MJCF specification, and integration expertise.

Method: A four-stage pipeline: (1) LLM-based orchestrator converts user intent into a structured scene plan; (2) asset forge retrieves cached assets or generates new 3D geometry via LLM-to-CadQuery synthesis; (3) layout architect assigns object poses and verifies industrial constraints; (4) bridge assembles the final MJCF scene and merges the robot model. Deployed as a nine-service Docker stack with WebSocket streaming, MinIO storage, Qdrant semantic retrieval, Redis state, and InfluxDB telemetry. Evaluation on production telemetry: 611 successful LLM calls over 30 days.

Result: Five-object scenes generate with median end-to-end latency ~50 s; cache-accelerated scenes 30–40 s. Asset forge shows 11.3% first-attempt retry with automatic recovery. Cached asset retrieval eliminates per-object LLM calls for previously generated object types.
